title: The Boy Who Couldn't Breathe
content:
The jade testing stone pulsed with a soft, ethereal light as Shen He pressed his palm against its cool surface. The crowd of disciples gathered in the Grand Assembly Hall held their breath, their eyes fixed on the boy who stood at the center of the marble platform.

Shen He's heart hammered against his ribs. He'd waited fifteen years for this moment—the Meridian Awakening Ceremony, where every disciple of the Azure Cloud Sect discovered their cultivation potential. His mother had been a peak-level alchemist before she disappeared. His father had been an Elder of the Sect. Surely, surely, the heavens wouldn't curse their only son with crippled meridians.

The stone flickered once. Twice.

Then nothing.

Elder Zhao's weathered face twisted into a mask of barely concealed contempt. He lifted the testing stone high for all to see, displaying its dead, gray surface to the hundreds of disciples packed into the hall.

"Shen He," the Elder's voice boomed across the chamber, carrying notes of theatrical disappointment, "your meridians are completely sealed. No qi flows through your channels. You have failed the Awakening."

Gasps rippled through the crowd. Whispers spread like wildfire.

"The Cripple."

"His mother was a genius. How did she produce such garbage?"

"Maybe the rumors about his father were true—tainted bloodline."

Shen He's fists clenched at his sides, his knuckles white. He kept his face neutral, a mask he'd perfected over years of disappointment. But inside, something cracked.

"I don't understand," he said, his voice steady despite the tremor in his hands. "My mother was Grandmaster Yao. She created the Phoenix Rebirth Pill. My father—"

"Your father abandoned this sect before you were born," Elder Zhao interrupted coldly, setting the testing stone aside with deliberate carelessness. "And your mother's talents clearly did not pass to you. The meridians are the foundation of cultivation. Without them, you are—"

"Worthless," someone in the crowd finished.

Laughter erupted.

Shen He turned slowly. The speaker was Wei Chen, a disciple from the Outer Sect who'd never missed an opportunity to remind Shen He of his inadequacy. Wei Chen had awakened with three open meridians—average, but infinitely better than zero.

"Outer Disciple Shen He," Elder Zhao continued, consulting a jade slip, "your enrollment in the Azure Cloud Sect is hereby terminated. By sect law, those who cannot cultivate must be expelled to maintain the purity of our teachings. You have three days to vacate the premises."

Three days. Fifteen years of training, of memorizing pill recipes and herb classifications, of hoping that somehow his bloodline would manifest—all reduced to a three-day eviction notice.

Shen He bowed stiffly. "I understand, Elder."

He didn't wait for dismissal. He turned and walked away, his footsteps echoing against the marble floor, each step carrying him further from the only home he'd ever known. Behind him, the whispers grew louder.

"Good riddance."

"At least the kitchens will have one less mouth to feed."

"Maybe he'll find work as a herb picker. That's all he's good for."

Shen He's quarters were sparse—a narrow room in the Outer Sect dormitory, furnished with a wooden bed, a writing desk, and a small cauldron he'd inherited from his mother. He'd never known her; she'd disappeared on an expedition when he was three. But the cauldron was all he had left of her, a copper vessel etched with alchemical arrays that still pulsed with faint warmth after all these years.

He sat on the edge of his bed, staring at his hands. They were steady. Trained. He'd spent years learning to identify herbs by touch, by smell, by the subtle vibrations they emitted when ground between his fingers. He knew the exact temperature required to extract the essence of a Crimson Lotus petal. He knew that Fire-Spirit Grass had to be crushed with a mortar carved from Cold Jade, or it would lose seventy percent of its potency.

But none of that mattered. Without qi, without cultivation, he was just a cook with fancy equipment.

The rage came suddenly, a hot wave that crashed through his carefully constructed walls. He grabbed the nearest object—a ceramic jar of dried Moonlight Blossoms—and hurled it against the wall.

It shattered.

Petals scattered across the floor.

And something else fell out.

A small, leather-bound journal, its pages yellowed with age. It had been wedged between the jar and the shelf, hidden so deliberately that Shen He knew immediately who had put it there.

His mother's handwriting.

He recognized it from the few documents she'd left behind—the elegant, precise strokes of a master alchemist who'd spent decades recording recipes. He opened the journal with trembling fingers.

The first page read:

*To my son, if you're reading this, you've discovered my secret. The Awakening Ceremony is a lie. The meridians are not the only path to power. True cultivation begins not with the breath, but with consumption.*

Shen He's breath caught in his throat. He read on, his eyes devouring each word.

*The body can absorb qi through the stomach as easily as through the lungs. Meridians are merely channels—convenient, but not necessary. I have spent my life perfecting a method of pill refinement that bypasses the need for open meridians entirely. The pills I create don't simply enhance cultivation—they are cultivation, condensed into physical form.*

*But there is a price. The body must be conditioned to accept this foreign qi. One wrong ingredient, one incorrect dosage, and the results are... catastrophic. I have included my research in these pages. Use it wisely, my son.*

*And remember: they will call you cripple. They will call you worthless. But when you surpass them all, when you stand at the peak of the cultivation world with a pill in one hand and a cauldron in the other, you will understand the truth.*

*The strongest cultivators are not born. They are forged.*

Shen He read the entire journal in one sitting. It took four hours. When he finished, the sun had set, and his quarters were bathed in moonlight.

The journal contained recipes—dozens of them, ranging from simple Body Tempering Pills to complex Soul Refining Elixirs that could reshape a cultivator's very being. But one recipe caught his attention immediately.

*The Qi Absorption Pill (Basic)*

*Ingredients:*
- *1 Crimson Lotus petal (crushed)*
- *3 drops of Moondew Essence*
- *5 grams of Ground Spirit Root*
- *1 Fire-Spirit Grass stem (burned to ash)*
- *A single drop of the user's blood*

*Instructions: Combine ingredients in a cold vessel. Heat to exactly 98 degrees—no higher, no lower. Stir counter-clockwise for 27 rotations. Allow to cool for 3 minutes. Consume immediately.*

*Warning: The first pill will feel like swallowing fire. Your body will reject the foreign qi. You must force it to accept. If you fail, the backlash will seal your meridians permanently—if they weren't already sealed.*

Shen He smiled grimly. He had nothing to lose. He was already expelled, already labeled a cripple, already destined for a life of mediocrity and shame. What was the worst that could happen?

Death, he supposed. But even death seemed preferable to the life awaiting him outside the sect.

He gathered the ingredients from his personal stores. His mother's journal had given him a lifetime of knowledge, but she'd also left behind a small collection of rare herbs, carefully preserved and hidden throughout his quarters. He'd always thought they were gifts from anonymous well-wishers, tokens of pity for the cripple who couldn't cultivate.

Now he knew the truth. They were provisions for this exact moment.

The cauldron hummed as he activated its arrays, pouring a small stream of his own qi into the activation matrix. Wait—he didn't have qi. How was the cauldron responding?

He looked closer. The arrays weren't absorbing his qi. They were resonating with his blood, pulsing in time with his heartbeat. His mother had designed the cauldron to work with mortal energy, bypassing the need for cultivation entirely.

The genius of it made his head spin.

He crushed the Crimson Lotus petal between two smooth stones, watching as its vibrant red essence seeped out like liquid fire. He measured three drops of Moondew Essence with a dropper made from carved bone, each drop catching the moonlight and refracting it into tiny rainbows. The Ground Spirit Root he ground into a fine powder, the earthy scent filling his small room.

The Fire-Spirit Grass was the trickiest part. He had to burn it to ash without losing its essence, a process that required precise temperature control. He held the stem over a candle flame, rotating it slowly, watching as it blackened and crumbled.

When it was done, he had a small pile of gray ash that seemed to shimmer with internal light.

He pricked his finger with a needle and added a single drop of blood to the mixture. It sizzled on contact, the ingredients combining into a thick, viscous paste that smelled of ozone and wildflowers.

The cauldron's temperature read exactly 98 degrees. He scraped the paste into a small copper dish and set it over the heat source, stirring counter-clockwise with a wooden rod.

One rotation. Two. Three.

The paste bubbled and hissed, releasing steam that curled into strange, geometric shapes. He counted each rotation carefully, his lips moving silently.

Twenty-seven.

He removed the dish from the heat and waited three minutes, watching as the paste solidified into a single, perfect pill. It was the size of a marble, dark red with veins of silver running through it like lightning trapped in stone.

It smelled like power.

Shen He didn't hesitate. He popped the pill into his mouth and swallowed.

The fire hit him instantly.

It wasn't metaphorical. It wasn't a sensation of heat. It was actual, physical fire, roaring through his chest, his stomach, his veins. He doubled over, clutching his abdomen, his vision going white.

The pain was excruciating. It felt like his insides were being ripped apart and reassembled, his bones breaking and mending in the same breath, his blood boiling and freezing simultaneously.

*Your body will reject the foreign qi*, his mother's words echoed in his mind. *You must force it to accept.*

He focused through the pain, concentrating on the burning sensation, willing it to spread through his body instead of consuming him. He imagined his cells opening up, drinking in the qi like parched earth absorbing rain.

The fire began to change.

It spread through his limbs, his fingers, his toes. It crawled up his spine and settled behind his eyes. And then, suddenly, it stopped hurting.

He felt... full. Like he'd been empty his entire life and never known it. Like he'd been walking around with a hole in his soul, and now, finally, it was filled.

He opened his eyes.

The world looked different. Brighter. He could see the qi swirling in the air around him, tiny motes of golden light that danced and spun like fireflies. He could feel the life force of the plants growing outside his window, the ambient energy of the earth beneath his feet.

He raised his hand and clenched his fist. Power surged through him—raw, untamed, intoxicating.

He was no longer a cripple.

He was a cultivator.

No, he realized with a start. He was something more. The pill hadn't just opened his meridians—it had created new pathways, bypassing the old ones entirely. His body was now a vessel for qi, absorbing it through

title: A Pill for Every Problem
content:
The morning sun cast long shadows across the Outer Sect training grounds, where disciples moved through sword forms like a river of flowing silk. Shen He watched them from the shade of an ancient pine, his fingers absently tracing the pattern of a pill formula on his thigh.

*They breathe. I refine.*

Three days had passed since the Elder Council's verdict. Three days since Shen He had been officially declared meridian-crippled, relegated to the Sect's lowest status—a pill boy who would never cultivate. The humiliation should have crushed him. Instead, it had clarified something he'd always suspected: the path to power wasn't always straight.

His stomach growled.

Not from hunger—from the Morning Dew Pills he'd taken at dawn. Three of them, crushed and dissolved in tea, their qi spreading through his body like warm honey through cracks in parched earth. The sensation was nothing like the descriptions in cultivation manuals. No resonant chakras. No flowing rivers of power. Just... absorption. Quiet and inevitable, like water seeping into stone.

"Shen He!"

The voice cut through his thoughts like a blade. He turned to find Elder Feng approaching, his gray robes billowing despite the still air. The old alchemist's face was pinched with barely contained fury.

"You were supposed to deliver the Foundation Pills to the Inner Sect an hour ago."

Shen He rose, brushing pine needles from his worn trousers. "I was told the batch needed more time. The third refinement was incomplete."

"The batch is fine. You're stalling."

"Elder Feng, with respect, I've been studying pill refinement for three years. The volatility index was—"

"I don't care about your theories." Feng's voice dropped to a venomous whisper. "You're a pill boy. Your job is to follow instructions, not question them. Deliver the pills. Now."

Shen He inclined his head, the gesture costing him more than the elder would ever know. "As you command."

He walked toward the alchemy pavilion, his steps measured and calm. Inside, he was counting. Counting the pills he had left. Calculating the rate of his absorption. Projecting when he would need more.

The pavilion's interior was dim, lit only by the glow of formation arrays beneath the cauldrons. Fourteen pill refiners worked in silent concentration, their movements precise and economical. Shen He moved to his station—the smallest, furthest from the ventilation arrays—and checked the pill furnace.

Forty-seven Foundation Pills, each the size of a pearl, gleamed in their ceramic dish. Standard quality. Acceptable for Inner Sect disciples who wouldn't know good alchemy if it bit them on their superior qi pathways.

He packed the pills into a jade box, sealing it with a preservation talisman. The walk to the Inner Sect would take twenty minutes. Plenty of time.

*Plenty of time to think.*

The path wound through the Sect's middle grounds, past gardens where Spirit Herbs grew in geometrically perfect beds. Shen He's fingers itched to touch them—to examine their qi patterns, to test their reactions to different refinement temperatures. But he was a pill boy. Pill boys didn't touch Spirit Herbs. They only processed what the alchemists gave them.

Unless they found their own.

He'd discovered the patch three months ago, hidden in a crevice where two cliffs met. Moonlight Lotus, Spirit Bamboo Shoots, even a single stalk of Thousand-Year Ginseng—all growing wild, untended, their qi concentrated by the natural formation of the terrain. He'd harvested carefully, never taking more than a third of any plant, and he'd refined them in secret, using techniques he'd developed himself.

The result was a stash of pills that would make an Inner Sect elder weep with envy. Meridian Opening Pills that actually worked. Qi Condensation Pills that doubled a cultivator's absorption rate. And his masterpiece: a pill he'd tentatively named the Body-Shattering Pill, designed to force a body to accept qi through any means necessary.

He hadn't tested it yet. The calculations suggested a 73% chance of survival.

*Good odds for a cripple.*

"The delivery," said a voice like grinding stone.

Shen He looked up. The Inner Sect's gate stood before him, guarded by two disciples in blue silk robes. Their auras pulsed with the pressure of the Qi Condensation realm—the stage Shen He should have reached years ago if his meridians had been functional.

*If. If. If.*

"I have forty-seven Foundation Pills for Elder Zhou," Shen He said, holding up the jade box.

The taller guard sniffed. "You're the cripple. The one who tried to cultivate."

"I'm the pill boy. The delivery is on schedule."

"Schedule." The guard laughed, a harsh sound. "You think schedules matter to Inner Sect disciples? We don't wait for trash."

Shen He's jaw tightened. "The pills will degrade if left in this box too long. The preservation talisman only holds for—"

"I don't care about your talisman." The guard stepped closer, his qi pressing against Shen He like a physical weight. "You want to enter the Inner Sect? Prove you're worth our time."

The challenge hung in the air. Shen He could feel the other disciples watching, their attention drawn by the commotion. A crowd was gathering, drawn by the promise of entertainment.

*They want to see me humiliated.*

Shen He had two choices. Back down, deliver the pills through an intermediary, and accept the loss of face. Or accept the challenge and risk exposure.

He chose option three.

"Worth your time?" He reached into his robe and pulled out a small ceramic vial. "I have something better than Foundation Pills. A Meridian Opening Pill of my own refinement. Guaranteed to work on any cultivator below the Core Formation stage."

The guard's eyes widened. "Impossible. Only elders can refine Meridian Opening Pills."

"Then consider this a demonstration of my skills." Shen He held out the vial. "Take it. Test it. If it works, you'll remember who gave it to you."

Silence. The guard looked at the vial, then at Shen He, then back at the vial. His hand moved slowly, almost reverently.

"No," said a cold voice. "I'll take it."

The crowd parted. A young man stepped forward, his robes embroidered with silver thread that formed the image of a coiled dragon. His face was sharp, aristocratic, and his eyes held the calculating gleam of a predator.

*Lin Chen. The Sect Master's son.*

Shen He's blood ran cold. Lin Chen was everything he wasn't: talented, wealthy, powerful. A disciple of the Sword Peak, already at the peak of Qi Condensation at age nineteen. And he had a reputation for cruelty that bordered on artistic.

"A Meridian Opening Pill from a cripple?" Lin Chen's smile was thin and dangerous. "This I have to see."

He took the vial, uncorked it, and sniffed. His expression flickered—surprise? Interest?—before settling back into disdain.

"Interesting aroma. But I doubt it's genuine."

"Only one way to find out," Shen He said, keeping his voice steady.

Lin Chen tilted his head. "And if it's a fake? If this pill damages my cultivation? What then?"

"Then you'll have me executed. The Sect would burn a cripple for trying to poison the Sect Master's son."

Gasps rippled through the crowd. Lin Chen's smile widened.

"You have courage. I'll give you that." He brought the pill to his lips. "Let's see if you have skill."

He swallowed.

For a moment, nothing happened. Then Lin Chen's eyes went wide. His body tensed, qi flaring around him like a storm. The crowd stepped back as pressure waves radiated from his position, shattering a nearby lantern and cracking the stone beneath his feet.

"Impossible," someone whispered.

But it was happening. Lin Chen's aura was expanding, strengthening, the strands of his cultivation base thickening and deepening as the pill's effects spread through his body. His meridians, long since opened and trained, were being forced to expand further, to accept more qi, to grow.

The process took thirty seconds. When it was over, Lin Chen stood in the center of a cracked circle of stone, his robes billowing, his eyes bright with power.

He had advanced. Two minor realms, maybe three. In the space of a single pill.

The crowd erupted.

Shen He didn't wait for the chaos to settle. He turned and walked away, leaving the jade box of Foundation Pills on the ground. Let them have their mediocre pills. He had just demonstrated something far more valuable.

*My pills work.*

Back in the alchemy pavilion, Elder Feng was waiting. His face was pale, his hands trembling.

"What did you do?" he hissed. "Lin Chen is telling everyone. The Sect Master has called for a full investigation. They're going to find—"

"They're going to find a pill boy who knows his craft," Shen He interrupted. "Nothing more."

"Your craft? Your craft is sweeping floors and carrying cauldrons! You're not an alchemist! You're not even a cultivator!"

"Then how did I refine that pill?"

Feng's mouth opened and closed. No sound came out.

Shen He stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "I've been refining pills for three years, Elder. Better pills than any in the Sect's stores. I've been studying the ancient texts, the lost formulas, the forbidden techniques. And I've been getting stronger."

"Stronger?" Feng laughed, hysterical. "You're meridian-crippled! You can't cultivate!"

"No. I can't *breathe* qi. But I can *absorb* it. Through pills. Through elixirs. Through anything I refine." He held up his hand, and for a moment, a faint golden light flickered at his fingertips. "I've been building a cultivation base through alchemy alone. And it's working."

Feng stared at the light. His face went through a series of expressions—disbelief, fear, calculation—before settling on something that looked almost like respect.

"How far?" he asked.

"I don't know. But I'm close to a breakthrough. A pill that could change everything."

"You mean the Body-Shattering Pill."

Shen He's eyes narrowed. "How did you know about that?"

"I'm the head alchemist, boy. I know when ingredients are being stolen. I know when experiments are being conducted in secret. I've been watching you for months, waiting to see if you'd make a mistake."

"And now?"

Feng's expression hardened. "And now I'm giving you a choice. Share your formula with me, and I'll protect you from the Sect Master's investigation. Refuse, and I'll tell them everything. The secret pills. The stolen ingredients. The forbidden experiments."

The threat hung in the air like a blade.

Shen He considered his options. He could fight, but Feng was a Qi Condensation cultivator with decades of experience. He could run, but where would he go? The Sect controlled everything within a hundred miles.

Or he could lie.

"Fine," Shen He said, letting his shoulders slump. "I'll share the formula. But I need time to write it down. The refinement process is... complex."

Feng smiled, a predator's smile. "Take all the time you need. I'll be waiting."

He left the pavilion, his robes swishing against the stone floor. Shen He watched him go, counting his heartbeats.

*One. Two. Three.*

When the door closed, he moved.

His stash was hidden in the ceiling, in a hollowed-out beam that no one had ever thought to check.

title: The Price of a Perfect Pill
content:
The dawn light crept through the cracks in Shen He’s shuttered window like fingers of gold, but he paid it no mind. His world had narrowed to a single, trembling point of focus: the pill furnace before him.

Three days. He’d been refining for three straight days without sleep, without food, without so much as a sip of water. His robes were stained with sweat and herb residue, and his hands—steady as a surgeon’s when he’d started—now shook with exhaustion. But the pill inside that furnace was close. So close he could taste it.

The *Meridian Restoration Pill*.

Elder Wu had called it impossible. The sect’s master alchemist had laughed in Shen He’s face when he’d proposed the formula. “A cripple dreaming of alchemy? You’d have better luck growing wings.”

But Shen He had seen the ancient scroll hidden in the library’s restricted section. The formula was real. Its ingredients were rare, expensive, and dangerous—the last of his savings had gone to procure them through a back-alley dealer in the outer city. And if it worked, if he could actually refine a Meridian Restoration Pill, it would change everything.

It would *fix* him.

The furnace hummed, a low vibration that rattled his teeth. Shen He pressed his palm against its bronze surface, feeling the heat ripple through his meridians. Or rather, where his meridians should have been. The doctors had called him “meridian-crippled” at birth—a polite way of saying his qi channels were knotted, twisted, and utterly useless for cultivation. No amount of breathing exercises, meditation, or spiritual guidance could force qi through them.

But pills were different. Pills bypassed the meridians entirely. They dissolved in the stomach, seeped into the blood, and delivered their power directly to the dantian—the energy core every cultivator possessed, even the broken ones.

Shen He had discovered this at age twelve, when he’d stolen a Qi Gathering Pill from the sect’s infirmary. The elders had been too busy celebrating a prodigy’s breakthrough to notice the crippled boy swallowing their precious medicine. And for one glorious moment, Shen He had felt qi—real, flowing, *alive* qi—surge through his body. It had lasted only a few seconds before his twisted meridians strangled it.

But those seconds had been enough to change his life.

He’d spent the next six years learning everything about alchemy. Not from the sect’s masters, who refused to teach a cripple, but from stolen scrolls, discarded notes, and patient observation. He’d learned to identify herbs by their qi signatures, to control furnace temperatures with nothing but his will, to sense the exact moment when ingredients transformed from raw material into medicine. He’d become an alchemist in secret, refining pills in the dead of night, swallowing them in desperate hope that each one would be the key.

None had been. But this one—this Meridian Restoration Pill—was different.

The furnace shuddered. Shen He’s heart leaped.

“Almost,” he whispered, his voice cracked from disuse. “Just a little more.”

He fed his own qi into the furnace, a trickle of power drawn from the pills he’d stockpiled over the years. His dantian held enough energy to refine a dozen normal pills, but this one was a monster. It devoured qi like a starving beast, demanding more and more as the ingredients merged and transformed.

The furnace glowed red, then orange, then white-hot. Shen He gritted his teeth, pouring everything he had into it. Sweat evaporated from his skin. The air shimmered with heat. The pill inside—he could sense it now, a swirling nexus of potential, a star being born in a crucible of bronze.

*Just a little more.*

A crack appeared in the furnace’s surface. Then another. The bronze groaned, splitting under the pressure. Shen He’s eyes went wide. No, no, no—he was too close to fail now. He pushed harder, ignoring the screaming protest of his qi reserves, ignoring the smoke rising from the furnace, ignoring the faint smell of his own flesh beginning to burn.

The pill was forming. He could see it in his mind’s eye: a perfect sphere of gold and jade, pulsing with restorative power. It would fix his meridians. It would let him cultivate. It would make him *whole*.

The furnace exploded.

Shen He was thrown backward, slamming into the wall with a force that knocked the breath from his lungs. Shards of bronze rained around him, some embedding themselves in his arms and chest. The room filled with smoke and the acrid smell of failure.

But through it all, he saw it: a single, glowing pill, hovering in the air where the furnace had been.

He reached for it, ignoring the blood dripping down his face, ignoring the screaming pain in his ribs. His fingers closed around the pill—

And it crumbled to dust.

Shen He stared at the gray powder trickling through his fingers. For a long moment, he didn’t move. Didn’t breathe. The weight of failure pressed down on him, heavier than any physical wound.

“No,” he said, his voice hollow. “No, no, no—”

He’d done everything right. The formula, the ingredients, the timing—all perfect. But the furnace hadn’t been strong enough. He’d needed a higher-grade furnace, one designed for peak-level pill refinement, but those cost more spirit stones than he’d ever seen in his life.

So he’d used a cheap one. And it had failed.

Shen He slumped against the wall, staring at the wreckage of his room. The explosion had destroyed everything: his remaining ingredients, his notes, his other pills. Six years of work, gone in an instant.

A knock at the door made him flinch.

“Shen He?” A familiar voice, sharp with impatience. “Open up. The sect master wants to see you.”

Elder Wu.

Of course. The explosion must have been noticed. Shen He looked down at himself: bloodied, burned, covered in soot. He looked like a corpse that had crawled out of a fire.

“Give me a moment,” he called, his voice steadier than he felt.

He dragged himself to his feet, wincing as his injured ribs protested. The pain was familiar, almost comforting. He’d lived with pain his whole life. It was the one constant he could rely on.

He found a clean robe—the only one the explosion hadn’t destroyed—and pulled it on. Then he opened the door.

Elder Wu stood outside, flanked by two disciples. The elder’s face was a mask of cold disapproval, his gray robes immaculate, his beard trimmed to perfection. He looked at Shen He the way one might look at a cockroach that had crawled onto a banquet table.

“The sect master is waiting,” Elder Wu said. “You will follow me. Now.”

Shen He didn’t argue. He followed, limping slightly, leaving the ruins of his room behind.

The sect master’s hall was at the top of the mountain, a vast chamber carved into the living rock. Pillars of jade supported a ceiling painted with scenes of ancient cultivators battling sky-beasts. The floor was polished obsidian, black as a starless night, reflecting the light of a thousand lanterns.

Sect Master Yun sat on a throne of white stone, his expression unreadable. He was an old man, but ageless, his face smooth as a child’s, his eyes deep as wells. He’d reached the Nascent Soul stage, a level of power that Shen He could barely comprehend.

Beside him stood a young woman in blue robes, a sword at her hip. She looked at Shen He with undisguised contempt.

*Lin Xue.* The sect’s prodigy. The one who had achieved Foundation Establishment at sixteen, the one everyone whispered would become a grandmaster before she was thirty. She was beautiful in the way a blade was beautiful: sharp, cold, and deadly.

“Shen He,” the sect master said, his voice carrying no emotion. “You have been accused of stealing restricted materials from the sect’s library.”

Shen He’s blood ran cold. “I—what?”

“The Meridian Restoration Pill formula,” Elder Wu said, stepping forward. “It was locked in the restricted section. Only senior alchemists have access. And yet, you somehow obtained it.”

“I found it,” Shen He said. “It was—I was cleaning the library, and it fell from a shelf—”

“Liar,” Lin Xue said, her voice like ice. “I saw you in the restricted section three weeks ago. You were copying the scroll.”

Shen He’s heart stopped. She’d seen him. Of course she had. Nothing escaped the sect’s prodigy.

“I can explain,” he said, but his voice came out weak, pathetic.

“Explain what?” Elder Wu sneered. “That a cripple thought he could perform alchemy beyond his station? That he stole from the sect that took him in out of charity? That he—“

“Enough.” The sect master raised a hand, and Elder Wu fell silent. “Shen He. You have always been... an anomaly. A boy with no cultivation potential, yet one who shows remarkable knowledge of alchemy. I have turned a blind eye to your activities, hoping you would find your place in the sect’s support staff.”

He leaned forward, and suddenly the weight of his presence pressed down on Shen He like a mountain.

“But this cannot be ignored. The Meridian Restoration Pill is a forbidden formula. It was locked away for a reason. Do you know why?”

Shen He shook his head, unable to speak.

“Because it doesn’t work,” the sect master said. “The ancient alchemists who created it were fools who thought they could rewrite the laws of heaven. They failed. And everyone who has tried to replicate their work has failed as well. The pill does not restore meridians. It destroys them. It kills the user.”

Shen He’s legs gave out. He fell to his knees, the words hitting him harder than any physical blow.

“I didn’t know,” he whispered.

“Ignorance is no excuse,” Lin Xue said. “You stole from the sect. You attempted a forbidden refinement. And you nearly blew up a quarter of the outer compound. The punishment is clear.”

“Exile,” Elder Wu said, his voice dripping with satisfaction. “You are banished from the Azure Cloud Sect, effective immediately.”

Shen He looked up at the sect master, hoping for mercy. He found none.

“You have one hour to collect your belongings,” the sect master said. “After that, you are no longer a disciple of this sect. Should you return, you will be killed on sight.”

The words hung in the air, final as a death sentence.

Shen He rose to his feet. His body ached. His heart ached. But something else stirred inside him—a cold, hard anger that he’d never felt before.

“Fine,” he said, his voice steady. “I’ll go.”

He turned and walked out of the hall, feeling their eyes on his back. He didn’t look back.

An hour later, Shen He stood at the sect’s gates, a small bag slung over his shoulder. Inside were the few possessions he had left: a change of clothes, a handful of spirit stones, and a single, crumpled page of notes he’d managed to salvage from the explosion.

The gates groaned open, revealing the winding path down the mountain. Beyond it lay the mortal world—a world

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The Road to Nowhere

The mountain path stretched before Shen He like a scar cut into the earth. Five hundred and thirty-seven steps. He'd counted them every day for the past three years, running up and down with buckets of water, stacks of scrolls, and the weight of everyone's contempt. Now he walked them one last time, and each step felt like a nail being driven into his past.

The morning mist clung to his robes, damp and cold. Behind him, the Azure Cloud Sect's gates loomed like the jaws of some great beast, ready to swallow anyone foolish enough to look back.

He didn't look back.

"You're actually leaving."

The voice came from his left, where a figure emerged from behind a gnarled pine. Lin Xue. Her white robes seemed to glow in the dim light, and her jade hairpin caught the first rays of sunrise like a captured star.

"What do you want?" Shen He asked, not slowing his pace. "Come to watch the cripple stumble?"

She fell into step beside him, her movements fluid and effortless. "I came to give you a warning."

"I've had enough warnings for a lifetime."

"The sect master lied to you."

Shen He stopped. He turned to face her, searching her perfect features for any trace of mockery. He found none. "What?"

"The Meridian Restoration Pill," Lin Xue said. "It works."

The words hit him like a physical blow. "But the sect master said—"

"The sect master says many things." Her voice dropped to barely a whisper. "The pill was created by the sect's founding alchemist, Wei Chen. He succeeded. The records are clear. But the formula was locked away because of what it cost."

"What did it cost?"

Lin Xue's eyes met his, and for a moment, he saw something he'd never seen in them before. Fear. "The alchemist who refines it must sacrifice their own cultivation to complete the pill. Not their meridians. Their entire cultivation base. They become mortal."

Shen He's mind raced. "So the sect master knew the pill worked, but he hid it because—"

"Because no alchemist in the sect would ever sacrifice their power to create it. And the elders feared that if the formula became known, someone might try anyway. That someone might succeed, and create a warrior with restored meridians who would owe no loyalty to the sect."

"A warrior who could challenge them."

Lin Xue nodded. "The sect master didn't exile you because you broke the rules. He exiled you because you were too close to the truth. He couldn't kill you outright—too many witnesses, too many questions. But out here, in the mortal world, accidents happen."

Shen He felt the cold anger from the judgment hall flare back to life. "And you're telling me this why? You're the one who exposed me."

"Because I was ordered to." Lin Xue's voice hardened. "Elder Wu suspected you were working in the restricted section for months. He had me watch you, report your movements. I followed orders."

"But now you're breaking them."

"Now I'm evening the score." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small leather pouch. "I've been studying Wei Chen's notes for two years. I've made copies. This is everything I know about the Meridian Restoration Pill, including the modifications I believe can reduce the sacrifice required."

Shen He stared at the pouch. "Why would you help me?"

"Because the sect master is wrong about something else too." Lin Xue pressed the pouch into his hands. "He thinks you're a cripple who stumbled onto a forbidden formula by accident. But I've read your alchemical notes. I know what you're trying to create. The Ultimate Pill."

"How—"

"When you copied the Meridian Restoration formula, you left fingerprints on the scroll. Sweat. Oils. I had them analyzed. Traces of thirty-seven different pill compounds, most of them experimental. You've been testing pill combinations for years, haven't you? Trying to find a way to absorb qi without standard cultivation."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "You've been watching me that closely?"

"I'm the sect's prodigy. It's my job to notice things others miss." A ghost of a smile crossed her lips. "Your work is brilliant, Shen He. Crude, dangerous, and utterly insane. But brilliant. If anyone can perfect the Meridian Restoration Pill, it's you."

"And if I can't?"

"Then you die, and the sect loses nothing." She stepped back, her expression shifting to cold professionalism. "The village of Greenwillow is three days south. The local alchemist there owes me a favor. Tell him Lin Xue sent you, and he'll give you access to his lab and supplies."

Before he could thank her, she turned and walked back up the mountain path, her white robes disappearing into the mist like a ghost.

Shen He stood alone on the mountain path, the leather pouch heavy in his hands. He opened it. Inside was a thick stack of papers covered in dense alchemical notation, diagrams of pill formations, and detailed analyses of meridian energy flows. Lin Xue's handwriting was immaculate, each character drawn with surgical precision.

He read the first page. Then the second. By the time he reached the tenth page, his hands were shaking.

She'd done it. She'd actually done it.

The original Meridian Restoration formula required the alchemist to channel their entire cultivation base into the pill during the final refinement stage—a one-way transfer that left the alchemist drained forever. But Lin Xue had found a workaround. If the pill was refined in stages, with the alchemist's energy fed in gradually over a period of weeks instead of all at once, the sacrifice could be reduced to a temporary depletion. The alchemist would be weakened for months afterward, but they would recover.

And the pill itself? It would work.

Shen He read the notes three more times, memorizing every detail. By the time he reached the bottom of the mountain, the sun was high overhead, burning away the morning mist.

The road stretched before him, rutted and dusty, lined with fields of yellow wheat that swayed in the breeze. Farmers worked the land, their backs bent, their clothes stained with sweat. They glanced at him as he passed—a boy in sect robes, walking alone, carrying nothing but a small bag and a leather pouch.

He must have looked like a deserter. A failure. A nobody.

They weren't wrong.

But they weren't right either.

Three days to Greenwillow. Three days to reach the alchemist who could give him a lab. Three days to start refining the pill that would change everything.

He walked.

The first day passed in a haze of road dust and aching feet. He'd never walked this far before. The sect compound was large, but he'd always been within its walls, surrounded by stone and wood and the familiar faces of people who despised him. Out here, the world was vast and empty, and he was nothing but a speck on the road.

He stopped at a small village as the sun began to set, using one of his spirit stones to buy a meal and a bed for the night. The innkeeper looked at him strangely—a boy in sect robes, alone, paying with spirit stones—but said nothing. In the mortal world, a guest with money was a guest worth serving.

The meal was simple: rice, pickled vegetables, and a piece of grilled fish that had been sitting out too long. Shen He ate it all, forcing himself to swallow even when his stomach rebelled. He needed strength. He couldn't afford to be picky.

That night, he spread Lin Xue's notes across the rough wooden table in his room and read them by candlelight until his eyes burned. The modifications were elegant, almost beautiful. She'd found a way to stabilize the meridian-destroying compounds using the essence of thousand-year ginseng, something he never would have thought of. And her analysis of the pill's energy flow patterns was years ahead of anything he'd ever seen.

But there was a problem.

The modified formula required a specific catalyst—one that Lin Xue hadn't included in the notes. It was mentioned in passing, a single line buried in the middle of a page: "The catalyst must be refined from a Grade 5 demon core, as only Grade 5 or higher contains enough concentrated qi to trigger the meridian restoration without overwhelming the body's natural defenses."

Grade 5 demon core.

Shen He's heart sank. The Azure Cloud Sect's entire stockpile of demon cores was guarded by elders of the Nascent Soul realm. He'd never get within a hundred feet of them.

And a Grade 5 demon in the wild was the equivalent of a Core Formation cultivator. He'd be dead before he could draw his first breath.

He stared at the notes until the candle burned down to a stub, then blew it out and lay in the darkness, his mind churning.

There had to be another way.

The second day, he woke before dawn and continued south. The road grew rougher, the villages fewer and farther between. By midday, the cultivated fields had given way to wild grasslands, and the only signs of civilization were the occasional cart track or abandoned farmhouse.

He was making good time. Too good. Something felt wrong.

The attack came just after noon.

Shen He was passing through a narrow gorge when the first arrow struck the ground three feet in front of him, burying itself in the dirt with a solid *thwack*. He dove to the side, rolling behind a rock outcropping as a second arrow whistled past his ear.

"Hold your fire," a voice called out. "I want to see his face."

Footsteps. Four sets, maybe five, crunching on the gravel path. Shen He pressed himself against the rock, his heart hammering. He had no weapon. No cultivation. No way to fight.

A figure stepped into view. He was tall and lean, dressed in dark leather armor, a bow slung across his back and a curved sword at his hip. His face was scarred, his eyes cold and calculating.

"Shen He of the Azure Cloud Sect," the man said. "Or should I say, Shen He the exile."

"How do you know my name?"

"I was paid to know it." The man smiled, revealing yellowed teeth. "Word travels fast in the cultivation world. A cripple who steals forbidden formulas and gets expelled? That's the kind of news that sells."

"Sells?"

"To the people who want you dead." The man gestured, and three more figures emerged from the shadows—all armed, all hard-eyed. "You've made some powerful enemies, boy. The question is, who's paying the highest price for your head?"

Shen He's mind raced. The sect wouldn't send assassins so quickly. They'd want to distance themselves from him, not draw attention to his death. Which meant someone else was involved.

Someone who knew about the pill.

Someone who wanted to make sure he never finished it.

"I don't have anything worth stealing," Shen He said, trying to keep his voice steady. "I left the sect with nothing but clothes and a few spirit stones."

"You have the formula," the scarred man said. "Give it to me, and I'll make your death quick. Hold out, and I'll make it slow. Your choice."

Shen He's hand drifted to the leather pouch at his belt. Lin Xue's notes. Months of his own research. The key to everything.

He couldn't let them take it.

But he couldn't fight them either.

So he did the only thing he could think of.

He ran.

He burst from behind the rock and sprinted down the gorge, his feet pounding against the gravel. Arrows whistled past him, one grazing his shoulder, another embedding itself in the pack on his back. He

title: The Corpse in the Cart
content:
The mountain path twisted downward like a serpent's spine, each step taking Shen He further from the only home he'd ever known. Dawn painted the eastern sky in shades of bruised purple, and the morning mist clung to his robes like a reluctant farewell.

He'd walked for three hours. His legs burned. His lungs ached. But the cold anger inside him kept moving, kept pushing forward.

*Exiled. For trying to fix what they said couldn't be fixed.*

The memory of Lin Xue's face flashed through his mind—that perfect, pitying expression she'd worn when she condemned him. She'd seen him in the restricted section. She'd known for three weeks and said nothing. Why? Why wait until the pill was nearly complete?

Unless she'd wanted him to fail.

Unless she'd wanted him gone.

Shen He stopped at a fork in the road. Left led toward the mortal villages. Right led toward the Blackwood Forest—a place of legend, of danger, of opportunity.

"I didn't come this far to live as a farmer," he muttered, and turned right.

The forest swallowed him within minutes. Ancient trees towered overhead, their branches weaving a canopy so thick that daylight barely pierced through. The air grew heavy with the smell of damp earth and decaying leaves. Strange sounds echoed from the shadows—the chirp of insects, the rustle of small animals, and something else. Something larger.

Shen He's hand drifted to the pouch at his belt. Inside were the salvaged notes from the explosion, along with three pills he'd managed to hide before the elders raided his quarters. They weren't much—a Qi Recovery Pill, a Healing Pill, and one half-finished Meridian Restoration Pill that had survived the blast.

*The sect master said it would kill me. But the explosion should have killed me too. And I'm still standing.*

Something wasn't adding up.

He found a clearing as the sun reached its zenith—a small circle of flattened grass surrounded by moss-covered stones. A stream ran along its edge, clear and cold. Shen He collapsed beside it, dipping his hands into the water and splashing it across his face.

The reflection that stared back at him was haggard. Dark circles under his eyes. A bruise on his cheek from the blast. But his eyes—his eyes burned with something that hadn't been there before.

*Determination. Or madness. Hard to tell the difference anymore.*

He pulled out the half-finished pill and held it up to what little light filtered through the canopy. It was smaller than his thumbnail, a pale blue sphere with cracks running through its surface like veins. The explosion had damaged it, but the core was intact.

*If I finish it here, I could test it. See if the sect master was lying.*

*If he wasn't, I'd die alone in this forest.*

Shen He tucked the pill away. Not yet. He needed more information first. He needed to understand why the Meridian Restoration Pill was forbidden, why the ancient alchemists had failed, and why his body seemed to react differently to pills than anyone else's.

A scream tore through the forest.

Shen He was on his feet before he realized he'd moved, his heart hammering against his ribs. The scream had come from the east—high-pitched, desperate, cut short as if someone had clamped a hand over the victim's mouth.

*Don't get involved. You're exiled. You have nothing.*

But his feet were already moving.

He crashed through the underbrush, following the sound. Branches scratched his face. Thorns tore at his robes. The scream had been close—maybe a hundred yards away—but the forest twisted and turned, disorienting him.

He burst into another clearing and stopped dead.

A merchant cart lay overturned in the center, its wheels still spinning. Goods were scattered across the ground—bolts of silk, jars of spices, and a single body sprawled face-down in the mud.

Shen He approached slowly, his senses on high alert. The body wasn't moving. Blood pooled beneath it, dark and thick, spreading across the damp earth.

"Hello?" he called out. "Is anyone alive?"

No response.

He knelt beside the body and turned it over. The man's eyes were open, staring at nothing. His throat had been cut—a clean, precise slash that spoke of professional work. Not bandits. Bandits were messy. This was execution.

Shen He's hand went to his pouch again. The Healing Pill would be useless. The man was dead.

*But who killed him? And why?*

He found the answer in the man's hand. Clutched so tight that rigor mortis had set in, a piece of jade was embedded in the corpse's palm—a token, the kind used by couriers to identify themselves to their clients. Shen He pried it loose and examined it.

The jade was warm to the touch, pulsing with a faint light. On one side, a symbol: a cauldron with seven stars above it. On the other, a single word carved in ancient script.

*Alchemy.*

Shen He's breath caught. The Alchemy Guild. The same organization that had been hunting alchemists across the continent, recruiting them by force or eliminating those who refused. The same guild that the Azure Cloud Sect had secretly cooperated with, providing them with talented refiners in exchange for resources.

*This courier was delivering something to the guild. And someone killed him for it.*

He searched the cart, his hands moving quickly. Under a false bottom in the merchant's trunk, he found a leather-bound case, locked with a mechanism that required spiritual energy to open. Shen He didn't have spiritual energy—not in the traditional sense—but he pressed his palm against the lock anyway, feeding his own strange qi into the mechanism.

It clicked open.

Inside the case lay a single scroll, yellowed with age. Shen He unrolled it carefully, his eyes scanning the characters written in faded ink.

*My Dearest Student,*

*If you are reading this, I am dead. The Alchemy Guild has found me, as I always knew they would. But I have hidden my greatest discovery where they will never find it—in the blood of one who cannot cultivate.*

*You are that one. You are the key.*

*The pills you have been taking are not accidents. Your body does not absorb qi through your meridians because your meridians are not meant to absorb qi. They are meant to store it. To compress it. To refine it into something greater.*

*You are not a cripple, Shen He. You are a vessel.*

*Find the Heart of the Earth. Below the Blackwood Forest, in the ruins of the First Refinery. There, you will find the answers you seek. And the weapon you will need.*

*Hurry. They are coming.*

The letter was unsigned, but Shen He knew the handwriting. He'd seen it a thousand times, scrawled in the margins of alchemy texts, correcting errors, adding insights.

*Old Chen.*

The librarian who had taught him everything. The man who had slipped him forbidden scrolls and pretended not to notice. The man who had been found dead three days ago, supposedly of old age.

*They killed him. The guild killed him, and the sect covered it up.*

Shen He's hands trembled. He looked down at the corpse of the courier, then at the letter, then at the jade token still clutched in his fingers.

*They're coming.*

A twig snapped behind him.

Shen He spun around, his heart leaping into his throat. A figure stood at the edge of the clearing, cloaked in black, their face hidden by a hood. In their hand, a blade gleamed—the same kind of blade that had killed the courier.

"You shouldn't have come here, cripple," the figure said, their voice distorted, as if speaking through a mask. "That scroll was not meant for you."

Shen He's mind raced. He had no cultivation. No weapons. No allies. He was alone in a forest with a killer who had already murdered once today.

*But I have pills.*

His hand moved to his pouch. The Qi Recovery Pill. The Healing Pill. The half-finished Meridian Restoration Pill.

*One of them might kill me. But one of them might save me.*

"Who sent you?" Shen He asked, buying time. "The guild? The sect?"

"The sect grows tired of your interference," the figure said, taking a step forward. "And the guild wants what you carry. Hand over the scroll, and I'll make your death quick."

*Lin Xue. She sent them. She knew about Old Chen. She knew about everything.*

"You'll have to take it," Shen He said, his voice steady despite the fear crawling up his spine.

The assassin laughed—a cold, hollow sound. "Brave words for a dead man."

They lunged.

Shen He's hand closed around the first pill he touched—the half-finished Meridian Restoration Pill—and he shoved it into his mouth.

The world exploded into white.

Pain. More pain than he'd ever known. It raced through his veins like fire, like lightning, like every nerve in his body was being torn apart and remade at the same time. His vision blurred. His knees buckled. He felt himself falling, felt the assassin's blade whistling toward his throat—

And then he felt *everything*.

The ground beneath him. The air around him. The qi in the forest, in the trees, in the very stones—it all rushed toward him, pouring into his body through channels that had never existed before. Channels that were *opening* inside him, tearing through flesh and bone and spirit.

The Meridian Restoration Pill wasn't destroying him.

It was *building* him.

Shen He opened his eyes. The assassin's blade was an inch from his throat—frozen, stopped by a barrier of golden light that pulsed from his skin.

"What—" the assassin hissed, trying to pull back.

Shen He grabbed the blade. His fingers closed around the edge, and the metal *shattered*.

The assassin stumbled backward, their eyes wide with shock. "Impossible. You're a cripple. You have no cultivation."

"I'm not a cripple," Shen He said, and his voice echoed with power he'd never possessed before. "I'm a vessel."

He raised his hand, and the golden light gathered around his palm, condensing into a sphere of pure energy. He didn't know how he was doing it. He didn't know what he was becoming. But he knew one thing with absolute certainty:

*I am not dying today.*

The assassin recovered quickly, drawing a second blade from their belt. "It doesn't matter what you are. The guild will hunt you to the ends of the earth. The sect will—"

"The sect exiled me," Shen He interrupted. "And the guild killed the only person who ever believed in me."

He stepped forward, the golden light blazing around him like a second skin.

"Tell them I'm coming."

The assassin laughed again, but this time there was fear in it. "You have no idea what you're facing. The Alchemy Guild has existed for ten thousand years. They've crushed empires. They've killed gods. You're just a boy with a pill."

"Then I'll make more pills."

Shen He hurled the sphere of energy at the assassin's feet. The explosion threw them backward, slamming them into a tree. They crumpled, unconscious, their cloak smoking.

The golden light faded. Shen He's legs gave out, and he collapsed to his knees, gasping. His body screamed in protest. The Meridian Restoration Pill had done something to him—something irreversible—but he could feel the damage it had caused. Cracks in his foundation. Fractures in his newly-formed meridians.

*It

title: The Shadow That Follows
content:
The mountain path wound downward through a forest of ancient pines, their branches creaking in the cold wind. Shen He walked with measured steps, each one taking him farther from the only home he'd ever known. The anger that had carried him through the judgment hall had cooled into something harder—a bitter resolve that settled in his bones like frozen mercury.

He'd been walking for three hours when he felt it.

A presence. Faint, but unmistakable. Someone was following him.

Shen He didn't turn around. He kept walking, his ears straining against the forest sounds. The crunch of leaves. The whisper of wind. And beneath it all—the almost imperceptible rhythm of footsteps that matched his own.

He quickened his pace. The footsteps quickened too.

*Shit.*

The path ahead curved around a massive boulder, and Shen He ducked behind it, pressing his back against the cold stone. His heart hammered against his ribs. He had no cultivation. No weapons. No defensive techniques. He was a pill junkie with a single page of notes and a handful of spirit stones.

The footsteps stopped.

Silence stretched for ten agonizing heartbeats.

Then a voice, smooth and familiar, cut through the quiet: "You can come out, Shen He. I know you're there."

Lin Xue.

Shen He's blood ran cold. Of course. Of course the prodigy wouldn't let him leave without one final humiliation.

He stepped out from behind the boulder, schooling his face into a mask of weary indifference. Lin Xue stood ten feet away, her white robes immaculate despite the forest dust, her jade hairpin catching the last rays of sunlight. She looked like a painting come to life—beautiful, untouchable, and utterly lethal.

"Come to gloat?" Shen He asked.

"Come to warn you." Lin Xue's eyes held no mockery, no triumph. That was almost worse. "The Forgotten Pill isn't just forbidden. It's cursed. The alchemists who created it didn't fail because they were incompetent. They failed because something *ate* their research. Something that's still out there."

Shen He stared at her. "You expect me to believe that?"

"I expect you to survive." Lin Xue reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small, leather-bound journal. She tossed it at his feet. "This is everything I've found about the pill. Every attempt. Every failure. Every death."

"Why would you help me?"

"Because I'm not your enemy, Shen He." Her voice softened, just slightly. "I never was. I was the one who reported you because if I hadn't, Elder Wu would have killed you on the spot. Exile is a chance. Death is permanent."

Shen He picked up the journal. The leather was warm, worn smooth by countless readings. "And if I still try to make the pill?"

"Then you're a fool." Lin Xue turned and walked back up the path. "But at least you'll be an informed fool."

She vanished into the trees without another word, leaving Shen He alone with a journal full of secrets and a head full of questions.

---

He found shelter in an abandoned hunter's cabin three miles down the mountain. The roof leaked, the door hung crooked, and something had nested in the corner, but it was walls and a roof. That was more than he'd had an hour ago.

Shen He lit a small fire with the last of his flint, then opened Lin Xue's journal.

The first page made his blood freeze.

*The Meridian Restoration Pill does not exist.*

*It is a lie. A trap. A filter for the desperate.*

*The true formula was never meant to restore anything. It was meant to create a vessel.*

He read on, his eyes devouring each word. According to Lin Xue's research, the ancient alchemists hadn't been trying to heal broken cultivators. They'd been trying to build a body capable of holding the power of a god—a mortal shell that could contain the infinite. The pill wasn't medicine. It was a key. A key to a door that had never been meant to open.

And the reason all previous attempts had failed?

*The pill requires a sacrifice. A living soul, refined into the formula. Every previous alchemist who attempted it died because they refused to pay the price.*

*Except one.*

Shen He turned the page, and his hands started shaking.

*The Last Alchemist succeeded. His name was Shen Wu. He created the pill, consumed it, and vanished from existence. But before he vanished, he wrote one final note:*

*"The pill works. But the body must be empty. No qi. No cultivation. No connection to the heavens. Only then can it be filled."*

*Shen Wu was my ancestor.*

*He was also meridian-crippled.*

The journal slipped from Shen He's fingers. He stared at the fire, his mind racing. No qi. No cultivation. No connection to the heavens.

*Like me.*

This wasn't a curse. This wasn't a punishment. This was a *condition*. His body wasn't broken—it was *ready*. Ready for something that no cultivator in the world could achieve.

But the price. A living soul.

He thought of Lin Xue's words. *Something ate their research. Something that's still out there.*

The fire crackled, and in its depths, Shen He saw a face. Old. Withered. Hungry.

He slammed the journal shut.

"Focus," he muttered to himself. "One problem at a time. Shelter. Food. Ingredients."

He had spirit stones, but they were worthless in the mortal world. He had knowledge, but knowledge didn't fill an empty stomach. He had a formula for a pill that could make him a god—if he could figure out how to avoid becoming a sacrifice.

The cabin creaked around him. Wind howled through the cracks in the walls. And somewhere in the darkness, something stirred.

Shen He's head snapped up.

The fire flickered, casting dancing shadows across the walls. For a moment, he thought he saw a figure standing in the corner—tall, gaunt, with eyes that burned like embers.

Then it was gone.

"Just the wind," he said, his voice too loud in the silence. "Just the goddamn wind."

He didn't sleep that night.

---

Dawn came gray and cold. Shen He emerged from the cabin to find the world shrouded in mist, the trees barely visible as dark shapes in the white. His breath fogged in front of his face as he surveyed his surroundings.

The path continued downward, but to the east, he could see the glimmer of a river. Water meant civilization. Civilization meant supplies.

He started walking.

Three hours later, he found the town.

It was small—maybe a hundred buildings clustered around a central square—but it had what he needed. A market. An apothecary. And, most importantly, anonymity. No one here knew his face. No one here knew he was a disgraced sect disciple with a death wish.

The apothecary shop was cramped and dusty, run by an old woman with rheumy eyes and hands that shook when she poured tea. Shen He bought what he could with his spirit stones—basic herbs, a mortar and pestle, and a cauldron that was barely big enough to boil rice.

"You're an alchemist?" the old woman asked, her voice scratchy.

"A student," Shen He said. "Learning the trade."

"Learning, eh." She studied him with eyes that were sharper than they looked. "You know, young man, there's a reason most alchemists stay in the cities. The ingredients here are poor. The knowledge is poorer. If you want to learn, you should go to the capital."

"I'll keep that in mind."

He paid and left, his bag heavier with supplies but his mind heavier with questions. The capital. The Alchemy Guild. The place where secrets were bought and sold like vegetables at a market.

The place where the Shadow Alchemist was last seen.

Lin Xue's journal had mentioned the name only once, in a footnote that Shen He had almost missed:

*The Shadow Alchemist is the only living person who has seen the complete formula. He is also the only person who has survived attempting it. He is hiding in the capital, beneath the Alchemy Guild itself. If you find him, you may find the truth.*

*Or you may find death.*

Shen He stood in the middle of the dusty street, townspeople flowing around him like water around a stone. The capital was two weeks' journey on foot. He had no money for a horse, no protection for the road, and no guarantee that the Shadow Alchemist would even speak to him.

But he had a body that was ready. A formula that was waiting. And a hunger that burned hotter than any fire.

He started walking east.

---

The attack came on the third night.

Shen He had made camp in a clearing, the journal spread open on his lap as he studied the incomplete formula. He was so focused that he didn't hear the footsteps until they were right behind him.

"You shouldn't have read that."

The voice was a whisper, dry as autumn leaves. Shen He spun around, his heart slamming against his ribs.

A figure stood at the edge of the firelight—tall, wrapped in black robes, with a face that was barely human. Its skin was stretched tight over its skull, and its eyes were pits of absolute darkness.

The Shadow Alchemist.

"You're real," Shen He breathed.

"I'm dead," the figure said. "I've been dead for a hundred years. But the formula keeps me tethered. Waiting. *Hungry*."

It took a step forward, and the fire dimmed as if a cold hand had been laid upon it.

"The pill requires a sacrifice," the Shadow Alchemist said. "But the sacrifice doesn't have to be *living*. Do you understand what I'm offering you, boy?"

Shen He's mind raced. "You want me to refine your soul into the pill."

"I want you to *free* me. I've been trapped in this half-existence for a century, bound to the formula by my own greed. Consume me into your pill, and I will finally know peace. And you will know power."

"And if I refuse?"

The Shadow Alchemist smiled, and the expression cracked its papery skin.

"Then I'll take your body by force. One way or another, boy, I'm getting out of this cage. I'd prefer it be through your cooperation. But I'm very, *very* patient."

It reached out a skeletal hand, and the fire died completely.

In the darkness, Shen He heard it whisper:

"Make your choice."

title: The Serpent in the Shadows
content:
The road stretched before Shen He like a scar across the earth. Three days had passed since his exile from the Azure Cloud Sect, and the mountain peaks that had once been his home now looked like distant, mocking monuments against the sky.

He walked.

That was all he could do. Walk, eat the meager rations he'd scavenged, and sleep in ditches when the sun went down. The mortal world was exactly as he remembered it from his childhood—dusty, indifferent, and full of people who would step over a dying man if it meant saving a copper coin.

But the anger hadn't faded. It burned in his chest, a constant flame that kept him moving when his legs wanted to give out.

*They threw me away like garbage*, he thought, kicking a stone off the road. *Called me a cripple. Said I was worthless.*

His hand went to the crumpled page in his pocket. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula that had cost him everything.

*It doesn't work*, the sect master had said. *It kills the user.*

But Shen He had read the scroll. He'd memorized every character, every measurement, every obscure ingredient. And he'd seen something the ancient alchemists had missed.

A pattern.

A flaw in their reasoning that had led them to disaster.

The pill didn't destroy meridians. It *rewired* them. The problem was that the ancient alchemists had used the wrong binding agent—a common herb called Nightlotus Root that, when combined with the pill's primary components, created a toxic backlash that would burn through any cultivator's qi channels.

But if you replaced the Nightlotus Root with something else—something that could absorb the excess energy instead of redirecting it—the pill might actually work.

The question was *what*.

"Boy."

Shen He looked up. A man stood in the middle of the road, blocking his path. He was tall, lean, dressed in robes of deep crimson that seemed to drink the sunlight. His face was weathered, his eyes sharp and dark, and he carried no visible weapon—which meant he didn't need one.

"Who are you?" Shen He asked, his body tensing.

The man smiled. It wasn't a friendly smile. "I've been looking for you, Shen He of the Azure Cloud Sect. Or should I say, *former* disciple of the Azure Cloud Sect?"

Shen He's blood ran cold. "Word travels fast."

"News of a cripple who nearly destroyed an entire compound with a failed alchemical refinement? Yes, that travels very fast indeed." The man took a step closer. "I represent people who are very interested in your... talents."

"I don't have talents. I'm meridian-crippled. Everyone knows that."

"Everyone knows that you're a failure," the man corrected. "But I know that you're not. I know about the pills you've been refining in secret. The ones that should have been impossible for someone without qi to create. The ones that showed remarkable—if crude—innovation."

Shen He's heart hammered. "Who are you?"

"My name is not important. What matters is the offer I bring you." The man reached into his robes and produced a small jade token, green as spring leaves, etched with a symbol Shen He didn't recognize. "The Serpent's Fang Alchemy Guild. We've heard of your work. We want you to join us."

"Another sect? I just got kicked out of one."

"This is not a sect. We are... independent operators. Alchemists who work outside the rigid structures of the major sects. We refine what we want, when we want, without elders breathing down our necks." The man's smile widened. "And we have resources. Libraries. Ingredients. Equipment. All the things a budding alchemist would need to perfect his craft."

Shen He stared at the token. It was beautiful—flawless jade, carved with a precision that spoke of master craftsmanship. The symbol was a fang wrapped around a mortar and pestle. Deadly and elegant.

*This is a trap*, he thought. *It has to be a trap.*

But what choice did he have? He had no home. No resources. No future. He was a cripple wandering alone in a world that would crush him without a second thought.

"If I join," he said slowly, "what's expected of me?"

The man's smile turned wolfish. "You refine. You experiment. You push the boundaries of what alchemy can achieve. And in return, we give you everything you need to succeed." He paused. "Including protection."

"Protection from whom?"

"The Azure Cloud Sect, for starters. They've put a bounty on your head. Nothing official, but the whispers have already spread. Anyone who turns you in will be rewarded handsomely."

Shen He felt the ground shift beneath his feet. "They want me dead?"

"You know too much. You saw the Meridian Restoration Pill formula. You attempted to replicate it. Even if your attempt failed, the fact that you could *try* makes you dangerous." The man's voice softened, almost sympathetic. "The sects hoard knowledge like dragons hoard gold. They don't share. They don't allow others to learn. And they certainly don't allow cripples to surpass them."

The anger in Shen He's chest flared. It was true. Everything the man said was true.

"Take me to your guild," he said.

The man nodded, as if he'd expected nothing less. "Follow me. And don't worry about the bounty. No one will find you once you're with us."

They walked for two more days.

The man—who finally introduced himself as Wei Chen, a middle-grade alchemist of the Serpent's Fang—led Shen He through winding mountain paths, across rushing rivers, and into a forest so dense that sunlight barely touched the ground. The air grew thick with spiritual energy, heavy and rich, like breathing honey.

"Close," Wei Chen said, his voice barely a whisper. "The guild's main compound is just ahead. But I must warn you—not everyone will welcome you with open arms. There are those who believe that only cultivators should practice alchemy. They will test you."

"Let them test me."

Wei Chen chuckled. "That's the spirit."

They emerged into a clearing. And Shen He stopped breathing.

The compound was massive—a sprawling complex of black stone buildings, connected by raised walkways and surrounded by walls that seemed to grow from the earth itself. Towers rose like fingers reaching for the sky, each one topped with a bronze serpent that seemed to writhe in the torchlight. Alchemical furnaces glowed from dozens of windows, casting the entire compound in an eerie, amber glow.

But it was the symbol that caught Shen He's attention.

Emblazoned on the main gate, carved into the stone, painted on banners—the serpent fang symbol. Except now that he saw it up close, he noticed something he hadn't before.

The serpent's eye was a single character, written in ancient script: *Yan*. The character for fire. But it was also the character for *experiment*. For *sacrifice*.

His instincts screamed at him to run.

"Wei Chen," he said slowly, "what exactly does this guild do?"

"We refine pills," Wei Chen said, his voice smooth as oil. "But we also refine *alchemists*. You see, Shen He, the major sects have it all wrong. They believe that cultivation is about harmony, about balance, about following the natural order of heaven and earth."

He turned to face Shen He, and his eyes held a light that was not quite sane.

"We believe in pushing forward. Breaking limits. Doing whatever it takes to achieve perfection." He gestured to the compound. "The Serpent's Fang has no rules. No restrictions. Our alchemists are free to experiment with any formula, any ingredient, any method they choose."

"Even forbidden ones?"

"*Especially* forbidden ones."

Shen He's hand drifted to his pocket, where the Meridian Restoration Pill formula rested. *They would let me test it*, he realized. *They would give me everything I need to perfect it.*

It was everything he wanted.

It was also terrifying.

"I need to think about this," he said.

Wei Chen's smile flickered. "I'm afraid you don't have that luxury. The bounty hunters are already on your trail. The Azure Cloud Sect has sent out search parties. And there are... other parties interested in your capture."

"Other parties?"

"The Sword Sect. They've heard about your work. They want to study you." Wei Chen's voice dropped. "And when the Sword Sect says 'study', they mean 'dissect'."

Shen He felt cold sweat trickle down his spine. "You're saying I have no choice."

"I'm saying the Serpent's Fang is your only option. But it's a good one. A great one, in fact." Wei Chen's smile returned, warm and inviting. "Join us, Shen He. Become one of us. And together, we will push alchemy to heights the world has never seen."

Shen He looked at the compound. At the glowing furnaces. At the serpent symbols that seemed to watch him like living things.

He thought about Lin Xue's cold eyes. Elder Wu's sneer. The sect master's dismissal.

He thought about the Meridian Restoration Pill. About what it could do. About what he could become.

"Fine," he said. "I'll join."

Wei Chen clapped him on the shoulder. "Excellent. The master will be pleased."

They passed through the main gate, and Shen He felt something wash over him—a barrier of some kind, spiritual energy so dense it made his skin prickle. The compound was warded. Heavily.

*No going back now*, he thought.

Wei Chen led him through winding corridors, past alchemists who stared at him with open curiosity or barely concealed hostility. The air smelled of herbs and chemicals and something metallic—blood, maybe. Shen He tried not to think about it.

Finally, they reached a door at the end of a long hallway. It was made of black iron, etched with the same serpent symbol, and it pulsed with a low, rhythmic energy, like a heartbeat.

"The master awaits," Wei Chen said. He pushed open the door.

The room beyond was circular, lit by a single brazier in the center. Shadows danced on the walls, making it impossible to see the edges of the chamber. And in the center, seated on a throne of what looked like fused bone and metal, was a woman.

She was old. Ancient. Her skin was like parchment stretched over bones, her eyes white and blind, her hair a wispy silver that hung past her shoulders. But when she spoke, her voice was strong, resonant, filling the room like a physical presence.

"Shen He of the Azure Cloud Sect. I have been expecting you."

He dropped to one knee before he even realized what he was doing. Something about her demanded submission, demanded respect.

"Master," he said, his voice barely a whisper.

"I have heard much about you," she continued. "A cripple who defies heaven's laws. A boy who refines pills without qi. A mind that sees patterns where others see chaos."

She rose from her throne, and the shadows seemed to part around her. She walked toward him, her steps silent, her blind eyes somehow fixed on his face.

"You want to perfect the Meridian Restoration Pill," she said.

Shen He's heart stopped. "How did you—"

"I know everything that happens in the world of alchemy. The formula you found. The explosion you caused. The exile you suffered." She stopped inches from him, close enough that he could smell incense and something else—something sour, like rotting herbs

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Serpent's Invitation

The morning light filtered through the leaves of the ancient banyan tree, casting dappled shadows across Shen He's face. Three days had passed since his failed assassination attempt on Elder Wu. Three days of hiding in abandoned farmhouses, stealing herbs from neglected gardens, and refining pills in the dead of night.

His right hand still trembled from the backlash of the Blood Coagulation Pill. The meridians in his arm—if one could call his twisted channels meridians—felt like they'd been stretched too thin and left to fray. But the pain was manageable now. More importantly, he was alive.

And Elder Wu was not.

Shen He held up the jade bottle, watching the morning light catch the crimson liquid inside. The Assassination Pill, he called it. A refinement that would dissolve into the victim's bloodstream upon contact, causing their qi to combust from within. It had taken him seven tries to get the formula right. Seven failures. Five near-deaths. One successful dose delivered through a cup of wine.

*Elder Wu's wine.*

The memory of that night still felt unreal. The way the old man's eyes had widened in recognition just before the flames consumed him. The satisfying *pop* of his dantian rupturing. The chaos that followed as the sect's outer disciples scrambled to understand what had happened.

Shen He smiled. It wasn't a pleasant smile.

"You look pleased with yourself."

The voice came from behind him, soft and feminine, carrying a hint of amusement. Shen He spun around, a pill already between his fingers—a Purple Smoke Pellet, ready to shatter and obscure his escape.

She stood ten feet away, leaning against the banyan tree's trunk as if she'd been there all along. Lin Xue. The Azure Cloud Sect's prodigy. The girl who had betrayed him.

She wore simple traveling clothes instead of her sect robes, and her hair was tied back in a practical braid. No weapons. No obvious cultivation aura. But Shen He knew better than to underestimate her.

"You're hard to find," she said, as if commenting on the weather. "I've been tracking you for two days."

"Then you've wasted your time." Shen He kept his voice flat, emotionless. "If you're here to finish what Elder Wu started, I'd recommend you leave now. I'm not the same cripple you threw out of the sect."

"No. You're not." Lin Xue's eyes swept over him, cataloging every detail. "You're stronger. Your qi feels... different. Like you've been consuming something." She tilted her head. "Pills. You've been refining pills."

Shen He said nothing. His fingers tightened around the smoke pellet.

"I'm not here to fight you." Lin Xue raised her hands in a placating gesture. "I'm here to warn you."

"I don't need warnings from a traitor."
