"Then call it information." She reached into her robes, and Shen He tensed—but she only pulled out a scroll, tied with a black ribbon. "The Alchemy Guild has put a bounty on your head. Fifty thousand spirit stones for your capture. One hundred thousand for your death."

The number made Shen He's breath catch. A hundred thousand spirit stones. That was enough to buy a small city. Enough to fund a war.

"Why?" he asked. "I'm nobody. A crippled alchemist with no cultivation base."

"You're the man who killed an elder of the Azure Cloud Sect using alchemy alone." Lin Xue tossed the scroll to his feet. "Word travels fast. The Guild is terrified. They've spent centuries building a monopoly on pill refinement, controlling who learns the art and who doesn't. You represent something they can't control—a rogue alchemist who bypasses cultivation entirely."

Shen He picked up the scroll, unrolling it to reveal his own face skillfully drawn in ink. Underneath, characters described him as "dangerous," "unpredictable," and "a threat to the natural order."

"Naturally, the Guild has also offered a separate bounty for the formula of your assassination pill," Lin Xue continued. "Ten thousand spirit stones for a stable recipe. Twenty thousand if it can be mass-produced."

"And you're telling me this why?" Shen He's voice dripped with suspicion. "You made your choice at the trial. You stood by while Elder Wu pronounced my exile. You called me a thief."

Lin Xue's expression flickered—the first crack in her composure. "I did what I had to do to survive. The sect master was already leaning toward execution. If I'd defended you, we'd both be dead."

"Spare me your justifications."

"It's not a justification. It's a fact." She stepped closer, and Shen He let her, curious despite himself. "You're not the only one who's been watching the restricted sections all these years, Shen He. You're not the only one who's been taking notes."

His blood ran cold. "What are you saying?"

"I'm saying I know more about forbidden alchemy than anyone in the sect." Lin Xue met his eyes, and for the first time, he saw something other than cold calculation in her gaze. Fear. "And I know that the Meridian Restoration Pill wasn't the only formula locked away in that vault."

She reached into her robes again, and this time she pulled out a small wooden box, no larger than her palm. The surface was covered in seal characters, glowing faintly with qi.

"I've been watching you since you left the sect," she said. "I watched you kill Elder Wu. I watched you refine pills in abandoned temples and steal herbs from the Black Market. I've been impressed."

"Get to the point."

"The point is that you're going to need help." Lin Xue held out the box. "The Alchemy Guild has enforcers—cultivators trained specifically to hunt rogue alchemists. They'll be here within the week. And they won't make the mistake of underestimating you."

Shen He stared at the box, his mind racing. Everything she said made sense. Too much sense. And that was exactly what worried him.

"Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't." Lin Xue smiled, and it was a cold, sharp thing. "But you should trust what's in that box. It's a map to the location of the original Meridian Restoration Pill formula—the one written by the ancient alchemists themselves. Not a copy. The original."

Shen He's heart stopped.

"The original was destroyed," he said, his voice barely a whisper. "The sect master said—"

"The sect master lied." Lin Xue's smile widened. "He's been hiding it for decades, using it as leverage with the Alchemy Guild. You want to fix your meridians? You want to become something more than a crippled alchemist? That map is your only chance."

She tossed the box to him. He caught it instinctively, his fingers tracing the seal characters.

"The enforcers will arrive in five days," she said. "If you're still in the region by then, you'll die. The map leads east, through the Dead Marsh and into the Ruins of the First Alchemist. If you survive the journey, you might find what you're looking for."

"And what do you get out of this?"

Lin Xue's eyes glittered. "I get to watch the Alchemy Guild burn."

She turned and walked away, disappearing into the morning mist as silently as she'd appeared. Shen He stood alone under the banyan tree, holding the wooden box, his mind a storm of questions.

He opened it.

Inside was a single piece of parchment, yellowed with age, covered in characters so ancient they predated the current cultivation era. At the bottom, a seal: the symbol of the First Alchemist, a serpent eating its own tail.

Shen He traced the characters with his finger, translating them slowly, carefully.

*To the one who seeks the truth of the Meridian Restoration:*
*Know that the path is paved with death.*
*Know that the formula you seek is not meant for the living.*
*Know that the First Alchemist's work remains unfinished—*
*And that the final ingredient is your own soul.*

He read the words three times, cold dread settling in his stomach.

Below the warning, the map showed a winding path through the Dead Marsh, marked with symbols he didn't recognize. At the end of the path, a structure labeled simply: THE FOUNDRY.

Shen He looked up, eastward, toward the Dead Marsh.

The Alchemy Guild was coming to kill him.

The only path forward led through certain death.

And somewhere in the ruins of the First Alchemist's workshop, a formula was waiting that could either save him or destroy him completely.

He laughed. It was a hollow, broken sound.

"What else could go wrong?"

As if in answer, a pulse of qi exploded from the direction of the Azure Cloud Sect—so powerful that the ground shook beneath his feet. Shen He turned, watching as a pillar of golden light shot into the sky from the sect's main hall.

Sect Master's aura. At full power.

Which meant he knew.

Elder Wu's death had been discovered.

And the hunt for Shen He had just begun.

title: The Poison Garden's Secret
content:
The rain came down in sheets, turning the dirt road into a river of mud. Shen He trudged forward, his clothes plastered to his body, each step a battle against the sucking earth. Three days had passed since his exile from the Azure Cloud Sect. Three days of walking, of hunger, of the cold realization that he was alone in a world that had no use for a cripple.

He stopped beneath a twisted old tree, its branches offering little shelter from the storm. Water dripped from his nose, his chin, the tips of his fingers. He pulled out the crumpled page of notes—his only possession of value—and stared at the diagrams he'd memorized a hundred times.

The Meridian Restoration Pill.

The sect master said it was a lie. A trap. A formula designed to kill anyone foolish enough to attempt it.

But Shen He's body had absorbed qi from pills before. His meridian-crippled body. A body that, by all logic, should have been incapable of cultivation. If the pill was supposed to be impossible, then wasn't he already living proof that the impossible could happen?

He crumpled the page and shoved it back into his robes.

"Keep telling yourself that," he muttered. "Maybe you'll actually believe it someday."

The rain began to ease as dusk fell, the clouds parting to reveal a bruised purple sky. Shen He spotted lights in the distance—a town. Small, but large enough to have an inn, perhaps a market. Somewhere he could rest, gather information, figure out his next move.

He reached the town's outskirts an hour later, his body shivering from cold and exhaustion. The sign at the gate read "Greenvine Settlement" in faded characters. A farming community, by the look of it. Modest. Unremarkable.

Perfect for someone who wanted to disappear.

The inn was a two-story building with a crooked sign and a warm glow spilling from its windows. Shen He pushed open the door, and the smell of stew hit him like a physical force. His stomach groaned audibly.

The innkeeper—a stout woman with graying hair and kind eyes—looked him up and down. "Rough journey, young man?"

"You could say that." Shen He fished out a spirit stone from his bag. "A room for the night. And food."

The woman's eyes widened at the spirit stone. "That's... are you sure? This is worth more than a month's stay."

"I'm sure." He didn't have the energy to haggle. Besides, spirit stones were common currency among cultivators, but in a mortal town like this, they were treasure. He'd have to be careful not to draw attention.

The innkeeper—her name was Mama Li, she told him—gave him a bowl of stew, a chunk of bread, and a room on the second floor. Shen He ate like a starving wolf, then collapsed onto the bed, not bothering to remove his wet clothes.

Sleep came instantly, and with it, dreams of fire and screaming.

---

He woke to sunlight streaming through a cracked window. His body ached, but the rest had done him good. He sat up, stretched, and immediately noticed something wrong.

The crumpled page of notes was gone.

Shen He's blood turned to ice. He tore through his bag, his robes, the bed. Nothing. The page had been in his inner pocket when he fell asleep. He'd checked it a dozen times during the journey. It was his only link to the Meridian Restoration Pill formula.

Someone had taken it.

He burst out of the room and down the stairs, nearly colliding with Mama Li. "Did anyone go into my room last night?"

Mama Li blinked at his frantic tone. "No, child. I lock the doors myself after the last guest retires. Why? Is something missing?"

"Everything." The word came out as a snarl. "Everything is missing."

He stormed out of the inn, his eyes scanning the street. The town was waking up, merchants setting up stalls, children running to school. Normal. Peaceful. And somewhere among them was a thief who'd stolen his only hope.

Shen He's fists clenched at his sides. He had nothing. No cultivation base. No allies. No weapons. Just a burning, impotent rage that threatened to consume him.

"Looking for something?"

The voice came from behind him—smooth, amused, feminine. He spun around.

A woman leaned against the inn's wall, arms crossed, a lazy smile on her lips. She was young, perhaps a few years older than him, with sharp features and eyes that gleamed like polished jade. She wore simple traveler's clothes, but the fabric was too fine, too clean for someone who'd been on the road.

"You," Shen He said. "You took my notes."

"I didn't take them. I borrowed them." She pulled the crumpled page from her sleeve and waved it tauntingly. "You really shouldn't leave such interesting things lying around. Anyone could find them."

"Give it back."

"Or what?" She raised an eyebrow. "You'll challenge me to a fight? I'd win, by the way. Even if I suppressed my cultivation to the first Qi Condensation level, I'd still snap you in half like a twig."

Shen He's jaw tightened. She was right. He knew she was right. And that knowledge burned worse than any insult.

"What do you want?" he asked through gritted teeth.

"Now that's the right question." The woman pushed off the wall and walked toward him, her movements fluid, almost predatory. "I want to know where you got this formula."

"Why should I tell you?"

"Because I'm the one asking." She stopped inches from him, close enough that he could smell the faint scent of herbs on her clothes. Alchemy herbs. "That formula is a death sentence. Anyone with half a cultivation base knows that. And yet, here you are—a cripple—walking around with it like it's a recipe for dumplings."

Shen He's eye twitched. "I'm not a—"

"Cripple? Yes, you are. I can sense it." She tapped her temple. "I have a gift for sensing qi pathways. Yours are shattered. Like someone took a hammer to glass. And yet..." She leaned closer, her eyes narrowing. "You have traces of alchemical residue in your body. Faint, but there. You've been consuming pills. Many pills. More than any cripple should be able to digest."

Shen He's heart hammered against his ribs. She was dangerously close to his secret.

"My name is Yun Mei," she said, straightening. "And I'm going to make you an offer."

"I don't want your offer."

"You will." She tapped the crumpled page against her palm. "That formula you have? It's incomplete. The sect masters were right—the original Meridian Restoration Pill kills the user. But that's because the ancient alchemists missed something. A key ingredient. A catalyst that turns poison into medicine."

Shen He's breath caught. "You know what it is?"

"I know where to find it." Yun Mei's smile widened. "The Crimson Lotus. A flower that blooms once every hundred years in the Poison Garden of the Southern Wastes. It's the only substance in the world that can heal shattered meridians without destroying the body."

The Poison Garden. Shen He had heard stories. A cursed forest where even the air was toxic, where demonic beasts roamed freely, where countless cultivators had gone to seek fortune and never returned.

"You're insane," he said.

"Maybe." She shrugged. "But I'm also the only person who can help you refine that pill. I've spent ten years studying forbidden alchemy. Ten years searching for the Crimson Lotus. And now, I've found someone who actually needs it."

"Why would you help me?"

"Because I need someone to hold the cauldron while I work." Her eyes sparkled with dark amusement. "And because helping a cripple become a cultivator? That's the kind of legend that gets written in history books."

Shen He stared at her. Every instinct screamed at him to run, to refuse, to find another way. But he had no other way. He had nothing.

"One condition," he said slowly. "If we find the lotuses, if we refine the pill, I'm the one who takes it. Not you."

Yun Mei's smile never wavered. "Of course. I have no use for it. My meridians are perfect." She extended her hand. "Do we have a deal?"

Shen He looked at her hand. Then at the crumpled page in her other hand. Then at the small town that would never be his home.

He took her hand.

"We leave at dawn," Yun Mei said.

---

The Southern Wastes were everything the stories promised and worse.

The air was thick with poisonous miasma, forcing Shen He to wrap a cloth soaked in neutralization herbs around his face. The ground was cracked and barren, littered with the bones of beasts—and cultivators—who hadn't survived the journey.

Yun Mei walked ahead of him, seemingly unaffected by the toxic environment. She moved with practiced ease, her eyes scanning the horizon. Every few minutes, she'd stop and sniff the air, then adjust their course without explanation.

"How much farther?" Shen He asked, his voice muffled by the cloth.

"Close. The lotuses should be blooming any day now. We need to reach the inner garden before the demonic beasts claim them."

"Demonic beasts?"

"Terrifying creatures mutated by the miasma. They're drawn to the lotuses like flies to honey." She glanced back at him. "Don't worry. I'll protect you."

"That's not comforting."

"It wasn't meant to be."

They walked for another hour before Yun Mei stopped abruptly. Her hand went to the dagger at her hip.

"There," she whispered, pointing ahead.

Shen He followed her gaze. In the distance, nestled in a valley between two craggy hills, was a garden. Crimson flowers bloomed in clusters, their petals glowing with an inner light that pushed back the darkness of the wastes.

The Crimson Lotuses.

But they weren't alone.

A massive serpent coiled around the valley's entrance, its scales black as obsidian, its eyes burning with malevolent intelligence. It was easily thirty meters long, its body thick as a tree trunk. Venom dripped from its fangs, sizzling where it hit the ground.

"Shadow Viper," Yun Mei breathed. "Second stage Qi Condensation. This is going to be tricky."

"Tricky?" Shen He's voice cracked. "It's a giant snake that breathes poison!"

"Don't exaggerate. It doesn't breathe poison. It just has venomous fangs." She drew her dagger. "I'll distract it. You get the lotuses."

"Me?!"

"You're the one who wants to be a cultivator, aren't you?" She grinned, and for a moment, she looked completely insane. "Time to earn your pill."

Before he could argue, Yun Mei shot forward, a blur of motion that collided with the serpent. The beast reared back, hissing, its tail lashing out like a whip.

Shen He's legs wouldn't move. Fear rooted him to the spot.

But then he thought of the sect. Of Lin Xue's cold eyes. Of Elder Wu's sneer. Of the sect master's final, damning words.

*You are banished.*

No. He wouldn't let them win. He wouldn't die as a cripple.

He ran.

The serpent's attention was fixed on Yun Mei, who danced around its strikes with supernatural grace. Shen He sprinted past them, his lungs burning, his legs screaming. He reached the garden and fell to his knees, hands

title: The Beast in the Bottle
content:
The wilderness swallowed Shen He whole.

Three days had passed since his exile, and the mortal world had proven far less forgiving than he'd imagined. The road from Azure Cloud Sect wound through forests teeming with beasts that would have been beneath a cultivator's notice but were deadly to a man who could barely light a candle with his qi.

He'd survived on wild herbs and stolen glances at his crumpled notes, searching for meaning in the sect master's warning about the Meridian Restoration Pill. Something didn't add up. If the pill was truly a death sentence, why had the formula been locked away instead of destroyed? Why had ancient alchemists risked their lives to create something that "failed"?

The questions gnawed at him as he made camp beneath an ancient oak, its roots forming a natural shelter. He was down to his last spirit stone and three silver coins. The mortal villages he'd passed had eyed him with suspicion—a young man in tattered cultivator robes, covered in soot and carrying nothing but a bag of herbs.

They knew what he was: a failure.

"You look like you've seen better days."

Shen He's hand shot to his waist, grabbing a dagger he'd stolen from the sect's kitchens. "Who's there?"

A figure emerged from the shadows between two trees. Tall, lean, with a face that seemed too young for the grey streaking his hair. He wore simple traveler's clothes, but his eyes held the sharp, calculating look of someone who had seen too much.

"Relax, boy. If I wanted you dead, you'd be dead." The man sat down across from him, uninvited. "Name's Zhao. I've been watching you since you left the sect."

"You were following me?"

"Observing." Zhao pulled a pipe from his sleeve and lit it with a finger that briefly glowed orange. A cultivator. But not from Azure Cloud. "You made quite the impression. Stealing a forbidden formula, attempting a legendary refinement, nearly blowing yourself to kingdom come. That takes balls, kid. Stupid balls, but balls nonetheless."

Shen He tensed. "What do you want?"

"An alchemist who can read ancient scripts and isn't afraid to break rules? In this economy?" Zhao grinned, revealing teeth stained brown from his pipe. "I want to offer you a job."

"I'm not interested."

"You will be." Zhao reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade bottle, tossing it to Shen He. "Open it."

Shen He caught it reluctantly. The bottle was warm to the touch, and something inside it moved. He uncorked it carefully, and a wisp of golden smoke rose out, forming the shape of a tiny serpent before dissipating.

"What was that?"

"Essence of a Golden River Serpent. Caught it myself in the Thousand Venom Swamps." Zhao took a long drag from his pipe. "That little bottle is worth more than your entire sect's treasury. And I have a hundred more like it."

Shen He's hands trembled. Not from fear—from recognition. The Golden River Serpent was mentioned in the notes he'd salvaged. Its essence was one of the key ingredients in the Meridian Restoration Pill.

"You're an alchemist," Shen He breathed.

"Was. Before I got kicked out of the Violet Dawn Pavilion for 'heretical practices.'" Zhao made air quotes with his fingers. "Sound familiar?"

"What do you want from me?"

"The formula you stole. I know you have it. I know you've been studying it. And I know you're desperate enough to try it again." Zhao's eyes bored into him. "I want to help you succeed where everyone else has failed."

"Why?"

"Because I'm old, I'm bored, and I've spent forty years trying to figure out what the ancient alchemists missed. I think you have the key." Zhao tapped his pipe against a rock, scattering ash. "You see, everyone who's tried to recreate the Meridian Restoration Pill has followed the original instructions exactly. But the instructions are wrong."

Shen He's heart raced. "Wrong?"

"Deliberately sabotaged. The ancient alchemists who created it didn't want anyone else to succeed. They left out a critical component—a catalyst that stabilizes the pill's energy. Without it, the pill destroys the meridians instead of repairing them." Zhao leaned forward. "I've spent decades searching for that catalyst. And last month, I found it."

"Where?"

"In the stomach of a dead cultivator. Specifically, the cultivator who was the last person to successfully refine the pill, three hundred years ago." Zhao pulled out another bottle, this one black as night. Inside, a single drop of liquid floated in the center, defying gravity. "This is his remnant qi, preserved in his dantian even after death. It contains the memory of the successful refinement."

Shen He stared at the bottle. The drop of liquid seemed to pulse with an inner light, and he could feel its power even from a distance.

"Why are you telling me this?" he asked. "Why not just refine the pill yourself?"

"Because I can't." Zhao's grin faded. "I'm dying, boy. My cultivation base is collapsing. I have maybe six months before my qi burns out entirely. I don't have the strength to refine a pill of this magnitude."

"And you think I do?"

"You have something I don't. Desperation. And a body that can absorb qi through pills. I've been watching you for weeks, Shen He. I know about your... unique constitution."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "How?"

"I'm an alchemist. I can see the way qi flows through a person's body. Yours doesn't flow at all—it pools. Like a reservoir with no outlet. But when you consume pills, the qi doesn't escape. It stays." Zhao's eyes gleamed. "You're not meridian-crippled. You're meridian-absorbent. The sect was right about one thing—you can't cultivate normally. But you can do something far more valuable. You can refine pills that would kill any other alchemist, because the backlash gets absorbed by your body instead of dispersing into the environment."

Shen He's mind raced. Everything he'd been told about himself, everything he'd believed, was being turned upside down.

"If I help you refine the pill," he said slowly, "what happens to me?"

"You get a functioning set of meridians. You become a real cultivator. And you get to keep the recipe." Zhao extended his hand. "I get to die knowing I didn't waste my life chasing a ghost. Deal?"

Shen He looked at the offered hand. Then at the black bottle containing the remnant qi. Then at his own notes, crumpled and stained, filled with formulas that had nearly killed him.

"If the sect master was telling the truth," he said, "if the pill really does kill people—"

"The sect master is a fool who repeats what he was told by fools before him." Zhao's voice hardened. "The pill doesn't kill people. The *formula* kills people. There's a difference. And I have the missing piece."

Shen He reached out and shook Zhao's hand.

"Good." Zhao stood up, brushing dirt from his robes. "We need to move. There's a cave three days east of here that has a natural qi concentration perfect for refinement. We'll set up there."

"What about supplies? Equipment?"

"I have everything we need. Except one thing." Zhao looked at him with an expression that was almost apologetic. "We need a test subject."

Shen He's stomach dropped. "What?"

"To test the pill. We can't refine just one—we need to make sure it works before either of us takes it." Zhao's voice was matter-of-fact, clinical. "I have a prisoner back at my hideout. A bandit who's been terrorizing the local villages. He's going to die anyway—this way, he serves a purpose."

"You want to use a human as a guinea pig?"

"Alchemy is not a gentle art, boy. Every pill that has ever been created was tested on someone. Usually the alchemist themselves, which is why so many died young." Zhao started walking. "I'm not asking you to be cruel. I'm asking you to be practical. Can you do that?"

Shen He followed, his thoughts churning. A part of him wanted to refuse, to cling to the morality he'd been taught in the sect. But another part—the part that had been called cripple, burden, worthless—whispered that morality was a luxury for people who had options.

He had none.

They walked through the night, Zhao setting a punishing pace that Shen He struggled to match. By dawn, they reached a small clearing where a cart waited, loaded with supplies and covered by a tarp.

Under the tarp, bound and gagged, was a man.

His eyes were wild with fear, and he struggled against his ropes as Zhao pulled back the covering. He was young, barely older than Shen He, with a face that might have been handsome if not for the bruises and the terror twisting his features.

"Meet our test subject," Zhao said. "Don't bother learning his name. He won't have it for long."

The man screamed into his gag, and Shen He felt something crack inside him—the last remnant of the person he'd been before exile.

"Let's go," he said, his voice hollow. "We have work to do."

As they set off toward the cave, Shen He's hand drifted to his pocket, where the jade bottle containing the serpent essence sat warm against his thigh. For the first time since his exile, he felt something other than despair.

He felt hope.

And that was the most dangerous feeling of all.

---

*Three days later, in a cave deep in the mountains, Shen He stood over a bubbling cauldron, watching as a golden liquid swirled within. Zhao stood beside him, his face pale and drawn, the effort of the journey having clearly taken its toll.*

*"Almost ready," Zhao said. "Add the serpent essence."*

*Shen He uncorked the jade bottle and poured. The liquid hit the cauldron and erupted in a flash of light, sending a shockwave through the cave that knocked them both off their feet.*

*When the light faded, the cauldron was empty.*

*And in the corner of the cave, the bound man was screaming.*

*His body was changing. His skin rippled like water, and golden light shone from his eyes. His ropes snapped, and he rose to his feet, taller than before, stronger, radiating a power that made the air itself hum.*

*"What have you done?" Shen He whispered.*

*Zhao's face had gone white. "I... I don't understand. The catalyst was supposed to stabilize it, not—"*

*The man turned to face them, and his voice echoed with a thousand other voices layered beneath it.*

*"You have freed me," he said. "After three hundred years, you have freed me."*

*And he smiled with teeth that were no longer human.*

title: The Debt That Binds
content:
The demon's blood still dripped from Shen He's fingertips as he stumbled through the forest, his breath coming in ragged gasps. Three days had passed since he'd escaped the sect's execution squad, three days since Lin Xue had watched him fall into the ravine and said nothing.

But the memory of her face—those cold, knowing eyes—clung to him like a second skin.

*Why didn't she expose me?*

He didn't have time to dwell on the question. The forest around him had gone silent. No birds. No insects. Nothing but the wet sound of his own footsteps on the damp earth.

Then he smelled it.

Blood. Fresh. Human.

Shen He dropped into a crouch, his hand moving to the pouch at his waist. Inside were twelve pills—his entire stock. Three Healing Pills, two Qi Recovery Pills, and seven of the experimental compound he'd been developing in secret. The one that could theoretically supercharge a cultivator's meridians for sixty seconds before burning them out completely.

He called it the Combustion Pill. The name was honest, if nothing else.

The blood trail led him to a clearing. In the center, a woman lay sprawled across the grass, her robe torn, her face pale as death. A sword lay three feet from her hand, its blade stained black with demon qi.

Shen He approached cautiously. The woman's cultivation level was hard to read—her qi signature fluctuated wildly, like a candle guttering in the wind. But the robes she wore were unmistakable.

Black silk. Silver embroidery. The symbol of a crescent moon swallowing a star.

The Shadow Moon Sect.

One of the three great demon sects. Enemies of the Azure Cloud. Enemies of everything Shen He had once belonged to.

He should turn around. Walk away. Let her die.

But the healer in him—the part that had memorized every scroll in the sect's library, that had dreamed of creating pills that could save lives—that part wouldn't let him move.

"Damn it," he muttered, and knelt beside her.

The wound was bad. A claw mark ran from her shoulder to her hip, deep enough that he could see the white gleam of bone. Demon qi had already begun to corrupt the edges of the wound, turning the flesh black and necrotic.

"Don't bother," the woman rasped, her eyes fluttering open. They were a startling shade of amber—unusual, even for cultivators. "I'm dead anyway."

"Not yet you're not." Shen He pulled out a Healing Pill and crushed it between his fingers, sprinkling the powder into the wound. "But you will be if you don't shut up and let me work."

The woman laughed—a wet, painful sound. "Bossy little thing, aren't you?"

"I'm a pharmacist. We're all bossy." He pressed another pill into her hand. "Eat this. Slowly. Your meridians are in shock, and if you try to cycle qi too fast, you'll rupture them."

She looked at the pill, then at him. "You're not from the Shadow Moon."

"Does it matter?"

"It matters if you're going to betray me to my enemies."

"I don't have the energy for betrayal." Shen He sat back on his heels, suddenly exhausted. "Besides, I have enough enemies of my own. I don't need to borrow yours."

The woman studied him for a long moment. Then she popped the pill into her mouth and swallowed.

The effect was immediate. Color returned to her cheeks. The wound stopped bleeding. The black corruption at its edges began to recede, pushed back by the pill's healing properties.

"Impressive," she said, pushing herself upright. "That's a high-grade Healing Pill. Better than anything our sect's alchemists can produce." Her amber eyes narrowed. "Where did you get it?"

"I made it."

"You're lying."

"I'm not." Shen He stood, brushing dirt from his knees. "I'm a pharmacist. A real one. Not a cultivator who dabbles in alchemy as a side hobby."

The woman's expression shifted. Interest. Calculation. The look of a predator who had just spotted unexpected prey.

"Interesting," she said. "What's your name, pharmacist?"

"Shen He."

"Shen He." She tested the name on her tongue. "I am Mei Ling, third disciple of the Shadow Moon Sect's inner circle. And I am in your debt."

"I didn't save you to collect a debt."

"No, but you saved me nonetheless." Mei Ling rose to her feet, moving with a grace that belied her injuries. "And in the demon cultivation world, debts are not forgotten. They are repaid. With interest."

Shen He felt a chill run down his spine. "I don't want anything from you."

"Too late. You already have my gratitude. And my attention." She smiled, and there was nothing friendly about it. "Tell me, Shen He. Why does a pharmacist of your skill find himself alone in the wilderness, hunted by Azure Cloud cultivators?"

"How did you—"

"I can smell them on you. The righteous purity of their qi. The self-righteousness that clings to their robes like perfume." She stepped closer. "You're a fugitive. An exile. A man with nowhere to go and no one to trust."

Shen He's hand moved to his pouch again. "What do you want?"

"To offer you a proposition." Mei Ling's smile widened. "The Shadow Moon Sect is always looking for talented alchemists. And from what I've seen, your talent far exceeds that of our current pharmacists."

"You want me to join a demon sect."

"I want you to consider it. That's all." She reached into her robe and pulled out a jade token, carved with the crescent moon sigil. "Take this. If you ever find yourself in the Blackwind Wastes, present it at the gates of our sect. You will be granted safe passage."

Shen He stared at the token. It gleamed in the fading light, dark and tempting as a poisoned fruit.

"I don't trust you," he said.

"And I don't trust you." Mei Ling pressed the token into his hand anyway. "But trust is for fools and children. This is a transaction. You saved my life. I'm offering you a lifeline."

She turned and walked toward the treeline, her steps steady despite her injuries.

"One more thing," she called back. "The demon that wounded me. It was no ordinary monster. It was summoned."

"Summoned by who?"

"That's the question, isn't it?" Mei Ling glanced over her shoulder, her amber eyes glinting. "Someone is hunting Shadow Moon disciples. Killing them one by one. And the trail of bodies leads back to the Azure Cloud Sect."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "You're saying the Azure Cloud is targeting your sect?"

"I'm saying nothing. I'm only giving you information." She laughed. "Consider it the first payment on my debt."

And then she was gone, melting into the shadows like smoke.

Shen He stood alone in the clearing, the jade token heavy in his hand. His mind raced. The Azure Cloud was hunting demon cultivators. The Shadow Moon was being picked off one by one. And somewhere in the middle of it all, he was a rogue variable—a pharmacist with no allegiance, no protection, and a growing list of enemies.

He tucked the token into his pouch and started walking.

Three hours later, he found the ruins.

They rose from the forest like the bones of a dead giant—crumbling stone walls, shattered pillars, and the remains of what had once been a grand courtyard. Vines crawled over everything, and the air smelled of moss and decay.

Shen He should have kept moving. Should have found shelter elsewhere.

But something drew him forward. A faint pulse of qi, so old and faded that he almost missed it.

He pushed through a collapsed archway and found himself in what had once been a laboratory. Broken glass crunched under his feet. Dust-covered tables lined the walls. And in the center of the room, bolted to the floor, sat a stone cauldron.

It was massive—easily twice his height—and covered in inscriptions so ancient that he couldn't read them. But the qi emanating from it was unmistakable.

*This is an alchemy cauldron. A real one.*

Shen He approached it slowly, his heart hammering. The inscriptions pulsed with a faint, rhythmic light, like a heartbeat.

And then he saw the words carved into the cauldron's base.

*For the Undying One.*

He froze.

The Undying One. The legendary figure from alchemy texts—the first mortal to achieve immortality through pills alone. A myth. A story told to apprentices to inspire them.

But here, in this forgotten ruin, someone had built a cauldron in their name.

Shen He reached out to touch the stone—

A hand clamped down on his wrist.

"Don't," a voice said, rough as gravel. "Touch that, and you'll never leave this place."

Shen He spun around, his heart leaping into his throat.

An old man stood behind him. Ancient. Wrinkled. His eyes were the color of ash, and his robes were so tattered that they barely qualified as clothing.

But the qi radiating from him was unlike anything Shen He had ever felt.

It was empty.

Not weak. Not depleted. *Empty.* As if the man had no cultivation at all—and yet, he had appeared without a sound. Without a whisper of spiritual energy.

"Who are you?" Shen He demanded.

"Me?" The old man grinned, revealing yellowed teeth. "I'm the last guardian of this place. The keeper of the cauldron. The fool who's been waiting three thousand years for someone to finally show up."

"Waiting for what?"

"For you." The old man's grin widened. "The Undying One's successor."

The words hit Shen He like a physical blow.

"I'm not anyone's successor," he said. "I'm just a pharmacist."

"No." The old man shook his head, his eyes boring into Shen He's soul. "You're the one who survived the Meridian Restoration Pill. The one who absorbed demon qi without dying. The one whose body rejects cultivation but embraces alchemy."

Shen He's blood turned to ice. "How do you know about that?"

"I know everything about you, Shen He." The old man released his wrist and stepped back. "I've been watching you since the day you were born."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" The old man tilted his head. "Then explain how you survived the explosion. Explain how you walked away from the Azure Cloud with nothing but a few bruises. Explain why the demon qi in your body hasn't killed you yet."

Shen He couldn't answer.

"Come," the old man said, gesturing toward the cauldron. "I have much to show you. And very little time."

"Time for what?"

"For the storm that's coming." The old man's voice dropped to a whisper. "The Azure Cloud knows about the Undying Pill. The Shadow Moon knows about it. And soon, every sect in the empire will be hunting for it."

He turned to face Shen He fully, his eyes gleaming with ancient knowledge.

"The war for immortality is about to begin. And you, Shen He, are the key."

The cauldron pulsed. The ground trembled.

And somewhere in the distance, Shen He heard the sound of approaching footsteps.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Alchemist's Gambit

The darkness was absolute.

Shen He had been in the Shadowed Marsh for three days now, and he'd learned two things: first, the marsh was alive in ways that made the sect's spirit beasts look like harmless puppies, and second, someone was following him.

He crouched behind a twisted black tree, breathing slow and shallow. The qi in his dantian pulsed, a warm current that had grown stronger with every pill he consumed. Twenty-seven pills in three days. His body hummed with power, but it was unstable—a storm waiting to break.

The footsteps came again. Soft. Measured. Whoever it was knew how to move in these cursed lands.

Shen He reached into his pouch and pulled out a Smoke Mirage Pill. One toss and he'd be gone in a cloud of illusions. But that would reveal his position. Better to wait.

A figure emerged from the mist.

Tall. Wearing grey robes that seemed to drink the light. A woman, by the shape of her, though her face was hidden beneath a hood. She stopped twenty feet away and spoke.

"You can come out, Shen He. I know you're there."

He didn't move.

"I smelled the pills you've been making. Crude work, for someone who copied the Meridian Restoration formula. The proportions are wrong—too much Crimson Lotus, not enough Nightshade Root."

Shen He's blood ran cold. She'd identified his ingredients from scent alone. That kind of knowledge belonged to a master alchemist.

"I'll ask once more," she said. "Come out, or I'll burn this entire section of marsh down around you."

He stepped out, pill ready in his hand. "Who are you?"

The woman lowered her hood.

Shen He's jaw dropped.

She was young—no older than twenty-five—with sharp features and eyes that held the weight of centuries. Her hair was silver-white, cut short in a practical style. A scar ran from her left temple to her jaw, a jagged line that pulled her mouth into a permanent half-smile.

"My name is Yun Mei. Until six months ago, I was the head alchemist of the Crimson Lotus Sect." She smiled, and it wasn't pleasant. "Now I'm a fugitive, just like you."

"The Crimson Lotus Sect?" Shen He's mind raced. They were one of the major sects, rivals to Azure Cloud. "Why would their head alchemist be a fugitive?"

"Because I discovered something I wasn't supposed to." Yun Mei reached into her sleeve and pulled out a scroll—old, yellowed, sealed with wax that glowed faintly red. "The Meridian Restoration Pill isn't a failure, Shen He. It was never meant to restore meridians at all."

"What are you talking about?"

She tossed the scroll to him. He caught it, instincts screaming at him to be careful. The wax seal was warm to the touch.

"Open it."

He broke the seal and unrolled the scroll. The characters were ancient, written in a style that predated the current cultivation era by millennia. But he'd spent years in the library, and he could read them.

The words made his heart stop.

*The Pill of Heavenly Ascension. Refined from the Meridian Restoration formula, but inverted. Where the false formula destroys, this pill rebuilds. Where the false formula kills, this pill grants eternal life.*

"This was locked in the same vault as the Meridian Restoration formula," Yun Mei said. "Your sect's founders put it there deliberately. They wanted people to find the false formula, to think it was the truth."

"Why?"

"Because the real formula works." Her eyes burned with intensity. "And the powers that be don't want anyone to have it. A pill that can restore any meridian, heal any wound, grant immortality—don't you see? It would destroy the entire cultivation system. No more need for sects. No more need for elders and masters. Anyone could become immortal."

Shen He's hands trembled. "You're lying."

"I wish I were." She stepped closer. "Your sect exiles you for attempting the false formula. My sect tried to kill me for finding the real one. I barely escaped with my life." She touched the scar on her face. "This was a parting gift from my former master."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you're the only other person I've found who's close to the truth." She gestured at the marsh around them. "You're here, in the Shadowed Marsh, looking for ingredients to refine the false formula. You think it'll fix your meridians. But it won't—it'll kill you."

Shen He's anger flared. "So what? You're here to save me?"

"I'm here to offer you an alternative." She pulled out a second scroll, smaller, wrapped in black silk. "This is the true formula. The Pill of Heavenly Ascension. I've spent six months refining it, testing it, perfecting it. It's ready."

She held it out to him.

"You want me to take it?"

"I want you to refine it." She smiled again, that sharp, unsettling expression. "I can't do it alone. The ingredients require two alchemists working in perfect synchronization. One to guide the flames, one to infuse the qi. I've been searching for a partner with the right foundation."

"You barely know me."

"I know you survived an explosion that should have killed you. I know you copied a forbidden formula and lived to tell the tale. I know you're sitting in the middle of the Shadowed Marsh, alive, when most cultivators would have died within hours." She tilted her head. "That's not luck, Shen He. That's potential."

He looked at the scroll in his hands. The false formula that had ruined his life. The real formula that could change everything.

"What's the catch?"

Yun Mei's smile faded. "The refinement process requires a blood sacrifice. Not death—but a significant amount of life essence. The alchemist who infuses the qi will be weakened for months afterward. Possibly permanently."

"And the other alchemist?"

"Guides the flames. No lasting effects, but they must be precise. Any mistake will destroy the pill and kill us both."

Shen He was silent for a long moment. The marsh's sounds crept back in—the drip of water, the rustle of unseen creatures, the distant howl of something hungry.

"Why me?" he asked. "Surely there are better alchemists out there."

"Better alchemists, yes. But none who would take this risk." She looked at him with something that might have been pity. "You've already lost everything, Shen He. Your sect, your home, your future. What do you have to fear from taking one more chance?"

He thought of Lin Xue's cold eyes. Elder Wu's sneer. The sect master's words—*you are no longer a disciple.*

He thought of the explosion, the heat, the pain.

He thought of the pills he'd been making in the marsh. Crude, unstable, barely keeping him alive.

"I'll do it," he said.

Yun Mei's smile returned. "I knew you would."

They spent the next hour preparing. Yun Mei laid out ingredients Shen He had never seen before—herbs that glowed with their own light, crystals that hummed with trapped qi, a single black lotus that seemed to drink the darkness around it.

"The ritual requires absolute silence," she said. "Once we begin, there can be no interruptions. Do you understand?"

"Yes."

She lit the cauldron. Green flames rose, unnatural and cold.

"I'll guide the flames. You infuse your qi when I tell you. Follow my rhythm exactly."

Shen He placed his hands on the cauldron's edges. The metal was freezing, despite the flames.

They began.

For the first hour, nothing happened. Shen He fed his qi into the mixture, feeling it swirl and merge with the ingredients. Yun Mei's hands moved constantly, adjusting the flames, adding ingredients at precise moments.

Then everything changed.

The cauldron screamed.

A high-pitched wail that pierced Shen He's ears and made his blood feel like it was boiling. His qi went wild, surging out of control.

"Don't stop!" Yun Mei shouted. "Keep the flow steady!"

"I can't—" His vision blurred. The marsh around them seemed to twist and warp, shadows becoming solid, the trees bending toward them like grasping hands.

"Fight it! The pill is alive—it's testing you!"

Shen He gritted his teeth and pushed more qi into the cauldron. The scream intensified, became words—

*You are unworthy.*

*You are broken.*

*You are nothing.*

"Shut up," he growled.

The voice laughed. *The cripple thinks he can ascend. The cripple thinks he can become a god. You cannot even walk the path of cultivation. What makes you believe you can walk the path of heaven?*

He thought of every insult. Every sneer. Every moment of being called worthless.

And he thought of the explosion. The power he'd felt, raw and uncontrolled. The moment when his body had done something impossible.

"I'm not a cripple," he said.

The scream stopped.

The cauldron went silent.

Shen He collapsed, gasping. His dantian was empty, drained completely. He felt hollow, weak, barely conscious.

But the cauldron was glowing.

A soft golden light emanated from within, warm and gentle. The scent that rose from it was like nothing he'd ever experienced—ancient, pure, alive.

"Open it," Yun Mei said. Her voice was barely a whisper. She looked pale, aged, as if the ritual had taken years from her.

Shen He reached for the cauldron's lid. His hands shook.

He lifted it.

Inside, a single golden pill sat in the center of the cauldron, pulsing with light like a heartbeat.

The Pill of Heavenly Ascension.

"It worked," he breathed.

A sound came from the edge of the marsh. Applause.

Shen He spun around.

A man stepped out of the shadows. Tall, thin, wearing robes of pure black. His face was smooth, ageless, his eyes like chips of obsidian.

"Well done," he said. "I was beginning to think you'd never succeed."

Yun Mei's face went white. "You."

"Me." The man smiled. "Did you really think I'd let you run free with my formula, Yun Mei? Did you think I'd allow you to share it with anyone?"

Shen He stepped in front of the cauldron. "Who are you?"

The man's smile widened. "I am Elder Mo, head of the Shadowed Marsh Sect." He spread his arms. "And I've been waiting for this moment for a very long time."

He looked at the pill in the cauldron, and his eyes gleamed with hunger.

"Give me the pill, and I might let you live."

title: The Abyssal Merchant's Price
content:
The Crimson Lotus Arena had fallen silent.

Shen He stood in the center of the bloodstained platform, his robes still smoking from the explosion that had turned his opponent into a fine red mist. The crowd of cultivators who had been cheering for his death now stared at him with something between fear and hunger.

He'd won five matches in a row. Five death matches. Five alchemical concoctions that had pushed the boundaries of what pill refinement could achieve in combat.

But the price was mounting.

His left hand wouldn't stop trembling. The backlash from that last pill—a volatile mixture of Phoenix Blood Essence and crushed Void Lotus—had sent a shockwave through his compromised meridians. He could feel them splintering, tiny fractures spreading like cracks in ice.

"The victor," the arena announcer called out, his voice hollow, "is the Alchemy Rat."

Shen He hated that name. But he'd learned not to show it. In the Nightmare City, showing weakness was the same as signing your own death warrant.

Three months had passed since his exile from the Azure Cloud Sect. Three months of running, scraping, and fighting for survival. He'd washed dishes in taverns, gathered herbs in monster-infested forests, and once spent a week hiding in a latrine pit while a bounty hunter tracked him through the slums.

Then he'd found the Crimson Lotus Arena.

A place where cripples could become kings. Where the only law was the law of the pill.

"Your payment," a scarred attendant said, tossing a leather pouch at Shen He's feet. "One hundred low-grade spirit stones. And an invitation."

Shen He caught the pouch with his good hand. "What kind of invitation?"

"The kind you don't refuse."

The attendant was already walking away, disappearing into the crowd of bloodthirsty spectators. Shen He opened the pouch, counting the stones. They were real. He'd learned to spot fakes after the second time someone tried to cheat him.

But the invitation—a black jade slip carved with a single character—made his blood run cold.

*Alchemy.*

The character wasn't written in any language he recognized. But he understood it. The meaning burned into his mind like a brand, bypassing his conscious thought and resonating with something deep inside his soul.

He pocketed the jade and limped off the platform.

The arena's lower chambers were a maze of tunnels and cells, designed to keep the fighters separated and paranoid. Shen He found an empty storage room and collapsed against the wall, his breath coming in ragged gasps.

He pulled out the jade slip again.

"What are you?" he whispered.

The character pulsed once, and then the jade began to dissolve. It turned to liquid in his hand, flowing between his fingers like black mercury. Shen He tried to drop it, but the liquid was already sinking into his skin, burning its way through his flesh and into his bloodstream.

He screamed.

The pain was worse than the meridian backlash. Worse than the explosion that had nearly killed him. Worse than anything he'd felt since awakening in that sect infirmary years ago.

Then it stopped.

Shen He opened his eyes. He was lying on the stone floor, drenched in sweat. His left hand was still trembling, but the cracks in his meridians had stopped spreading. No—they were *healing*.

"What in the seven hells..."

"An invitation," a voice said from the shadows. "And a gift."

Shen He scrambled to his feet, reaching for the pill pouch at his belt. A figure stepped into the dim light—a woman dressed in robes of midnight blue, her face obscured by a veil of stars that shifted and danced like living constellations.

"Who are you?"

"Someone who's been watching you, Shen He of the Azure Cloud Sect. Or should I say, Shen He of nowhere?"

He didn't flinch. He'd learned to control his expressions. "I don't know what you're talking about."

The woman laughed, and the sound was like wind chimes in a storm. "You're a terrible liar. But that's why I chose you. The honest ones never survive in this city."

She raised her hand, and Shen He felt a pull in his chest. The jade liquid that had seeped into his skin began to glow, forming patterns beneath his flesh like living tattoos.

"What did you do to me?"

"I gave you a connection to the Abyssal Exchange," she said. "A market that exists between worlds. A place where anything can be bought, sold, or traded—if you have the right currency."

Shen He's mind raced. He'd heard rumors of such places. Forbidden markets that operated outside the laws of heaven, where cultivators could purchase techniques, pills, and artifacts that would get them executed in the orthodox sects.

"I don't have anything worth trading."

"On the contrary." The woman stepped closer, and he caught a whiff of something ancient and powerful. "You have the most valuable commodity of all. You have *potential*."

"I'm meridian-crippled."

"Your meridians are unusual. Not broken. The sect elders were fools who couldn't see past their own dogma." She tilted her head, studying him like a curious animal. "Your body absorbs qi through pills. It converts refinement into power. Do you know how rare that is?"

Shen He said nothing.

"In a thousand years, I've only encountered three others with your condition. One became a demon lord. One ascended to immortality. And one..." She paused. "One made a terrible enemy of the Abyssal Exchange."

"What happened to him?"

"He's still paying off his debt. After five hundred years."

A chill ran down Shen He's spine. "I'm not interested."

"You will be." The woman reached into her robes and produced a scroll, bound with a ribbon of living shadow. "This contains the location of the first ingredient you need for your Ultimate Pill. The true one. Not the cheap imitation you've been cobbling together."

Shen He's heart stopped. "How do you know about that?"

"I know everything about you, Shen He. I know about the scroll you copied in the restricted section. I know about the explosion. I know about Lin Xue's betrayal." Her voice hardened. "I even know that she watched you leave the sect and felt nothing."

The anger he'd buried for three months surged up again, hot and raw. He forced it down.

"What do you want?"

"Your service. For one year, you will refine pills for the Abyssal Exchange. In return, I will give you the ingredients you need, the knowledge you seek, and the power to make those who wronged you *beg* for mercy."

Shen He stared at the scroll. It was everything he wanted. Everything he'd been searching for since the day he was cast out.

But he'd learned that nothing in this world came without a price.

"The last person who made this deal," he said slowly, "spent five centuries in debt. What makes you think I'll be different?"

The woman smiled beneath her veil. "Because you, Shen He, are the cleverest fool I've ever met. You'll find a way to cheat me. I'm counting on it."

She turned and began to walk away.

"Wait," Shen He called out. "At least tell me your name."

She paused at the doorway, her silhouette framed by the dim torchlight.

"In the mortal world, they call me the Night Hag. In the heavenly courts, I am known as the Shadow Empress. But to my friends and enemies alike, I go by a simpler name."

She looked back at him, and for just a moment, her veil parted. He saw eyes like twin black holes, consuming all light that dared to enter.

"Call me Wei Wei."

Then she was gone, and Shen He was alone in the storage room, holding a scroll that pulsed with forbidden knowledge.

He unrolled it with trembling fingers.

The first line read: *The Heart of a Living Star can only be obtained by one who has looked into the Abyss and not blinked.*

Beneath it, a single instruction: *Come to the Temple of Forgotten Gods at midnight. Bring your finest pill.*

Shen He rolled up the scroll and tucked it into his robe.

He had one hour until midnight.

As he left the storage room, he passed a mirror hanging crookedly on the wall. For a moment, he didn't recognize the face staring back at him. Gaunt cheeks. Dark circles. A scar running from his temple to his jaw—a souvenir from his second death match.

But his eyes.

His eyes were different now. They held a fire that hadn't been there before. A hunger that went beyond survival.

He was no longer the weak disciple who had begged for mercy in the sect master's hall.

He was Shen He, the Alchemy Rat.

And he was going to change the world.

Or die trying.

The Temple of Forgotten Gods stood at the edge of the Nightmare City, a crumbling structure that had been abandoned for longer than anyone could remember. The locals called it cursed. They said that anyone who entered after dark never came out the same.

Shen He didn't believe in curses. He believed in pills.

He found the temple's entrance hidden beneath a tangle of dead vines and pushed through. The interior was vast and empty, the ceiling lost in shadow. A single altar stood at the center, carved from obsidian and stained with something dark.

He approached slowly, his hand on his pill pouch.

"I'm here," he called out. "As promised."

The shadows shifted. A figure emerged from behind the altar—but it wasn't Wei Wei.

It was a man. Tall, broad-shouldered, with a face that had been carved by pain and violence. He wore the robes of a wandering cultivator, but his aura was wrong. Corrupted. Tainted by something dark.

"Shen He," the man said, and his voice was like grinding stones. "I've been looking for you."

Shen He's blood ran cold.

The man smiled, and there was nothing friendly about it.

"Lin Xue sends her regards."

Shen He's hand flew to his pills, but before he could throw them, the man moved. He crossed the distance between them in a single heartbeat, his hand closing around Shen He's throat.

"Don't struggle," the man whispered. "She wants you alive."

Shen He's vision began to darken as the pressure on his windpipe increased. He tried to reach for his pills, but his arms were pinned, his body going limp.

The last thing he saw before unconsciousness claimed him was the altar behind the man, where the obsidian surface had begun to glow with a light that shouldn't exist.

And in that light, he saw a figure standing in the shadows.

A figure that looked exactly like him.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Alchemist's Gambit

The wasteland stretched before Shen He like a wound in the earth—cracked soil, twisted rock formations, and the skeletal remains of trees that had died centuries ago. He’d been walking for three days, following the directions the refugee girl had given him, and every step felt like he was walking toward his own grave.

*The Pill of One Thousand Devils.*

The name alone should have been enough to make him turn around. But he’d already raided the refugee camp’s meager supply of herbs, and his body was screaming for more. The qi inside him had grown ravenous, devouring everything he fed it and demanding more. If he didn’t find something soon, he’d start to break down.

*One step at a time,* he told himself. *One pill at a time.*

The sun beat down mercilessly, and his water skin was nearly empty. He’d have to find a stream soon, or he’d die of dehydration before any demon got the chance to kill him.

That’s when he felt it.

A shift in the air. A subtle change in the pressure around him, like something massive had just taken a breath.

Shen He froze.

*Intruder detected,* the alchemical formula spirit said, its voice flat and clinical. *Hostile entity approaching. Estimated threat level: High. Recommend immediate evasion.*

“Thanks for the warning,” Shen He muttered, already moving toward the nearest rock formation. He pressed himself against the stone, trying to make himself as small as possible.

A figure emerged from the shimmering heat haze.

It was a man—or what had once been a man. His skin was cracked and blackened, like old leather left too long in the sun. His eyes glowed with a sickly yellow light, and his robes hung in tatters around a frame that was too thin, too wrong. He moved with a jerking, unnatural gait, as if his joints were grinding together with every step.

“Shen He,” the figure said, and its voice was like rocks grinding together. “The sect wants you dead. But I want you alive.”

Shen He’s blood ran cold. “Who are you?”

“A messenger.” The figure smiled, and his teeth were black, rotten. “The elders have placed a bounty on your head. Twenty thousand spirit stones for your capture. Fifty thousand for your corpse.”

“That’s... a lot of money for a cripple.”

“You’re no cripple.” The figure took another step forward, and Shen He could smell the decay radiating off him. “We know what you are. The sect knows what you are. The whole cultivation world will know soon enough.”

*The whole cultivation world.*

Shen He’s mind raced. The Azure Cloud Sect had exiled him quietly, hoping he’d just disappear. But someone had talked. Someone had spread the word about the Meridian Restoration Pill, about the explosion, about the cripple who could refine forbidden formulas.

“Who sent you?” Shen He asked, his hand moving to the pouch at his belt. Inside were three pills he’d refined in desperation—crude, unstable, but effective.

“The Demon Sovereign,” the figure said. “He has... an interest in your abilities.”

*Demon Sovereign.*

The name hit Shen He like a physical blow. The Demon Sovereign was a legend, a figure of nightmare whispered about in the mortal world. He was said to command an army of corrupted cultivators, to have conquered a third of the continent, to be searching for the secret to immortality.

And he was interested in *Shen He*.

“I’m flattered,” Shen He said, his voice steady despite the fear clawing at his chest. “But I have other plans.”

“Your plans don’t matter.” The figure lunged.

Shen He was ready. He grabbed a pill from his pouch and crushed it in his palm. A burst of purple smoke exploded outward, filling the air with a cloying, sickly sweet smell. The figure stumbled, his movements slowing as the smoke invaded his lungs.

“Grasping Flower’s Sorrow,” Shen He said, backing away. “Refined from withered nightshade and crushed bone dust. It won’t kill you, but it’ll slow you down for a few minutes.”

The figure laughed, a horrible grating sound. “Clever. But not clever enough.”

He reached into his own robes and pulled out a small, black pill. He swallowed it, and his body began to change.

His skin cracked further, revealing molten light beneath. His limbs elongated, his fingers turning into claws. His eyes blazed with infernal fire, and when he spoke, his voice was no longer human.

“The Demon Sovereign does not take no for an answer.”

Shen He ran.

He didn’t think. He didn’t plan. He just ran, his legs pumping, his heart hammering in his chest. The terrain blurred around him as he dodged rocks and leaped over crevices, the sound of the pursuing demon growing closer with every second.

*Calculating escape routes,* the formula spirit said. *Probability of survival: 12%. Recommend drastic action.*

“What kind of drastic action?!”

*The Pill of One Thousand Devils. Consume it.*

“I haven’t finished refining it!”

*It is 73% complete. The incomplete formula will cause severe damage to your body, but it will also grant you temporary power sufficient to defeat your pursuer.*

“And then I die from the backlash?!”

*Probability of death from backlash: 89%. Probability of death from current pursuer: 88%. The odds are statistically similar.*

Shen He wanted to scream. The formula spirit was technically correct—he had slightly better odds if he took the pill. But “slightly better” still meant he was almost certainly going to die.

Unless...

*The soul crystals,* he thought. *Ling’er said the demon cultivators use them to amplify their power. If I can get my hands on one...*

He changed direction, veering toward the twisted rock formation that marked the entrance to the wasteland’s heart. The demon was close behind him, close enough that he could feel the heat radiating off its corrupted body.

“Running won’t save you!” the demon roared. “The Sovereign always gets what he wants!”

Shen He didn’t answer. He was too focused on the path ahead, on the narrow crevice that led into the darkness beneath the rocks. He dove into it, scraping his arms and shoulders against the rough stone, and emerged into a vast underground chamber.

The chamber was filled with crystals.

They grew from the floor and ceiling like frozen waterfalls, pulsing with a faint, blue light. They hummed with energy, a low vibration that Shen He could feel in his bones. And at the center of the chamber, suspended from the ceiling by chains of crystallized qi, was a body.

A cultivator’s body. Dead. Drained.

*Soul crystal extraction site,* the formula spirit said. *The demon cultivators harvest the souls of their victims here, refining them into power.*

Shen He’s stomach turned. He’d heard rumors of such places, but seeing one in person was different. The body at the center was a warning—a reminder of what happened to those who crossed the Demon Sovereign.

*You can save him,* a voice whispered in his mind. *You have the knowledge. You have the power.*

It was insane. The man was clearly dead. His soul was gone, harvested and refined into crystals that lined the walls. There was nothing left to save.

But Shen He couldn’t stop staring at the body. At the empty eyes, the slack jaw, the hands that had once reached for something, anything, before the darkness took him.

*I could save him,* Shen He thought. *If I had the right pill. If I had enough time.*

“Found you.”

The demon emerged from the crevice, its body now fully transformed. It was a hulking beast of molten rock and shadow, its claws scraping sparks from the stone floor. Its yellow eyes fixed on Shen He with predatory glee.

“No more running, little alchemist. The Sovereign wants your mind, and I intend to deliver it.”

Shen He’s hand went to his pouch. He still had the Grasping Flower’s Sorrow, two unstable Qi Surge pills, and the incomplete Pill of One Thousand Devils.

*Choose,* the formula spirit said. *Surrender. Escape. Or fight.*

Shen He took a breath.

Then he pulled out all three pills, crushed them together in his palm, and swallowed the mixture before he could think about what he was doing.

The effect was immediate—and catastrophic.

Fire flooded his veins. His vision went white. His organs felt like they were being torn apart and reassembled in the wrong order. He screamed, but the sound was lost in the roar of power that exploded from his body.

*Incomplete formula detected. Multiple drug interactions occurring. Body rejecting 47% of compounds. Recommend immediate medical attention.*

“Shut up,” Shen He growled, his voice distorted, layered with something that wasn’t quite human.

He looked at the demon.

The demon looked back.

And for the first time, Shen He saw fear in its yellow eyes.

---

*Continue to Chapter 15?*

title: The Serpent's Secret
content:
The road stretched before Shen He like an open wound—unfamiliar, unwelcoming, and bleeding into darkness. Three weeks had passed since his exile from the Azure Cloud Sect, and the memory of Lin Xue's cold eyes still burned in his chest like embers that refused to die.

He'd traveled south, following rumors of a fallen star that had crashed into the Whispering Vale. Mortals spoke of it as an omen. Cultivators dismissed it as celestial debris. But Shen He knew better. Nothing fell from the sky without purpose.

The Whispering Vale lived up to its name. Strange winds coiled through the narrow canyon, carrying voices that weren't quite human. Shen He pressed forward, one hand hovering over the pouch at his belt. Inside lay twelve pills he'd refined during his journey—crude things, barely worthy of the name, but enough to keep him alive if things turned violent.

*If I survive this, I'm going to need better ingredients,* he thought, sidestepping a patch of glowing moss that pulsed with malevolent energy. *And a lab. And maybe a small army to keep the sects off my back.*

The fallen star had carved a crater the size of a village into the valley floor. Shen He approached cautiously, his breath catching as the scene came into view. The impact site glowed with residual heat, the earth fused into black glass that reflected the dying light of dusk. But it wasn't the crater that made him stop.

It was the man standing at its center.

He was tall, lean, with silver hair that seemed to float in the still air despite the absence of wind. His robes were torn and blackened, revealing skin that shimmered with faint golden patterns—tattoos that moved, shifting like living things beneath his flesh.

"You're early," the man said without turning around. His voice carried a musical quality, smooth and dangerous. "I was told I'd have another hour before the scavengers arrived."

Shen He didn't move. Every instinct screamed at him to run. This man radiated power in a way he'd never felt before—not even from the sect master. It was like standing before a furnace, the heat pressing against his skin, threatening to consume him.

"I'm not a scavenger," Shen He said, keeping his voice level. "I was tracking the star."

"Were you now?" The man finally turned, and Shen He's blood ran cold.

His eyes were wrong. Both were silver, but the left one had a vertical pupil, like a serpent's. It fixed on Shen He with predatory intensity, while the right remained human, curious, almost amused.

"Interesting," the man said, stepping out of the crater. The glass cracked beneath his feet, each step leaving perfect prints. "A cripple with the scent of alchemy on him. No cultivation base to speak of, yet you walk into a place where even Qi Condensation cultivators fear to tread. Either you're very brave, or very stupid."

"I've been called both," Shen He replied. "Usually by the same person."

The man laughed—a genuine sound that seemed out of place in the desolate valley. "I like you. That's rare. Most people I meet either want to kill me or worship me. You seem to want neither."

"I want information," Shen He said. "That star you rode in on—I need to know what it was."

The man's smile faded. For a moment, something flickered in his serpentine eye—recognition, perhaps, or suspicion. "You don't know what I am, do you?"

"Should I?"

"Everyone in the cultivation world knows the name Yan Meng. The Serpent Emperor. Disgraced son of the Celestial Serpent Clan. Wanted for crimes against the Heavenly Court." He spread his arms, revealing more of the golden tattoos—scales, Shen He realized. They were scales. "I've been hunted across three continents and five oceans. And you, a meridian-crippled boy with a bag full of mediocre pills, stand before me asking questions."

Shen He's mind raced. The Celestial Serpent Clan was legend—beings who stood at the border between mortal cultivators and true immortals. If this man was truly one of them, then his arrival wasn't random. It was an escape. A fall from grace.

"You're running," Shen He said.

"I'm surviving," Yan Meng corrected. "There's a difference."

"Is there?"

The serpentine eye narrowed. "You have a sharp tongue. It'll get you killed one day."

"Not today, I hope."

Yan Meng studied him for a long moment, then laughed again. "No. Not today. You interest me, boy. Most people who learn my identity either attack or beg. You do neither. You treat me like an equal."

"I treat everyone like an equal," Shen He said. "It's less work that way."

"Then perhaps we can help each other." Yan Meng reached into his torn robes and pulled out a small box—black, carved from some material that seemed to drink the light around it. "This star wasn't a random meteor. It was a prison. I was meant to be sealed for a thousand years, but someone broke the formation early. Someone who wanted me free."

Shen He's eyes locked onto the box. It hummed with power, a low thrum that vibrated in his bones. "What's inside?"

"A key," Yan Meng said. "To the Sky-Rending Array. A formation that can tear open the boundary between our world and the Immortal Realm." He held the box out, his expression unreadable. "I was carrying it when they caught me. They wanted to destroy it, but I hid it where they couldn't find it. In my own blood."

"Why are you showing me this?"

"Because I can't open it." Yan Meng's voice dropped, losing its musical quality, becoming raw and tired. "The seal requires a specific key—one that only exists in formulaic records. Alchemical records. I've spent fifty years searching for someone who could decipher them. The sects have all refused me. The Alchemy Guild branded me a heretic."

Shen He's heart pounded. He could feel it—the pull of forbidden knowledge, the promise of power beyond imagining. But he'd learned his lesson. The Meridian Restoration Pill had taught him that some secrets were locked away for good reason.

"I'm not interested in tearing open the heavens," he said carefully.

"Neither am I." Yan Meng's human eye held Shen He's gaze. "I want to close them."

"Come again?"

"There's something on the other side," Yan Meng said, his voice barely a whisper. "Something that's been trying to break through for centuries. The Sky-Rending Array was designed by the Celestial Serpent Clan as a failsafe—a way to seal the boundary permanently. But before we could activate it, we were betrayed. The array was shattered. Its pieces scattered across the mortal world."

Shen He's mind reeled. He'd heard whispers of the Immortal Realm's instability in the sect's restricted section, but nothing like this. Nothing about a conspiracy spanning generations. "You're telling me that the Celestial Serpent Clan, the most powerful beings this side of godhood, were destroyed trying to keep the Immortal Realm out?"

"Not destroyed. Silenced." Yan Meng's jaw tightened. "Those who survived were branded traitors. Our names were erased from history. Our deeds twisted into legends of villainy. And the ones who betrayed us? They sit on the Heavenly Court, pretending to be humanity's protectors."

Shen He wanted to dismiss it as madness. But something in Yan Meng's voice rang true—a desperation that couldn't be faked. "What do you need from me?"

"A pill," Yan Meng said. "One that can break the seal on this box without destroying its contents. The formula is lost, but I have fragments. Enough to reconstruct it with the right mind." He looked at Shen He with something approaching hope. "I've watched you since you left the Azure Cloud Sect. I saw what you did with the Meridian Restoration Pill. You have a gift, boy. A talent for seeing what others cannot."

"You were watching me?"

"I told you. I've been hunted across three continents. I know how to track prey without being seen." Yan Meng's smile returned, but there was no cruelty in it. "I also know when someone is worth taking a risk for."

Shen He's hand drifted to his pouch. Twelve pills. None of them powerful enough to face even a single Qi Condensation cultivator. But the knowledge in his head—the formulas, the theories, the desperate experiments he'd conducted in the shadows of the sect's library—that was worth more than any pill.

"What's the pill called?" he asked.

Yan Meng's serpentine eye gleamed. "The Heart-Erosion Pill. It dissolves creation's bindings at the fundamental level. Dangerous to refine. Fatal if mishandled." He paused. "Sound familiar?"

Shen He laughed—a hollow, bitter sound. "You're asking me to risk my life for a formula that could kill me."

"I'm asking you to risk your life for the chance to save everyone else's." Yan Meng held out the black box. "I can't force you. But I can offer you something in return."

"What?"

"Protection." The word hung in the air, heavy with meaning. "The sects want you dead. The Alchemy Guild wants your secrets. The moment you step into any city with a cultivation presence, you'll be arrested, tortured, or killed. I can offer you sanctuary. A place where you can refine in peace."

"And in exchange, I help you close the boundary."

"In exchange, you help me prevent an apocalypse." Yan Meng's eyes locked onto his. "What do you say, Shen He? Do you want to spend the rest of your life running? Or do you want to make them regret ever underestimating you?"

Shen He looked at the box. Then at the path behind him, winding back toward a world that had already condemned him. Then at the sky above, where stars winked in the darkness, hiding secrets that could destroy everything.

He reached out and took the box.

"I'll need a lab," he said. "And ingredients. And no one watching over my shoulder while I work."

Yan Meng's smile widened, revealing teeth that were just slightly too sharp. "I know just the place."

As they turned to leave, a sound cut through the wind—a whistle, high and piercing, carrying across the valley like a herald's call. Yan Meng froze, his expression shifting from triumph to alarm.

"They found me," he said.

"Who?"

"The Heavenly Court's hunters." He grabbed Shen He's arm, his grip like iron. "Run. Now."

"Run where?"

"Anywhere." Yan Meng's serpentine eye blazed with silver light. "Because if they catch us, we won't just die. We'll be erased from existence itself."

The whistle came again, closer this time. And in the distance, Shen He saw lights—dozens of them, like falling stars—descending toward the valley with terrifying speed.

He clutched the black box to his chest and ran.

title: The Phoenix's Gambit
content:
The Obsidian Tower loomed before Shen He, its surface so dark it seemed to drink the moonlight. Each of its twelve floors pulsed with a faint crimson glow, like veins of blood running through black stone. The alchemists who had captured him—Guild Master Feng's personal enforcers—had dragged him through the city's underground passages for what felt like hours before emerging into this courtyard.

"You know what this place is," one of the enforcers said. It wasn't a question.

"The Tower of Last Breath," Shen He replied, his voice hoarse from the gag they'd only just removed. "Where the guild tests forbidden formulas on condemned criminals."

"Or ambitious fools who think they can steal from the guild's vaults and live to brag about it."

Shen He didn't bother correcting them. He hadn't stolen anything. He'd *bought* the Fire Lotus Essence from a merchant who'd claimed it was legitimate stock. But the moment he'd tried to sell it on the open market, the guild's black-iron badges had appeared from every shadow, and he'd found himself in chains.

"Guild Master Feng has decreed that you will undergo the Trial of Three Flames," the enforcer continued, shoving Shen He toward the tower's entrance. "If you survive, your debt is paid. If you don't..." He shrugged. "At least your organs will be useful for future experiments."

The tower's door swung open without anyone touching it, revealing a spiral staircase that descended into darkness. No, not darkness. The stairs were *moving*, shifting and rearranging themselves like the gears of some impossible clockwork.

"First floor," the enforcer said. "The Chamber of Endless Hunger. There's a door at the bottom. Reach it before the walls close in, and you pass. Simple."

Shen He looked down. The stairs were still moving, but now he noticed the walls—they were *breathing*. Expanding and contracting like a living creature's ribcage. And at the bottom, barely visible, a door that glowed with the same crimson light as the tower's exterior.

"How long do I have?"

"You don't. The walls move at their own pace. Some make it in minutes. Others..." The enforcer smiled, revealing teeth filed to points. "Others learn that the tower is never truly empty."

Before Shen He could ask what that meant, the enforcer shoved him forward. The door slammed shut behind him, and the tower's interior plunged into absolute darkness.

For a moment, Shen He stood still, letting his eyes adjust. They didn't. The darkness here was complete, total—the kind of darkness that had weight, that pressed against your skin like a physical thing. He could feel the stairs beneath his feet, but he couldn't see them.
