*Think. The pill. Use the pill.*

He reached into his sleeve and pulled out the Night-Eye Pill he'd prepared before the enforcers had caught him. It was crude, unstable—he'd only had minutes to craft it while hiding in a gutter—but it would have to do. He crushed it between his fingers and inhaled the powder.

The effect was immediate. The darkness peeled back, revealing the stairs in sharp, crystalline detail. But the walls... the walls were already halfway to his position. They'd moved faster than he'd expected.

Shen He ran.

The stairs were treacherous, constantly shifting beneath his feet. One step would be solid stone, the next would crumble into dust that reformed a second later. Twice, he nearly fell, catching himself on railings that writhed like snakes beneath his grip.

"The tower is never truly empty."

The enforcer's words echoed in his mind as he heard the first whisper. It came from the walls themselves, a soft, sibilant voice that seemed to speak directly into his skull.

*Shen He. Shen He. Why do you run?*

He ignored it, focusing on the stairs. The door was closer now. Twenty steps. Fifteen.

*They abandoned you. The sect. The prodigy. All of them. You're alone.*

"Shut up," he muttered, leaping over a section of stairs that collapsed into nothing.

*You were always alone. Even in the sect, you were nothing but a cripple playing at alchemy. Your master never believed in you. Lin Xue never saw you as anything but a curiosity.*

Ten steps. The door was so close he could see the runes carved into its surface.

*But here, in this tower, you can prove them all wrong. Survive this, and you'll be stronger than any of them. The guild will have to respect you.*

Eight steps. The walls were closing in, and the movement was making him dizzy. The Night-Eye Pill was wearing off, the edges of his vision starting to blur.

*Or die. That's fine too. At least you'll be remembered.*

"I said *shut up*!"

He threw himself forward, crashing through the door just as the walls slammed together behind him. The impact sent shockwaves through the stone, and Shen He lay on the ground, gasping for breath.

"Interesting."

The voice was new. Not the whispering from the walls, but a clear, feminine voice that seemed to come from everywhere at once. Shen He looked up.

A woman stood at the center of the second floor, dressed in robes of pure white silk. Her hair was silver, her eyes were gold, and she held a staff made of what looked like crystallized starlight. She was beautiful in the way a blade was beautiful—sharp, deadly, and utterly without mercy.

"You're the first person to pass the first floor in three hundred years," she said. "Most of them panic. The whispers get inside their heads, make them forget about the stairs. They end up crushed."

"Lucky me," Shen He said, climbing to his feet. "Who are you?"

"I am the Phoenix. Guardian of this tower. Servant of no one." She tilted her head, studying him like a curious cat. "And I have been watching you, Shen He. Since the moment you entered this city. Since before you even knew this tower existed."

The words hit him like a physical blow. "What? How?"

"Because you carry something within you. Something that burns." She stepped closer, and Shen He felt the temperature in the room rise. "The sect master told you that the Meridian Restoration Pill was poison. He lied."

Shen He's heart stopped. "What?"

"The pill works. It always worked. The sect master knew that. But he also knew that if you succeeded in restoring your meridians, you would become something the Azure Cloud Sect could not control." The Phoenix smiled, and her teeth were too white, too sharp. "So he told you it was a lie. Made you believe you were broken. Kept you weak."

The rage that surged through Shen He was so pure, so absolute, that he couldn't breathe. All those years. All those years of being treated like a cripple, like a failure. And it had been a *lie*.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I want to make you an offer." The Phoenix raised her staff, and the runes on the walls began to glow. "The Tower of Last Breath has two more floors. If you pass them both, you will reach the center—the Heart of the Phoenix. There, you can claim the fire that burns at the core of this tower. The same fire that was used to create the first Meridian Restoration Pill, three thousand years ago."

Shen He's mind raced. "You're telling me the formula is here?"

"Better than the formula. The *source*." The Phoenix's eyes gleamed. "With it, you could create a pill so powerful that no sect, no guild, no power in this world could ever control you again. You could become the greatest alchemist in history. Or..."

"Or?"

"Or you could die. The third floor has never been passed. Even I do not know what lies beyond it." She spread her arms. "So what will it be, Shen He? Will you continue your climb? Or will you take the door behind you, walk out of this city, and live the rest of your life as a nobody?"

The door she gestured to was plain, unadorned, and open. Beyond it, Shen He could see the streets of Blackwood City, the lanterns flickering in the night.

One door to freedom. One door to power.

He thought of the sect master's cold eyes. Lin Xue's indifference. Elder Wu's contempt. He thought of the alchemists who had chased him, the guild that had imprisoned him, the world that had always told him he was worthless.

"I'll climb," he said.

The Phoenix smiled. "I was hoping you'd say that."

She raised her staff, and the floor beneath Shen He's feet vanished.

He fell.

For a moment, there was nothing but darkness and wind, the sensation of dropping into an endless void. Then the ground rushed up to meet him, and he landed in a crouch, absorbing the impact with practiced ease.

He was in a room of mirrors. Thousands of them. They lined the walls, the ceiling, the floor—every surface reflected his image back at him, distorted and twisted. One mirror showed him as an old man, withered and dying. Another showed him as a child, crying in the dark. A third showed him as a king, crowned and triumphant.

"Welcome to the third floor," the Phoenix's voice echoed. "The Hall of a Thousand Truths."

Shen He turned, trying to find a path through the mirrors. But every reflection showed him something different, something wrong.

"Each mirror shows a version of yourself. A path you could have taken. A choice you could have made. Some are beautiful. Some are terrible." The Phoenix's voice grew softer. "But only one is true. Find the mirror that shows the real you, and the door will open."

Shen He approached the nearest mirror—the one showing him as a king. He reached out to touch it, and the image shifted. The king turned to look at him, and Shen He saw his own face, but older, harder, crowned with gold.

"Welcome, brother," the king said. "I'm the version of you that stayed in the sect. That groveled. That made himself useful enough to keep around." The king's smile was cruel. "You should have seen Lin Xue's face when I proposed to her. Surprise. Disgust. And then... acceptance. Because she knew I was the only one who could match her."

Shen He pulled his hand back. The king laughed, the sound echoing through the hall.

"Too painful? Try another."

He moved to the next mirror—the child, crying in the dark.

"Please," the child whispered. "Please, someone help me. I can't cultivate. I can't do anything. I'm worthless."

Shen He turned away.

Mirror after mirror showed him different versions of himself. The cripple who gave up. The alchemist who sold his soul. The man who became a monster. Each one was familiar, terrifying, *possible*.

And then he found it.

In the corner of the hall, half-hidden behind a curtain of shadows, was a mirror that showed him exactly as he was. Tired. Bruised. Bloodied. But standing.

"It's not the strongest version," the Phoenix said, appearing beside him. "Not the richest. Not the happiest. It's just... you."

Shen He looked at his reflection. For the first time in his life, he didn't see failure. He didn't see a cripple. He saw a man who had survived.

"Real enough," he said, and touched the glass.

The mirror shattered, revealing a door of pure white light. Beyond it, Shen He

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Poison That Remembers

The Crimson Lotus Sect's compound sprawled across three connected valleys, its buildings a garish mix of blood-red pagodas and gold-trimmed halls that screamed wealth and violence in equal measure. Shen He had spent the last six weeks inside these walls, and he still hadn't gotten used to the aesthetic.

"Eat faster," Zhao Min said, slamming a bowl of medicinal herbs onto the table before him. "Master wants the Foundation Establishment pills ready by sundown."

"I know." Shen He ground the herbs without looking up. The motion had become automatic now, his hands moving with precision that belied his supposed cripple status. "This batch needs another hour of settling."

"The master doesn't wait for herbs to settle."

"Then the master can explain to Elder Feng why his precious pills explode on contact with qi." Shen He finally looked up, meeting Zhao Min's gaze. "Or I can do it right. Your choice."

Zhao Min's jaw tightened, but she said nothing. That was the thing about being useful—people tolerated insolence they'd never accept otherwise.

Six weeks. Six weeks since the Azure Cloud Sect had thrown him out like garbage. Six weeks since Lin Xue had stood there, ice in her eyes, watching him fall. Six weeks since he'd learned that everything he thought he knew about cultivation was wrong.

The Crimson Lotus Sect had found him three days after his exile, half-dead from a qi deviation that had nearly torn his body apart. They'd saved him. For a price.

*Three pills a week. All the ingredients you need. A place to work.*

He'd agreed without hesitation. Not because he trusted them—he trusted no one now—but because they had something the Azure Cloud Sect didn't.

A library of forbidden texts.

"Shen He." The voice came from the doorway, smooth as silk and twice as dangerous. Sect Leader Yan Li stepped into the alchemy hall, her crimson robes flowing behind her like a river of blood. "I trust your work proceeds well?"

"Nearly done with the Foundation Establishment pills. The Meridian Cleansing formula needs another day."

"Good." She walked past him, her fingers trailing across the shelves of ingredients he'd organized. "I have a new task for you. One that requires... discretion."

Shen He's hands stilled. "I'm listening."

"I've acquired a scroll. An ancient text detailing a pill formula that hasn't been successfully refined in three hundred years." She turned to face him, and he saw hunger in her eyes—not for power, but for knowledge. "The Soul Severing Pill."

The name hit him like a physical blow. "That's a myth. A legend."

"It's real. And you're going to refine it."

"Impossible. The ingredients alone—"

"Are already gathered." She pulled a jade slip from her sleeve and tossed it onto the table. "The formula is inside. Study it. In one week, you will begin the refinement."

Shen He stared at the jade slip, his mind racing. The Soul Severing Pill. It wasn't just forbidden—it was *cursed*. Every alchemist who had attempted its refinement had died horribly, their souls ripped from their bodies and scattered across the void.

"Why me?" he asked. "You have dozens of alchemists. Ones with real cultivation bases, real talent."

"Because you're desperate." Yan Li's smile was cold, beautiful, and utterly without mercy. "The desperate don't hesitate. The desperate don't second-guess. And you, Shen He, are the most desperate man I've ever met."

She left without another word, her robes whispering against the stone floor.

Shen He picked up the jade slip. It was warm to the touch, pulsing with a faint energy that made his skin crawl. He pressed it to his forehead, and the formula flowed into his mind like poison.

*Nine ingredients. Three forbidden. Two extinct.*

*One impossible final step.*

He read it again. And again. And when he finally understood what the formula required, he laughed—a hollow, broken sound that echoed through the empty hall.

The final step required the alchemist's own life force. Not qi. Not cultivation base. *Life itself*. The Soul Severing Pill would only complete its refinement if the creator poured their entire existence into the cauldron.

"Desperate," he muttered. "She has no idea."

He worked through the night, grinding ingredients, testing reactions, making notes that would have sent any orthodox alchemist into cardiac arrest. The formula was mad. Brilliant. Deadly.

And it might just work.

Dawn broke over the valley, painting the Crimson Lotus compound in shades of gold and red. Shen He hadn't slept. He didn't need to. His body had changed over the past six weeks, adapting to the constant flow of pill qi in ways he didn't fully understand.

He no longer felt tired. He no longer felt hungry. And sometimes, when he closed his eyes, he could see the flow of qi in the world around him—bright streams of energy that moved through everything like blood through veins.

It should have been impossible. A cripple couldn't see qi. A cripple couldn't absorb qi. A cripple couldn't *do* any of the things he'd been doing for weeks now.

But here he was. Living proof that the elders were wrong. That Lin Xue was wrong. That everything he'd been taught about cultivation was built on lies.

"Shen He." Zhao Min appeared at his elbow, her face pale. "There's someone at the gate. She says she knows you."

He looked up, his heart suddenly pounding. "Who?"

"She didn't give a name. Just said to tell you... the library shelf fell."

The pestle slipped from his fingers, clattering against the table.

*No. It can't be.*

He moved before he could think, his feet carrying him through the compound at a speed that should have been impossible. Disciples jumped out of his way, their faces a blur as he passed. He burst through the main gate and skidded to a halt.

She stood alone on the path, dressed in simple traveler's robes, her hair pulled back in a functional knot. No sect insignia. No weapons. Just a small bag and a face that held more guilt than he'd ever seen.

"It's been a while, He'er," Lin Xue said, and her voice cracked on the words.

Shen He's fists clenched at his sides. "What are you doing here?"

"Running." She took a step forward, and he stepped back. "The Azure Cloud Sect—something's wrong. Elder Wu, the sect master, they're not who they appear to be. I found records. Documents. Things that don't make sense."

"Not my problem anymore."

"It is, because they mention you." She pulled a scroll from her bag and held it out. "This was hidden in the sect master's private chambers. It's a list. Names of every cripple who's ever been taken in by a major sect. And your name is on it. With a note."

Shen He took the scroll with trembling hands. He unrolled it, scanned the contents, and felt the world tilt beneath his feet.

*Subject #47: Shen He. Meridian degeneration confirmed. Entered Azure Cloud Sect at age six. Monitored for twelve years. Anomalous qi absorption detected. Recommend extraction and study.*

"What the hell is this?" he whispered.

"I don't know," Lin Xue said. "But when I found it, someone tried to kill me. I barely escaped. They'll be hunting me now. Hunting us both."

"Why would you come to me?" He looked up, meeting her eyes. "You watched them throw me out. You didn't say a word."

"I couldn't." Tears welled in her eyes—the first time he'd ever seen the sect's prodigy cry. "If I had spoken, they would have killed me too. And then I wouldn't have been able to find this. To warn you."

"Your timing is terrible."

"I know."

They stood there, the morning sun rising behind them, the Crimson Lotus Sect's walls at Shen He's back and Lin Xue's truth hanging between them like a blade.

"You should leave," he finally said. "This sect isn't safe either."

"I didn't come here for safety." She stepped closer, and this time he didn't move back. "I came here because you're the only person I've ever known who fights against the current. Who refuses to accept what the world tells him he is."

"Pretty words, coming from someone who called me a thief."

"I was wrong." She grabbed his hand, and he felt the tremble in her fingers. "I was arrogant. I was blind. And now I'm begging you—help me figure out what's happening. Because whatever the sects are doing, whatever they've been doing for centuries... it ends with you."

Shen He looked down at their joined hands. He thought about the Soul Severing Pill. The forbidden formula. The impossible final step.

He thought about the list of names in his hand. Dozens of cripples, all monitored, all studied, all marked for "extraction."

And he thought about the cold, burning anger that had been growing in his chest for six weeks.

"I'll help you," he said, pulling his hand away. "But not because I forgive you. Not because I trust you. Because someone needs to burn this world down, and I've got plenty of fuel."

Lin Xue nodded, her face pale but determined. "Where do we start?"

"We start," Shen He said, turning toward the Crimson Lotus compound, "by refining a pill that's killed every alchemist who's tried to make it. And then we use it to kill everyone who's been lying to us."

"You want to kill the sect masters?"

"I want to kill the *system*." He looked back at her, and in his eyes, she saw something that made her take a step back. "The sects, the guilds, the elders—they're all part of the same machine. And I'm going to shatter it. Piece by piece. Pill by pill."

He entered the compound without waiting for her response.

Behind him, Lin Xue stood frozen, watching him disappear into the shadows of the Crimson Lotus Sect.

And in the distance, hidden in the morning mist, a figure in gray robes watched them both, a scroll half-unrolled in his hands.

On it, written in blood-red ink, was a single line:

*Subject #47 has been located. Extraction team dispatched.*

title: The Last Ingredient
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

He'd been walking for three days since leaving the Azure Cloud Sect, following the winding roads that led through the foothills and into the untamed wilds beyond. The world outside the sect was different—noisier, dirtier, and utterly indifferent to his existence. Mortal farmers tilled their fields. Merchants hawked their wares. Children laughed and played in the streets of small villages he passed through. None of them cared that he'd been a cultivator. None of them knew he was a cripple.

It was almost refreshing.

But the anger hadn't faded. It burned in his chest like a coal that refused to die, feeding on every memory of that night. Lin Xue's cold eyes. Elder Wu's smug satisfaction. The sect master's pity. They'd thrown him out like garbage, and they'd done it believing he was worthless.

They were wrong.

Shen He stopped at the edge of a stream, kneeling to cup water into his mouth. The cool liquid did nothing to quench the fire inside him. He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page he'd salvaged from the explosion. The notes were incomplete, half the characters smudged or burned away, but the core formula was still there. The Meridian Restoration Pill.

*The pill does not restore meridians. It destroys them.*

The sect master's words echoed in his mind, but Shen He had spent the last three days thinking about them, and something didn't add up. If the pill was useless, why was it locked away in the restricted section? Why were there so many warnings around it? Why had Elder Wu's face gone pale when he'd seen Shen He with the scroll?

Fear.

Not concern for Shen He's safety. Fear of what the pill might actually do.

Because the sect master had lied about one thing—the pill *did* work. Shen He knew that now. He'd felt the strange energy during his failed refinement, the way the pill had tried to connect to his broken meridians before the explosion. It hadn't destroyed anything. It had strained against something. Something that was blocking the connection.

A seal.

The thought hit him like a thunderbolt. He dropped the page, his hands trembling. No. That was impossible. Who would seal a cripple's meridians? What purpose would that serve?

But the more he thought about it, the more it made sense. His meridians weren't damaged—they were blocked. Locked. And someone had put a lot of effort into making sure they stayed that way.

"Interesting theory," a voice said from behind him.

Shen He spun around, his heart slamming against his ribs. A woman stood twenty feet away, leaning against a tree with her arms crossed. She was young, maybe a few years older than him, with sharp features and eyes that gleamed like polished jade. Her robes were dark blue, trimmed with silver thread that seemed to drink the light.

She was a cultivator. A strong one.

"Who are you?" Shen He demanded, his voice steadier than he felt.

"Someone who's been looking for you," she said. "You're harder to find than I expected, Shen He. Most banished disciples head straight for the nearest city to drown their sorrows. You went into the wilderness. Impressive survival instincts."

"You've been following me?"

"Since you left the sect." She pushed off the tree and walked toward him, her steps light and deliberate. "I wanted to see what you'd do. Where you'd go. Whether you'd give up or keep fighting."

"And?"

She stopped a few feet away, tilting her head as she studied him. "You're still alive. Still thinking. Still planning. That's more than most would manage after what happened to you."

Shen He's hand drifted toward the pouch at his belt, where he kept the remnants of his alchemy supplies. "If you're here to kill me, get it over with. I've had a long week."

The woman laughed—a genuine, surprised sound. "Kill you? No. I'm here to offer you a job."

"A job?"

"The Alchemy Guild has been watching you, Shen He. We know about the pills you've been making. We know about the failed refinement. And we know about your... unique condition." Her eyes flickered to his chest, where his sealed meridians lay dormant. "You're not a cripple. You're a puzzle. And the guild loves puzzles."

Shen He's mind raced. The Alchemy Guild. They were one of the major powers in the cultivation world, rivaling even the great sects in influence. If they'd been watching him, it meant his activities hadn't gone unnoticed. It meant he might have more value than he'd realized.

"Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't." She pulled a token from her sleeve—a bronze medallion engraved with a mortar and pestle, the symbol of the guild. "But you should trust that I have something you need. Information. Resources. Protection." She tossed the token to him, and he caught it on reflex. "The guild has archives that date back ten thousand years. If anyone can help you understand what was done to your meridians, it's us."

Shen He stared at the token in his hand. It was warm, humming with a faint energy that he could almost feel through his sealed channels. "What's the catch?"

"The catch is that you work for us. You refine pills, you share your discoveries, and you don't ask too many questions about the guild's other operations." She smiled, but there was something sharp behind it. "We're not a charity, Shen He. We're a business. But we're a business that rewards talent."

"And if I refuse?"

"You don't." She turned and began walking away, her voice carrying back to him over her shoulder. "You have three days to make your way to the guild's eastern branch in Clearwater City. After that, the offer expires. And trust me—you don't want to be out here alone when the things that hunt in the dark find you."

She vanished into the trees, leaving Shen He alone with the token and a thousand new questions.

---

Clearwater City was a sprawling metropolis of stone and wood, built at the confluence of three rivers that gave it its name. Merchants and cultivators mingled in the streets, their auras a chaotic mix of mortal and transcendent energy. Shen He had never seen so many people in one place. The Azure Cloud Sect's outer compound held maybe five hundred disciples. Clearwater City held tens of thousands.

The guild's eastern branch was a massive compound near the city center, its walls lined with defensive formations that made Shen He's skin prickle. He presented the token at the gate, and the guards let him through without a word.

Inside, the compound was a maze of halls and courtyards, each one dedicated to a different aspect of alchemy. There were pill refinement chambers, ingredient storage vaults, and libraries that made the sect's restricted section look like a child's bookshelf. Shen He was led to a small office where a portly man in guild robes sat behind a desk, reviewing a stack of documents.

"Ah, Shen He," the man said, looking up with a smile that didn't reach his eyes. "I'm Councilor Feng. Welcome to the guild. I trust your journey was uneventful?"

"It was... educational," Shen He said, taking a seat across from him.

"Good, good." Councilor Feng set down his papers and folded his hands on the desk. "I'll be frank with you, young man. The guild doesn't usually recruit banished disciples. It's bad for our reputation. But your case is... unusual. The woman who found you, Senior Examiner Yue, was quite insistent that we bring you in."

"Why?"

"Because she believes you have potential that the Azure Cloud Sect was too blind to see." Councilor Feng's eyes narrowed. "And because she believes you can help us with a problem we've been having."

Shen He's instincts screamed at him to be careful. "What kind of problem?"

"A refinement problem." Councilor Feng pulled a scroll from his desk and unrolled it, revealing a complex diagram of a pill formula. "This is the formula for an Evergreen Pill—a mid-grade pill that extends a cultivator's lifespan by fifty years. It's one of our most profitable products. But recently, our refiners have been unable to produce it at the required purity. The failure rate has skyrocketed, and we're losing money."

Shen He studied the diagram. The formula was elegant, but there was a flaw in the energy flow—a bottleneck that would cause instability during the final stage of refinement. It was subtle, something most alchemists would miss unless they had a deep understanding of how different ingredients interacted.

"You're using the wrong catalyst," he said.

Councilor Feng's eyebrows rose. "Explain."

"The formula calls for Rainbow Lotus extract as the primary catalyst, but Rainbow Lotus has been overharvested in recent years. The batches you're getting now are lower quality, which means they can't handle the strain of the refinement. If you switch to a different catalyst—something with a more stable energy signature—the problem should solve itself."

"And what catalyst would you recommend?"

"Phoenix Tail Grass. It's harder to find, but it's more resilient. You'll need to adjust the refinement temperature, but the result will be more consistent."

Councilor Feng stared at him for a long moment, then broke into a genuine smile. "Senior Examiner Yue was right about you. You see what others miss." He rolled the scroll back up and tucked it away. "We'll test your theory. If it works, you'll be rewarded handsomely."

He reached into his desk and pulled out a small jade token, handing it to Shen He. "This is your guild identification. It grants you access to the basic facilities—living quarters, training rooms, and the first floor of the archives. As you prove yourself, you'll gain access to higher levels."

Shen He took the token, feeling its weight in his palm. It was lighter than the bronze one he'd been given earlier, but it felt more significant. This was his foot in the door. His chance to rebuild.

"One more thing," Councilor Feng said, his voice dropping to a serious tone. "The guild has enemies. Rival alchemy organizations, demonic cultivators, and even some of the great sects who don't appreciate our influence. You'll be expected to contribute to the guild's defenses if needed. Are you prepared for that?"

Shen He thought of Lin Xue's cold eyes. Elder Wu's sneer. The sect master's pity.

"I've been preparing my whole life," he said.

---

Three weeks passed.

Shen He threw himself into his work, refining pills for the guild during the day and sneaking into the archives at night. The first floor of the archives was vast, filled with scrolls and books on basic alchemy, but he was looking for something specific—anything that mentioned meridian seals.

He found nothing.

The guild's records were thorough, but they focused on pill formulas and refinement techniques, not the esoteric arts of sealing and binding. He was beginning to think he'd hit a dead end when he found a reference in an old journal, buried in a section on ancient cultivation methods.

*The Heaven's Mandate Seal: A technique used by ancient emperors to bind the meridians of their most powerful enemies. The seal is nearly impossible to break, as it is tied to the very essence of the sealed individual's soul. Only a pill of equal complexity can hope to unravel it.*

Shen He's hands trembled as he read the entry. A pill. That was the key. A pill that could break the seal on his meridians and unlock his true potential.

But the entry didn't provide the formula

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Debt Collector's Price

Shen He pressed himself flat against the alley wall, his breath coming in short, controlled bursts. The golden formation disc hummed in his hand, its surface still warm from activation. Three days of hunting, three days of running, and he'd finally cornered what he'd been chasing.

A Blackwind Viper. Juvenile. Maybe fifty years old, its scales still carrying the telltale iridescence of youth.

It shouldn't have been here.

The Azure Cloud Sect's outer territories were supposed to be safe. Cleared of threats. That was the whole point of having a sect—protection for the mortal villages that paid tribute. But this viper had somehow slipped through, and it had already killed two farmers and a child.

Shen He watched it slither through the shadows at the end of the alley, its tongue flicking out to taste the air. It was hunting. Looking for more prey.

*Good,* Shen He thought. *So am I.*

He reached into his pouch and pulled out a small vial. The liquid inside was a pale, sickly green—a paralytic agent he'd refined from spider lilies and crushed dreamroot. Not enough to kill a cultivator. More than enough to drop a juvenile beast.

But he only had one dose.

The viper paused, its head turning in his direction. It had sensed him. Of course it had. He was a cripple with no cultivation base to hide. To a beast like this, he probably smelled like prey.

*Perfect.*

Shen He stepped out of the alley, the vial held openly in his hand. The viper's eyes locked onto him, and its body coiled, muscles bunching for a strike.

"Come on," Shen He whispered. "I'm right here."

The viper lunged.

Shen He didn't dodge. He didn't try to run. He stood his ground and waited until the last possible second, then flicked his wrist, sending the vial spinning directly into the viper's open mouth.

The beast's momentum carried it forward, its jaws snapping shut around the glass. Shen He threw himself to the side, rolling as the viper crashed into the wall where he'd been standing. Brick and mortar exploded outward.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the viper's movements slowed. Its coils loosened. Its eyes, once bright with predatory hunger, went glassy and unfocused.

Shen He got to his feet, brushing dust off his robes. "Took you long enough."

He walked up to the paralyzed beast and drew a small knife from his belt. The viper's gaze tracked him, aware but unable to move. Shen He met its eyes.

"I'm sorry about this," he said. "But I need your core. And your venom glands. And your blood."

He worked quickly, methodically. He'd learned anatomy from the books in the sect's library, refined his technique through trial and error on smaller beasts. The viper was his biggest kill yet, and he treated it with the respect it deserved.

By the time the sun rose, he had what he needed.

---

The village of Green Hollow was small, maybe a hundred families. Shen He had passed through it three days ago, seen the fear in the villagers' eyes, heard the stories of the beast that had taken their children.

Now he stood in the center of the square, the viper's head held aloft by its tail.

"I've killed your monster," he announced.

The villagers stared at him. A boy with dirty robes and hollow cheeks, holding the severed head of a demon beast. It should have been a moment of triumph.

Instead, the village elder stepped forward, his face hard. "And who are you to bring such trouble to our doorstep?"

Shen He blinked. "Trouble? I just saved—"

"You brought a Blackwind Viper to our lands!" the elder shouted. "Do you think we're fools? These beasts don't wander into human territory on their own. Someone had to draw it here. Someone like you."

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. Accusations. Suspicion.

*Of course,* Shen He thought bitterly. *Why would anything ever be simple?*

"I'm a wandering alchemist," he said, keeping his voice steady. "I hunt beasts for their materials. I found this viper three miles from here. It had already killed two of your people."

"Four," a woman's voice cut through the crowd. "It killed my husband last night. While you were playing hero."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "Last night? That's impossible. I tracked this beast for three days. It couldn't have—"

"Are you calling my wife a liar?" The village elder stepped closer, and Shen He saw the glint of a knife in his hand. "I don't know who you are, boy, but I know a scapegoat when I see one. The sect sends their rejects down here all the time, thinking they can make a name for themselves by killing a few beasts. But you? You're different. You're weak. And this viper was too strong for you to handle alone."

Shen He's grip tightened on the viper's head. "I killed it. By myself. With no cultivation. That should tell you something."

"It tells me you're either very lucky or very dangerous," the elder said. "And I don't like either option."

The crowd pressed in, and Shen He found himself backing toward the well at the center of the square. He could run. He could fight. Both would end badly.

"I'm leaving," he said. "I'll take the viper's remains and go."

"No." The elder shook his head. "You'll leave the head. And the body. As compensation for the trouble you've caused."

Shen He laughed. It came out bitter, hollow. "You want the beast that killed your people? Fine. Take it." He tossed the viper's head at the elder's feet. "But know this—there are more where that came from. The sect's barrier is failing. I saw the cracks in it when I was tracking this beast. Whatever protection the Azure Cloud Sect was supposed to give you? It's gone."

The elder's face went pale. "You're lying."

"I'm an alchemist," Shen He said. "I don't lie. I deal in facts." He turned and walked toward the village gates. "You have a week. Maybe two. Then the beasts will come in force. I'd suggest you start praying to whatever gods you worship."

He didn't look back. He didn't need to. He could feel their eyes on his back, just like he'd felt them at the sect. Always the same. Rejection. Fear. Hatred.

*Welcome to the mortal world, Shen He. It's exactly as welcoming as you remember.*

---

The forest outside Green Hollow was dense, the canopy thick enough to block out the sun. Shen He moved through it with practiced ease, his senses on high alert. The viper's blood was still fresh in his pouch. He needed to process it before it spoiled.

He found a small clearing, barely ten feet across, and set up his portable alchemy station. A bronze cauldron. A spirit stone burner. A mortar and pestle. His tools of the trade.

The viper's blood went into the cauldron first, followed by crushed dreamroot and a pinch of powdered silver. He stirred the mixture with a steady hand, watching as it turned from red to a murky purple.

*Almost there.*

He added the venom glands, squeezing every last drop of toxin into the brew. The liquid hissed and bubbled, releasing a pungent steam.

*Come on. Come on.*

The mixture stabilized. Shen He let out a breath he didn't realize he'd been holding. He'd done it. A Rank 2 Body Tempering Elixir. Enough to strengthen his physical form, even without cultivation.

He was about to bottle it when he heard the footsteps.

He spun, a knife already in his hand. Standing at the edge of the clearing was a woman in white robes, her face half-hidden by a veil.

She clapped slowly. "Impressive. Most alchemists need a proper workshop to refine a Rank 2 elixir. You did it under a tree with scraps."

Shen He didn't relax. "Who are you?"

"Someone who's been looking for you." The woman stepped closer, and Shen He caught a whiff of her scent—something floral, with an undertone of copper. "You're Shen He, yes? The cripple who was expelled from the Azure Cloud Sect?"

"Word travels fast."

"It does when you blow up a quarter of the outer compound." The woman smiled behind her veil. "Don't worry. I'm not here to judge you. I'm here to offer you a job."

Shen He's hand tightened on his knife. "I'm not interested."

"You haven't heard the offer yet."

"I don't need to." He started packing up his equipment. "Everyone who's offered me a job so far has wanted me dead at the end of it."

"This one is different." The woman reached into her sleeve and pulled out a scroll. "The Alchemy Guild has been watching you, Shen He. Ever since you refined that Meridian Restoration Pill. We were impressed."

Shen He froze. "You know about that?"

"We know everything that happens in the alchemy world." She tossed the scroll at his feet. "That's an invitation. A real one. Not a trap, not a trick. The Alchemy Guild wants you as an apprentice."

Shen He stared at the scroll. It was sealed with wax, a golden cauldron emblem pressed into it. The mark of the Alchemy Guild.

"Why?" he asked. "I'm a cripple. I can't cultivate. I can't even use qi to refine pills."

"And yet you made a forbidden pill that should have killed you." The woman's eyes gleamed. "Don't you think that's worth investigating?"

Shen He picked up the scroll. It felt heavy in his hands. Too heavy.

"What's the catch?"

"There's always a catch." The woman's smile widened. "But I'll let you read the details yourself. Just know this—the Guild has enemies. Powerful ones. And if you accept this invitation, you'll be making those enemies yours as well."

She turned to leave, then paused. "Oh, and Shen He? That viper you killed? It wasn't a stray. Someone planted it there. Someone wanted to see what you'd do."

Before he could respond, she was gone, melting into the shadows like smoke.

Shen He stood alone in the clearing, the scroll in one hand and the elixir in the other. Two paths. Two choices.

And somewhere in the distance, he heard the howl of something much larger than a viper.

title: The Demon in the Vial
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

Three days had passed since he left the Azure Cloud Sect, and the mortal world was exactly as he remembered it—dirty, dangerous, and utterly devoid of qi. He'd traveled south, away from the sect's territory, following a crumbling road that wound through increasingly wild lands.

His bag held little. The spirit stones clinked with each step. The crumpled page of pill formula rustled against his chest, tucked inside his robe. And in his right hand, he carried something new—a small jade vial, warm to the touch, that he'd found tucked in a hollow tree the night before.

He didn't know who left it there. He didn't care. The moment his fingers brushed the vial, he'd felt it—a resonance that made his blood sing.

Whatever was inside, it was powerful.

He found shelter in an abandoned hunter's cabin as dusk fell. The roof sagged. The door hung crooked on leather hinges. But the walls were thick stone, and the hearth still held dry kindling from some long-dead hunter's last fire.

Shen He lit a flame with a spark from his pill-enhanced qi—a small miracle that still made him grin—and settled onto the dirt floor.

The jade vial sat in his palm, catching the firelight.

He'd been afraid to open it. Afraid of what might pour out. But sitting here, alone, with nothing left to lose, that fear felt like a luxury he couldn't afford.

He pulled the stopper.

A wisp of black smoke curled out, thick and oily, carrying the scent of crushed herbs and something else—something metallic. Blood.

The smoke coalesced into a face. Old. Wrinkled. Eyes like pits of darkness.

"You finally opened it," the face said, its voice like grinding stones.

Shen He didn't flinch. "Who are you?"

"I am the memory of a man who died a thousand years ago. I am the formula that was never written down. I am the pill that should never have been refined." The face smiled, and its teeth were black. "But you can call me Master Yi."

"I don't have a master."

"Everyone has a master, boy. Even the ones who think they don't." The face drifted closer, and Shen He felt a cold pressure against his skin. "You've been poisoned."

"What?"

"Look at your arm."

Shen He rolled up his sleeve. A thin black line ran from his wrist to his elbow, branching like a river delta. He hadn't noticed it before. Hadn't felt it.

"The Meridian Restoration Pill," Master Yi said. "The incomplete one you refined. It left a trace in your blood. In three months, it will reach your heart. In four, you'll be dead."

Shen He's throat tightened. "You're lying."

"I have no reason to lie. I'm already dead." The face drifted back to the vial, hovering above its mouth. "But I can help you. I know the true formula. I know the technique that makes it work. I know things that the Azure Cloud Sect's ancient alchemists never dreamed of."

"And what do you want in return?"

"Clever boy." The face's smile widened. "I want you to find my body."

Shen He stared at the apparition. "Your body?"

"I was murdered. My killer took my research—my life's work—and scattered the pieces across the continent. The true Meridian Restoration Formula is one of those pieces. The others..." Master Yi's eyes glowed. "They're even more valuable. But they're worthless to you without my body. My body holds the key to understanding them."

"So I find your body, and you give me the cure for this poison?"

"And more. I'll teach you alchemy that will make the Azure Cloud Sect's elders weep with envy. I'll show you pills that can reshape your meridians, strengthen your soul, grant you powers that cultivators spend centuries chasing."

Shen He's hand tightened around the vial. "Why me?"

"Because you're desperate. Because you have nothing. Because when a man has nothing, he's willing to do anything." The face began to fade. "Rest tonight. Tomorrow, follow the smoke. It will lead you to the first piece."

The face dissolved. The black smoke retreated into the vial.

Shen He stared at the jade container for a long time. Then he laughed—a bitter, hollow sound that echoed in the empty cabin.

Of course. Of course there was a catch. There was always a catch.

But he was already dying. What was one more deal with a demon?

---

He dreamed of fire.

A great crimson pill hovering above an ancient cauldron. Figures in black robes chanting in a language he couldn't understand. A man screaming as his body dissolved into golden light.

And then—silence. A single hand reaching out of the darkness, holding a jade vial.

*Take it,* a voice whispered. *Take it and become what you were meant to be.*

Shen He woke with a gasp.

Morning light streamed through the cabin's broken window. His body ached. His arm throbbed where the black line traced its path. But something was different.

He felt... stronger.

He looked at his hands. The calluses from years of scrubbing floors were fading. His fingers moved with a precision they'd never had before. And when he focused, he could feel the qi in the air—faint, distant, but present.

The pill fragments he'd absorbed in the sect were finally settling. His body was adapting. Growing.

The jade vial sat beside him, warm as a living thing.

"Fine," he said, his voice rough with sleep. "Let's see where this smoke leads."

He pulled the stopper. Black smoke poured out, forming a thin thread that drifted through the cabin door and into the forest beyond.

Shen He followed.

---

The smoke led him through dense woods, across a frozen river, and into the foothills of a mountain range he didn't recognize. The terrain grew rougher. The air thinner. And the smoke moved faster, as if eager to reach its destination.

By noon, he found the first sign that he wasn't alone.

Footprints. Fresh ones. At least a dozen people, heading in the same direction.

Shen He crouched, studying the tracks. These weren't hunters. The prints were too deep, too evenly spaced. Cultivators. Probably from a sect he didn't know.

He followed more carefully now, staying off the main path, moving through the shadows of the trees.

The smoke led him to a clearing. In the center stood a stone altar, covered in moss and ancient carvings. And around the altar, a group of black-robed figures chanted in that same language from his dream.

At their center, a young woman knelt, bound and gagged.

She looked up as Shen He approached, her eyes wide with terror. She was beautiful—high cheekbones, dark hair, skin like porcelain. But her beauty was secondary to the runes carved into her arms. Glowing runes that pulsed with each chant.

The black-robed figures turned as one.

"An observer," the leader said, his voice hollow. "We were not expecting company."

Shen He's hand went to his bag, to the jade vial. "I'm just passing through."

"You're passing through at a very unfortunate time." The leader raised his hand, and the other figures began to move, surrounding him. "This is a sacred ritual. Outsiders are not welcome."

"I'll leave, then."

"You'll leave when the ritual is complete. As a sacrifice."

The figures lunged.

Shen He moved on instinct, his body responding faster than his mind. He ducked under a sword swing, rolled past a grasping hand, and came up behind the altar. The young woman stared at him, tears streaming down her face.

He didn't think. He grabbed her, pulled her to her feet, and ran.

The forest exploded around them. Trees shattered. The ground split. The black-robed figures pursued with a speed that defied reason.

"This way!" the young woman gasped, pulling him toward a cliff face. "There's a cave!"

They reached it just as the first figure caught up—a blade of black energy slicing through the air where Shen He's neck had been a moment before.

He threw himself into the cave, dragging the woman with him. Darkness swallowed them.

The figures stopped at the entrance.

"She's inside," one said. "The barrier will hold."

"Then we wait," the leader replied. "She can't stay in there forever."

Shen He's breath came in ragged gasps. The woman beside him was shaking, her runes still pulsing with a faint light.

"Thank you," she whispered. "I thought I was going to die."

"You might still," Shen He said, not unkindly. "Who are you? What were they doing to you?"

She looked at him, and in the darkness, her eyes seemed to glow. "My name is Yun Mei. I'm the last of the Crimson Phoenix bloodline. And those men were trying to extract my blood for a forbidden pill."

Shen He's blood ran cold.

The Crimson Phoenix. He'd heard stories in the sect's library—a legendary bloodline that granted its bearers immortality through reincarnation. The last Crimson Phoenix had died a thousand years ago.

"When they take your blood," he said slowly, "what happens to you?"

"I die," Yun Mei said. "But I come back. That's what the phoenix does. We rise from our own ashes."

"Then why are you running?"

She met his eyes, and he saw something ancient in them—a weariness that went beyond years. "Because I'm tired of dying," she said. "I've died a hundred times. A thousand. Each time, I forget a little more of who I was. One day, I'll rise and remember nothing. And then I'll be nothing."

Shen He stared at her.

The jade vial in his bag grew warm, and he heard Master Yi's voice echo in his mind.

*The Crimson Phoenix. The key to immortality. The final ingredient for the Ultimate Pill.*

Shen He looked at Yun Mei, at her desperate, ancient eyes.

He looked at his arm, at the black line creeping toward his heart.

And he smiled.

"Let me tell you about a pill," he said. "A pill that could change everything."

Outside, the black-robed figures began to chant again.

The barrier around the cave pulsed once, twice—and began to crack.

title: The Pilgrim's Last Breath
content:
The gates of the Azure Cloud Sect groaned shut behind Shen He, the sound echoing through his bones like a funeral bell. He stood on the worn stone steps, the morning mist curling around his ankles, and for a long moment, he didn't move.

He had nowhere to go.

Eight years. Eight years of scrubbing floors, organizing scrolls, and enduring the contempt of every disciple who crossed his path. Eight years of sneaking into restricted sections, copying forbidden formulas by candlelight, and feeding his starving meridians with whatever pills he could scrape together. Eight years of believing that if he just worked hard enough, learned enough, endured enough—he would find a way.

And now it was over. Not because he'd failed, but because he'd succeeded. The Meridian Restoration Pill was real. He'd felt it work. For three glorious seconds, his meridians had hummed with power, and his body had drunk in qi like a man dying of thirst.

But the sect master's words echoed in his mind: *It doesn't work. It kills the user.*

Shen He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page of notes. His handwriting, cramped and desperate, covered every inch of the yellowed paper. Calculations. Ingredient substitutions. Temperature logs. And at the bottom, scrawled in the margin: *"Reverberation pattern suggests incomplete activation. Need secondary catalyst. Something alive."*

He'd been so close. The pill had worked for three seconds before the backlash. The issue wasn't the formula—it was the *method*. The ancient alchemists had been refining the pill wrong for centuries. They'd treated it as a single-stage transformation, when it was actually a two-stage process. First, the meridians had to be *broken*—dissolved into raw potential. Then, they had to be *rebuilt* using a living catalyst to guide the reconstruction.

But what living catalyst? And more importantly, how the hell was he supposed to figure that out when he'd just been thrown out of the only place with access to ancient texts?

Shen He shoved the notes back into his bag and started walking.

The path down the mountain was treacherous, winding through bamboo groves and past sheer cliffs that dropped into mist-shrouded valleys. He'd walked it a hundred times on supply runs, but it had never felt like this. Each step carried him further from the only home he'd ever known, and closer to... what?

The mortal world. A place he barely remembered. He'd been six when the sect recruiters found him, starving in a village that had been ravaged by a rogue beast. They'd taken him in because his spiritual roots showed promise. They'd kept him because they didn't have the heart to throw a child out. And they'd let him stay because, crippled or not, he was useful.

Now he was useless. And useless people didn't survive long in the world beyond.

The sun climbed higher, burning away the mist. By midday, Shen He had reached the base of the mountain, where the sect's territory ended and the mortal kingdoms began. A weathered stone arch marked the boundary, its surface covered in fading inscriptions warning travelers of the dangers ahead.

Shen He paused beneath the arch and looked back. The Azure Cloud Sect was hidden by clouds, its peaks invisible from this angle. He could pretend it didn't exist. He could pretend the last eight years hadn't happened.

But the burn in his meridians told a different story. The pill had changed something. Even now, hours after the backlash, his body felt... different. The damaged meridians that had plagued him since birth were still there, twisted and fragile as ever. But they were *warm*. Vibrating with a faint energy that hadn't been there before.

Shen He closed his eyes and focused, reaching for the qi that had always eluded him. Normally, it was like trying to catch smoke with his bare hands. But this time—

A spark. Tiny, barely perceptible, but *there*. A thread of qi, no thicker than a hair, connecting his dantian to his meridians. It flickered and died almost immediately, but Shen He's eyes snapped open, his heart racing.

The pill had worked. For three seconds, his meridians had been whole. And even after the backlash, some of that wholeness remained. A scar, maybe. A memory of what he could become.

If he could stabilize the formula, if he could find the right catalyst, if he could survive the process—

"Move along, boy. You're blocking the path."

Shen He turned to find an old man driving a cart of vegetables, his face weathered and indifferent. Shen He stepped aside, and the man trundled past without a second glance.

Right. The mortal world. Where people didn't care about cultivation or meridians or forbidden pills. Where the biggest concerns were weather, taxes, and whether the harvest would last through winter.

It was almost relaxing.

Shen He followed the road, letting his feet carry him where they would. He had no destination, no plan, no resources beyond a handful of spirit stones and a change of clothes. But he had his knowledge. He had his notes. And he had the faint, stubborn hope that somewhere out there, he could find the answers he needed.

The road led him to a small town as the sun began to set. Pilgrim's Rest, according to a weathered sign at the entrance. A trading post, by the look of it—a scattering of wooden buildings around a central square, with merchants hawking everything from cloth to cooking pots to medicinal herbs.

Shen He's stomach growled. He hadn't eaten since yesterday, and his body was screaming for fuel. He found a noodle stall and spent one of his precious spirit stones on a bowl of broth and noodles. The vendor looked at the stone like it was a foreign object, then shrugged and made change in copper coins.

Shen He ate slowly, savoring each bite. The broth was thin, the noodles overcooked, but it was the first meal he'd had in eight years that wasn't part of the sect's communal rations. It tasted like freedom.

"Looking for work?"

Shen He looked up. A woman stood by his table, her arms crossed. She was middle-aged, with calloused hands and sharp eyes that missed nothing. Her robes were practical, stained with ink and what looked like blood.

"I might be," Shen He said carefully. "What kind of work?"

"The kind that pays." She pulled out a chair and sat down without waiting for an invitation. "I'm a field medic. I go where the fighting is, patch up whoever pays. My last assistant got his throat cut by a bandit who didn't appreciate being saved. I need someone who can follow instructions and doesn't faint at the sight of blood."

Shen He considered. He knew alchemy. He knew anatomy from his studies. He could probably handle basic field medicine.

"How much?" he asked.

"Room, board, and a share of the profits. If we survive, you'll walk away with enough coin to buy a house." She leaned forward. "I'm heading north tomorrow. There's a skirmish brewing between the Crimson Serpent Clan and the Iron Mountain Sect. Plenty of bodies to practice on."

Shen He's ears perked up at "sect." The Iron Mountain Sect was a minor power, known for their body cultivation and crude but effective alchemy. They might have resources. Texts. Information.

"I'll take it," he said.

The woman—her name was Granny Wei, though she looked barely fifty—led him to a rundown cart piled high with bandages, salves, and tools. She tossed him a bedroll and gestured to the ground beneath the cart.

"You sleep there. We leave at dawn. Don't snore."

Shen He didn't sleep. He lay on the hard ground, staring up at the cart's wooden underbelly, and ran through his notes in his head. The Meridian Restoration Pill. Two-stage process. First stage: dissolution. Second stage: reconstruction. The pill he'd made had only handled the first stage. The second required a living catalyst to guide the rebuilding.

But what kind of catalyst? A beast's inner core? A cultivator's blood essence? His own life force?

The last option was the most terrifying. If the pill required his own vitality to complete the transformation, he would have to gamble everything on a single, irreversible moment.

He fell asleep with the taste of copper on his tongue.

Dawn came too soon. Granny Wei kicked the cart's wheel, rattling the frame and jolting Shen He awake. "Up, boy. We've got a long road ahead."

They traveled north, following a winding path through forests and across rivers. Granny Wei talked constantly, sharing stories of battles she'd patched up, cultivators she'd saved, and the ones she'd let die because they couldn't pay.

"Never work for free," she said, chewing on a piece of dried meat. "Generosity gets you killed. The moment people think you'll help without compensation, they'll bleed you dry."

"You don't seem to lack compassion," Shen He observed.

"Compassion is a luxury. I save it for people who remind me of my grandchildren." She glanced at him. "You remind me of my youngest. Too smart for his own good. Died trying to prove he was worthy of a sect's attention."

Shen He didn't know what to say to that, so he said nothing.

By evening, they reached a ridge overlooking a valley. Below, campfires dotted the landscape like fallen stars. The Iron Mountain Sect's encampment. On the far side of the valley, a rival force—the Crimson Serpent Clan—had set up their own camp.

"Get some rest," Granny Wei said, unpacking her supplies. "Tomorrow, the real work begins."

Shen He sat on the ridge, watching the two camps. Somewhere down there were cultivators, alchemists, and secrets he could use. Somewhere down there was the key to completing his pill.

But first, he had to survive.

He touched the crumpled notes in his bag, feeling the faint warmth of the paper. The answer was there, hidden in the margins of his failed experiment. He just had to find it.

The first battle cry rang out at dawn. Shen He stood at the edge of the ridge, watching cultivators clash in the valley below. Qi blasts lit up the morning sky. Swords clashed. Men screamed.

Granny Wei was already descending, her medical kit strapped to her back. "Come on, boy. Time to earn your keep."

Shen He followed, his heart pounding. He was walking into a warzone with nothing but his wits and a handful of incomplete knowledge.

But somewhere in the chaos, the answer was waiting.

And he was going to find it.

title: The Ghost Who Walks
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

Three days had passed since his exile, and the mountain paths of Azure Cloud Sect had long since vanished behind layers of mist and ancient trees. He walked without direction, without purpose, his feet carrying him forward simply because stopping meant admitting defeat.

The pill furnace in his storage ring felt heavier than it should have.

He found shelter in a hollow beneath an uprooted oak, its roots forming a natural cave just large enough for him to curl up inside. Rain fell in sheets outside, and he watched it with empty eyes, his fingers tracing the crumpled page of notes he'd salvaged.

The Meridian Restoration Pill.

A lie. A death sentence. A formula that had cost him everything.

But as he stared at the faded characters, something nagged at the back of his mind. The sect master had said the pill didn't work. That it killed the user. But Shen He had been refining it for weeks, and he'd felt something during those sessions—a resonance, a pull, as if the pill wanted to be made.

*No*, he thought, crushing the paper in his fist. *It's a dead end. Move on.*

But where?

The mortal world stretched before him, vast and indifferent. He had no family, no connections, no skills that any village would value. A cripple who knew too much about alchemy and too little about survival.

He was still pondering his options when the footsteps came.

Three men emerged from the rain, their boots squelching in the mud. They wore dark cloaks, hoods pulled low, but Shen He could see the weapons at their belts—curved blades, the kind favored by assassins.

"Shen He of the Azure Cloud Sect?" one of them asked, his voice flat.

"I'm not with the sect anymore."

"That's not what we asked."

Shen He's hand drifted toward his storage ring. "Who wants to know?"

"The Alchemy Guild sends its regards."

The words hit him like a punch to the gut. The Guild. Of course. They'd been watching him for months, ever since he'd started producing pills that shouldn't have been possible for someone with no cultivation base. He'd thought the sect's walls would protect him.

*Stupid*, he cursed himself. *You're always stupid.*

The lead assassin drew his blade. "You have two options. You come with us quietly, or we bring your corpse to the Guild for examination. Either way, they get what they want."

Shen He's mind raced. He was no fighter. His body was weak, his qi nonexistent. But he had one advantage these men didn't know about.

He had pills.

"Option three," he said, and threw a handful of powder into their faces.

The assassins recoiled, coughing, as a cloud of white dust filled the hollow. It wasn't anything deadly—just a mixture of crushed herbs he'd used for sleeping draughts—but it bought him three seconds.

He ran.

The forest blurred around him as he crashed through underbrush, branches tearing at his clothes, mud sucking at his feet. He could hear them behind him, cursing, gaining ground. They were cultivators. Even low-level ones would be faster, stronger, more durable than him.

He needed a plan.

A cliff loomed ahead, the ground falling away into a ravine. The river below churned white and angry. If he jumped, he might die. If he didn't, he definitely would.

Shen He made a choice.

He leaped.

The fall was longer than he'd expected. The wind screamed in his ears, and the water rushed up to meet him like a hungry mouth. He hit the surface hard, the impact driving the air from his lungs, and then the current had him, dragging him under, spinning him like a leaf in a storm.

He didn't fight it.

When he finally broke the surface, gasping, the assassins were gone. The cliff towered above him, a wall of black stone against the gray sky. He'd survived.

Barely.

He dragged himself onto a rocky bank, his body screaming in protest. Every muscle ached. His ribs felt cracked. And his storage ring—still there, thank the heavens—held only a handful of pills and a broken furnace.

*This is my life now*, he thought bitterly. *Running. Hiding. Dying slowly.*

But as he lay there, staring up at the clouds, something caught his eye. A flicker of movement on the cliff's edge. A figure in white, watching him.

Lin Xue.

She stood there, rain plastering her hair to her face, her expression unreadable. She'd followed him. She'd seen everything.

*Why?*

Shen He's hand moved to his storage ring, ready to defend himself, but she made no move to attack. Instead, she turned and disappeared into the treeline, leaving him alone with more questions than answers.

---

Three days later, Shen He stumbled into a town called Thornwood.

It was a miserable place—a collection of mud huts and wooden shacks clustered around a central square where a gallows stood as a constant reminder of the local lord's justice. The people looked at him with suspicion, their hands never far from their knives.

He found work at a herbalist's shop, grinding roots and sorting leaves for a few copper coins a day. The pay was terrible, but it gave him access to ingredients. And ingredients meant pills.

The first night, he made a simple Qi Recovery Pill. It was crude, barely passable, but when he swallowed it, the warmth spread through his body like a familiar friend.

He could still absorb power through pills. His body still worked.

The thought gave him hope.

Over the following weeks, he refined his craft. He made pills for strength, for speed, for healing. Each one pushed his body further, made him faster, stronger, harder to kill. He wasn't a cultivator—not in the traditional sense—but he was becoming something else.

Something dangerous.

The assassins found him again on the thirty-seventh day.

He was in the back room of the herbalist's shop, grinding herbs for a batch of Bone-Strengthening Pills, when the door splintered inward. Three men in dark cloaks, just like before. Same weapons. Same flat, emotionless voices.

"Shen He. You're difficult to kill."

"I try," he said, and threw a handful of pills into his mouth.

The effect was instantaneous. His vision sharpened. His muscles tightened. His heart hammered like a war drum. He moved before they could react, his fist connecting with the lead assassin's jaw with enough force to send him crashing through the wall.

The other two drew their blades, but Shen He was already moving, his body a blur of motion. He wasn't a fighter, but the pills made him one. They made him *more*.

He caught the first blade with his bare hand, the edge biting into his palm, but he didn't feel the pain. He twisted, wrenching the weapon free, and drove it into the assassin's chest.

The second man ran.

Shen He let him.

He stood in the ruined shop, blood dripping from his hand, the bodies of two assassins at his feet. The herbalist had fled. The townspeople were watching from the street, their faces pale with fear.

*Good*, he thought. *Let them be afraid.*

But as the adrenaline faded, the pain came. His hand throbbed. His ribs ached. And the pills were wearing off, leaving him hollow and weak.

He needed more ingredients. Better ingredients. The kind you couldn't find in a backwater herbalist's shop.

He needed to find the Alchemy Guild.

---

The Guild's presence in Thornwood was subtle—a single shop on the eastern edge of town, unmarked and unassuming. Shen He stood outside it now, his bandaged hand tucked into his sleeve, his heart pounding.

He pushed open the door.

Inside, the shop was cluttered with jars and vials, their contents glowing with faint qi. An old woman sat behind the counter, her eyes sharp and knowing.

"You're the one they're looking for," she said.

"I'm the one who's looking," Shen He replied. "I need ingredients. Rare ones. And I need information."

The woman smiled, revealing yellowed teeth. "The Guild doesn't give handouts, boy. What do you have to trade?"

Shen He reached into his storage ring and pulled out a single pill. It was small, unassuming, but it glowed with an inner light that made the old woman's eyes widen.

"The Meridian Restoration Pill," he said. "Or as close as I could get. It doesn't kill the user. It *awakens* them."

The woman leaned forward, her hunger obvious. "Prove it."

Shen He set the pill on the counter. "Find me a test subject. Someone with broken meridians. Someone who has nothing left to lose."

"A cripple," she said.

"Yes," Shen He agreed. "Like me."

The woman's smile widened. "I know just the person. But boy—if this pill kills them, the Guild will hunt you to the ends of the earth."

Shen He met her gaze without flinching.

"They're already hunting me. What's one more reason to run?"

---

The test subject was a boy, no older than fourteen, with hollow eyes and a body that had been broken by a failed cultivation attempt. His name was Wei, and he lay in a cot at the back of the shop, waiting to die.

"This is cruelty," the old woman said, but she didn't stop Shen He from placing the pill on the boy's tongue.

"It's mercy," Shen He replied. "If it works."

The pill dissolved. For a moment, nothing happened. Then Wei's body arched off the cot, his scream tearing through the silence. His meridians—what was left of them—glowed beneath his skin like rivers of fire.

And then, slowly, impossibly, they began to *reknit*.

Shen He watched as the boy's cultivation base reformed, as qi flooded through channels that had been dead for years. It was beautiful. It was terrifying.

It was proof that the ancient alchemists had been wrong.

The pill worked.

Wei opened his eyes, and they burned with power. "What—what did you do to me?"

"I gave you a second chance," Shen He said. "Don't waste it."

But as he turned to leave, the old woman grabbed his arm. "The Guild will hear of this. They'll come for you with everything they have."

"Let them."

"You don't understand. The Guild isn't just an organization. It's a power that controls the flow of alchemy across the entire continent. If they want you dead, there's nowhere you can hide."

Shen He looked at his bandaged hand, at the blood seeping through the cloth.

"Then I'll stop hiding," he said. "I'll make them come to me."

The old woman's face twisted with something like pity. "You're a fool, boy."

"No," Shen He said, stepping out into the night. "I'm a pharmacist. And I'm about to change the world."

---

The message reached the Alchemy Guild's headquarters three days later.

It was a single line, written in Shen He's blood on a fragment of parchment:

*"If you want the formula, come find me. I'll be waiting at the end of the world."*

Below it, he'd drawn a map—a winding path through mountains and forests, leading to a place that shouldn't exist. A place where the laws of heaven broke down and miracles became possible.

The Guild Elder read the message three times, then burned it.

"Find him," he said to the shadows that filled his chamber. "Bring me his head.

title: The Debt Collector's Arrival
content:
The path down Azure Cloud Mountain was longer than Shen He remembered.

Perhaps because he'd never walked it before. For ten years, he'd climbed up as an orphan taken in by charity, and now he stumbled down as a disgraced exile, the weight of failure pressing against his shoulders like a physical force.

The morning mist curled around his ankles as he descended, and with each step, the ambient qi grew thinner, weaker. The mortal world didn't have qi. That was the first lesson every cultivator learned. Qi existed in the heavens, in the sacred mountains, in the bloodlines of the gifted. Mortals lived and died without ever touching it.

Shen He touched the crumpled page in his pocket.

Half the Meridian Restoration Formula. Charred at the edges, missing crucial steps. The other half had turned to ash in the explosion, along with three months of painstaking transcription.

"Useless," he muttered. "I'm useless."

The words tasted bitter, but they felt true. He'd spent a decade sweeping floors and organizing scrolls, sneaking glances at alchemy texts whenever the elders weren't watching. He'd memorized two hundred pill recipes, understood the theory behind a thousand more, and could identify any herb in the Eastern Continent by scent alone.

And none of it mattered.

Without cultivation, he couldn't control the reaction properly. Without qi pathways, the pill's energy had nowhere to go but outward, exploding in his face like a child's failed experiment.

The forest thickened as he descended, ancient pines giving way to oaks and birches. The air grew warmer, richer with the smell of earth and decay. Shen He kept walking, his feet carrying him forward even when his mind screamed to stop, to turn back, to beg for another chance.

They wouldn't give it.

Lin Xue had made sure of that.

He reached the bottom of the mountain three hours later. A small trading post stood at the crossroads, a collection of wooden buildings that served travelers heading to and from the sect. Merchants hawked talismans and low-grade pills. Farmers sold vegetables and rice. Children played in the mud, their laughter sharp and foreign.

Shen He had no money for an inn.

He found a spot behind a grain warehouse, sat down with his back against the weathered wood, and closed his eyes.

*What now?*

The question echoed in his empty stomach.

---

He woke to a boot pressing against his ribs.

"Get up, cripple."

Shen He's eyes snapped open. Three men stood over him, their robes cheap and threadbare, their faces hard with the kind of desperation that came from having nothing left to lose. Bandits. Or maybe just travelers who'd decided robbing an exile was easier than honest work.

"I don't have anything worth taking," Shen He said, his voice rough from sleep.

The man with the boot laughed. "You're from the sect. Even their garbage is worth something." He nudged harder, and Shen He felt something crack. "Hand over your bag. Your clothes. Everything."

Shen He's hand moved to his pocket, where the pill formula rested.

"No," he said.

"Didn't ask, cripple."

The boot swung back, aiming for his head.

Shen He moved.

It wasn't fast. It wasn't graceful. It was pure, animal desperation. He rolled sideways, grabbed a handful of mud, and threw it into the man's face. The bandit cursed, clawing at his eyes, and Shen He scrambled to his feet, his side screaming in protest.

He ran.

Footsteps pounded behind him, but he didn't look back. He crashed through the underbrush, branches tearing at his clothes, his breath coming in ragged gasps. His body was weak. His lungs burned. His legs felt like they were made of wet paper.

But he didn't stop.

He ran until the footsteps faded, until the forest swallowed the sounds of pursuit, until he collapsed against a tree, his heart threatening to burst through his chest.

"I need to get stronger," he whispered.

The words hung in the air, hollow and impossible.

---

He walked for three days.

The forest gave way to farmland, which gave way to a small village whose name he never learned. He traded his last spirit stone for a bowl of porridge and a promise of work. The village elder, a stooped woman with kind eyes and calloused hands, put him to work mending fences and hauling water.

It was honest work. Simple work.

It was nothing like alchemy.

At night, Shen He sat by the village well and stared at the charred formula, trying to reconstruct the missing pieces. The Meridian Restoration Pill required seven core ingredients, each one rarer and more volatile than the last. He'd managed to find substitutes for three of them in the sect's storage, but the remaining four were beyond his reach.

Mountain Heart Lotus. Abyssal Root. Phoenix Tear Resin. Starlight Dew.

Just the names made him want to laugh. These weren't ingredients you found in a garden. They were treasures hoarded by sects and ancient beasts, guarded by formations and deadly traps.

And he was a cripple with no cultivation, no backing, and seven copper coins to his name.

"Hopeless," he said, folding the paper.

"That depends on your definition."

Shen He spun around, his heart jumping into his throat.

A woman stood at the edge of the well, her silhouette sharp against the moonlight. She wore robes of deep crimson, the color of dried blood, and her face was half-hidden by a wide-brimmed hat. But her eyes—her eyes glowed with a faint, amber light that marked her as a cultivator.

A strong one.

"I'm nobody," Shen He said quickly. "Just a passing traveler."

"You're Shen He," the woman said. "Exiled from the Azure Cloud Sect three days ago for attempted theft of a forbidden formula." She smiled, and there was nothing kind about it. "I've been looking for you."

Shen He's blood ran cold.

"Who are you?"

"My name is Wei Yan. I represent the Black Lotus Society." She stepped closer, and the air around her grew heavy, thick with suppressed power. "We've been watching you for some time, Shen He. Your little... experiments in the library. Your obsession with the Meridian Restoration Pill. Your unique constitution."

"What about my constitution?"

"You absorb qi through pills," Wei Yan said simply. "We know."

Shen He's legs wanted to give out, but he forced them to stay. "How?"

"Because we have records. Because we've studied anomalies like you for centuries." She reached into her sleeve and produced a scroll, bound with black ribbon. "The Black Lotus Society collects those whom the orthodox sects reject. Cripples. Degenerates. Those born with talents that don't fit the established system." Her eyes locked onto his. "We offer a place. Resources. Protection."

"Why would you do that?"

"Because we want what you want." She tossed the scroll at his feet. "The Meridian Restoration Pill. You think you're the first to seek it? You think the Azure Cloud Sect is the only one who's failed?" She laughed, a cold, brittle sound. "Every major sect has tried. Every major sect has failed. But they approached it as cultivators, trying to force their qi into the formula."

She leaned in, her voice dropping to a whisper.

"You're different. You don't force qi. You consume it. You absorb it through the pill itself. That's not a flaw, Shen He. That's the solution."

He stared at the scroll.

"What's the catch?"

Wei Yan's smile widened. "Nothing comes free. The Black Lotus Society will give you everything you need to complete the formula. Ingredients. Equipment. Access to our libraries. In exchange, when the pill is perfected, you share the knowledge with us."

"And if I refuse?"

"You'll be dead by morning." She said it like she was commenting on the weather. "I'm not here to threaten you, Shen He. I'm here to offer you a choice. Join us, and live. Walk away, and die—either from starvation, or from the sect members I know are already hunting you for what you stole."

She wasn't wrong.

Lin Xue wouldn't let him leave quietly. She'd want the formula destroyed, and him with it.

"One more thing," Wei Yan said. "The Black Lotus Society has information about your parents."

Shen He's head snapped up.

"They abandoned you at the Azure Cloud Sect's gates," she continued. "No note. No explanation. Just a baby wrapped in rags. But we know where they went. We know why they left you." She paused, letting the words sink in. "Join us, and I'll tell you everything."

His parents.

He'd spent years wondering. Years inventing excuses for why they'd left him. Years pretending he didn't care.

He picked up the scroll.

"Fine," he said. "I'll join."

Wei Yan nodded, as if she'd expected nothing less. "Good. Follow me."

She turned and walked into the forest, her crimson robes disappearing into the shadows. Shen He hesitated, his hand clutching the scroll, his heart pounding.

This was a trap.

It had to be a trap.

But what choice did he have?

He followed.

---

The Black Lotus Society's camp was hidden in a valley three days' journey from the village. It wasn't a camp, really—it was a fortress, carved into the side of a cliff, its walls gleaming with formation scripts that pulsed with dark energy.

Shen He stood at the entrance, staring up at the massive iron gates.

"Impressive, isn't it?" Wei Yan said from beside him. "We've been building this for two hundred years. The orthodox sects don't even know we exist."

"They will eventually."

"Eventually." She smiled. "But by then, it'll be too late."

The gates groaned open, revealing a courtyard filled with cultivators in crimson robes. They moved with purpose, their faces hard, their eyes sharp. Some carried pill cauldrons. Others practiced martial forms. All of them wore the same symbol on their chests—a black lotus bloom, its petals edged in gold.
