Wei Yan led him through the courtyard, past the training grounds, into a central hall lit by hanging lanterns that burned with blue fire.

"Shen He," she said, gesturing to a figure seated on a raised platform. "Allow me to introduce our leader."

The figure rose, and Shen He felt his breath catch.

It was a man, tall and gaunt, with silver hair that fell past his shoulders. His eyes were the color of molten gold, and his skin was covered in intricate scar patterns—formations carved directly into his flesh.

"Shen He," the man said, his voice like grinding stone. "I am Mo Tian, founder of the Black Lotus Society." He stepped down from the platform, his robes trailing behind him. "I've been waiting for you."

"Why?" Shen He asked, his voice steadier than he felt.

"Because you're going to help me achieve immortality."

Mo Tian smiled, and Shen He saw the truth in his eyes.

The Black Lotus Society didn't want to help him.

They wanted to use him.

And the scroll Wei Yan had given him? The information about his parents?

It was all bait.

But Shen He was already hooked.

"What do I need to do?" he asked.

Mo Tian's smile widened into something hungry and terrible.

"First," he said, "you need to survive the initiation."

title: The Mountain Remembers
content:
The path down Azure Cloud Mountain should have taken three hours. Shen He made it in two, driven by the burning weight of humiliation in his chest. Each step carried him further from the only home he'd known, and closer to... what? A mortal world that would see a cripple as less than nothing?

He stopped at the first crossroads, where the stone path gave way to packed earth. To the east lay the capital. To the west, the borderlands. And straight ahead—

A figure stood in the middle of the road.

Lin Xue.

She wore simple traveling robes, her hair tied back in a practical knot. No sect insignia. No jade ornaments. Just a sword at her hip and an unreadable expression on her face.

"Did they send you to make sure I actually leave?" Shen He asked, his voice bitter. "Or did you volunteer to deliver the final kick?"

"I came to give you this." She tossed something small and metallic through the air. He caught it instinctively—a bronze token, warm to the touch, engraved with the symbol of a mortar and pestle.

"What is this?"

"A marker for the Alchemy Guild in Crescent City. It grants access to their basic archives and allows you to purchase materials at member prices." She paused. "It's the least I could do."

Shen He stared at the token, then back at her. "You're the one who turned me in."

"I'm the one who tried to save your life." Lin Xue's voice hardened. "Do you have any idea what that pill would have done to you? The Meridian Restoration formula isn't just dangerous—it's cursed. Every alchemist who attempted it in the last three centuries died. Not from the refinement. From what the pill did to their bodies when they tested it."

"I didn't test it."

"You were going to. I saw it in your eyes the first time I caught you copying that scroll. You weren't studying it out of academic curiosity. You were planning to consume it."

Shen He's grip tightened on the token until the edges bit into his palm. "And if I was? My life is worthless anyway. A cripple in a world of cultivators. At least if I died trying, I would have done something meaningful."

"Meaningful." Lin Xue laughed, but there was no humor in it. "You think dying for a failed experiment is meaningful? You think that's what makes a cultivator?"

"I'm not a cultivator. I'm a failed janitor with delusions of grandeur."

"Then stop deluding yourself." She stepped closer, and for a moment, the mask slipped. Beneath the cold prodigy was something raw, almost desperate. "The Alchemy Guild archives have thousands of formulas. Hundreds of documented cases of meridian-crippled individuals finding alternative paths to power. There's a whole section on body cultivation that doesn't require qi channels at all. But you wouldn't know that, because you were too busy chasing a suicide pill to bother with the basics."

Shen He's breath caught. "You studied this?"

"Someone had to, before you killed yourself." She turned and started walking back up the mountain. "The token is good for one month. After that, you'll need to earn your keep. The guild isn't a charity."

"Wait." The word left his mouth before he could stop it. "Why are you helping me?"

Lin Xue paused but didn't turn around. "Because I was wrong about you, Shen He. When I saw you in the restricted section, I assumed you were a thief. But I watched you for three weeks after that. Three weeks of you sneaking into that library, studying scrolls you could barely read, making notes in that terrible handwriting of yours. You weren't stealing for profit. You were stealing because you were desperate." She looked back over her shoulder. "Desperation isn't a crime. Stupidity is. Don't be stupid."

She walked away.

Shen He stood at the crossroads for a long time, the bronze token warm in his hand. Then he turned east, toward Crescent City.

---

The journey took four days.

Shen He traveled light, sleeping in roadside inns and eating cheap grain porridge. The spirit stones he'd saved were barely enough to cover expenses, and by the time Crescent City's walls appeared on the horizon, he had exactly three copper coins left to his name.

The city was enormous—easily ten times the size of the town outside Azure Cloud Mountain. Walls of white stone rose forty feet high, gleaming with formation scripts that pulsed with ambient qi. Merchants hawked wares at the gates, their voices blending into a cacophony of competing promises.

"Shen He."

The voice came from his left, soft and familiar. He turned.

A woman stood in the shadow of a fruit stall, her face partially hidden by a wide-brimmed hat. She wore plain robes, but the fabric was high-quality silk, and the sword at her hip was no ordinary blade. The hilt was carved from spirit jade, and the scabbard bore the emblem of a crescent moon.

"Do I know you?" Shen He asked, his hand instinctively moving to his bag.

"You know of me." She pushed back her hat, revealing a face that was strikingly beautiful—high cheekbones, sharp eyes, and a small, knowing smile. "I'm Mo Qingyun, leader of the Crescent Blade mercenaries. And I have a proposition for you."

"I'm not interested in mercenary work."

"You should be." She stepped closer, and Shen He noticed that the people around them seemed to unconsciously give her space. "Word travels fast in the underworld. A crippled alchemist, exiled from Azure Cloud Sect, carrying a bronze Guild token. You're already a target."

"I don't have anything worth stealing."

"You have knowledge. That's worth more than gold." She held up a hand before he could protest. "I'm not here to rob you, Shen He. I'm here to offer you a deal. The Crescent Blade needs an alchemist. Our last one was killed during a raid three weeks ago, and we've been scrambling to find a replacement. You have Guild access. You have basic training. You're perfect."

"And what do I get in return?"

"Protection. Resources. Access to the Guild's restricted section—the one that actually contains useful information." She smiled. "And a share of the profits from our operations. I'm not asking you to fight. I'm asking you to brew."

Shen He's mind raced. It was too convenient. Too perfect. A mercenary group appearing the moment he arrived in the city, offering exactly what he needed?

"How do I know you're telling the truth?"

Mo Qingyun reached into her robes and pulled out a folded piece of paper. She handed it to him. "Read this."

He unfolded it. It was a Guild commission, stamped with the official seal of Crescent City's Alchemy Guild. It requested "qualified alchemists to assist in the verification and refinement of a recently discovered ancient formula." The formula in question was listed at the bottom, in elegant calligraphy.

*Meridian Restoration Pill. Revised Variant. Origin: Unknown.*

Shen He's hands started shaking.

"How did you get this?"

"I have connections." Mo Qingyun's smile widened. "The Guild Council has been sitting on this formula for months. They're afraid to test it, afraid it might actually work. They're looking for desperate volunteers." She leaned in. "And you, Shen He, are desperate."

"What do you want from me?"

"Brew the pill. Test it. If it works, you become a legend. If it doesn't..." She shrugged. "Well, at least you'll die trying to do something meaningful."

The words echoed Lin Xue's, but twisted. Where Lin Xue had warned him away from death, Mo Qingyun was inviting him to embrace it.

Shen He looked at the commission again. The formula was different from the one he'd stolen. More refined. More detailed. It included annotations from multiple alchemists, corrections in the margins, and a final note that made his blood run cold:

*"This variant addresses the original formula's fatal flaw. The meridian-destroying component has been neutralized through the addition of Phoenix Tear essence. However, the purification process is extremely volatile. Only attempt refinement under controlled conditions with proper protective formations."*

It was possible. For the first time in his life, it was actually possible.

"I'll do it," he said.

Mo Qingyun's smile became a grin. "I knew you'd see reason. Follow me."

She led him through the city gates, past the bustling markets and crowded streets, to a compound on the eastern edge of the city. It was a modest estate, surrounded by high walls and guarded by cultivators in matching uniforms.

"Welcome to the Crescent Blade headquarters," Mo Qingyun said. "Your laboratory is in the basement. I've already had it stocked with basic equipment."

"You prepared for me."

"I prepared for the alchemist I needed. You just happen to be the one who showed up."

They descended a stone staircase into a well-lit basement. The laboratory was clean and organized, with cauldrons, mortars, and shelves of ingredients arranged in neat rows. In the center of the room sat a large bronze cauldron, its surface etched with formation scripts.

"The Guild delivered the ingredients yesterday," Mo Qingyun said. "Everything you need is here. You can start immediately."

Shen He walked around the laboratory, examining the equipment. It was high quality, far better than anything he'd had access to at the sect. The cauldron alone was worth more spirit stones than he'd seen in his entire life.

"This is incredible," he breathed.

"I'm glad you approve." Mo Qingyun moved to the door. "I'll leave you to your work. If you need anything, just call for a guard."

She left, and the door clicked shut behind her.

Shen He stood alone in the laboratory, surrounded by the tools of his trade. The commission was still in his hand, the words burning into his memory.

*Meridian Restoration Pill. Revised Variant.*

He walked to the cauldron and placed his palm on its surface. The formation scripts hummed with latent energy, ready to be activated.

This was it. This was his chance.

He was so focused on the cauldron that he almost missed the faint scratching sound from the corner of the room. He turned, his heart pounding.

A small piece of paper lay on the floor, as if it had been slipped under the door. He picked it up and read the message written in hurried, shaky handwriting:

*"Don't trust Mo Qingyun. The formula is a trap. She's working for the Sword Sect. They know what you are. They know what you can become. Get out while you still can."*

Shen He's blood turned to ice.

He looked at the door. At the cauldron. At the commission in his hand.

And then he heard footsteps outside. Heavy. Purposeful. Coming toward the laboratory.

The door handle began to turn.

title: The Serpent's Deal
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

Three days since his exile, and the world had become an endless expanse of gnarled roots and dappled shadows. His boots—once the sturdy leather issued to all Azure Cloud disciples—were now caked with mud and beginning to fray at the seams. The small bag on his shoulder felt lighter with every step, the spirit stones inside depleted to a handful of chips and dust.

He hadn't eaten in two days.

His stomach growled, a hollow, insistent complaint that he couldn't ignore. Shen He found a stream, knelt, and drank until his belly sloshed. The water was cold, clean, but offered no nourishment. He sat back on his heels, staring at his reflection in the rippling surface.

Gaunt. Hollow-eyed. A stranger's face.

*This is what exile looks like.*

He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page—the only remnant of his life's work. The Meridian Restoration Pill formula. The forbidden knowledge that had cost him everything. The ink had smudged in places, water damage from the explosion making some characters illegible. But enough remained.

*Three parts Starfall Blossom. One part Dragon's Breath Root. A catalyst of Nine-Turn Golden Lotus...*

The last ingredient was missing. Torn away by the blast.

Shen He crumpled the page in his fist. What did it matter? The sect master had said the pill was a death sentence. Every alchemist who'd attempted it had died. And here he was, a cripple with no cultivation base, no resources, no laboratory, no *anything*—thinking he could succeed where ancient masters had failed.

*Delusional. That's what you are.*

He shoved the page back into his bag and forced himself to stand. His legs wobbled. The world spun. When the dizziness passed, he looked up at the canopy, trying to gauge the direction of the setting sun.

West. He'd been heading west. Toward the nearest mortal town, if his memory of the sect's geography lessons served him.

"Move," he told himself. "Or die here."

The forest thinned as evening approached. The trees gave way to rolling hills, and beyond them, smoke rose from chimneys. A village. Small, but alive. Shen He's feet carried him forward before his mind caught up with the decision.

By the time he reached the village gates, the sun had set and the moon hung pale and thin in the sky. The gates were closed, wooden beams reinforced with iron bands. A guard tower stood to one side, a single lantern burning within.

"Halt!" A voice from above. "State your business."

Shen He looked up, squinting against the lantern's glare. "I'm... a traveler. Looking for shelter."

"A traveler? Dressed like that?" The guard laughed. "You look like a beggar who crawled out of a ditch."

"Something like that," Shen He said, too tired to argue.

There was a pause. Then: "Sect robes. You're from Azure Cloud."

Shen He's heart clenched. "Was. I was from Azure Cloud."

"Exiled?"

The word hung in the air like a curse. Shen He nodded, though the guard probably couldn't see it.

"Wait there."

Footsteps retreated. Shen He leaned against the gate, his legs threatening to give out. Minutes passed. Then the gate groaned open, just a crack, just enough for a single figure to slip through.

The guard was a middle-aged man with a scar running from his temple to his jaw. He held a lantern in one hand and a short sword in the other.

"Exiled cultivators don't usually wander into mortal villages," he said, studying Shen He with obvious suspicion. "Most of them head for the city. Better opportunities there."

"I'm not a cultivator," Shen He said. "I was the sect's librarian. I never had any cultivation to begin with."

The guard's eyes widened. "Meridian-crippled? You?"

A bitter laugh escaped Shen He's lips. "Is it that obvious?"

"Can't sense any qi from you. Most cultivators leak at least a little, even when they're trying to hide it." The guard sheathed his sword. "Alright. You can stay the night, but you leave at dawn. We don't need trouble following you here."

"Thank you. I won't be any trouble."

The guard led him through the gate and into the village. It was small—maybe fifty households—with a single main street lined with shuttered shops. A few people were still out, carrying baskets or leading oxen. They stared at Shen He with open curiosity but said nothing.

"Old Man Hu has a spare room," the guard said, pointing to a rundown building at the end of the street. "He'll charge you three copper for a bed and a bowl of stew. If you can't pay, you can sleep in the stable."

"I have spirit stones," Shen He said.

The guard snorted. "Spirit stones are worthless here, boy. We deal in coin. Mortal coin."

Shen He's stomach dropped. Of course. Of *course* the mortal world used mortal currency. He'd been so focused on cultivation resources that he'd never considered the basics of survival.

"Then I suppose I'll be sleeping in the stable."

The guard sighed. "Figured as much. Come on, I'll talk to Old Man Hu. Maybe he'll let you work off the debt."

The stable was warmer than Shen He expected, filled with the smell of hay and horse. Old Man Hu had agreed to let him stay for one night in exchange for mucking out the stalls in the morning. It was honest work, and Shen He found he didn't hate it.

He sat in a corner, his back against a wooden pillar, and pulled out the crumpled page again. The missing ingredient gnawed at him. Without it, the formula was useless. A puzzle with a crucial piece torn away.

*What was it?* He'd memorized the original scroll before the explosion, but his memory was hazy, clouded by panic and pain. *Something rare. Something dangerous. Something...*

A shadow fell over him.

Shen He looked up, his heart seizing. A figure stood in the stable doorway, silhouetted against the moonlight. Tall. Wrapped in a dark cloak. And radiating a qi pressure so dense that Shen He's non-existent cultivation base *felt* it like a physical weight.

"You're hard to find," the figure said. A woman's voice. Low. Melodic. Dangerous.

"Who are you?" Shen He's voice cracked. He scrambled to his feet, the page clutched to his chest.

"Someone who's been looking for you." The figure stepped forward, and the moonlight caught her face. She was beautiful—impossibly so. High cheekbones, eyes like liquid silver, hair as black as a starless night. A cultivator. A powerful one.

"I don't know you," Shen He said.

"No, you don't." She smiled, and it didn't reach her eyes. "But I know you, Shen He. The meridian-crippled boy who stole the Meridian Restoration Pill formula and was exiled from the Azure Cloud Sect. I've been tracking you since the moment you left those gates."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "What do you want?"

"The formula." She extended a hand. "Give it to me, and I'll let you live."

"I don't have it."

"Liar." The word cracked like a whip. The qi pressure intensified, and Shen He felt his legs buckle. He fell to his knees, gasping. "I can sense its residue on you. The ink. The paper. The faintest trace of the ingredients you used before your little explosion."

"You're with the sect," Shen He said, the realization hitting him like a hammer. "They sent you to finish the job."

"They didn't send me." The woman crouched, bringing her face level with his. Her silver eyes bore into him, ancient and cold. "I came of my own accord. The Azure Cloud Sect is full of fools who don't understand what they have. They locked away a treasure because they were too afraid to use it."

"You're not going to use it either," Shen He said through gritted teeth. "The pill kills everyone who attempts it."

"Everyone who attempts it *wrong*." She smiled again, and this time it was sharp, predatory. "But I have resources you can't imagine. Laboratories. Ingredients. Knowledge that would make your sect's library look like a child's picture book. I can finish what you started."

"And why would I help you?"

"Because if you don't, I'll kill you. Slowly. Painfully. And then I'll take the formula anyway."

Shen He stared at her. The anger he'd felt in the sect master's hall stirred again, cold and hard. "You think I'm afraid of death?"

"You should be."

"I've been dead since the moment I was born," he said. "A cripple in a world that worships power. A nobody. A failure. Death would be a mercy compared to the life I've lived."

The woman's smile faltered. For a moment, something flickered in her eyes—surprise, perhaps, or recognition. Then it was gone.

"You have spirit," she said. "I like that. It's rare among mortals." She rose to her full height. "Very well. I'll make you another offer. Help me recreate the formula, and I'll give you something in return."

"What could you possibly give me?"

"Functioning meridians."

The words hit Shen He like a thunderbolt. He stared at her, his mind reeling. "That's impossible. The sect master said—"

"The sect master is a coward who repeats old lies." She reached into her cloak and pulled out a small jade bottle. "This is a Pill of Nascent Pathways. It can repair damaged meridians. Not restore them completely, but enough to allow basic cultivation. It's imperfect. Experimental. But it works."

She held it out. Shen He's hand trembled as he reached for it.

"And in exchange?"

"Help me find the missing ingredient. The one that was torn from your formula."

He froze. "How do you know about that?"

"Because I've studied the Meridian Restoration Pill for longer than you've been alive." She placed the bottle in his palm. Her fingers brushed his, and he felt a jolt of cold energy. "The missing ingredient is the key. Without it, the pill is poison. With it..." She leaned in, her voice dropping to a whisper. "With it, the pill becomes something else entirely. Something that could change the world."

Shen He looked at the bottle in his hand. Then at the crumpled page. Then at the woman with her silver eyes and impossible promises.

"Who are you?" he asked again.

She smiled, and for the first time, it seemed almost genuine.

"My name is Wei Yan. And I am the only person in this world who can give you what you want."

She turned and walked out of the stable, her cloak billowing behind her.

"I'll be waiting at the crossroads at dawn," she called over her shoulder. "Don't keep me waiting, Shen He. I'm not a patient woman."

The stable fell silent. Shen He stood there, the jade bottle heavy in his hand, his heart pounding against his ribs.

He opened the bottle. A single pill rolled into his palm—golden, faintly glowing, pulsing with a warmth that he could *feel* even without qi.

*This could be a trap.*

*This could be a lie.*

*This could be everything I've ever wanted.*

He looked at the pill. Looked at the door where the woman had vanished.

And for the first

title: The Demon's Bargain
content:
The night air carried the scent of death.

Shen He crouched behind a collapsed shrine, his fingers pressed against the blood-soaked earth. Three days since the Scorpion Emperor's assassins had found him. Three days of running, hiding, and barely surviving. His body screamed for rest, but the pills he'd crushed into his last waterskin kept him moving—kept his meridians from tearing themselves apart.

The Meridian Restoration Pill had worked. But not as he'd expected.

His meridians were no longer crippled. They were something else now—fractured, yes, but also... hungry. They drank qi from the air faster than his body could process it, leaving him in a constant state of half-poisoning. Every step sent spikes of pain through his limbs. Every breath burned.

But he was alive.

That was more than he could say for the seven assassins he'd left in the ravine three miles back. Their poison-coated blades had been useless against a body that had consumed enough toxins to kill a hundred men. They'd learned that lesson too late.

*Footsteps.*

Shen He's hand shot to his waist pouch, fingers closing around a fistful of Crimson Night powder. The footsteps were light—deliberate. A cultivator, and a skilled one.

"I know you're there, alchemist."

The voice was female, melodic, and completely unfamiliar. Shen He didn't move.

"I'm not here to kill you," she continued. "Though I understand why you'd assume otherwise. You've made quite the impression on the underworld. A cripple who escaped the Azure Cloud Sect, then killed seven of the Scorpion Emperor's best assassins? People are starting to talk."

Shen He rose slowly, powder ready to throw. A woman stood twenty feet away, her robes the color of dried blood. Her face was half-hidden by a hood, but he could see her smile—sharp, predatory.

"Who are you?" he asked.

"Someone with a proposition." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a jade slip. "I have information about the Meridian Restoration Pill. The real formula. Not the flawed copy locked away in the Azure Cloud's library."

Shen He's heart hammered. "How do you know about that?"

"Everyone knows about that." The woman tossed the slip at his feet. "The explosion was felt for miles. The Azure Cloud Elders have been scrambling to cover it up, but the alchemy community has ears everywhere. They know someone attempted the forbidden refinement. They know someone *survived*."

Shen He didn't pick up the slip. "What do you want?"

"A partnership." The woman's smile widened. "I've spent ten years studying the ancient texts. I know why every attempt at the Meridian Restoration Pill has failed. But I lack one crucial ingredient—a living subject who has already undergone the refinement. You're the first person to survive the process in three hundred years. Your blood alone is worth a fortune."

"So you want to bleed me dry."

"Temporarily." She laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "I need samples. Seven vials, taken over the course of a month. In exchange, I give you the corrected formula and enough resources to refine it without blowing yourself up."

Shen He stared at her. Every instinct screamed that this was a trap. But the memory of Elder Wu's sneer, of Lin Xue's cold eyes, of the sect master's dismissive words—they burned hotter than any poison.

"If you're lying—"

"I'm not." The woman pulled back her hood, revealing a face that was beautiful in the way a knife was beautiful. "I am Wei Yan, former fourth elder of the Black Lotus Alchemy Pavilion. I was exiled for attempting the very same refinement you just survived. The difference is that I failed. You succeeded. That makes you the most valuable alchemist alive."

Shen He's mind raced. The Black Lotus Pavilion was legendary—one of the four great alchemy sects, destroyed by internal conflict fifty years ago. Their techniques were considered lost.

"Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't." Wei Yan's eyes glittered. "But you have no other options. The Scorpion Emperor has put a bounty on your head that would buy a small kingdom. The Azure Cloud Sect has declared you a traitor. And the Alchemy Guild will dissect you the moment you show your face in any civilized city. I am your only ally."

She was right. He hated that she was right.

"What's the catch?"

"The catch?" Wei Yan tilted her head. "The corrected formula requires a catalyst. Something that doesn't exist in this world anymore."

"What?"

"Dragon's Blood Qi. The refined essence of a celestial dragon. Without it, the pill will still kill you—just slower. You'll have perhaps a year before your meridians collapse completely."

Shen He's blood ran cold. Celestial dragons hadn't been seen in a thousand years. They were legends, myths, stories told to children.

"Then it's impossible."

"No." Wei Yan's smile turned cruel. "There's one place where Dragon's Blood Qi still exists. The Dead Emperor's Tomb. It's buried beneath the Forbidden Desert, guarded by formations that have killed every cultivator who's attempted to breach them."

"You're insane."

"Perhaps." She shrugged. "But I've spent ten years mapping the tomb's outer defenses. I know the way in. What I don't know is the way through the inner chamber—that requires a poison-resistant body like yours. Together, we might actually survive."

Shen He looked down at his hands. They were shaking—from exhaustion, from pain, from the constant hum of wild qi in his veins. He'd wanted to become strong. He'd wanted to prove them all wrong.

He hadn't wanted to die in a forgotten tomb beneath a desert of bones.

"I need time to think."

"You have until sunrise." Wei Yan turned and vanished into the shadows. "I'll be at the Broken Blade Inn in Thornwood. Come find me if you want to live."

She was gone.

Shen He stood alone in the darkness, the jade slip gleaming at his feet. He bent down and picked it up, feeling the cool surface against his fingers.

*One year.*

He'd thought surviving the pill was his victory. But it was just the beginning of a longer, more dangerous game. The sect wanted him dead. The underworld wanted his blood. And now a madwoman wanted to drag him into a tomb that had claimed a thousand lives.

He laughed.

It was a hollow, broken sound that echoed through the empty night. Three months ago, he'd been a librarian's assistant, dreaming of becoming an alchemist. Now he was the most hunted man in the cultivation world.

*Fine.*

If he was going to die anyway, he might as well die chasing a legend.

He pocketed the jade slip and started walking toward Thornwood.

---

The Broken Blade Inn smelled like cheap wine and desperation.

Shen He found Wei Yan in a corner booth, a half-empty bottle of rice wine in front of her. She looked up as he approached, her eyes gleaming with something like satisfaction.

"I knew you'd come."

"Don't flatter yourself." Shen He sat down heavily. "I came because I have no other choice. But I have conditions."

"Name them."

"First, you give me half the corrected formula now. I'm not going into a death trap without knowing what I'm risking my life for."

Wei Yan nodded. "Reasonable."

"Second, you teach me everything you know about alchemy. I'm tired of stumbling in the dark. If I'm going to be a target for the rest of my life, I need to be strong enough to fight back."

"You want me to take you as an apprentice?" She raised an eyebrow.

"I want you to give me what the Azure Cloud Sect refused to give me. Knowledge."

A long silence. Then Wei Yan laughed, the sound genuine for the first time.

"You're more interesting than I expected, Shen He. Fine. I accept your conditions. In return, I have one of my own."

"Name it."

"When we find the Dragon's Blood Qi, I get half. The tomb's treasures are mine to claim. And if we survive..." Her smile turned cold. "You owe me a favor. One I can call in at any time, for any purpose."

Shen He's instincts screamed at him to refuse. Favors to alchemists were dangerous things—binding contracts that could cost a man his soul.

But what choice did he have?

"Deal."

Wei Yan extended her hand. Shen He took it. Her grip was firm, her skin cool as death.

"Then we have an accord." She pulled out a scroll and slid it across the table. "The first half of the formula. Memorize it, then burn it. We leave at dawn."

Shen He unrolled the scroll. Ancient characters danced before his eyes—alchemical symbols he'd only seen in forbidden texts. His heart raced as he read the opening lines.

*The Meridian Restoration Pill, Corrected Form.*

*Step One: The foundation must be laid in living flesh, not dead stone...*

He looked up. Wei Yan was watching him with an unreadable expression.

"One more thing," she said. "The Scorpion Emperor's assassins aren't the only ones hunting you. I've heard rumors that the Azure Cloud Sect sent someone as well."

"Who?"

Wei Yan's eyes glittered. "Lin Xue. The sect's prodigy. She volunteered for the mission personally."

The name hit Shen He like a physical blow. Lin Xue. The woman who had exposed him. The woman whose cold eyes had watched him kneel before the elders.

"Why would she come after me herself?"

"Who knows?" Wei Yan shrugged. "Maybe she wants to finish what she started. Maybe she feels guilty. Maybe she wants to see if the cripple she destroyed has become something worth killing."

Shen He's hands clenched into fists. The anger he'd felt in the sect hall surged back, hotter than ever.

*Let her come.*

He'd spent his whole life being looked down on. Being dismissed. Being told he was worthless.

It was time to show them all what a "cripple" could do.

"Let her come," he said aloud. "I'll be waiting."

Wei Yan's smile was sharp as a blade.

"That's the spirit."

Outside, the first light of dawn touched the horizon. Somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled.

The hunt had begun.

title: The Demon's Bargain
content:
Three days. Three days since Shen He had been cast out of the Azure Cloud Sect, and he still couldn't stop looking over his shoulder.

The forest path wound through ancient pines, their needles creating a carpet so thick it swallowed his footsteps. His bag held nothing but old clothes and a single page of notes—the only piece of the Meridian Restoration Pill formula he'd managed to salvage. The rest had turned to ash in the explosion.

Or so he'd thought.

He stopped walking, his hand going to his chest. Beneath his robes, a thin leather pouch hung against his skin. He'd found it that morning, tucked into the lining of his bag. He didn't remember putting it there.

Slowly, carefully, he pulled it out and untied the string.

Inside was a single jade slip, no larger than his thumb. He pressed his spiritual sense into it—a technique that required no meridians, only intent—and nearly dropped it when the information flooded his mind.

It wasn't the full Meridian Restoration Pill formula.

It was better.

The slip contained a complete alchemical treatise on what the ancient author called the "Body-Forging Path"—a method of cultivation that bypassed meridians entirely by refining the physical body through pill consumption alone. No breathing techniques. No qi circulation. Just pure, brutal alchemical transformation.

And at the end of the treatise, a note:

*"For the one who finds this: I too was born without meridians. I too was cast out. Come find me in the city of Blackstone, at the Alchemist's Respite. I have much to teach you."*

Shen He's hands trembled. He read the note again. And again. And again.

Someone else. Someone else like him.

He looked up at the sky, at the sun that had barely moved since he'd left the sect. Blackstone was a five-day journey east. He had no money, no supplies, and no idea what he would find when he got there.

But he had a path.

He started walking.

---

Three weeks later, Shen He understood why they called it Blackstone.

The city rose from the plains like a fist of obsidian, its walls carved from volcanic rock that absorbed the sunlight and refused to give it back. Even at noon, the streets were dim, shadowed by the dark stone that paved every surface. The air smelled of ash and metal and something acrid that burned the back of his throat.

He'd spent his last spirit stone on a bowl of rice gruel that morning. His clothes were stained with road dust. His body ached from sleeping under trees and in ditches.

But he was here.

The Alchemist's Respite sat at the end of a crooked alley, its sign hanging crookedly from a single rusted chain. The door was warped, the windows grimy, and the whole building looked like it might collapse if someone sneezed too hard.

Shen He pushed open the door.

Inside, the air was thick with the smell of herbs and smoke. A single oil lamp flickered on a counter cluttered with mortar bowls, scattered herbs, and stacks of yellowed papers. Behind the counter, a figure hunched over a cauldron, stirring slowly.

"Welcome, welcome," the figure said without turning. "If you're looking for potions, I'm closed. If you're looking for pills, I'm out. If you're looking for trouble, you've found the wrong place."

"I'm looking for the author of this," Shen He said, holding up the jade slip.

The stirring stopped.

Slowly, the figure turned. It was a woman—or had been, once. Her face was a roadmap of scars, some old and white, others fresh and pink. One eye was clouded white, the other sharp and black as a bead of obsidian. Her hair was gray, cropped short, and her hands were stained with dozens of different colors.

"Where did you get that?" she asked, her voice suddenly hard.

"An Azure Cloud Sect library," Shen He said. "It was hidden in a book. I think... I think it was meant for me."

The woman studied him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a dry, rasping sound that turned into a cough.

"You're the one," she said. "The meridian-crippled boy who blew up half the outer compound. Word travels fast." She leaned on the counter. "I'm Old Meng. And yes, I wrote that treatise. Took me forty years to perfect it. Forty years of failures, explosions, and near-death experiences." She grinned, revealing a gap where teeth should have been. "You'll fit right in."

Shen He felt a weight lift from his shoulders. "Can you teach me?"

"Teach you?" Old Meng's grin widened. "Boy, I'm going to do more than teach you. I'm going to make you into something the cultivation world has never seen. But first..." She held up a finger. "Payment."

"I don't have any spirit stones."

"I don't want spirit stones." She reached under the counter and pulled out a small wooden box. Inside, nestled on a bed of red silk, was a pill the color of midnight. It seemed to absorb the light around it, creating a small pocket of darkness.

"This is a Soul-Tethering Pill," Old Meng said. "It's incomplete. I need one more ingredient to finish it—the heartblood of a living Nascent Soul cultivator."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "That's impossible. Nascent Soul cultivators are practically immortal. I can't—"

"You can," Old Meng interrupted. "There's one in Blackstone. A rogue cultivator named Feng Hao. He's been using the city as his personal hunting ground for years, preying on weak cultivators and mortals alike. No one's been able to stop him because he's too powerful."

She pushed the box toward him. "Kill him. Bring me his heartblood. And I'll teach you everything I know."

Shen He stared at the pill. At the darkness that seemed to pulse within it. At the impossible task she'd set before him.

"I can't kill a Nascent Soul cultivator," he said. "I'm barely Qi Condensation. I can't even cultivate."

"Ah, but you can," Old Meng said. "That's the beauty of it. You don't need to defeat him in combat. You just need to poison him." She tapped the pill. "This isn't just a Soul-Tethering Pill. It's a key. Feed it to him, and his soul will be bound to the pill. When he dies, his heartblood will flow into it naturally."

"How am I supposed to get close enough to feed it to him?"

Old Meng shrugged. "That's your problem. I'm just the teacher. You're the student." She turned back to her cauldron. "You have one month. After that, I'm leaving Blackstone. If you want what I have to offer, bring me the heartblood before then."

Shen He picked up the box. The pill felt cold against his fingers, heavy with the weight of what he was about to do.

"One question," he said. "Why me?"

Old Meng didn't turn around. "Because you're desperate enough to try. Because you have nothing left to lose. And because..." She paused. "Because I was you, once. A long time ago. And someone gave me a chance."

She waved her hand dismissively. "Now go. I have work to do."

---

Shen He spent the next three days gathering information.

Feng Hao was exactly what Old Meng had described—a monster in human form. He'd taken over the city's underground, demanding tribute from every merchant and cultivator who passed through. Those who refused were found dead, their bodies drained of blood, their cultivation bases shattered.

He operated from a compound in the city's center, surrounded by hired guards and a personal retinue of poison-tasters. No one got close to him without being searched. No one entered his presence without being stripped of weapons and cultivation tools.

But there was one way.

Shen He stood at the edge of the city's market district, watching a line of people form outside a large pavilion. A banner hung overhead: "Feng Hao's Open Audition—Seeking New Alchemists. Generous Compensation."

The rogue cultivator was expanding his operation. He needed more alchemists to produce pills for his growing army.

And Shen He was going to walk right into his den.

He joined the line, his heart pounding. The pill was hidden in a hollow compartment in his shoe. His clothes were plain, his face covered in dirt, his posture hunched to make him look smaller, weaker, less threatening.

The line moved slowly. By the time he reached the front, the sun had set and torches had been lit.

"Name?" the examiner asked, not looking up from his ledger.

"Shen He."

"Rank?"

"Qi Condensation, first level."

The examiner finally looked up, his eyes narrowing. "You're a cripple."

"Yes."

"And you think you can work for Lord Feng?"

"I'm an alchemist," Shen He said. "Cultivation rank doesn't affect pill refinement."

The examiner studied him for a long moment. Then he laughed. "You've got guts, kid. I'll give you that." He gestured to a guard. "Search him."

The guard patted him down thoroughly, checking every fold of his clothing, every seam of his shoes. But the compartment was cleverly hidden, and the pill's darkness seemed to repel spiritual sense. The guard found nothing.

"He's clean," the guard said.

The examiner nodded. "Welcome to Lord Feng's service, boy. Try not to die too quickly."

Shen He stepped through the pavilion's entrance, and the world changed.

The compound was a fortress. Guards patrolled every corridor. Spiritual formations hummed in the walls, ready to activate at a moment's notice. And in the center of it all, sitting on a throne of piled spirit stones, was Feng Hao.

The Nascent Soul cultivator was young—deceptively young, with a face that looked like it belonged to a scholar, not a murderer. His eyes were pale gray, almost colorless, and they swept over Shen He like he was examining an insect.

"Another alchemist?" Feng Hao's voice was soft, almost gentle. "How delightful. We can always use more talented hands." He smiled. "What's your specialty, boy?"

Shen He bowed deeply, hiding his face. "I specialize in pill refinement, Lord Feng. Specifically, I have knowledge of a rare formula—the Soul-Tethering Pill."

Feng Hao's smile froze.

"Soul-Tethering?" he repeated. "That's a lost art. How does a crippled boy from the Azure Cloud Sect know about such things?"

Shen He's blood turned to ice. Feng Hao knew where he was from. Of course he did—the underground was connected. News traveled faster than he'd anticipated.

But he'd come too far to back out now.

"I found the formula in the sect's library," he said, keeping his voice steady. "I've been working on replicating it. I think I've succeeded."

He reached into his shoe and pulled out the pill.

The moment it was exposed to the air, the darkness within it pulsed. The torches in the room flickered. The guards drew their weapons.

Feng Hao leaned forward, his eyes fixed on the pill. "Bring it to me."

"Lord Feng, it's dangerous—" one of the guards started.

"Bring. It. To. Me."

Shen He walked forward, each step feeling like he was walking to his own execution. The pill grew heavier in his hand, colder, more alive.

He stopped before the throne

title: The Serpent's Hidden Fangs
content:
The morning sun cast long shadows through the iron bars of Shen He's cage, painting stripes of light across the dirt floor. He'd been in the Chu caravan's "guest quarters" for three days now—a wheeled prison designed for transporting spirit beasts, complete with reinforced walls and a suppression array that made his skin crawl.

But the array couldn't suppress what he'd already absorbed.

Shen He sat cross-legged in the corner, eyes closed, breathing slow and controlled. The Meridian Restoration Pill had nearly killed him, but it had also left behind something unexpected—a faint, persistent warmth in his dantian, like a ember refusing to die.

*Three days since the caravan caught me. Three days since I've had a pill.*

The withdrawal was real. His fingers trembled. His thoughts came in fragments, scattered like leaves in a storm. But beneath the craving, something else stirred—that cold, hard anger from the sect's judgment hall.

They'd thrown him out like garbage. Called him a cripple. A thief. A fool.

*And now this caravan thinks they can sell me to some backwater alchemist for experimentation.*

The sound of footsteps pulled him from his thoughts. Heavy boots. Multiple people. The caravan master's voice, oily and pleased: "He's in here. Healthy as a ox, despite the burns. You'll get good use out of him."

A woman's voice answered, sharp and familiar: "Open the cage."

Shen He's eyes snapped open.

The cage door swung wide, and Lin Xue stepped inside.

She looked different outside the sect's walls—no flowing robes, no jade hairpin marking her as a core disciple. Just simple travel clothes and a hood pulled low over her face. But he'd recognize those eyes anywhere. Cold. Calculating. And currently, very amused.

"Shen He," she said, as if greeting an old friend. "You look terrible."

"What are you doing here?" His voice came out hoarse, cracked from days of silence.

"Rescuing you, obviously." She tossed a small pouch at his feet. It clinked with the sound of spirit stones. "Paid the caravan master handsomely for the privilege. He thinks I'm a rogue alchemist looking for test subjects."

"You're not."

"No, I'm not." She crouched down to his level, her voice dropping to a whisper. "I'm here because I know what you're trying to do, and I want to help."

Shen He laughed—a bitter, broken sound. "Help? You're the one who exposed me. You're the reason I was exiled."

"I exposed you because Elder Wu was already watching you. If I hadn't acted, he would have caught you himself, and you'd be dead instead of exiled." She held his gaze. "I saved your life, Shen He. You just didn't know it."

The words hit him like a physical blow. He wanted to deny them, to rage at her, but the logic was inescapable. Elder Wu had been hunting him for weeks. If Lin Xue hadn't forced the issue when she did...

"Doesn't matter," he said, turning away. "I still failed. The pill formula was incomplete. The sect master was right—it kills the user."

"Did you take it?"

"What?"

"The pill. Did you take it?" Lin Xue's eyes bore into him. "I felt the explosion from the inner compound. I saw the aftermath. A normal person would have been vaporized. But you're still alive, Shen He. Bruised, burned, and locked in a cage, but alive."

He said nothing.

"Show me your meridians."

"What?"

"Your meridians. Show me." She reached for his wrist, and he flinched back. "Don't be a fool. If I wanted to hurt you, I wouldn't have paid fifty spirit stones for the privilege."

Slowly, reluctantly, Shen He extended his arm.

Lin Xue's fingers pressed against his pulse point, and he felt a thread of qi enter his body—gentle, probing, nothing like the brutal inspections the sect elders had performed. It flowed through him like water, tracing the pathways of his ruined meridians.

Her breath caught.

"Impossible," she whispered.

"What?"

"The Meridian Restoration Pill. It's not supposed to work. Every recorded attempt has ended in death. But your meridians..." She looked up at him, and for the first time, he saw something other than cold calculation in her eyes. Wonder. "They're regenerating. Slowly, but it's happening."

Shen He's heart hammered against his ribs. "What?"

"Your meridians are healing. The pill didn't kill you—it *started* a process. A slow, agonizing process, but a process nonetheless." She released his wrist. "The sect master was wrong. The ancient alchemists weren't fools. They just had the wrong method."

"Then what's the right method?"

Lin Xue smiled—a dangerous smile that made his blood run cold. "That's what I'm going to help you find out."

"Why?" The question came out harsher than he intended. "Why would you help me? What do you get out of this?"

"Freedom." The word hung in the air between them. "I've been a prodigy my entire life. The sect's golden girl. The one who would become an elder, then a grand elder, then maybe even sect master. But I never asked for any of it. I was chosen because I was talented, not because I wanted to be."

She leaned closer, her voice barely a whisper. "I want to leave the Azure Cloud Sect. I want to forge my own path. But they won't let me go. Not alive."

"So you're using me."

"I'm *allying* with you. There's a difference." She stood, brushing off her knees. "The Meridian Restoration Pill isn't just a way to fix your meridians. It's a way to rewrite the rules of cultivation entirely. If we can perfect it, we'll have something the great sects would kill for. Something that gives us leverage."

Shen He stared at her, weighing her words. Everything he knew about Lin Xue told him not to trust her. She was ambitious, calculating, and had already betrayed him once.

But she was also right. He couldn't do this alone. He needed resources, knowledge, protection. And she needed a way out.

"What's the catch?" he asked.

"There's always a catch." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. "This is the complete Meridian Restoration Pill formula. The real one, not the fragment you found in the library. I've had it for years."

Shen He's hands trembled as he took it. "If you had it, why didn't you use it?"

"Because I don't need it." Lin Xue's smile turned cold. "My meridians are perfect. But I know someone who does. And I know that if I give him this, he'll owe me a debt he can never repay."

"You mean—"

"I mean the sect master has a son. And that son was born with crippled meridians, just like you. The sect master has spent decades trying to find a cure. He's failed every time." She paused. "If I give him this, he'll let me leave. Quietly. Permanently."

"And what about me?"

"You come with me. As my personal alchemist. The sect master won't ask questions he doesn't want answered. And we both get what we want."

Shen He's mind raced. It was a good plan. Too good. "What aren't you telling me?"

Lin Xue's expression flickered—the first crack in her perfect composure. "The sect master's son is dying. He has maybe a month left. The pill will save him, but only if it's perfect. If we fail..."

"We both die."

"Yes."

Shen He looked down at the scroll in his hands. The weight of it pressed against his palms, heavier than any weapon. Inside was the key to everything he'd ever wanted. And the key to his destruction.

"One more question," he said. "Why me? There are plenty of alchemists better than me."

"Better, yes. But none of them have survived the pill." Lin Xue's eyes met his. "You're the first person in a thousand years to take the Meridian Restoration Pill and live. That makes you more valuable than any grandmaster alchemist in the world."

Shen He stood, his legs shaky but steady. "Then let's go make a pill that will save a dying boy."

"Or kill us both."

"Either way, it'll be a blast."

Lin Xue laughed—a genuine sound that surprised them both. "Elder Wu always said you had a death wish."

"Elder Wu can choke on a spirit stone." Shen He stepped out of the cage, the morning sun warming his face. "Where do we start?"

"We start by leaving this caravan. I have a traveling shrine set up a mile north. We'll work there." She turned, then paused. "One more thing, Shen He. If I die because of you, I'll find a way to haunt you for eternity."

"Noted."

They walked side by side through the camp, past the curious stares of the caravan guards and the suspicious glares of the merchants. Lin Xue moved with the confidence of someone who'd never been challenged in her life.

Shen He followed, the scroll clutched against his chest like a lifeline.

Neither of them noticed the figure watching from the tree line. Nor did they see the messenger hawk take flight moments later, its wings carrying a sealed message toward the distant peaks of the Azure Cloud Sect.

Elder Wu received the message three hours later. He read it once, twice, then smiled—a thin, predatory expression that made his junior disciples flinch.

"Bring me my travel robes," he said. "It seems our traitorous prodigy has decided to make things interesting."

"And the cripple, Elder Wu?"

"The cripple?" Elder Wu's smile widened. "The cripple just became the most valuable piece on the board. Make sure he arrives in one piece. I have *plans* for him."

title: The Shadow in the Cauldron
content:
The mountain wind howled through the canyon, carrying the scent of pine and blood. Shen He pressed himself against the cliff face, his heart hammering against his ribs as the sound of pursuit faded into the distance.

Three days.

Three days since he'd been cast out of the Azure Cloud Sect, and they were still hunting him. Not the sect itself—they'd made their judgment and moved on. No, these were different. Shadow Hunters. Mercenaries who specialized in tracking down rogue cultivators and selling them to the highest bidder.

Someone wanted him alive.

The thought should have terrified him. Instead, it fueled the cold fire burning in his chest.

He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page of notes—the only thing he'd salvaged from the explosion. The ink was smudged, the characters barely legible, but he'd memorized every stroke months ago. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The forbidden formula that supposedly didn't work.

But Shen He knew better now.

The explosion hadn't been a failure. It had been a success—for a split second. He'd felt it: the pill reaching completion, the energy condensing into something perfect and terrible. And then the backlash had torn everything apart because his body couldn't handle the strain.

The pill worked. It was his body that was the problem.

He laughed bitterly, the sound swallowed by the wind. A cripple who could absorb qi through pills, but couldn't refine them properly. The universe had a sick sense of humor.

A stone skittered down the canyon wall above him.

Shen He froze.

He'd been careless. Lost in thought while enemies were hunting him. He pressed his back against the rock, forcing his breathing to slow as he listened.

Footsteps. Soft, deliberate. Not the heavy boots of the Shadow Hunters.

Someone else.

"Come out, little mouse."

The voice was female, melodic, and completely at odds with the menace in her words. Shen He's hand drifted to the pouch at his belt—his last resort. A single pill he'd refined in desperation the night before. Untested. Unstable. Probably deadly.

Better than being captured.

"I know you're there," the voice continued. "I can smell the half-failed pill on you. Impressive, actually. Most cripples can't even get that far."

Shen He's eyes widened. She could *smell* the pill residue? That meant—

"High-level alchemist," he muttered. "Perfect."

He stepped out from behind the rock.

The woman standing twenty feet away was young—no older than twenty-five—with silver hair that fell to her waist and eyes that shifted between gold and crimson like liquid fire. She wore robes of deep purple embroidered with golden cauldrons, the insignia of a master alchemist.

But not from any sect Shen He recognized.

"There you are," she said, smiling. "I was beginning to think you'd holed up and died. That would have been terribly inconvenient."

"Who are you?" Shen He asked, keeping his voice steady. "What do you want?"

"My name is Wei Ying. As for what I want..." She tilted her head, studying him like a specimen. "I want to know how a meridian-crippled boy managed to almost successfully refine the Meridian Restoration Pill."

The world tilted beneath Shen He's feet.

"How do you know about that?"

Wei Ying laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "Little mouse, the explosion you caused was felt by every alchemist within a hundred miles. The resonance alone told me what you were attempting. But the fact that you survived? That you managed to reach the condensation stage at all?" Her smile widened. "That's impossible."

"And yet here I am."

"Yes. Here you are." She took a step closer, and Shen He tensed, ready to run. "I have a proposition for you."

"I'm not interested."

"You haven't heard it yet."

"I don't need to. You're a master alchemist from an unknown sect. You tracked me through three provinces. And you knew exactly what pill I was trying to refine before anyone else could have figured it out." Shen He's hand tightened around the pill pouch. "That means you have resources I don't, knowledge I don't, and an agenda I definitely don't want to be part of."

Wei Ying clapped slowly. "Brilliant. Truly brilliant. Most cripples would be begging for my help by now. But you..." Her eyes gleamed. "You think."

"I think I should run."

"You should. But you won't." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a scroll, tossing it at his feet. "Read that. Then decide if you still want to run."

Shen He didn't move. "Why would I trust anything you give me?"

"Because I just saved your life."

Before he could ask what she meant, a beam of golden light lanced through the space where he'd been standing, carving a trench through the canyon floor.

Shen He dove to the side, rolling as another beam struck the cliff face behind him, sending chunks of stone raining down. He looked up to see three figures descending from the sky, their auras blazing with killing intent.

Shadow Hunters. But not the ones from before.

These were stronger. Faster. Their leader—a man with a scarred face and eyes like molten gold—landed twenty feet away, his sword already drawn.

"Shen He of the Azure Cloud Sect," he said, his voice flat and mechanical. "You are wanted for crimes against the Celestial Alchemy Guild. Surrender now, and your death will be merciful."

The Celestial Alchemy Guild.

Shen He's blood ran cold. The guild was the most powerful alchemy organization in the world—above sects, above nations, above the laws of heaven itself. If they wanted him, there was nowhere he could run.

Wei Ying sighed dramatically. "See? I told you. Saved your life."

"You led them here!"

"Of course I did. How else was I supposed to find you?" She stepped forward, placing herself between Shen He and the Shadow Hunters. "But now that I have, I thought I'd give you a choice. Come with me willingly, or let them take you to the guild."

"And what's the difference?"

"The guild will dissect you. They'll study your body, your meridians, your brain. They'll take you apart piece by piece until they understand how you can do what you do. And then they'll throw what's left into a furnace." Wei Ying's smile turned sharp. "I, on the other hand, just want to talk."

The lead Shadow Hunter raised his sword. "Last warning. Surrender or die."

Shen He looked between them—the three killers who wanted him dead or captured, and the mysterious alchemist who wanted him alive for reasons she wouldn't explain.

Neither option was good.

But one of them gave him time.

"I'll go with you," he said to Wei Ying. "But if you try anything—"

"You'll blow yourself up with one of those unstable pills you're hiding in your pouch?" She laughed. "I know. That's what makes this so interesting."

She raised her hand, and a wave of purple mist erupted from her palm, spreading across the canyon in seconds. The Shadow Hunters tried to retreat, but the mist caught them, freezing them in place like statues in amber.

"Wei Ying," the leader growled, his voice strained. "The guild will—"

"The guild can kiss my perfectly refined ass." She snapped her fingers, and the mist solidified, turning the hunters into stone. "They'll be free in about an hour. More than enough time for us to have our little chat."

She turned to Shen He, extending her hand. "Well? Are you coming, or do you want to wait for the next batch?"

Shen He hesitated. Every instinct told him to run, to disappear into the wilderness and never trust anyone again.

But she knew about the pill. She knew about his body. And if she was connected to the Celestial Alchemy Guild, she might have answers he desperately needed.

He took her hand.

The world dissolved into purple light.

---

They appeared in a cave—no, a workshop. The walls were lined with shelves holding thousands of bottles, jars, and scrolls. In the center stood a massive cauldron, its surface etched with characters that glowed with a soft, golden light.

Wei Ying released his hand and gestured grandly. "Welcome to my humble laboratory. Please don't touch anything. Most of it will kill you."

Shen He's eyes swept across the room, cataloging everything. Rare herbs, spirit stones, refining tools he'd only ever read about in forbidden texts. This wasn't just a master alchemist's workshop. This was the workshop of someone who had access to resources that even the Azure Cloud Sect's elders would envy.

"Who are you really?" he asked.

"Already told you. Wei Ying."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only one you're getting for now." She walked to the cauldron, placing her hand on its surface. "What I want to know is how you managed to reach the condensation stage of the Meridian Restoration Pill without any cultivation base. The formula requires the refiner to channel qi through seven different meridian points simultaneously. You have none. It should have been impossible."

Shen He was quiet for a long moment. He'd never told anyone about his ability—the way his body could absorb qi through pills instead of breath. It was his secret, his only advantage in a world that had declared him worthless.

But if Wei Ying already knew about the pill, what else did she know?

"I have a theory," she continued when he didn't answer. "I think your body does something unusual when it processes pills. Something that shouldn't be possible."

"How do you know that?"

"Because I've been studying anomalies like you for years." She turned to face him, her eyes burning with intensity. "People born without cultivation potential who somehow manage to refine pills. People who survive explosions that should kill them. People who bend the rules of alchemy without understanding how."

She stepped closer.

"I think you're not a cripple, Shen He. I think you're something else entirely. Something the world hasn't seen in ten thousand years."

Shen He's breath caught in his throat. "What are you talking about?"

"I'm talking about the Body of the Undying Pharmacist."

The words hit him like a physical blow. He'd heard that term before—in the forbidden texts he'd read in the sect's library. A legend. A myth. The story of an ancient alchemist who could consume pills endlessly, growing stronger without cultivation, building a body that could withstand anything.

A story everyone believed was just a story.

"That's impossible," he whispered. "The Undying Pharmacist is a legend. It's not real."

"Isn't it?" Wei Ying smiled, and there was something almost sad in her expression. "I've been searching for someone like you my entire life. And now that I've found you..." She reached into her robe and pulled out a small, black pill. "I have a gift."

"What is it?"

"The first step toward becoming what you were meant to be." She held it out to him. "Eat this, and I'll tell you everything."

Shen He stared at the pill. It pulsed with a dark energy, whispering promises of power and transformation. He could feel it calling to him, urging him to take it, to consume it, to become something more.

But he'd learned long ago that nothing in this world came free.

"What's the catch?"

Wei Ying's smile faded, replaced by something cold and calculating. "The catch is that once you take it, you belong to me. You'll help me with my experiments. You'll

title: The Demon's Bargain
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

Three days had passed since he fled the Azure Cloud Sect's outer perimeter, and the cold mountain air had given way to the humid, suffocating breath of the Blackwood Forest. Ancient trees twisted toward a gray sky, their roots breaking through the soil like the grasping fingers of buried corpses. Shen He pressed forward, his body screaming for rest, his mind racing with fragments of the Meridian Restoration Pill's formula.

*Pulse the Lunar Petal at midnight. Grind the Phoenix Tear against jade. Never let the crucible touch iron.*

He repeated the words like a prayer, even as his legs threatened to give out. The explosion had cost him more than his place in the sect. It had cost him his supplies, his notes, and most of his remaining spirit stones. What little he had left sat in his bag—three stalks of dried Nightbloom, a cracked mortar and pestle, and the single crumpled page he'd salvaged.

But the formula was burned into his memory. Every word. Every measurement. Every warning.

*The pill does not restore meridians. It destroys them. It kills the user.*

The sect master's words echoed in his skull, but Shen He shook them away. He'd felt something during that failed refinement—a resonance, a connection to the ingredients that no textbook had ever described. The formula wasn't wrong. It was incomplete. The ancient alchemists had been missing something, and Shen He was going to find out what.

A twig snapped behind him.

Shen He froze, his hand instinctively moving to the dagger at his belt. The forest had been eerily quiet for the past hour—no birds, no insects, no rustling of small animals in the underbrush. That meant something was hunting.

He didn't turn around. Instead, he listened.

Breathing. Slow. Deliberate. Not human.

"Show yourself," Shen He said, his voice steadier than he felt.

A laugh bubbled up from the darkness between two ancient oaks. It was a woman's laugh, musical and wrong, like wind chimes in a hurricane. "You're a difficult one to track, little pharmacist. I've been following you since the sect gates."

Shen He turned slowly, his eyes adjusting to the gloom.

She emerged from the shadows like smoke given form. Tall. Pale. Her robes were the color of dried blood, and her hair fell in a cascade of silver-white that seemed to glow in the dim light. But it was her eyes that seized his attention—one gold, one silver, both burning with an intelligence that felt ancient and predatory.

She smiled, and he saw that her teeth were slightly too sharp.

"Do you know who I am?" she asked.

"Should I?"

"You should. I'm the reason you're still alive." She stepped closer, and Shen He's hand tightened on his dagger. "When Elder Wu's men came to finish you off that night—the ones he sent after you left the sect—I was the one who killed them."

Shen He's blood went cold. He'd heard the screams in the darkness two nights ago, but he'd assumed it was wild beasts. He'd been too exhausted to investigate.

"Why?" he asked.

"Because I want something you have." Her smile widened. "The Meridian Restoration Pill."

"I don't have it. The refinement failed."

"I know. I was watching." She circled him slowly, her robes whispering against the fallen leaves. "But you have the knowledge. You have the instinct. And you have something else—something the sect never knew about."

Shen He's heart hammered against his ribs. "What are you talking about?"

"The qi in your body." She stopped directly in front of him, tilting her head like a curious bird. "You think they couldn't sense it? The elders? The sect master? They knew you were absorbing qi through your pills. They just couldn't figure out how. But I can."

"You're lying."

"I never lie." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small jade bottle. "Drink this, and I'll prove it to you."

"Poison?"

"If I wanted you dead, you'd already be dead. Twice over." She pressed the bottle into his hands. Her fingers were ice-cold. "It's a spirit detection elixir. Common among demon cultivators. It will reveal the true nature of your qi."

Shen He stared at the bottle. Demon cultivators. The words sent a chill through him that had nothing to do with the forest's cold. The Azure Cloud Sect had taught him that demon cultivators were the lowest of the low—savage, immoral, a threat to all righteous cultivators.

But the sect had also thrown him out like garbage.

"What do you want in return?" he asked.

"Your help." Her eyes glittered. "I've been searching for the Meridian Restoration Pill's secret for three hundred years. I've killed for it. I've died for it." She laughed again, that wrong, musical sound. "Literally. I died twice. But the formula always eluded me. Until I saw you refine it."

"You watched me."

"Every step. Every mistake. Every near-death experience." She leaned in close, and he smelled incense and blood. "You have a gift, little pharmacist. A gift that no amount of cultivation can teach. You can *feel* the ingredients. You can *hear* what they want to become. That's rare. That's priceless."

Shen He uncorked the bottle. The liquid inside was black as ink, and it smelled of ozone and rotting flowers. He hesitated.

"If I drink this, and I don't like what I see?"

"Then you walk away. I won't stop you." She spread her hands, a gesture of openness that felt anything but. "I'm not your enemy, Shen He. I'm your only ally."

He drank.

The elixir burned down his throat like molten lead, and for a moment, Shen He thought she'd poisoned him after all. His vision swam. His bones ached. And then—

*He saw himself.*

Not his reflection, but his qi. It swirled inside him like a galaxy of light, pinpricks of gold and silver and deep, pulsing crimson. It was beautiful. It was wrong. It was *impossible*.

"Do you see it now?" the demon woman whispered. "Your body doesn't just absorb qi. It *transforms* it. Every pill you take, every ingredient you refine—your body learns. It adapts. It evolves."

Shen He stared at the swirling light. "What am I?"

"What you've always been. A freak. An anomaly. A miracle." She smiled, and this time there was no mockery in it. "The sects would call you a curse upon the heavens. The demon clans would call you a weapon. But I call you... interesting."

The vision faded, and Shen He found himself on his knees, gasping for breath. The jade bottle lay shattered beside him.

"What do you want?" he asked again, his voice raw.

"The Meridian Restoration Pill. But not the failed version. The *true* version." She crouched down to his level, her mismatched eyes boring into his. "I know where the final ingredient is hidden. The one the ancient alchemists locked away because they were too afraid to use it."

"Where?"

"Deep in the Blackwood Forest. In a tomb that has never been opened." She extended her hand. "Come with me. Help me retrieve it, and I'll give you everything you need to complete the formula. Resources. Protection. Knowledge."

Shen He looked at her hand. It was pale and cold and beautiful, and it belonged to a demon who had killed Elder Wu's men without a second thought.

"Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't." Her smile sharpened. "But the alternative is dying alone in this forest, hunted by the sect's assassins, with nothing but a failed formula and a pocket full of dead dreams."

She wasn't wrong.

Shen He took her hand.

---

The tomb was hidden beneath a collapsed temple, its entrance sealed by a stone door covered in ancient script. The demon woman—she called herself Yan Mei—traced the characters with her finger, her lips moving silently as she read.

"The Alchemist's Lament," she said finally. "A warning to all who seek what lies within."

"What does it say?"

She turned to him, and for the first time, he saw something like fear flicker in her eyes. "*He who opens this door shall face the judgment of heaven. He who takes what lies within shall be cursed for eternity. He who consumes the Heartwood Lotus shall become neither man nor demon, but something the world has not seen since the age of gods.*"

"The Heartwood Lotus?"

"The final ingredient." Yan Mei pressed her palm against the stone, and the door groaned. "The heart of the World Tree, preserved in alchemical stasis for ten thousand years. It's the key to the Meridian Restoration Pill. It's also"—she paused—"incredibly dangerous."

"How dangerous?"

"Dangerous enough that I've spent three centuries trying to find someone brave enough—or foolish enough—to help me retrieve it."

The door swung open, revealing a darkness so absolute it seemed to swallow the light.

Yan Mei stepped forward, but Shen He grabbed her arm. "Wait. If this is so dangerous, why do you need me? You're a demon cultivator. You've lived for three hundred years. You could just take the lotus yourself."

She looked at him, and her smile was sad. "Because I can't touch it. The lotus is protected by a purification array that would destroy any demon who tried to claim it. Only a human can retrieve it." She paused. "Only a *pure* human."

Shen He laughed bitterly. "I'm hardly pure."

"No. You're not." Her eyes met his. "But you're the closest thing I'm going to find, and I'm running out of time."

"Time for what?"

"To save someone I love." She turned away, her voice dropping to a whisper. "Someone who's been dying for a very long time."

She stepped into the darkness, and after a moment's hesitation, Shen He followed.

---

The tomb was larger than it had any right to be. Corridors stretched in every direction, lined with shelves of petrified books and jars of alchemical reagents that had long since turned to dust. The air was thick with the smell of decay and something else—something that made Shen He's skin prickle with recognition.

Qi. Pure, concentrated qi, so dense it felt like walking through water.

"The lotus is close," Yan Mei said, her voice echoing off the stone walls. "I can feel it."

They emerged into a vast chamber, its ceiling lost in shadow. At the center, suspended in a pillar of crystalline light, floated a flower the size of a man's head. Its petals were white as snow, shot through with veins of gold that pulsed with a light of their own.

The Heartwood Lotus.

Shen He walked toward it, his feet moving of their own accord. The qi in the room sang to him, calling to something deep in his bones. He reached out—

And stopped.

The lotus wasn't alone.

In the shadows behind it, a figure stirred. Tall. Emaciated. Wrapped in chains that glowed with runic script.

"Shen He," a voice rasped, ancient and terrible. "Do not touch that flower."

Yan Mei's hand shot out, grabbing Shen He's wrist. "Ignore him. It's just an echo—a guardian spirit bound to the tomb. It can't hurt us."

But the figure stepped forward, and the chains clinked against the stone floor. "I am no echo. I

title: The Serpent's Bargain
content:
The path down the mountain was longer than Shen He remembered.

Perhaps it was because he'd never actually walked it before. In his three years at the Azure Cloud Sect, he'd always been carried up by the disciple recruitment carriage, blindfolded according to tradition. The descent should have taken two hours. He'd been walking for four, and the peak still loomed behind him like an accusing finger pointed at the heavens.

His legs burned. His lungs ached. The Meridian Restoration Pill's aftereffects still crackled through his body like trapped lightning, making his fingers twitch and his vision blur at the edges.

*Great. I'm dying and exiled. What a wonderful combination.*

He stopped to rest against a gnarled pine, its roots gripping the cliffside like skeletal fingers. Below, the mortal world spread out in a patchwork of farms and forests, tiny villages dotting the landscape like scattered seeds. Somewhere down there, people lived without cultivation, without qi, without the constant struggle for power.

Shen He had never been one of them. Born to a minor noble family that had squandered its fortune, he'd been sent to the sect as a last resort—a final gamble to produce a cultivator who could restore their name. Instead, they'd produced a cripple who stole forbidden formulas and nearly blew up the outer compound.

*Mother and Father probably don't even know I exist anymore. Or care.*

He pushed off the tree and kept walking.

The path wound through a bamboo forest, the stalks creaking and groaning in the wind. Shadows danced across the ground, shifting and twisting in ways that didn't quite match the movement of the leaves. Shen He's hand went to his waist, where a small dagger rested—his only weapon.

"Show yourself," he said, his voice flat.

The shadows stopped moving.

For a long moment, nothing happened. Then a figure stepped out from behind a cluster of bamboo, moving with the fluid grace of a predator who had already decided you were prey.

She was beautiful. That was Shen He's first thought. Tall, with skin like porcelain and eyes the color of molten gold. Her robes were black silk embroidered with silver serpents, and her hair fell in a cascade of midnight blue that seemed to absorb the light around it.

"You're perceptive," she said, her voice a husky whisper that carried hints of amusement. "Most mortals don't notice my presence until I'm standing over their corpses."

"I'm not most mortals."

"No. You're not." She circled him slowly, her golden eyes roving over his body with an intensity that made his skin crawl. "You're the one who tried to refine the Meridian Restoration Pill. The cripple who nearly succeeded where generations of alchemists failed."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "How do you know about that?"

"I know many things." She stopped in front of him, close enough that he could smell her perfume—jasmine and something metallic, like blood. "I also know you failed because your technique was flawed. You used Yin Fire instead of Yang Flame. A common mistake among those who don't understand the true nature of meridian restoration."

"You're an alchemist?"

"I'm something far more interesting." She smiled, and her teeth were slightly too sharp. "I'm a solution to your problem."

"And what problem would that be?"

"The problem of being a cripple in a world that despises weakness. The problem of being hunted by the Azure Cloud Sect, who will send assassins to silence you within the week. The problem of having knowledge you can't use and power you can't access." She tilted her head. "Shall I continue?"

Shen He's hand tightened on his dagger. "Who are you?"

"My name is Yan Mei. I represent an organization that has been watching you for some time." She produced a scroll from her sleeve, unrolling it to reveal a detailed sketch of his face. "The Crimson Lotus Society has need of an alchemist with your... unique talents."

"The Crimson Lotus?" Shen He's stomach dropped. Even in the sect, he'd heard whispers of the Crimson Lotus Society—a shadow organization of rogue cultivators, demonic alchemists, and exiled masters who operated outside the law. They were said to be responsible for everything from assassination to rebellion, their reach extending across the entire continent.

"The very same." Yan Mei rolled up the scroll. "Before you refuse, consider your options. You can wander down the mountain and die within a week—either from the qi damage in your body or from the assassins the sect will send. Or you can accept my offer and live."

"Live as what? A tool for your society?"

"Live as an alchemist. A real one, with resources, knowledge, and protection." She stepped closer, and he caught a whiff of that metallic scent again. "We have libraries that would make the Azure Cloud Sect weep with envy. We have ingredients from every corner of the continent. And we have the freedom to pursue any formula we desire, no matter how forbidden."

Shen He's breath caught. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula that had cost him everything. If the Crimson Lotus had access to forbidden knowledge, they might have information he needed to perfect it.

But joining them would mean becoming a criminal. A demonic cultivator. Everything the sect had warned him against.

"Think carefully." Yan Mei's golden eyes seemed to glow. "The path you choose here will define the rest of your existence."
