"Why me?" Shen He asked. "There are thousands of alchemists in the world. Why go through all this trouble for a cripple?"

"Because you're not just any cripple." Yan Mei's smile widened. "You're the one who survived the Meridian Restoration Pill's backlash. Do you know how many have attempted that refinement and died? Seventy-three, in the past century alone. You're the first to walk away with nothing worse than some internal damage."

That cold anger stirred in his chest again. "So I'm a lab rat. A curiosity."

"You're a survivor. And survivors make the best alchemists." She extended her hand, palm up. "Join us, Shen He. Let the Azure Cloud Sect rot in their traditions and their cowardice. Come with me, and I'll show you what true alchemy can achieve."

Shen He stared at her hand. It was pale and perfect, with nails lacquered a deep crimson that matched the society's name. Everything about her screamed danger, from the serpent embroidery on her robes to the predatory glint in her eyes.

But she was right about one thing: he had nowhere else to go.

"What's the catch?" he asked.

"The catch?" Yan Mei laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "There's always a catch, isn't there? Fine. The catch is that you'll work for us. We'll provide resources and protection, and in return, you'll refine pills for our members. You'll experiment with forbidden formulas. You'll push the boundaries of alchemy to places the orthodox sects fear to tread."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then I leave you here to die." Her voice hardened. "Make no mistake, Shen He. I didn't come to give you a choice. I came to give you an alternative to death."

The anger flared hotter. He wanted to refuse on principle, to tell her exactly where she could shove her offer. But the logical part of his mind—the part that had survived three years of mockery and abuse—kept him silent.

He needed resources. He needed knowledge. And above all, he needed to survive long enough to figure out why his body could absorb qi through pills.

"I have conditions," he said.

Yan Mei's eyebrows rose. "Conditions?"

"First: I'm not a slave. If I join, I join as a member, not property."

"Agreed."

"Second: I get access to your libraries. All of them."

"Within reason. Some texts are restricted to certain ranks. But you'll have more access than any outer sect disciple could dream of."

"Third: if I want to leave, I can leave."

Yan Mei's smile faded. "That's not how the Crimson Lotus works. Once you're in, you're in for life."

"Then I'll die on this mountain."

Silence stretched between them. The bamboo creaked in the wind, and somewhere in the distance, a bird called out in alarm.

Finally, Yan Mei laughed again. "You have balls. I'll give you that." She extended her hand once more. "Fine. After three years, if you wish to leave, you may. But know this: leaving the Crimson Lotus is difficult. We have enemies everywhere, and former members have a tendency to die in unfortunate accidents."

"Three years," Shen He repeated. "That's my offer."

"Deal." She grabbed his hand, and her grip was iron. "Welcome to the Crimson Lotus Society, Alchemist Shen He."

The moment their skin touched, something cold slithered up his arm. He looked down to see a serpent tattoo forming on his wrist, coiling around his skin in intricate patterns. The ink seemed to writhe, alive with dark energy.

"A mark of membership," Yan Mei explained. "It allows us to track you, communicate with you, and ensure you don't betray us." Her eyes glittered. "Try to remove it, and it will kill you."

Shen He stared at the tattoo. It pulsed with a faint light, synchronizing with his heartbeat. He could feel it now—a presence in his qi network, foreign and hostile, but dormant.

*What have I done?*

"Come." Yan Mei turned and began walking down the path. "We have a long journey ahead. The nearest Crimson Lotus outpost is three days from here, and I'd rather not be caught in the open when the sect's assassins arrive."

Shen He followed, his feet moving of their own accord. The tattoo burned against his skin, a constant reminder of the bargain he'd just made.

They walked in silence for an hour, the bamboo forest giving way to rocky outcrops and sparse vegetation. The sun hung low in the sky, painting the world in shades of orange and red.

"You're wondering if you made the right choice," Yan Mei said, not turning around.

"The thought crossed my mind."

"You did. The Azure Cloud Sect would have killed you, eventually. Either through neglect or direct action." She glanced back at him. "The Crimson Lotus values talent above all else. As long as you prove useful, you'll be protected."

"And when I stop being useful?"

"That's a question for another day."

They rounded a bend in the path, and Shen He stopped dead.

Ahead, blocking the trail, stood a figure in white robes. The setting sun caught the silver embroidery on their sleeves—the symbol of the Azure Cloud Sect's inner disciples.

Lin Xue.

She held a sword in her hand, the blade gleaming with qi. Her face was composed, emotionless, but her eyes burned with something Shen He couldn't identify.

"Yan Mei of the Crimson Lotus," Lin Xue said, her voice cold as mountain snow. "Release the cripple. He belongs to the sect."

Yan Mei laughed. "Oh, this is delightful. The prodigy herself, come to reclaim her failed experiment." She turned to Shen He, her golden eyes dancing with amusement. "It seems your former sectmate has decided you're too dangerous to let live."

Lin Xue's grip tightened on her sword. "He stole forbidden knowledge. He attempted a refinement that could have destroyed half the sect. He must answer for his crimes."

"He's made a new deal." Yan Mei stepped forward, and shadows began to gather around her feet. "And I protect what's mine."

The two women faced each other, qi crackling in the air between them. Shen He stood frozen, caught between the sect that had exiled him and

title: The Corpse Alchemist's Bargain
content:
The Blood Lotus Gang's compound burned behind Shen He as he stumbled through the night, half-carrying a terrified mortal girl who kept sobbing about her family. Three days had passed since his exile, and he'd already managed to destroy a minor criminal organization, consume seventeen pills of questionable stability, and attract the attention of every cultivator within a hundred li.

Not bad for a cripple.

"Please," the girl whimpered. "They'll kill me if they find out I survived."

"They won't find out," Shen He said, though he wasn't sure he believed it. The Blood Lotus Gang's leader had been a Qi Condensation cultivator—barely worthy of the title, but still leagues above a meridian-crippled alchemist. Shen He had only won because the man had underestimated him, assuming the pills Shen He threw were mere distractions.

They were not distractions. They were his fists.

He found shelter in an abandoned temple, its roof half-collapsed, its walls covered in faded murals of immortals who had long since ascended. The girl—Mei Lin, she'd said—fell asleep immediately, exhausted by terror and grief. Shen He sat against a crumbling pillar, rolling a single pill between his fingers.

The Meridian Shattering Pill.

He'd completed the refinement during his flight, using stolen ingredients and desperate substitutions. The pill was unstable, volatile, and almost certainly lethal. The sect master had been right about one thing: everyone who'd attempted this formula had died.

But they didn't have Shen He's body.

He'd discovered it in the library, hidden in the pages of a forbidden text that had been mislabeled as a cookbook. The original alchemist—a madman named Wei Chong—had theorized that meridians could be reforged through controlled destruction. The body had to be broken completely, then rebuilt from the ashes.

The problem was the "dying" part.

Shen He had spent the last three days studying his own unique physiology. His body didn't absorb qi through breath, but it did absorb it through pills. What if the same principle applied to destruction? What if his body could absorb the damage, convert it into something useful?

He was still debating whether to risk it when the wall exploded inward.

Shen He threw himself over Mei Lin, shielding her from the debris. When the dust cleared, a woman stood in the gap—tall, angular, her hair white as bone, her eyes the color of dried blood. She wore robes of black silk embroidered with silver skeletons, and her aura pressed down on Shen He like a physical weight.

"Well, well," she said, her voice carrying a musical lilt that made the hair on Shen He's arms stand up. "The little pharmacist who burned down the Blood Lotus. I'd heard rumors, but I didn't expect to find you hiding in a ruin like a frightened mouse."

"Who are you?" Shen He demanded, though he already knew. He'd seen that emblem before, in the sect's restricted section.

"Lady Jiang," she said, as if the name should mean something. "Supreme Elder of the Corpse Flower Sect. And you, Shen He, are the most interesting thing I've seen in three centuries."

"How do you know my name?"

"I know everything that happens in this region." She stepped through the rubble, her movements fluid, predatory. "I know you were expelled from the Azure Cloud Sect for attempting a forbidden refinement. I know you've consumed more pills in the past week than most alchemists produce in a year. And I know you survived an explosion that should have killed you three times over."

She stopped an arm's length away, tilting her head as if examining a particularly fascinating specimen.

"You're not normal, Shen He. You're not even abnormal. You're a goddamn miracle."

"What do you want?"

"Your cooperation." She smiled, and it was not a pleasant expression. "I have a proposition for you. Join my sect. Become my personal alchemist. And I'll give you something you desperately need."

"And what's that?"

"Protection." She gestured vaguely toward the outside world. "The Azure Cloud Sect has put a bounty on your head. Fifty spirit stones for your capture, two hundred for your death. The Blood Lotus Gang's allies want revenge. And somewhere in the capital, a certain merchant family is very interested in the boy who stole their secret formula."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "How do you know about that?"

"I told you. I know everything." She leaned closer, and he caught the scent of embalming fluid and rotting flowers. "I also know you have a Meridian Shattering Pill in your pocket. And I know you're stupid enough to take it."

"Then you know I'll probably die."

"Probably." She laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "But if you survive, you'll be the most powerful cripple in history. And I want to be there to see it."

"What's in it for you?"

"Knowledge." Her eyes glittered with hunger. "I've spent my entire life studying the boundaries between life and death. You're a walking experiment, Shen He. A chance to understand how the body can be broken and remade. If you join me, I'll give you resources you can only dream of. Rare ingredients. Ancient texts. A laboratory where you can work without interruption."

"And if I refuse?"

She shrugged. "Then I'll kill you now, take your corpse, and study it anyway. Less satisfying, but still informative."

Shen He looked at Mei Lin, still sleeping, oblivious to the danger. He looked at the pill in his hand. Then he looked at Lady Jiang, this corpse-cultivating monster who offered him a deal with claws on every word.

"One condition," he said.

"Name it."

"The girl goes free. Safe passage to wherever she wants to go. No strings attached."

Lady Jiang's smile widened. "A cripple with a conscience. How quaint." She reached into her sleeve and produced a jade token, tossing it to Shen He. "Take her to the city of Blackwater. Present this token at the Jade Phoenix Inn. They'll give her passage to the coastal provinces, where the Blood Lotus has no reach."

Shen He caught the token, studying it. The jade was warm, pulsing with a faint energy that he couldn't quite identify. "And in return?"

"You come with me. Now. Tonight." She extended her hand. "We have work to do."

He took her hand, and the world dissolved into a blur of motion. When his vision cleared, they were standing in a vast underground chamber, its walls lined with shelves of preserved specimens. The air was thick with the smell of chemicals and death.

"Welcome to the Corpse Flower Sect," Lady Jiang said. "I hope you're not squeamish."

Shen He looked around, taking in the bodies suspended in glass tubes, the racks of bones, the bubbling vats of green liquid. A year ago, this would have horrified him. Now, it just looked like a laboratory.

"Show me your equipment," he said. "I need to refine a new batch of pills before I attempt the Meridian Shattering."

Lady Jiang laughed again. "Eager, aren't you? I like that." She led him through the chamber, past rows of workstations where pale-faced disciples hunched over bubbling cauldrons. They didn't look up as he passed.

At the far end of the chamber was a door of black iron, inscribed with characters that seemed to writhe in the torchlight. Lady Jiang pressed her palm against it, and the door swung open to reveal a room that took Shen He's breath away.

It was an alchemist's dream. Shelves of ingredients, organized by rarity and potency. A furnace that glowed with internal fire, its heat barely contained by the runes carved into its surface. Desks covered in scrolls, diagrams, and half-finished experiments.

And in the center of the room, mounted on a pedestal of white jade, was a pill.

It was the size of a fist, perfectly spherical, its surface a swirling pattern of gold and silver that seemed to shift as Shen He watched. The moment he saw it, every instinct in his body screamed at him.

That pill was alive.

"What is that?" he whispered.

Lady Jiang walked past him, trailing her fingers across the pill's surface. It pulsed under her touch, sending ripples of light through the room.

"That," she said, "is my life's work. A pill that can resurrect the dead."

Shen He's mind reeled. "That's impossible. Even the ancient alchemists couldn't—"

"I know what the ancient alchemists couldn't do." Her voice hardened. "But they didn't have what I have. They didn't have a subject like you."

"What are you talking about?"

She turned to face him, and for the first time, he saw something other than amusement in her eyes. He saw desperation.

"The Meridian Shattering Pill will destroy your body," she said. "But if you survive, if your body is remade, it will create a unique resonance. A frequency that my resurrection pill can lock onto." She stepped closer, her voice dropping to a whisper. "I need you to die, Shen He. I need you to die and come back. Because that's the only way I can learn to replicate the process."

"You want to study my resurrection."

"I want to perfect my pill." She reached out and touched his cheek, her fingers cold as grave dirt. "And I will do whatever it takes to achieve that goal."

Shen He looked at the massive pill, pulsing with stolen life. He looked at the alchemist who had saved him, who would destroy him, who might make him a god or a monster.

He should run. He should fight. He should do anything but accept this madness.

But somewhere deep in his chest, a voice whispered: *This is the only way.*

"Fine," he said. "But when I survive, you're going to owe me more than just resources."

Lady Jiang's smile was a razor's edge. "Name your price."

"Answers," Shen He said. "About my body. About why I can do what I do. About the people who made me this way."

The smile vanished. For just a moment, Lady Jiang looked almost human.

"You don't want those answers, Shen He."

"I need them."

She was silent for a long moment. Then she nodded slowly.

"Very well. Survive the Meridian Shattering, and I'll tell you everything I know." She gestured toward the furnace. "The ingredients are prepared. The furnace is ready. All that's left is for you to take the first step."

Shen He walked toward the furnace, the Meridian Shattering Pill heavy in his pocket. He could feel it calling to him, promising destruction and rebirth.

He was going to die tonight.

Or he was going to become something the world had never seen.

He placed the pill on his tongue and swallowed.

The world exploded into white-hot agony.

And somewhere in the darkness, Lady Jiang began to laugh.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Alchemist's Gambit

The rain came down in sheets, turning the mountain path to mud. Shen He pulled his tattered robe tighter, but the fabric offered little protection. Three days since his exile. Three days of walking, eating wild herbs, and trying not to think about the weight of failure pressing down on his chest.

He'd followed the road south, away from the Azure Cloud Sect's territory, toward the borderlands where cultivators rarely ventured. The mortal towns there would take him in—they always needed healers, and even a crippled alchemist knew more about medicine than any village doctor.

But the rain refused to stop, and his body refused to warm, and the crumpled page in his pocket felt heavier than any tome in the sect's library.

*The pill does not restore meridians. It destroys them.*

The sect master's words echoed in his mind, but something didn't fit. The ancient scroll had been detailed—incredibly so for a "failed" formula. The diagrams were precise, the ingredient ratios exact, the refinement steps laid out with mathematical clarity. Failed experiments didn't look like that. Failed experiments had crossed-out sections, margin notes, corrections.

This scroll had been perfect.

Too perfect.

Shen He stopped walking. The rain continued to fall, but he barely noticed. His mind raced, connecting pieces that hadn't fit before.

The scroll had been in the restricted section, but it wasn't locked in a vault. It was on a shelf, accessible to anyone who could bypass the basic wards. Elder Wu had caught him copying it, but Elder Wu was an enforcer, not a librarian. What was he doing in the restricted section that night?

And Lin Xue—the sect's golden child, the prodigy who never made mistakes—she'd seen him three weeks before the explosion. Three weeks. That was exactly how long it took to gather the ingredients for a Meridian Restoration Pill.

She'd known what he was planning. She'd let him do it.

The realization hit him like a physical blow. He'd been set up.

But why? What did the Azure Cloud Sect gain from exiling a cripple? He had no cultivation, no power, no influence. He was nothing to them.

Unless...

He reached into his pocket and pulled out the crumpled page. The notes he'd salvaged were sparse—just ingredient ratios and a single line of commentary:

*"The body is a cauldron. The spirit is the flame. Qi flows not through the meridians, but through the pill."*

He'd always thought that was metaphorical. But what if it wasn't?

What if the Meridian Restoration Pill wasn't meant to restore his original meridians, but to create *new* ones? A different kind of cultivation path—one that didn't rely on the body's natural channels, but on the interaction between pill and spirit?

The sect master had said the pill killed everyone who tried it. But the sect master was a politician, not an alchemist. He repeated what he'd been told.

And who had told him?

Elder Wu.

Shen He's hands clenched into fists. The rain seemed to fall harder, as if the heavens themselves were trying to drown him. But he didn't move. He stood there, letting the cold seep into his bones, letting the anger solidify into something harder.

Someone had sabotaged his refinement. Someone had wanted him to fail. And that same someone had made sure the punishment was exile, not death.

*They wanted me alive. They wanted me out here, alone, vulnerable.*

The question was: why?

A twig snapped behind him.

Shen He spun around, his heart lunging into his throat. The forest was empty—just trees and rain and shadows. But he knew better than to trust his eyes.

"I know you're there," he said, his voice steadier than he felt. "Show yourself."

Silence. The rain continued to fall.

Then, from behind a gnarled oak, a figure emerged. She moved like water, her steps silent despite the mud. Her robes were black, unmarked by any sect insignia. Her face was half-hidden by a hood, but Shen He recognized the sharp jawline, the cold eyes, the cruel curve of her lips.

Lin Xue.

"You're a fool, Shen He," she said, her voice carrying easily over the rain. "But you're not as stupid as I thought."

"What do you want?" He took a step back, his hand going to the pouch at his belt. Inside were three pills—one for healing, one for poison resistance, and one he'd never tested. The unstable one.

"I want to know what you saw," Lin Xue said. "You made it out of the explosion with half the library burned and your body barely intact. A cripple shouldn't have survived that."

"Lucky, I guess."

"Luck doesn't exist." She took a step forward, and Shen He took another step back. "You have something, Shen He. Something that shouldn't exist. And I'm going to find out what it is."

She moved.

Shen He didn't see her attack—he only felt the wind as she passed, the cold kiss of a blade against his throat. He fell backward, landing hard in the mud, scrambling away from her flashing steel.

"Stop running," she said, her voice bored. "You'll only prolong the pain."

"Tell me why," he gasped, pushing himself to his feet. "Tell me why you set me up. Tell me what you want, and I'll give it to you."

Lin Xue paused. Her head tilted, curiosity flickering in those cold eyes. "Interesting. Most people beg. You negotiate."

"Most people don't have what I have."

"And what's that?"

Shen He reached into his pouch and pulled out the unstable pill. It glowed faintly, a sickly green light that pulsed like a heartbeat. "This."

Lin Xue's eyes widened. For the first time, she looked uncertain. "What is that?"

"The last pill I made," Shen He said. "The one that should have killed me. But it didn't. And do you know why?"

She shook her head slowly.

"Because the Meridian Restoration Pill doesn't destroy meridians," he said, his voice dropping to a whisper. "It creates them. And this—" he held up the glowing pill, "—is version two."

"Version two of what?"

"Version two of the pill that's going to make me stronger than you."

Lin Xue laughed. It was a cold, brittle sound, devoid of humor. "You're delusional. That pill is going to kill you."

"Maybe." Shen He brought the pill to his lips. "But if I'm going to die, I'm going to die on my own terms."

He swallowed.

The pill hit his stomach like a bomb. Fire erupted through his veins, burning, tearing, *remaking*. He screamed, dropping to his knees, his body convulsing as something ancient and terrible awakened inside him.

Lin Xue watched, her expression shifting from amusement to confusion to horror. "What have you done?"

Shen He couldn't answer. His mouth was full of blood, his vision going white, his mind drowning in a sea of pain. But beneath the agony, he felt it—a new sensation, a new pathway, a new way for qi to flow.

His meridians were rebuilding themselves.

No, not rebuilding. *Growing*. New channels, branching out from his core, connecting to parts of his body that had never known qi before. His crippled cultivation base, the one that had been declared dead at birth, was *breathing*.

The rain stopped.

No, not stopped. It was being pushed away by an invisible force radiating from his body. The air around him crackled with energy, the mud beneath him drying and cracking as heat poured off his skin.

Lin Xue drew her sword. "I don't know what you've done, but I'm going to kill you before you finish it."

She lunged.

Shen He opened his eyes.

They were glowing—a deep, emerald green that matched the light of the pill he'd just consumed. He saw Lin Xue's attack coming before she made it, saw the trajectory of her blade, the weakness in her stance, the opening in her defense.

He moved.

His body responded faster than it ever had, his hand shooting up to catch her sword mid-strike. The blade bit into his palm, drawing blood, but he didn't feel the pain. He felt the qi flowing through his new meridians, felt it gathering in his hand, felt it *explode*.

Lin Xue flew backward, her sword spinning out of her grasp. She crashed into a tree, the impact cracking the trunk. Blood trickled from her mouth.

"How?" she whispered.

Shen He looked at his hand. The cut was already closing, the skin knitting together with supernatural speed. The green glow in his eyes began to fade, replaced by a clarity he'd never experienced before.

"I don't know," he said honestly. "But I'm going to find out."

He walked toward her, and for the first time in his life, Lin Xue looked afraid.

"Tell me who set this up," he said, his voice hard. "Tell me why, and I'll let you live."

She laughed, the sound wet and broken. "You think you've won? You think that pill makes you powerful?" She spat blood. "You've just painted a target on your back. Every sect in the realm will want you now. The Alchemy Guild will hunt you down and dissect you to find out how you did it."

"Let them try."

"They won't come alone. They'll come with armies, with cultivators who've trained for centuries, with weapons that can level mountains. And you—" she laughed again, "—you've only just learned to walk."

Shen He crouched down, meeting her eyes. "Then I'd better learn to run."

He stood up and walked away, leaving her bleeding against the tree. Behind him, he heard her voice, faint and fading:

"You don't understand. The pill you took—it's not just dangerous. It's a *key*. A key to something that should never be unlocked."

Shen He paused. "What do you mean?"

But Lin Xue had already fallen unconscious, her body limp, her breathing shallow.

He stood there for a long moment, the rain beginning to fall again, washing the blood from his hands. The pill's power thrummed through him, new and strange and terrifying. He could feel his body changing, adapting, growing stronger with every heartbeat.

But he could also feel something else—something dark, something hungry, something that had been sleeping inside the pill's formula, waiting for someone foolish enough to wake it.

And now it was awake.

And it was *him*.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Debt That Never Sleeps

The Black Lotus Consortium's compound rose from the marshlands like a tumor, all black stone and green-tinged torches that burned with an unnatural light. Shen He had been following its agents for three days, watching from the shadows as they moved through the border towns between the Azure Cloud Sect's territory and the lawless lands beyond.

They were efficient. Professional. And utterly ruthless.

He'd seen them break a merchant's fingers for failing to pay protection money. Watched them drag a young cultivator into an alley and emerge with his storage ring and a bloody knife. They operated with the casual brutality of those who had never faced consequences.

That was about to change.

Shen He crouched on a rooftop overlooking the compound's main gate, counting guards. Twelve on the walls. Four at the gate. Two patrolling the perimeter. Inside, probably double that number, plus whatever enforcer the Consortium had stationed here.

His hand drifted to the pill pouch at his belt. Three days of preparation. Eighteen pills, each one a carefully balanced cocktail of qi and poison. The Iron Skin Pill would handle arrows. The Shadow Step Pill would handle speed. And the Breath of the Dying Sun Pill—his newest creation—would handle everything else.

The Breath of the Dying Sun was a monster. He'd refined it from the forbidden scroll's principles, adapting the Meridian Restoration Pill's structure into something entirely different. Instead of trying to rebuild meridians, it superheated the user's qi, turning their entire body into a furnace for a few precious seconds.

The side effects included third-degree burns, temporary blindness, and possible organ failure.

But Shen He had tested it on a pig three days ago, and the pig had survived. Mostly.

"Perfect," he muttered, and leaped from the rooftop.

The guards saw him coming. That was intentional. The first one raised his spear, mouth opening to shout, but Shen He was already upon him. A flick of his wrist sent a powder into the guard's face—not deadly, just blinding. The man screamed, clawing at his eyes, and Shen He's palm connected with his chest.

The Iron Skin Pill activated. His hand felt like it was wrapped in stone as he drove the guard backward into the gate. Wood splintered. The guard crumpled.

"Intruder!" someone shouted.

Shen He smiled. "Finally."

The compound erupted into chaos. Guards poured from the buildings, weapons drawn, cultivation bases flaring. Shen He counted fifteen, then twenty, their auras ranging from the dull gray of Foundation Establishment to the sharper edge of Core Formation.

He was outnumbered. Outgunned. And completely out of his depth.

Perfect.

The Shadow Step Pill dissolved on his tongue, and the world blurred. He moved through the guards like water through rocks, impossible to catch, always a step ahead. His fists struck with the force of iron, his feet swept with the precision of a master.

But he wasn't a master. He was a pharmacist who'd learned to fight by reading about it.

A fist caught him in the ribs. He heard something crack. Another blow sent him spinning, and he crashed into a wall, the breath driven from his lungs.

The guards surrounded him, weapons raised. A woman stepped forward, her cultivation base blazing at the Peak Core Formation level. She was tall, scarred, and carried a sword that hummed with killing intent.

"You're the pharmacist," she said. Not a question.

Shen He spat blood. "I've been called worse."

"The Consortium wants you alive. But they didn't say we couldn't break you first." She raised her sword. "Any last words?"

He reached into his pouch. Her eyes widened, and she lunged—

Too slow.

The Breath of the Dying Sun Pill hit his tongue, and the world turned white.

Heat exploded from his core, rushing through his meridians like liquid fire. His skin began to smoke, then glow. The guards screamed and stumbled back, but it was too late. The shockwave hit them like a physical force, throwing them against the walls, the ground, each other.

Shen He stood in the center of the crater he'd created, steam rising from his body. His clothes were ash. His skin was red and blistered. But he was alive.

The scarred woman was still standing. She'd blocked the blast with her sword, but the weapon was cracked, and her arm hung limp at her side. She stared at him with something between fear and respect.

"What are you?" she whispered.

"I'm the guy who's going to give the Black Lotus Consortium a message." He stepped forward, and his foot cracked the stone beneath him. "Tell your masters that Shen He is coming for them. Tell them that every debt they've collected, every life they've ruined, every pill they've stolen—I'm going to make them pay it back."

She laughed, a broken sound. "You think you can threaten the Consortium? You're one man. One crippled pharmacist who can't even cultivate."

Shen He reached into his pouch and pulled out another pill. This one was black, shot through with veins of gold. He'd never tested it before. He didn't even know if it would work.

But he was angry, and angry men did stupid things.

"I can't cultivate," he said, popping the pill into his mouth. "But I've never needed to."

The golden veins in the pill dissolved, and something ancient woke inside him. Not qi. Not power. Something older. Something that had been sleeping in his blood since the day he was born.

His meridians, the ones the elders had declared crippled and worthless, began to hum. Not with qi—with something that predated qi. The energy of creation itself, the raw stuff that the first cultivators had used to shape the world.

The scarred woman's eyes went wide. "That's—that's not possible. You're a cripple. You have no—"

"I have something better," Shen He said, and the world shattered.

The compound's walls crumbled. The buildings collapsed. The guards fled, screaming, their cultivation bases flickering like candles in a hurricane. The scarred woman tried to run, but the energy caught her, lifted her, held her suspended in the air.

Shen He looked at her, and his eyes were no longer human. They were pools of gold, burning with the light of a thousand suns.

"Tell the Consortium," he said, his voice echoing with power, "that I'm just getting started."

He released her, and she fell to the ground, gasping. He turned and walked out of the ruined compound, his body still smoking, his heart still pounding.

The pill's effects were fading. He could feel his meridines cracking, the strain of containing that ancient energy tearing them apart. But he'd survived. Against all odds, he'd survived.

Three days later, the news reached him in a small tavern on the border of the lawless lands.

The Black Lotus Consortium had put a bounty on his head. Ten million spirit stones. Dead or alive.

But that wasn't the worst part.

The worst part was the message that came with it, delivered by a trembling messenger who'd tracked him down through the wilderness: "The Consortium knows about your sister."

Shen He's hand froze halfway to his cup.

"Your sister, Lin Xue," the messenger continued, sweating profusely. "The Azure Cloud Sect's prodigy. They have her. They're holding her in the Consortium's main fortress, deep in the Dead Marsh. They say if you want her back, you'll come alone. No pills. No tricks. Just you."

The cup shattered in Shen He's grip. Tea spilled across the table, mixing with the blood from his cut hand.

"When?" he asked, his voice barely a whisper.

"The message arrived yesterday. They gave you one week."

Shen He stood up, his chair scraping against the floor. The tavern's other patrons looked at him, then quickly looked away. They could feel the killing intent radiating from his body, a cold fury that made the air itself tremble.

He walked out into the night, the messenger's words echoing in his mind.

Lin Xue. His sister. The prodigy who'd testified against him. The woman who'd called for his exile.

They had her.

And despite everything, despite the betrayal and the pain and the years of watching her rise while he fell, she was still his sister.

He reached into his pouch and pulled out the last pill he'd made. The one he'd been saving for a rainy day.

The rain had come.

He swallowed the pill, and the world turned to fire.

*To be continued...*

title: The Blood Phoenix's Invitation
content:
The mortal world was not what Shen He had expected.

For three weeks, he had traveled south from the Azure Cloud Sect, following the winding roads through villages and towns that seemed to exist in a different reality entirely. Here, people did not cultivate qi. They did not meditate for hours on end or debate the finer points of meridians. They farmed. They traded. They lived and died without ever knowing the weight of a spirit stone in their palm.

And yet, Shen He found himself envying them.

He sat at a wooden table in a roadside inn called The Laughing Boar, staring at a bowl of rice porridge that cost three copper coins. Three copper coins. In the sect, he could have spent a hundred spirit stones on a single medicinal herb without blinking. Now, he counted every coin like a miser guarding his last treasure.

The innkeeper, a fat man with a stained apron, waddled over and dropped a small jar on the table. "Pickled radish. On the house."

Shen He looked up, surprised. "I didn't order this."

"Figured you could use it." The innkeeper jerked his chin toward Shen He's bag. "Ain't seen a cultivator come through here in months. You're either running from something or running to something. Either way, you look like you haven't had a good meal in days."

Shen He's hand moved instinctively to his bag. "How did you know I'm a cultivator?"

"Your eyes. They got that look. Like you're seeing something the rest of us can't." The innkeeper shrugged. "Plus, you got burns on your hands that should've scarred. They're healing clean. That's not normal."

Shen He looked at his palms. The innkeeper was right. The burns from the explosion had faded to faint pink patches, already smoothing over. His body was still absorbing the remnants of the failed pill's energy, slowly repairing the damage even as it tried to figure out what to do with the unstable compound still circulating in his blood.

"Smart man," Shen He said, offering a tired smile. "I'll take the radish."

"Good. Eat up. You got a visitor."

Shen He's spoon froze halfway to his mouth. "What?"

"Lady in red. Came in about an hour ago, asked if I'd seen a young man with cultivator eyes traveling south. I said no. She's waiting outside." The innkeeper's voice dropped. "Didn't want to cause trouble inside my establishment. But she's been circling around back, checking the stables. Thought you should know."

Shen He's mind raced. Lin Xue? No, she would have just walked in and demanded answers. The sect wouldn't send anyone after him—they'd made it clear he was dead to them. Which meant someone else was hunting him.

"How does she look?" Shen He asked.

"Beautiful. Dangerous. Carries a sword that hums."

That narrowed it down to about a hundred cultivators. "Thank you. I'll be leaving through the kitchen."

"Figured you might." The innkeeper slid a small wrapped bundle across the table. "Dried meat and bread. For the road."

Shen He stared at the bundle, then at the innkeeper. "Why are you helping me?"

"Because I got a daughter your age. She married a cultivator. Haven't seen her in seven years." The innkeeper's face was unreadable. "If she's ever in trouble, I hope someone helps her too."

Shen He didn't have words. He just nodded, grabbed the bundle, and slipped through the kitchen door.

---

The back alley of The Laughing Boar smelled of rotting vegetables and stale beer. Shen He pressed himself against the wall, listening. Footsteps. Soft. Deliberate. Someone was walking along the roof.

He moved.

Three years of scrubbing floors and dodging sect elders had made him good at staying quiet. He slid through the shadows, keeping low, his bag pressed tight against his chest. The alley opened into a narrow street that led to the eastern gate of the town. If he could reach the forest beyond—

"Going somewhere?"

The voice came from above. Shen He looked up.

She sat on the roof of a herbalist's shop, legs crossed, sword resting across her lap. She was young—maybe nineteen—with hair as red as autumn leaves and eyes that glowed faintly gold. Her robes were the color of fresh blood, embroidered with silver phoenixes that seemed to move in the corner of his vision.

She was not from the Azure Cloud Sect.

"I'm just passing through," Shen He said, keeping his voice calm. "I don't want any trouble."

"Then you should have picked a different road." She hopped down, landing without a sound. "Shen He. Former outer disciple of the Azure Cloud Sect. Expelled for theft and attempted refinement of a forbidden pill. Current status: wanted."

"Wanted by who?"

"By everyone who matters." She smiled, and it was not a kind smile. "Do you have any idea what you've done? The Meridian Restoration Pill is a legend. Every major sect has tried to replicate it. Every major sect has failed. And then a crippled nobody with no cultivation base somehow manages to create a stable version—"

"It wasn't stable."

"It didn't explode until you triggered the fire array. That's more stable than anyone else has managed in three hundred years." She took a step closer, and Shen He instinctively stepped back. "I'm not here to hurt you. I'm here to offer you a job."

"I'm not interested."

"You haven't heard the offer yet."

"I don't care. I've had enough of sects and politics and people who want to use me for what I can do." Shen He's voice hardened. "I'm done being a tool."

The woman's smile faded. For a moment, something flickered in her eyes—respect, maybe, or recognition. Then it was gone.

"My name is Yan Mei. I represent the Blood Phoenix Pavilion. We're not a sect. We're not a guild. We're an organization that values results over rules, ability over birthright." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a jade token, carved in the shape of a phoenix with ruby eyes. "This is a standing invitation. Show it at any Blood Phoenix safe house, and you'll be given resources, protection, and access to our library."

"Why would you do that?"

"Because we want to see what you become." Yan Mei tossed the token, and Shen He caught it reflexively. "The Azure Cloud Sect was a cage. We're offering you a laboratory. Think about it."

She turned and walked away, her red robes swirling in the evening breeze.

Shen He stared at the token in his hand. It was warm to the touch, pulsing faintly like a heartbeat. He had a thousand questions, a thousand reasons to throw it away.

But he didn't.

He tucked it into his pocket and continued walking toward the eastern gate.

---

Three days later, Shen He found himself in a city called Blackstone.

It was a merchant hub, sprawling and chaotic, filled with the sounds of haggling and the smell of exotic spices. He had hoped to lose himself in the crowds, find work as an apothecary's assistant, and slowly rebuild his life.

Instead, he found a notice board outside the city's central square.

His face was on it.

WANTED: SHEN HE
ALIVE. REWARD: 10,000 SPIRIT STONES.
DEAD. REWARD: 5,000 SPIRIT STONES.
INFORMATION LEADING TO CAPTURE: 1,000 SPIRIT STONES.

Below the notice, someone had scrawled in red ink: BRING HIM TO THE CRIMSON TOWER. NO QUESTIONS ASKED.

Shen He's blood ran cold. The Crimson Tower. He'd heard of it in the sect's library—a fortress in the eastern wastelands where rogue cultivators went to disappear. It was a place of lawlessness, of blood debts and vendettas.

And someone wanted him delivered there.

He tore the notice down and shoved it into his bag, drawing suspicious looks from the passersby. He needed to move. He needed to find a place to hide, to think, to figure out who was hunting him and why.

But as he turned to leave, a hand grabbed his wrist.

"Got you."

Shen He spun, his free hand already reaching for the dagger at his belt. But the person holding him was not a cultivator. It was a girl, no older than fourteen, with dirt on her face and desperate eyes.

"Please," she whispered. "You're the alchemist, right? The one who can fix broken meridians?"

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Liar." Her grip tightened. "My brother. He was a cultivator. He got poisoned in a dungeon raid. His meridians are rotting. The healers say he has a month. Please." Her voice cracked. "I don't have money. I don't have anything. But I'll do anything. I'll be your servant for life. Just please, help him."

Shen He looked at her. He saw the desperation, the hope, the fear of losing someone she loved.

He saw himself, three weeks ago, standing in the sect master's hall, hoping for mercy.

"I can't," he said, and the words tasted like ash. "I don't know what I'm doing. The last time I tried, I nearly killed myself."

"But you tried." The girl's eyes burned. "You're the only one who's ever tried."

Shen He was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page of notes from the failed pill.

"Show me your brother."

---

The girl's name was Lian. Her brother, Zhao, was a low-tier cultivator who had scraped together enough spirit stones to join a dungeon expedition, hoping to find a treasure that would change their lives.

Instead, he'd found a venomous centipede the size of a horse.

Shen He stood in their cramped apartment, staring at the young man lying on a straw mat. Zhao's skin was pale, almost gray, and black veins crawled up his arms like living vines. His breathing was shallow. His cultivation base had collapsed, and his meridians were dissolving from the inside.

"He's dying," Shen He said.

"I know." Lian's voice was barely a whisper. "Can you save him?"

Shen He looked at his notes. The Meridian Restoration Pill was supposed to rebuild damaged meridians. It had nearly killed him. But the theory was sound. The problem was stability. If he could find a way to stabilize the compound, to slow down the reaction—

"I need ingredients," he said. "Spirit herbs. A cauldron. A quiet place to work."

"I can get you a cauldron," Lian said. "The rest... I don't know."

Shen He reached into his pocket and pulled out the jade phoenix token. It pulsed warmly against his palm, a reminder of Yan Mei's offer.

The Blood Phoenix Pavilion. An organization that wanted to see what he could become.

Maybe it was time to find out.

"Shen He's eyes hardened. "I know where to go."

---

The Blood Phoenix safe house was hidden beneath a tea shop in the city's western district. Shen He showed the token to an old woman behind the counter, who looked at it, looked at him, and then nodded once.

"Follow me."

She led him down a narrow staircase into a basement that opened into a cavern. The walls were lined with shelves of herbs and scrolls. In the center stood a black ca

title: The Heartwood's Secret
content:
The Forest of Ten Thousand Echoes was not a place for the living.

Shen He had learned this in the first three days. The trees whispered. The ground shifted. And the beasts that prowled between the ancient trunks were not mere animals—they were guardians, each one a cultivator who had failed their tribulation and been consumed by the forest's hunger.

He pressed forward, his robes torn, his body battered. The pill furnace hung at his waist, still warm from the last batch he'd refined—a simple Qi Recovery Pill that had kept him alive through the second night.

But he was running out of ingredients.

And running out of time.

The Alchemy Guild had sent hunters. He'd spotted their tracks two days ago—three men, all at the sixth level of Qi Condensation, moving in formation. They were professionals. They knew how to track a wounded beast.

They didn't know they were hunting a pharmacist.

Shen He ducked beneath a curtain of hanging moss, his bare feet silent on the forest floor. The moss was wrong—it pulsed with a faint green light, and when he touched it, his fingers tingled with residual qi. This entire forest was saturated with spiritual energy, so dense it felt like wading through water.

Ahead, the trees parted to reveal a clearing.

And in the center of that clearing stood a tree unlike any he had ever seen.

It was massive—a hundred meters tall, its trunk wider than the Azure Cloud Sect's main hall. Its bark was silver, shimmering like liquid metal in the dappled light. And its leaves... its leaves were golden, each one hanging heavy with what looked like drops of liquid light.

Shen He's breath caught in his throat.

The Heartwood of the Eternal Tree.

It was a legend. A myth. Every alchemy text mentioned it, but none had ever claimed to find it. The Heartwood was said to contain the essence of creation itself—the primordial qi that had shaped the world. A single drop of its sap could refine a pill that would elevate a mortal to the Nascent Soul realm.

And it was right in front of him.

Shen He took a step forward.

The ground shook.

A roar split the air, so deep and powerful that Shen He felt it in his bones. The trees around the clearing bent backward, their branches snapping as something massive stirred in the shadows.

The beast that emerged was like nothing he had ever seen.

It had the body of a lion, the scales of a dragon, and three heads—each one different. One was a wolf's, one a serpent's, and one a human face, twisted and ancient, its eyes burning with intelligence.

"A Chimera," Shen He whispered.

The human head laughed. It was a horrible sound, like grinding stone and breaking glass.

"A cultivator," the head said. "How delicious. It has been so long since I tasted cultivator flesh."

Shen He's hand moved to his storage ring. He had twelve pills left. Three were offensive—Explosive Flame Pills that could level a house. The rest were healing and qi recovery.

Against a Chimera, they were nothing.

"You know what I am," the beast said, its wolf head sniffing the air. "Then you know you cannot run. Cannot fight. Cannot hide." The serpent head hissed, its forked tongue tasting the air. "I will give you a choice, little alchemist. Surrender your knowledge, and I will make your death quick. Resist, and I will consume your soul slowly—over days, weeks, years."

Shen He's mind raced. He needed time. Time to think. Time to find an angle.

"You're bound to this tree," he said.

The Chimera's human head blinked. "Clever. Yes. The Eternal Tree is my prison. I am its guardian, and it is my cage." The wolf head snarled. "But you are not the first to find this place. Do you know what happened to the others?"

"I don't care."

The serpent head laughed. "Then you are a fool."

"Maybe." Shen He reached into his storage ring and pulled out a single pill—the last one he'd refined before escaping the sect. It was imperfect. Unstable. A failed experiment he'd never had time to discard.

But it was his only chance.

"You see this?" he said, holding it up. "This is a Pill of Absolute Equilibrium. It forces the body's qi into perfect balance—or destroys it trying."

The Chimera's heads all stared at the pill. The human head's eyes narrowed. "You lie. Such a pill does not exist."

"Then I suppose you have nothing to worry about." Shen He tossed the pill toward the tree. It arced through the air, glinting in the golden light.

The Chimera moved faster than anything its size had a right to. All three heads lunged, jaws snapping, but Shen He had already thrown another pill—a Cloud Mist Pill that exploded into a dense fog of white smoke.

He ran.

Behind him, the Chimera roared. The sound was followed by an explosion—the Pill of Absolute Equilibrium detonating against the tree's trunk. The ground trembled. Light flashed. And then...

Silence.

Shen He didn't stop running. He crashed through the undergrowth, ignoring the branches that cut his face, the thorns that tore his clothes. He ran until his lungs burned and his legs screamed.

He burst out of the forest and into a clearing that was not the one he'd left.

This one was smaller. A circle of ancient stones surrounded a pool of crystal-clear water. And in the center of that pool, floating on the surface, was a woman.

She was beautiful in a way that hurt to look at—pale skin, hair like spun moonlight, eyes that held the depth of the ocean. She wore nothing, but the water clung to her body in a way that felt more concealing than any robe.

When she spoke, her voice was music given form.

"You have seen the Heartwood."

Shen He's hand went to his storage ring again. "Who are you?"

"A friend." She smiled, and it was like watching dawn break. "Or an enemy. That depends entirely on you, Shen He."

He froze. "You know my name."

"I know many things." She rose from the water, stepping onto the shore as if the laws of gravity did not apply to her. Water dripped from her skin, and where it fell, flowers bloomed. "I know you were expelled from the Azure Cloud Sect. I know the Alchemy Guild hunts you. I know you carry the knowledge of the Meridian Restoration Pill in your head, even though you failed to refine it." She stopped before him, close enough that he could smell her—like jasmine and rain. "And I know you are not meridian-crippled."

Shen He's blood turned to ice. "What?"

"You heard me." She reached out and touched his chest, right where his dantian should have been. "Your meridians are not broken. They are... different. A configuration that has not existed in this world for ten thousand years."

"Impossible. The sect elders tested me. The guild masters tested me. I have no cultivation potential."

"They were wrong." Her fingers pressed harder, and Shen He felt something stir inside him—a warmth that spread from her touch, flowing through channels he had never known existed. "You have been cultivating this entire time, Shen He. But not through your dantian. Through your blood. Your bones. Your very cells."

He wanted to pull away. He wanted to deny it. But the warmth felt too good, too right, like a part of him waking up after a lifetime of sleep.

"What are you talking about?"

"The ancient cultivators did not cultivate qi in the dantian," she said. "They cultivated their entire bodies. Every cell was a vessel. Every breath was a technique. They were not warriors—they were alchemists of the self." She smiled again, and this time there was something sharp in it. "You are the last of them, Shen He. The last Primordial Alchemist."

The word hit him like a physical blow. "That's impossible. The Primordial Alchemists are a myth. They never existed."

"They existed." She stepped back, and the warmth faded, leaving him cold. "And they were destroyed. Hunted to extinction by the same powers that now hunt you." Her eyes met his. "Do you know why the Alchemy Guild wants you, Shen He? It is not for your knowledge. It is not for the Meridian Restoration Pill. It is because you are a living relic. A key to a power that should have been lost forever."

"Who are you?" he demanded. "Why are you telling me this?"

"My name is Yun Mei." She bowed, and her hair fell forward, brushing the ground. "And I am the last guardian of the Primordial Path. I have waited ten thousand years for the return of one who could walk it." She straightened, and her eyes were ancient, old as the stars. "You are that one, Shen He. But before you can walk the path, you must learn its first lesson."

"And what's that?"

She smiled, and there was no warmth in it. "Trust is the death of alchemists."

The world spun.

Shen He blinked, and the clearing was empty. The pool was gone. The stones were gone. He was standing in the middle of the Forest of Ten Thousand Echoes, alone, the Chimera's roar echoing in the distance.

Had it been a dream?

He looked down at his chest. There, tattooed into his skin in lines of silver fire, was a pattern he had never seen before—a complex array of circles and lines, pulsing with a rhythm that matched his heartbeat.

Beneath it, written in a script he could somehow read, were the words:

*The Pill of Nine Transformations.*
*First Refinement: Consume the Heartwood's Sap.*
*Time Remaining: Twenty-Nine Days.*

Shen He stared at the message, his mind reeling.

Twenty-nine days to find the Heartwood again.

Twenty-nine days to refine a pill he'd never attempted.

Twenty-nine days before the array on his chest killed him.

Far away, in the Alchemy Guild's central tower, a bell tolled.

Grandmaster Feng Wei looked up from his scrolls, his ancient eyes narrowing. The bell had not rung in three hundred years—not since the last Primordial Alchemist had been hunted down and executed.

"Master?" his apprentice asked. "What does it mean?"

Feng Wei rose from his seat, his joints cracking with age and power. "It means," he said slowly, "that someone has activated the Primordial Seal. The last one." He walked to the window and looked out over the city, his gaze fixed on the distant mountains. "The Undying Pharmacist has returned."

His apprentice paled. "But that's impossible. They were all killed. The bloodline was ended."

"Apparently not." Feng Wei turned, and his eyes burned with a cold, ancient fire. "Send word to the Twelve Pillars. The hunt is no longer about capturing a thief. It is about preventing a catastrophe." He paused. "And send an invitation to the Azure Cloud Sect. Tell them their former disciple has become far more dangerous than they could ever imagine."

"What should I tell them?"

Feng Wei smiled, and there was no warmth in it, no mercy.

"Tell them the boy is no longer a cultivator. He is a plague. And plagues must be eradicated before they consume the world."

The bell tolled again, and somewhere in the Forest of Ten Thousand Echoes, Shen He felt the sound resonate in his bones.

He didn't know what it meant.

But he knew, with absolute certainty, that

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Debt Collectors Arrive

The door to the Crimson Lotus Sect's guest quarters splintered inward, and Shen He was already moving before the first fragment hit the floor.

He'd learned to read danger in the spaces between heartbeats—the subtle shift of qi in the air, the almost imperceptible change in pressure before an attack. Three years on the run had sharpened instincts he never knew he possessed.

His hand closed around a porcelain vial on the bedside table as he rolled sideways, coming up behind the wooden partition that served as a flimsy barrier between him and death.

"Found him," a voice growled. Male. Mid-level Core Formation. The qi signature felt wrong—tainted, like stagnant water mixed with blood. "Third floor, east wing."

Shen He's mind raced. The Crimson Lotus Sect was supposed to be neutral ground. A haven for alchemists seeking refuge from sect politics. He'd paid fifty spirit stones for a week's stay, and the clerk had assured him of absolute discretion.

So much for that.

He risked a glance around the partition. Three figures stood in the doorway, all wearing the same dark gray robes with crimson trim. No sect insignia. That was deliberate—mercenaries who didn't want to be identified.

The leader was a hulking man with a scar that split his face from forehead to jaw, the wound puckered and white despite what must have been years of healing. He held a curved blade that dripped with something black and viscous.

"He's in the back," the scarred man said. "Flush him out."

The other two moved forward, hands glowing with gathering qi. Shen He recognized the technique—a containment formation designed to immobilize anyone within a ten-meter radius. He had three seconds before it activated.

He uncorked the vial and drank.

The liquid burned going down, a mixture of crushed thunder lotus petals, concentrated viper venom, and something that might have been powdered monster bone. The taste was indescribable—like lightning and death mixed with bile.

The effect was instant.

Qi flooded his meridians with violent force, stretching channels that had never been meant to hold power. Pain lanced through his body, every nerve ending screaming as the pill's energy coursed through him. But with the pain came strength.

He burst from behind the partition.

The scarred man's eyes widened as Shen He materialized in front of him, movements too fast for a mere mortal to track. Shen He's palm struck the man's chest, and a thunderclap echoed through the room as the stored qi discharged.

The scarred man flew backward, crashing through the doorway and into the hallway beyond. He hit the opposite wall with a sickening crunch and slid to the floor, unconscious.

The other two mercenaries spun to face him, but they were too slow. Shen He had already consumed a second pill—a small purple pellet that dissolved on his tongue and sent lightning racing along his limbs.

He moved like a storm given flesh.

The first mercenary tried to raise a barrier, but Shen He was already past it, his fist connecting with the man's solar plexus. The barrier shattered. The man folded.

The second mercenary managed to get a blade halfway to Shen He's throat before Shen He caught his wrist and twisted. Bone snapped. The blade clattered to the floor. A knee to the gut, an elbow to the temple, and the third man joined his companions in unconsciousness.

Shen He stood in the wreckage of the room, breathing hard. The pill's effects were already fading, leaving behind a bone-deep exhaustion that threatened to drag him to his knees.

He didn't have time to rest.

Footsteps echoed in the hallway. More of them. A lot more.

He grabbed his bag from beside the shattered bed and vaulted through the window, shattering the glass with his shoulder. The fall was three stories, but he'd prepared for this—a small paper talisman activated as he fell, slowing his descent just enough for him to land without breaking his legs.

The courtyard was empty. For now.

He ran.

---

Two hours later, Shen He sat in a cramped teahouse in the city's seediest district, nursing a cup of lukewarm tea he had no intention of drinking. The establishment stank of cheap wine and desperation, the kind of place where questions were expensive and answers were cheap.

He'd changed clothes three times since escaping the inn. Taken seven different routes through the city's winding back alleys. Left false trails that would take even a skilled tracker hours to unravel.

It wouldn't be enough.

He knew who'd sent the mercenaries. Had known it was only a matter of time before they found him. The Sword Sect had long arms and deeper pockets, and they hadn't forgotten the cripple who'd slipped through their fingers three years ago.

But the Sword Sect wasn't his only problem.

A shadow fell across his table.

Shen He didn't look up. He'd sensed the newcomer's approach before they'd entered the teahouse—a qi signature that burned like white fire, refined and deadly.

"Mind if I sit?" The voice was female, cultured, with an edge of amusement that set Shen He's teeth on edge.

"It's a free city." He still didn't look up.

The chair scraped across the floor as she sat down across from him. "You've gotten better. Three years ago, I could have walked right up to you without you noticing. Now you knew I was coming two blocks away."

He finally raised his eyes.

Lin Xue looked exactly as she had the day she'd helped destroy his life—beautiful, composed, utterly untouchable. She wore civilian clothes instead of sect robes, but the sword at her hip was unmistakable. The Azure Cloud Sect's signature weapon, forged from sky-iron and inlaid with cloud patterns that seemed to shift in the light.

"What do you want?" Shen He asked.

"To talk." She signaled the serving girl, who brought her a cup of tea without being asked. She'd clearly been here before. "Is that so hard to believe?"

"Everything you do has a purpose, Lin Xue. What's this one?"

She took a sip of tea, her eyes never leaving his face. "You've been busy. Three years on the run, and you've managed to develop a reputation. The Pill Fiend, they call you. A mortal who consumes pills like a cultivator, growing stronger with every dose. Some say you've found the secret to cultivation without natural talent."

"Rumors are cheap," Shen He said.

"Are they?" She set down her cup. "I've heard other rumors too. That you've been researching forbidden alchemy. That you're trying to recreate the Meridian Restoration Pill."

His heart skipped a beat, but he kept his face neutral. "That pill doesn't work. Your sect master told me so himself."

"Your sect master," Lin Xue corrected. "I was promoted to inner disciple the month after you left. I answer to a different authority now."

"Congratulations." The word came out bitter. "Is that why you're here? To gloat?"

"No." She leaned forward, and for a moment, the mask slipped. He saw something in her eyes that might have been regret. "I'm here to warn you."

"I don't need warnings from you."

"You do." She reached into her robe and pulled out a scroll, sliding it across the table. "Read it."

He didn't touch it. "What is it?"

"A bounty. Issued by the Alchemy Guild's inner council. They want you alive, Shen He. They want to know how a cripple can consume pills meant for cultivators without dying. They want to take you apart and study the pieces."

He'd known this was coming. The Guild had been hunting him for two years, ever since he'd accidentally revealed his ability in a market square. But a formal bounty meant they were done being subtle.

"How much?"

"Enough to make every mercenary between here and the coast drool. But that's not the worst part." She paused. "The Guild has offered a bonus for your capture. They want you delivered to their headquarters in the capital within the next month."

"Why the deadline?"

Lin Xue's expression hardened. "Because they know something you don't. The Gathering of Sects is in six weeks. Every major sect in the kingdom will be present, including the Azure Cloud Sect. The Guild wants to present you as a prize—proof of their superiority in alchemical research."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "They want to use me as a spectacle."

"They want to dissect you in front of the most powerful cultivators in the realm. Show everyone that the Guild's knowledge surpasses even the secrets of a freak who can consume pills without dying." She stood up. "I didn't come here to save you. I came to give you a chance to disappear before they find you."

"Why?" The question escaped before he could stop it. "Why help me?"

Lin Xue looked at him for a long moment. "Because I know what it's like to be trapped by a system that sees you only as a tool. And because..." She hesitated. "Because you didn't deserve what happened to you."

She turned and walked away, disappearing into the crowd outside.

Shen He sat alone at the table, staring at the scroll she'd left behind. He didn't need to read it. He already knew what it said.

The Guild was coming.

The Sword Sect was coming.

And now the Gathering of Sects loomed on the horizon, threatening to drag him into a spotlight he'd spent three years trying to escape.

He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page of notes he'd salvaged from the explosion three years ago. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula that was supposed to be impossible.

The sect master had said it didn't work. That it killed everyone who tried to use it.

But Shen He had been consuming impossible pills his entire life. Pills that should have killed a mortal. Pills that shattered the boundaries of what alchemy was supposed to achieve.

Maybe he was the one person in the world who could make it work.

The thought was insane. Suicidal. Exactly the kind of thinking that had gotten him exiled in the first place.

But as he sat in the dim light of the teahouse, feeling the weight of every faction in the kingdom pressing down on him, he realized he had nothing left to lose.

He was going to refine the Meridian Restoration Pill.

Even if it killed him.

---

The knock on his new room at the city's edge came at midnight.

Shen He was awake, seated in meditation posture with a half-formed pill in his palm. He'd been working on the formula for hours, trying to reconstruct what he'd lost in the explosion.

"Come in."

The door opened, and a figure stepped inside—short, hooded, carrying a wooden box that hummed with contained energy.

"I heard you were looking for ingredients," the figure said, voice muffled by the hood. "I have something that might interest you."

Shen He's hand drifted toward the vial at his belt. "I wasn't looking for anything."

"No, but you need it." The figure set the box on the floor and stepped back. "Open it."

Shen He didn't move. "Who sent you?"

"Someone who wants you to succeed. Someone who remembers what it was like to be called worthless." The figure pulled back their hood, revealing a young woman with sharp features and eyes that burned with quiet intensity. "My name is Mei Ling. I'm an alchemist, like you. And I have a proposal."

"I'm listening."

"I can get you the ingredients you need. The rare ones. The dangerous ones." She smiled, and there was something predatory in the expression. "In exchange, you're going to help me destroy the Alchemy Guild."

Shen He stared at her.

The box hum

title: The Road of Broken Bones
content:
The gates of the Azure Cloud Sect clanged shut behind Shen He, the sound reverberating through his bones like a funeral bell. He stood there for a long moment, staring at the weathered stone path that wound down the mountain. Mist curled around his ankles, cold and damp, as if the mountain itself was trying to drag him back.

He didn't move.

Somewhere behind him, he could hear the murmurs of the disciples who'd gathered to watch his exile. Their voices carried on the wind, sharp as knives.

"The cripple finally got what he deserved."

"I heard he tried to make a forbidden pill. Nearly killed himself."

"Good riddance. He was always a waste of space."

Shen He's hands curled into fists at his sides. The anger he'd felt in the judgment hall had cooled into something harder, denser—a core of resolve that burned in his chest like a slow-burning ember.

*They threw me out like garbage*, he thought. *After everything I did for this sect. After all those years of scrubbing floors and organizing pills and never complaining about the scraps they threw me.*

He took a step forward. Then another. The path was steep, lined with bamboo groves that rustled in the wind. He'd walked this road a thousand times, carrying supplies, running errands, fetching ingredients for the senior alchemists. He'd never thought he'd walk it as an exile.

Halfway down the mountain, he stopped.

A figure stood in the middle of the path, silhouetted against the gray sky. Lin Xue. She was wearing her usual white robes, her long black hair tied back in a simple ponytail. Her sword hung at her hip, gleaming faintly in the dim light.

Shen He's jaw tightened. "Come to gloat?"

"No." Lin Xue's voice was quieter than he'd ever heard it. "I came to give you this."

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small jade bottle. Tossed it to him. He caught it reflexively, staring at the faint green glow emanating from within.

"What is it?"

"Foundation Establishment Pill. High grade." Lin Xue's eyes met his, and for a moment, he saw something flicker in them—was that guilt? "You'll need it if you want to survive out there."

Shen He laughed, the sound hollow and bitter. "You're the one who exposed me. And now you're giving me pills?"

"I didn't expose you to be cruel." Lin Xue's voice hardened. "I exposed you because you were going to kill yourself with that pill. The Meridian Restoration formula is poison. I've seen what it does to people. I didn't want that to happen to you."

"So you had me exiled instead? How kind."

"Better alive and exiled than dead and honored." Lin Xue turned away, her robes swishing against the stone. "The world outside these mountains is dangerous, Shen He. But you're smarter than most people give you credit for. Use that. Don't let the sect's judgment be the last word on your life."

She walked back up the path without looking back.

Shen He stared at the jade bottle in his hands. The Foundation Establishment Pill inside was worth more spirit stones than he'd seen in his entire life. High-grade pills required rare ingredients and months of careful refinement. Even the sect's best alchemists only produced a handful each year.

*Why would she give me this?*

He didn't have time to dwell on it. The sun was setting, and he needed to reach the bottom of the mountain before nightfall. The outer forests were dangerous after dark—spirit beasts roamed the shadows, and rogue cultivators preyed on the weak.

He tucked the bottle into his bag and continued down the path.

---

Three hours later, Shen He reached the base of the mountain. The forest stretched before him, dense and dark, the trees towering overhead like ancient sentinels. The air was thick with qi—wild, untamed, nothing like the refined energy of the sect.

He found a small clearing near a stream and set up camp. He had no tent, no bedroll, nothing but the clothes on his back and the meager possessions in his bag. He gathered dry wood and started a fire, the flames casting dancing shadows across the trees.

Sitting by the fire, Shen He pulled out the crumpled page of notes he'd salvaged from the explosion. The paper was singed at the edges, the ink smudged in places, but the core formula was still legible.

The Meridian Restoration Pill.

He stared at the characters, tracing them with his finger. The sect master had said it was a dead end. That everyone who'd tried to replicate it had failed. That it killed the user instead of healing them.

But Shen He had felt something during the refinement. A resonance. A pattern in the way the ingredients had interacted. The explosion hadn't been a failure—it had been a reaction he hadn't anticipated. The pill formula was incomplete. It was missing something.

*What if I'm not supposed to restore my meridians?* he thought. *What if I'm supposed to replace them?*

The idea was insane. Meridians were the foundation of cultivation—the channels through which qi flowed. You couldn't just replace them. It was like saying you could replace someone's blood vessels or nerves.

But Shen He's body was different. He'd known that since the first time he'd absorbed qi through a pill. His body didn't follow the normal rules. His meridians were blocked, but his cells *drank* qi like a parched man drinking water.

*What if I don't need meridians at all?*

The thought sent a shiver down his spine. It was heresy. Everything he'd ever been taught about cultivation said that meridians were essential. But everything he'd ever been taught had also told him he was worthless.

He looked at the pill formula again, his mind racing. If he could modify it—replace the meridian-restoration components with something that enhanced the body's natural absorption—he might be able to create a pill that didn't just let him cultivate, but let him *consume* qi directly.

No breathing techniques. No meditation. No years of slow accumulation.

Just pills. Pure, concentrated qi, directly absorbed into his body.

His hands trembled as he folded the paper and tucked it back into his bag. He needed ingredients. He needed equipment. He needed a lab.

And he had nothing.

---

The next morning, Shen He woke to the sound of footsteps.

He snapped upright, his heart pounding. Three men stood at the edge of his clearing, their robes tattered and stained, their eyes gleaming with malice. Rogues. The kind of cultivators who preyed on the weak instead of joining a sect.

"Well, well," the largest one said, grinning. "What do we have here? A little sect disciple, all alone in the forest?"

Shen He's hand drifted toward his bag. "I have nothing worth taking."

"Don't lie to me, boy." The rogue stepped closer, and Shen He could feel the pressure of his cultivation—Qi Condensation, fifth level. Far stronger than Shen He's crippled body could handle. "I saw that jade bottle you were holding last night. That's Foundation Establishment grade. Hand it over, and maybe we'll let you live."

Shen He's mind raced. He couldn't fight. He couldn't run—they'd catch him in seconds. He had one option.

He reached into his bag and pulled out the jade bottle. The rogues' eyes lit up.

"That's it. Give it here."

Shen He uncorked the bottle and tipped the pill into his palm. It was small, perfectly round, glowing with a soft golden light. The Foundation Establishment Pill. Enough qi to power a cultivator for months.

He popped it into his mouth and swallowed.

"NO!" The rogue lunged forward, but it was too late.

The pill hit Shen He's stomach, and the world exploded.

Qi flooded through him like a river breaking through a dam, raw and violent, burning through his veins. His body convulsed, his muscles spasming as the energy sought channels that didn't exist. Pain—white-hot, blinding—screamed through every nerve.

But then something shifted.

His cells opened up, drinking in the qi like a sponge. The energy that would have killed a normal cultivator poured into him, saturating his flesh, his bones, his organs. He felt himself growing stronger, faster, more *alive*.

The rogues stared at him, their mouths hanging open.

"What the hell?"

Shen He's eyes snapped open. They were glowing gold.

"Run," he said, his voice echoing with power.

The rogues ran.

Shen He stood there, trembling, as the last of the Foundation Establishment Pill's energy settled into his body. He looked down at his hands. They were steady. Strong.

For the first time in his life, he felt qi flowing through him—not through meridians, but through every cell, every fiber of his being.

He laughed, the sound wild and free.

"I'm not crippled," he whispered. "I'm *evolved*."

And somewhere in the forest, a shadow watched him from the treetops, its eyes gleaming with interest.

title: The Poison's True Taste
content:
The night air bit at Shen He’s exposed skin as he trudged down the mountain path. Every step sent fresh jolts of pain through his body—the lingering aftermath of the explosion, the weight of exile, the hollow ache of betrayal. Behind him, the Azure Cloud Sect’s gates had already faded into the darkness, swallowed by mist and memory.

He’d walked for three hours before his legs gave out. He collapsed against a moss-covered boulder, his breath coming in ragged gasps. The spirit stones in his pocket—a pitiful twenty-three—wouldn’t last him a week in the nearest town. The single page of salvaged notes was worthless without the rest of the formula.

“What now?” he muttered to himself, staring up at the stars. They seemed colder here, farther away. “What the hell do I do now?”

The answer came not as a revelation, but as a sharp, stabbing pain in his chest.

Shen He doubled over, clutching his ribs. The pain wasn’t from the explosion—it was deeper, more insidious. It crawled through his veins like liquid fire, settling in his meridians with the weight of molten lead.

He knew this feeling.

His hands trembled as he tore open his shirt, revealing the faint, black lines that had begun to spiderweb across his chest. The poison. The incomplete Meridian Restoration Pill had done its damage after all. The explosion hadn’t been the true failure—it had merely been the warning.

“No,” he whispered. “No, no, no—”

He forced himself to focus, to push his remaining qi through his damaged channels. What he found made his blood run cold.

The poison wasn’t destroying his meridians. It was *changing* them.

Pockets of corrupted energy had formed along his energy pathways, twisting them into something foreign. Where once his qi had flowed like water through a river, now it crawled through jagged, misshapen channels that felt *wrong*—like someone had taken a knife to the original design and carved new paths through the flesh.

And in those corrupted pockets, something was growing.

Shen He pressed his palm against his chest, feeling the faint pulse of heat beneath his skin. It wasn’t his heartbeat. It was something else. Something alive.

“Think,” he said, forcing calm into his voice. “You’re an alchemist. This is just another formula. Another problem to solve.”

But even as he said it, he knew the truth. He’d never encountered a poison like this. The ancient alchemists who’d created the Meridian Restoration Pill had intended it to heal—but something had gone wrong in their refinement. The pill had become a tumor, a parasite that latched onto the user’s own energy and began to grow.

And now it was growing in him.

The pain subsided, but the fear remained. Shen He pulled his shirt closed and forced himself to stand. He couldn’t stay here. He needed shelter, supplies, and—most importantly—answers.

---

The town of Clearwater was a pit.

Shen He had passed through it once before, during a sect errand, and remembered it as a sleepy trading post. Now, as he limped through its muddy streets at dawn, he saw it for what it truly was: a collection of broken buildings and broken people, held together by desperation and the occasional copper coin.

The innkeeper took one look at his torn robes and hollow eyes and demanded payment upfront. Shen He handed over five spirit stones—nearly a quarter of his savings—and received a room that smelled of mold and regret.
