He collapsed onto the straw mattress and stared at the ceiling, his mind racing.

The poison was spreading. He could feel it, inch by inch, as if something was tunneling through his insides. He had maybe a week before it reached his core. After that—

“Don’t think about after,” he told himself. “Focus on now.”

He pulled out the crumpled page, smoothing it against the mattress. The notes were incomplete, fragmented—a half-remembered transcription of a formula he’d only glimpsed once. But as he studied the characters, something caught his eye.

A single line, written in the margin in a hand that wasn’t his own:

*“The pill is born of heaven’s will. It devours the weak and empowers the strong. But devour it first, and you become the hunter, not the prey.”*

Shen He read the line three times, his heart pounding. The handwriting was different from the original text—smaller, more precise. Someone had added this note after the formula was locked away. Someone who had studied the pill and survived.

He traced the characters with his finger, memorizing every stroke. *Devour it first.* What did that mean? The pill had already been consumed. It was already inside him, spreading through his system like wildfire.

Unless—

His eyes widened.

Unless the pill wasn’t meant to be *refined* in a cauldron. Unless it was meant to be refined *inside the user’s body.*

The thought was insane. Alchemy was about control, precision, the careful manipulation of external forces. But the ancient alchemists had been pursuing something beyond conventional wisdom. They’d been trying to rewrite the laws of heaven.

What if the Meridian Restoration Pill wasn’t a pill at all? What if it was a *seed*?

Shen He sat up, his exhaustion forgotten. If the pill was a seed, then the poison spreading through him wasn’t corruption—it was *transformation*. The darkness in his meridians wasn’t destroying them; it was rebuilding them into something new.

But that transformation required energy. It required fuel.

And the only fuel available was *him*.

He pressed his hand to his chest again, feeling the heat pulse beneath his skin. The corruption was still there, still growing. But now he saw it differently—not as a disease, but as a furnace waiting to be stoked.

“If I feed it,” he whispered, “if I give it enough qi, enough energy—it might complete the process before it kills me.”

It was a gamble. A stupid, insane gamble that would likely end with his body melting from the inside out.

But it was the only option he had.

---

He spent the next three days in his room, working in a feverish haze. The innkeeper brought him food twice a day, but Shen He barely touched it. He was too focused on the formula, on the fragments of knowledge he’d pieced together from his stolen notes and his own failing body.

He needed ingredients. Not the refined materials used in proper alchemy, but raw, primal components. The kind that could be found in the earth itself, unprocessed and untamed.

On the fourth day, he ventured into the town’s market.

It was a pathetic collection of stalls, staffed by farmers and peddlers who eyed him with suspicion. Shen He moved through them like a ghost, his senses attuned to the faint traces of qi that clung to certain plants and minerals.

He found what he needed in a back-alley shop run by a toothless old woman who smelled of rot and secrets.

“You’re buying poison,” she said, her voice a dry rasp. “You know that, boy?”

“I’m buying ingredients,” Shen He replied, placing his remaining spirit stones on the counter. “What I do with them is my concern.”

The old woman laughed, a sound like stones grinding together. “You’ve got the scent of death on you. Stronger than the herbs. You’re not long for this world.”

“Then sell me what I need and let me die in peace.”

She shrugged and pushed a bundle of dark, withered roots across the counter. “Nightshade. Gathered under the new moon. It’ll burn through your insides like acid.”

“Perfect.”

Shen He took the bundle and left, ignoring the old woman’s cackling laughter following him out the door.

Back in his room, he laid out the ingredients on the floor. Nightshade. Bone dust. Iron shavings. A single, blackened petal from a deathbloom—the most dangerous component he’d managed to acquire.

He’d never attempted anything like this. No alchemist in recorded history had ever tried to refine a pill *inside* their own body. The standard process was external, controlled, safe. What he was about to do was suicide.

But the poison was already inside him. The furnace was already lit.

All he had to do was throw himself into the fire.

---

Shen He crushed the nightshade root between his fingers, feeling the dark energy pulse against his skin. He mixed it with the bone dust, the iron shavings, the deathbloom petal—a crude, violent combination that would have made any proper alchemist weep.

Then he swallowed it.

The effect was immediate.

Fire exploded through his veins, so intense that his vision went white. He collapsed to the floor, his body arching off the ground as the raw ingredients clashed with the poison already in his system. It felt like being torn apart from the inside, every cell of his body screaming in agony.

But beneath the pain, he felt something else.

The corruption in his meridians—the transformation—responded to the new energy like a starving beast. It *fed*. The nightshade, the bone dust, the iron—all of it was consumed, pulled into the growing furnace at the center of his being.

And as the ingredients burned, the transformation accelerated.

Shen He’s meridians twisted and reformed, the black lines spreading across his chest like cracks in porcelain. The pain intensified, becoming a constant, screaming presence in his mind. But through the haze, he saw something extraordinary.

His qi, which had always been weak and limited, began to *grow*.

It was faint at first—a trickle where there had been nothing. But as the transformation continued, the trickle became a stream. The stream became a river. The river became a flood.

For the first time in his life, Shen He felt what it was like to have real power.

And then the door to his room exploded inward.

---

Lin Xue stood in the doorway, her sword drawn, her eyes burning with cold fury. Behind her, Elder Wu’s face twisted into a mask of triumphant malice.

“Did you think you could run?” Lin Xue said, her voice cutting through the room like a blade. “Did you think we wouldn’t find you?”

Shen He tried to stand, but his body wouldn’t obey. The transformation was still consuming him, still burning through his system. He was caught between two fires—the one inside him and the one standing in the doorway.

“The sect master was merciful,” Elder Wu said, stepping forward. “He gave you exile. But I knew you would try to use your stolen knowledge. I knew you were a threat.”

“You planted that note,” Shen He said, his voice barely a whisper. “In the formula. You *wanted* someone to find it. To try it.”

Elder Wu’s smile widened. “The Meridian Restoration Pill has claimed many victims over the centuries. Each one brings us closer to understanding its true purpose. You’ve simply become the latest—and most promising—experiment.”

Lin Xue’s sword rose, its tip pointing directly at Shen He’s heart. “The sect master has issued a new decree. The exile is revoked. In its place, you are to be *retrieved*. Alive, if possible. Dead, if necessary.”

Shen He looked at her—at the girl he’d admired from afar, the prodigy who had once been his only friend in the sect. And he saw nothing but cold, calculating indifference.

“You knew,” he said. “You knew what

title: The City of Broken Blades
content:
The mountain path twisted downward through mist and shadow, each step carrying Shen He further from everything he'd known. The Azure Cloud Sect's gates had closed behind him with a sound like a tomb sealing shut, and now the world stretched before him—vast, indifferent, and hungry.

He walked for three hours before his legs gave out.

The forest had grown thick around him, the mountain's lower slopes giving way to ancient trees whose branches interlocked overhead, blocking out the sun. Shen He slumped against a moss-covered boulder, his breath coming in ragged gasps. The Meridian Restoration Pill sat heavy in his dantian, a coiled serpent of potential that he didn't dare unleash.

*It kills the user.*

The sect master's words echoed in his skull. But that couldn't be right. Shen He had felt the pill's energy coursing through him, had sensed the way it *wanted* to work, like a key searching for a lock. The problem wasn't the formula—it was the refinement. He'd rushed it. Cut corners. Used inferior ingredients because that's all a crippled disciple could steal.

If he could just get the right materials—

A twig snapped.

Shen He's head snapped up, his heart hammering. Figures emerged from the shadows between the trees—three of them, dressed in the dark gray robes of the Azure Cloud Sect's outer disciples. Their leader, a broad-shouldered youth with a scar across his jaw, smiled without warmth.

"Well, well. The cripple actually made it this far."

Shen He recognized him. Zhang Hu, an outer disciple who'd made Shen He's life miserable for years. The kind of bully who found joy in crushing those beneath him.

"Zhang Hu," Shen He said, keeping his voice flat. "The sect master gave me an hour. That hour passed. I'm no longer a disciple."

"Exactly." Zhang Hu cracked his knuckles. "Which means I don't have to follow sect rules when I deal with you."

The other two disciples spread out, flanking him. Shen He's mind raced. He had no weapons, no cultivation base to draw on, and his body was still recovering from the explosion. Against three trained disciples, he had exactly zero options.

Except one.

"You know about the pill," Shen He said.

Zhang Hu's eyes lit up with avarice. "Elder Wu told us. Said you might have remnants on you. Maybe some notes you managed to save." He took a step closer. "Hand them over, and we'll make it quick. Resist, and we'll make it *slow*."

Shen He's hand drifted to his bag. The crumpled page of notes was in there, along with his spirit stones and a few herbs he'd salvaged. But his fingers found something else—a small, jade vial he'd nearly forgotten.

The *failure*.

When he'd first attempted the Meridian Restoration Pill, he'd created a prototype. A flawed version that had congealed into a dark, oily liquid rather than forming a proper pill. He'd kept it out of curiosity, never expecting to use it.

But the liquid still contained power. Unstable, volatile, *dangerous* power.

"You want the notes?" Shen He said, slowly pulling out the vial. "Here. Take them."

He threw the vial at Zhang Hu's feet.

The scarred disciple's instincts were good. He leaped backward, his hand going to his sword. But Shen He had already reached into his bag and grabbed the handful of powdered fire-herb he'd been saving for experiments.

He threw it at the vial.

The powder ignited on contact, and the vial *exploded*.

A wave of dark, purple-tinged energy erupted outward, sending the three disciples flying. Trees splintered. The ground cracked. Shen He was thrown backward himself, his body slamming into a trunk with a crack that might have been his ribs.

But he was alive.

He forced himself to his feet, ignoring the screaming pain in his side. Zhang Hu lay groaning on the ground, his robes shredded, his skin covered in angry red burns. The other two were similarly incapacitated.

Shen He limped over to him.

"Tell Elder Wu," he said, his voice hoarse, "that if he wants my notes, he'll have to come get them himself."

He left them there, broken and bleeding, and disappeared into the forest.

---

Three days later, Shen He reached the city.

It rose from the plains like a scar on the earth—black stone walls, iron gates, and spires that pierced the gray sky. The sign above the main gate read *CITY OF BROKEN BLADES*, and below it, in smaller script: *All are welcome. None are safe.*

Shen He had heard of this place. It was one of the few independent cities in the region, not beholden to any sect or noble house. A haven for outcasts, exiles, and those who'd burned their bridges with the cultivation world.

In other words, exactly where a disgraced alchemist belonged.

The guards at the gate eyed him with practiced suspicion. One of them, a woman with a cybernetic eye that whirred softly as it focused on him, snorted.

"Another refugee from the sects?" she said. "You're the third one this week. What'd you do? Steal a technique manual? Sleep with an elder's wife?"

"Attempted forbidden alchemy," Shen He said.

The guard's human eye widened. The cybernetic one just kept whirring. "Seriously?"

"Seriously."

She laughed, a harsh sound that didn't reach her eyes. "Well, you'll fit right in. Welcome to the Broken Blades. Try not to die in the first week—it's bad for tourism."

She waved him through, and Shen He stepped into the city.

The streets were chaos. Merchants hawked their wares from rickety stalls, their voices competing with the clang of blacksmiths and the screams of arguments. Cultivators walked the streets openly, their auras flaring in casual displays of power. Shen He saw a man with three arms, a woman whose skin was covered in living flame, and a child whose eyes glowed with golden light.

This was a place where the normal rules didn't apply.

He found a cheap inn near the city's center—a crumbling building called the *Weeping Phoenix* that smelled of stale beer and desperation. The innkeeper, a wiry man with a missing ear and a patch over one eye, took Shen He's last spirit stone without comment and pointed him to a room on the third floor.

The room was small, barely large enough for a bed and a desk. But it had a lock on the door and a window that faced the street. It would do.

Shen He collapsed onto the bed, his body screaming in protest. The three-day journey had pushed him past his limits. He'd survived on wild herbs and stolen food, sleeping in trees and moving only at night. But he'd made it.

Now he needed to rebuild.

He pulled out the crumpled page of notes, smoothing it against the bed. The characters were smudged, the diagrams incomplete. But the core formula was there—the essential structure of the Meridian Restoration Pill.

*The ancient alchemists who created it were fools.*

The sect master's words again. But Shen He couldn't shake the feeling that the man was wrong. The pill *wanted* to work. He'd felt it in his dantian, a warm pulse of potential that responded to his qi in ways that shouldn't have been possible for someone with crippled meridians.

*Because my meridians aren't crippled.*

The thought struck him like a physical blow. He sat up, his mind racing. The sect's healers had declared him meridian-crippled when he was seven years old. They'd tested him with their best artifacts, confirmed by their most senior elders. For fifteen years, he'd accepted their verdict.

But what if they were wrong?

What if his meridians weren't destroyed, but simply... *blocked*?

The Meridian Restoration Pill was designed to restore broken channels. But if the channels weren't broken—if they were just clogged or sealed—then the pill might work differently. It might clear the blockage instead of destroying it.

He needed to test this theory. But to do that, he needed ingredients. Rare, expensive ingredients that a penniless exile couldn't afford.

He was still pondering this when a knock came at his door.

Shen He tensed, his hand reaching for the makeshift dagger he'd crafted from a broken bottle. "Who is it?"

"Room service," a voice said. Female. Young. With a hint of amusement.

"I didn't order anything."

"You didn't. But I did." A pause. "Open the door, Shen He. I come bearing gifts."

His blood ran cold. Whoever was on the other side of that door knew his name.

He crept to the door, his heart pounding. Through the crack, he could see a figure—slender, dressed in red, with long black hair that cascaded over her shoulders.

He opened the door.

The woman standing there was beautiful in a way that felt dangerous. Her eyes were the color of amber, sharp and knowing. Her lips curved in a smile that didn't quite reach her eyes. She held a small, wooden box in her hands.

"I'm Wei Ling," she said. "And I have a proposition for you."

"Who sent you?"

"No one. I sent myself." She stepped past him into the room, her movements fluid and graceful. "Word travels fast in the Broken Blades. A young alchemist, exiled from the Azure Cloud Sect, carrying the formula for the Meridian Restoration Pill." She set the box on the desk. "You're quite the celebrity."

Shen He's hand tightened on his dagger. "What do you want?"

"To help you." She opened the box, revealing rows of vials filled with glowing liquid. "I'm an information broker. I know things. For example, I know that the Azure Cloud Sect has put a bounty on your head. Five hundred spirit stones for your capture, alive."

His blood froze. "What?"

"The sect master is embarrassed. A cripple outsmarted him, stole his secrets, and escaped his justice." She picked up one of the vials, swirling the liquid. "He wants you back. Wants to make an example of you."

Shen He's mind raced. He'd thought exile was punishment enough. But the sect master was a proud man, and Shen He's escape had made him look weak.

"Why are you telling me this?" he asked.

"Because I want to offer you a deal." She set the vial down and met his eyes. "There's a man who lives in the city's underworld. He calls himself the Alchemist King. He's the one who originally discovered the Meridian Restoration Pill's secrets, centuries ago. He's been in hiding ever since."

Shen He's breath caught. "He's alive?"

"Alive, and very interested in meeting you." Wei Ling smiled. "I can take you to him. In exchange, you'll owe me a favor. One favor, to be called in at a time of my choosing."

His instincts screamed at him to refuse. This woman was trouble. Her offer was too convenient, too perfect.

But she was also his only lead.

"The Alchemist King," he said slowly. "He can help me perfect the pill?"

"He's the only one who can."

Shen He looked at the box of vials, at the glowing liquid within. Then he looked at his notes, at the formula he'd risked everything for.

"Fine," he said. "I accept."

Wei Ling's smile widened. "Excellent. We leave at midnight. Don

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Serpent's Smile

The mountain path wound downward through layers of mist, each step taking Shen He further from the only home he'd ever known. His sandals crunched on loose gravel, the sound unnaturally loud in the morning silence.

He'd walked for two hours before the first tear fell.

He wiped it away angrily, cursing his own weakness. What was there to cry about? The Azure Cloud Sect had never wanted him. They'd taken him in out of pity, kept him as a servant, and thrown him out the moment he became inconvenient.

But the ache in his chest refused to listen to reason.

The path curved around a massive outcropping of weathered stone, and Shen He stopped.

A figure stood in the middle of the road.

Lin Xue.

She wore traveling clothes instead of her usual disciple robes—a dark blue jacket fitted close to her body, practical pants tucked into leather boots, and her hair tied back in a severe ponytail. A sword hung at her hip, different from the ceremonial blade she carried in the sect. This one was plain, functional, its scabbard scarred from use.

"You're a long way from the sect," Shen He said, his voice flat. "Come to make sure I actually leave?"

"I came to give you this." She reached into her jacket and pulled out a folded piece of paper, holding it out to him.

Shen He didn't take it. "What is it?"

"The complete Meridian Restoration Pill formula. All seven pages."

His breath caught. "Why?"

"Because you were right." Lin Xue's face remained expressionless, but something flickered in her eyes. "The sect master lied to you. The pill doesn't kill the user. It works. It worked once, three hundred years ago, on a crippled disciple who went on to become one of the strongest cultivators in the Eastern Provinces."

"Then why did he say it was forbidden?"

"Because the formula is incomplete. The ancient alchemists who created it were geniuses, but they made a mistake—a flaw in the refinement process that causes the pill to become unstable after twenty-four hours. The disciple who took it survived, but he had to spend the rest of his life on a strict regimen of counter-poisons to keep the pill from killing him."

Shen He stared at the paper. "And you're giving this to me because...?"

"Because I want you to fix it." Lin Xue's voice dropped to a whisper. "There's a war coming, Shen He. Something bigger than the Azure Cloud Sect, bigger than the Alchemy Guild. The old powers are stirring, and when they do, everyone will need to choose a side. The sect master knows this. That's why he's been consolidating power, eliminating anyone who might be a liability."

"And I'm a liability."

"You're a variable he can't control. That's worse." She pressed the paper into his hand. "Take it. Go to the Black Lotus Market in the Southern Wastes. Find an alchemist named Old Meng. Tell him Lin Xue sent you, and that you want to learn the art of pill refinement."

"The Southern Wastes are lawless. Criminals and rogue cultivators."

"Exactly. No one will look for you there, and you'll have access to ingredients you could never find in the sect. The best alchemists in the world don't work for guilds or sects—they work for themselves, in places where the rules don't apply."

Shen He looked down at the paper in his hand. The characters were small, precise, written in a hand he recognized from the library's restricted section. "How do I know this isn't a trap?"

"You don't." Lin Xue turned and began walking back up the mountain. "But if I wanted you dead, I wouldn't have saved your notes from the explosion."

She disappeared into the mist before he could respond.

Shen He stood there for a long moment, the paper heavy in his hands. Then he unfolded it and began to read.

The formula was beautiful.

Seven pages of detailed instructions, each step building on the last, the alchemical theory more advanced than anything he'd ever encountered. The original creator had been a genius—no, something beyond genius. Someone who had looked at the fundamental laws of cultivation and found a way to cheat them.

But Lin Xue was right about the flaw. It was subtle, buried in the third refinement cycle, a mismatch in the spiritual resonance frequencies that would cause the pill to decay after exactly twenty-four hours. The resulting energy release wouldn't kill the user immediately, but it would slowly poison their core, forcing them to constantly consume counter-poisons to stay alive.

Shen He could see the solution.

It would require a secondary binding agent, something that could bridge the frequency gap without disrupting the pill's primary function. Spirit marrow root would work, but it was incredibly rare. Phoenix tears would be better, but they were worth more than Shen He would earn in a lifetime.

He needed the Black Lotus Market.

Three weeks later, Shen He understood why the Southern Wastes were called lawless.

The journey had been brutal—bandits on the road, wild beasts in the forests, and a constant, gnawing hunger that reminded him he no longer had the sect's meal halls to rely on. He'd spent his last spirit stone on a bowl of noodles in a roadside inn, watching the coin pouch grow lighter with each passing day.

But he'd made it.

The Black Lotus Market wasn't a city or a town. It was a sprawling encampment built in the shadow of a dead volcano, its streets a maze of tents, wooden shacks, and stone buildings that looked like they'd been assembled by people who didn't care about building codes. The air smelled of smoke, spices, and something metallic that Shen He couldn't identify.

He found Old Meng's shop at the edge of the market, tucked between a weapons dealer and a brothel. The sign above the door was faded, the characters for "Meng's Alchemical Solutions" barely legible.

Shen He pushed open the door.

The interior was chaos. Shelves lined every wall, packed with jars, vials, and containers of every size and shape. A large cauldron sat in the center of the room, bubbling with a viscous green liquid that gave off steam that smelled like rotting flowers. Behind a cluttered desk, an old man with a bald head and a long gray beard was reading a book, his lips moving silently.

"Old Meng?" Shen He said.

The old man looked up. His eyes were sharp, intelligent, and utterly devoid of warmth. "Who's asking?"

"Lin Xue sent me. She said you could teach me pill refinement."

Old Meng's expression didn't change, but his hand moved beneath the desk. Shen He heard the click of a mechanism being armed. "Lin Xue is dead. Has been for two years."

The words hit Shen He like a physical blow. "What?"

"She was killed during a raid on the Azure Cloud Sect's eastern vault. The sect claims it was rogue cultivators, but everyone knows it was the Alchemy Guild's assassins. She'd been feeding information to the underground for years." Old Meng's eyes narrowed. "So I'll ask again. Who sent you?"

Shen He's mind raced. The woman who'd given him the formula had been alive. She'd been standing in front of him, talking to him, pressing paper into his hands. "I saw her," he said. "Three weeks ago. She gave me this."

He pulled out the folded pages and held them up.

Old Meng's hand came out from under the desk, empty. His expression shifted from suspicion to something else—curiosity, mixed with caution. "Let me see that."

Shen He handed over the formula.

Old Meng read through it quickly, his eyes scanning the pages with practiced efficiency. When he finished, he let out a low whistle. "This is the real thing. The complete Meridian Restoration formula." He looked up at Shen He. "If Lin Xue gave you this, she must have trusted you. But Lin Xue is dead. So either you're lying, or..."

"Or what?"

"Or someone is wearing her face." Old Meng set the papers down carefully. "There are cultivators who specialize in illusion techniques. They can copy a person's appearance perfectly, mimic their voice, their mannerisms. The real Lin Xue would never have given this to anyone. She was protecting it, keeping it hidden until she could find someone worthy."

The words sent a chill down Shen He's spine. "Then who did I meet?"

"That's the question, isn't it?" Old Meng leaned back in his chair. "The person who gave you that formula wanted you to come here. They wanted you to find me. And they wanted me to see this." He tapped the papers. "Which means they're either setting us up, or they're trying to warn us about something."

A loud crash came from the back of the shop.

Old Meng was on his feet instantly, a dagger appearing in his hand from somewhere Shen He hadn't seen. "Stay here," he hissed.

He disappeared through a curtain of beads, and Shen He heard the sound of a door being kicked open, followed by a string of curses.

Then silence.

Shen He waited, his heart pounding. One minute passed. Two. He was about to go look when the bead curtain rattled and Old Meng emerged, his face pale.

"We have a problem," he said.

"What kind of problem?"

"The kind that just killed three Alchemy Guild agents in my back room." Old Meng held up a bloodstained dagger. "They were here for you, boy. They knew you were coming. Which means whoever gave you that formula also told them where to find you."

The front door burst open.

Shen He spun around, his hands already reaching for the pill pouch at his belt. A figure stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the market's lantern light.

It was Lin Xue.

Except it wasn't.

The eyes were wrong. The face was Lin Xue's, but the expression behind it was alien—a cold, predatory amusement that made Shen He's blood run cold.

"I'm disappointed," the figure said, and the voice was Lin Xue's voice, but layered beneath it was something else, something ancient and terrible. "I expected you to last longer than three weeks."

Old Meng stepped forward, his dagger raised. "Who are you?"

The figure smiled, and it was like watching a snake unhinge its jaw. "I'm the one who's been waiting for this formula to resurface for three hundred years."

It raised its hand, and the door slammed shut behind it.

title: The Demon's Bargain
content:
Three days. Three days since Shen He had stumbled down from Azure Cloud Mountain, penniless and branded a thief. Three days of sleeping in ditches, eating wild roots, and feeling the strange new energy pulsing through his crippled meridians.

The Meridian Restoration Pill had failed—or so they said. Yet here he was, alive, while the formula supposedly killed everyone who attempted it.

Something didn't add up.

Shen He crouched behind a moss-covered boulder, watching the dusty road that led to Greenpeak City. Merchants' caravans rolled past, guards eyeing the treeline with bored suspicion. His stomach growled. The last spirit stone in his pouch wouldn't buy more than a bowl of noodle soup.

"Thinking of robbing a carriage?"

Shen He nearly jumped out of his skin. He spun around, a defensive pill already pressed between his fingers.

A woman stood three feet away, and he hadn't heard her approach. She wore a tattered gray robe, her dark hair tied in a messy bun. A scar ran from her left temple to her jaw, pulling her lips into a permanent half-sneer. But her eyes—her eyes burned with a familiar light. Cultivation. And not the weak kind.

"Who are you?" Shen He demanded, his heart hammering.

"Someone who's been watching you since you left the sect." She tilted her head. "You're interesting. A cripple who practices alchemy. A thief who survived the forbidden pill. A dead man walking who doesn't know he's dead."

"Poetic. Do you have a point?"

The woman laughed, a harsh sound like grinding stones. "I'm Wei Qin. Former elder of the Black Lotus Sect. Current wanted fugitive. And your only way out of this mess."

Shen He's hand tightened around the pill. "I don't need help."

"No? You've got three days of provisions, zero cultivation base, and a bounty on your head that hasn't been posted yet but will be soon." Wei Qin stepped closer, and Shen He backed up until his spine hit the boulder. "The Azure Cloud Sect didn't just exile you. They sent word to every major sect in the region. You're marked, boy. No sect will take you. No city will shelter you. And the Alchemy Guild? They'll dissect you to figure out how you survived the forbidden formula."

"How do you know about the pill?"

"I have eyes inside the sect. I always have." Wei Qin's scarred face twisted into something like a smile. "The Black Lotus Sect fell ten years ago. The Azure Cloud Sect took our territory, our resources, and our research. Including the incomplete notes on the Meridian Restoration Pill."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "You helped create it?"

"I was the lead alchemist." Wei Qin's eyes grew distant. "We were close, boy. So close. But the formula had a flaw—a fatal one. The pill could restore meridians, yes. But it required the user's body to absorb qi at a rate that would tear them apart. We couldn't solve it. So the sect master locked it away."

"Then how am I alive?"

"I've been wondering the same thing." Wei Qin studied him with the intensity of a butcher appraising meat. "You should have died within the hour. Your meridians should have ruptured. But here you are, three days later, looking healthier than most outer disciples."

Shen He felt the weight of the crumpled page in his pocket—the only surviving fragment of his notes. He'd written down every observation from his failed refinement. The explosion. The strange warmth that had spread through his body. The way his crippled meridians had seemed to drink in the pill's energy instead of rejecting it.

"I don't know why I survived," he said honestly.

"No. But I can help you find out." Wei Qin extended her hand. "Join me. I have a lab hidden in the mountains. Resources. Knowledge. Together, we can perfect the formula."

"And if I refuse?"

Wei Qin's smile widened, and something dark flickered in her eyes. "Then I'll kill you right now and take your corpse apart piece by piece. Either way, I'm getting those answers."

Shen He's mind raced. He was weak. He was hunted. And this woman was offering him exactly what he needed—resources and knowledge. But she was also a former sect elder, a wanted criminal, and clearly insane.

"What's in it for you?" he asked.

"Revenge." The word came out sharp as a blade. "The Azure Cloud Sect destroyed everything I built. They took my research, my disciples, my reputation. I've spent ten years rebuilding in the shadows. But I need a breakthrough. You're that breakthrough."

"You want to use me."

"I want to collaborate." Wei Qin's hand remained extended. "You have something no one else has—a living body that survived the forbidden pill. I have everything else. Together, we remake the world."

Shen He thought about Lin Xue's cold eyes. Elder Wu's sneer. The sect master's dismissive words about cripples and charity.

*Fine. I'll go.*

He had said those words with defiance. But now, standing in the wilderness with nothing but a bag of clothes and a burning curiosity, he realized he had nowhere else to go.

"One condition," he said. "You teach me everything you know about alchemy. No secrets. No holding back."

Wei Qin's laugh was genuine this time, almost warm. "Boy, I've been alone for a decade. I'm dying to share. You have a deal."

They shook hands. Her grip was iron, and Shen He felt a pulse of qi travel up his arm—probing, testing. He gritted his teeth and didn't flinch.

"Interesting," Wei Qin murmured. "Your meridians are like a sieve. Qi passes through them without any resistance. That should be impossible."

"Everything about me is impossible."

"Then you'll fit right in."

---

The journey took two days. Wei Qin moved through the wilderness like a ghost, avoiding main roads and settlements. She spoke little, but when she did, it was always about alchemy—the properties of rare herbs, the theory behind pill refinement, the history of forbidden formulas.

Shen He drank it all in. For the first time in years, someone was treating him like an apprentice instead of a burden.

Their destination was a cave hidden behind a waterfall in the Jagged Spine Mountains. Inside, Wei Qin had carved out a crude laboratory—stone tables lined with mortars and pestles, shelves stacked with dried herbs, and a central cauldron that hummed with residual energy.

"Home sweet home," Wei Qin said, lighting a series of oil lamps. "It's not much, but it's secure. The Azure Cloud Sect knows these mountains are crawling with demon beasts. They don't patrol here."

Shen He set down his bag and looked around. The cave was cold, damp, and smelled of sulfur. But it was a roof over his head.

"Where do we start?"

"We start with you." Wei Qin pointed to a stone slab in the corner. "Lie down. I need to examine your meridians properly."

Shen He hesitated.

"Don't be shy. I've seen more bodies than a plague doctor." Wei Qin's scarred face softened. "I'm not going to hurt you, boy. If I wanted you dead, I'd have left your corpse on the road."

He lay down on the cold stone. Wei Qin placed her hands on his chest, and a wave of qi flowed into him—gentle, probing, like fingers tracing the contours of his soul.

Minutes passed. Wei Qin's expression shifted from curiosity to confusion to something Shen He couldn't name.

"This is... impossible," she whispered.

"What?"

"Your meridians aren't just damaged. They're *missing*." Wei Qin pulled back her hands, her eyes wide. "You don't have a cultivation pathway at all. It's like someone scooped out the channels and left empty space."

"I know. I've been told that my whole life."

"No, you don't understand." Wei Qin grabbed his shoulders. "The Meridian Restoration Pill—it doesn't fix damaged meridians. It creates new ones. But it needs existing channels to anchor to. Without that anchor, the energy has nowhere to go. It should have killed you instantly."

"But it didn't."

"No." Wei Qin's voice dropped to a whisper. "It didn't. Because somehow, your body *absorbed* the energy without channels. You're not a cripple, Shen He. You're a void. A living, breathing vessel that can hold qi without needing to circulate it."

Shen He sat up slowly. "What does that mean?"

"It means everything the Azure Cloud Sect taught you about cultivation is wrong." Wei Qin's eyes burned with manic intensity. "You don't need to breathe qi into your dantian. You don't need to cycle it through meridians. You just need to *fill* yourself. And the fastest way to do that?"

She picked up a pill from her workbench—a crude, lumpy thing that smelled of bitter herbs.

"Is to eat it."

Shen He stared at the pill. It was the size of his thumb, dark green, and oozing a viscous liquid.

"What is it?"

"A basic qi-infusion pill. I use it for experiments." Wei Qin held it out. "Take it. If I'm right, your body will absorb the energy directly into your core. If I'm wrong..."

"I die?"

"Probably not. But you'll have a hell of a stomachache."

Shen He took the pill. It was warm in his hand, pulsing with trapped qi. Everything he'd ever been told said this was suicide—that without meridians, the energy would tear him apart from the inside.

But everything he'd ever been told had been wrong before.

He swallowed the pill.

For a moment, nothing happened. Then the world exploded.

Qi flooded his body like a river breaking through a dam. It filled his limbs, his chest, his skull. He felt like he was drowning and burning and flying all at once. His vision went white, and he heard Wei Qin shouting something, but the words were lost in the roar of energy.

Then, as suddenly as it began, it stopped.

Shen He opened his eyes. He was lying on the stone floor, Wei Qin kneeling over him, her face a mask of concern.

"Boy? Can you hear me?"

"I'm fine," he croaked. And he was. More than fine. He felt *stronger*. Lighter. Like he'd been carrying a weight his whole life and had just set it down.

Wei Qin helped him sit up. "Your qi signature doubled in the span of three breaths. That's not possible. That's *not possible*."

"Apparently it is." Shen He looked at his hands. They were trembling—not from fear, but from the sheer amount of energy coursing through him. "I feel... full."

"Full?" Wei Qin's eyes narrowed. "That's not a normal sensation. Most cultivators describe qi as a flow, not a fullness."

"I don't know what to tell you. I feel like I just drank an ocean."

Wei Qin paced the cave, muttering to herself. "No meridians. No circulation. Just pure absorption. It's like you're a pill yourself—a container that holds energy without processing it."

"A container."

"Yes. And if we can figure out how to fill you efficiently, how to store more, how to *use* what you've stored..." She spun to face him. "Do you realize what this means? You could skip decades of cultivation. You could absorb pills that would kill normal cultivators. You could—"

She stopped. Her hand went to the knife

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Demon's Price

The night air carried the scent of rot and rain as Shen He pressed deeper into the Blackwood Marsh. Three days had passed since his exile, three days since he'd fled into the most dangerous region within a hundred miles of the Azure Cloud Sect. His clothes were torn, his body covered in scratches from thorns and branches, and his stomach growled with an emptiness that no amount of wild herbs could fill.

But he was alive.

And more importantly, the pill formula hidden in his robes was still intact.

He found shelter beneath the gnarled roots of a dead ironwood tree, its hollow trunk forming a natural cave just large enough for him to curl up inside. The ground was damp and cold, but it beat sleeping in the open where demon beasts might find him.

Shen He pulled out the crumpled page of notes—his only possession of real value. The Meridian Restoration Pill formula. The forbidden knowledge that had cost him everything.

*Except it doesn't work,* he thought, the sect master's words echoing in his mind. *It destroys meridians. It kills the user.*

But that couldn't be right. The explosion hadn't killed him. The incomplete pill he'd consumed had left him with a strange, tingling energy that still coursed through his body. His crippled meridians, previously incapable of holding even a thread of qi, now contained *something*. It wasn't proper cultivation energy—it felt more like raw potential, unstable and wild.

He'd tried to cultivate the night before, sitting in meditation and attempting to draw qi from the air. Nothing. His meridians remained as blocked as ever. But the tingling energy was still there, trapped inside him like a caged beast.

*What did I actually create?*

A sound interrupted his thoughts. Footsteps. Heavy ones, moving through the marsh with purpose.

Shen He pressed himself deeper into the hollow, holding his breath. Demon beasts usually thundered or slithered. These footsteps were measured, human-like. But no human would be stupid enough to wander the Blackwood Marsh at night.

Unless they were hunting.

The footsteps stopped directly outside his hiding spot.

"Come out, little rat." The voice was rough, gravelly, and utterly confident. "I can smell your fear."

Shen He's hand went to the small knife at his belt. A useless gesture against a cultivator, but it was all he had.

"Don't make me drag you out," the voice continued. "I've been tracking you for two days. You're not easy to find, I'll give you that. But the bounty on your head is substantial. A cripple who stole from the Azure Cloud Sect? The Alchemy Guild is *very* interested in you."

The Alchemy Guild. Of course. News traveled faster than Shen He could run.

He had two options: stay hidden and hope the hunter grew bored, or come out fighting. Neither was good.

"Fine," Shen He said, crawling out of the hollow. "Let's talk."

The hunter was a man in his forties, built like a bear, with a scar running from his left eye to his jaw. He wore the brown robes of a freelance bounty hunter, and at his waist hung a sword that pulsed with faint qi energy.

"A cripple with guts," the man said, grinning. "I appreciate that. Makes this less boring." He drew his sword. "Any last words?"

"Yeah," Shen He said. "Who hired you?"

"The guild, like I said. They want to study you. Something about your body's reaction to failed pills." The hunter's grin widened. "Apparently, you're a walking experiment."

Shen He's mind raced. The Alchemy Guild knew about his condition. They'd known before the sect even exiled him. Which meant someone inside the sect had been feeding them information.

Lin Xue. It had to be. She'd seen him copying the scroll. She'd reported him to Elder Wu. And now she was selling information to the guild.

"One more question," Shen He said, buying time. "What's your name? I want to know who killed me."

"Zhang Bao. Remember it well."

Zhang Bao lunged forward, his sword slicing through the air with blinding speed. Shen He dove to the side, rolling through mud and rotting leaves. The blade caught his shoulder, tearing through cloth and flesh.

Pain exploded through his arm.

But something else happened too.

The tingling energy inside him *surged*. It rushed to the wound on his shoulder, and Shen He felt the flesh knitting itself back together at an impossible speed. The bleeding stopped. The pain faded to a dull ache.

Zhang Bao's eyes widened. "What the—"

Shen He looked at his shoulder. The wound was gone. Not healed—*gone*. As if it had never existed.

"What are you?" Zhang Bao whispered, his confidence cracking.

"I don't know," Shen He said honestly. "But I'm about to find out."

He charged.

It was a stupid move. Zhang Bao was a fifth-stage Qi Condensation cultivator. Shen He had no cultivation at all. The difference in power was like comparing a candle to a bonfire.

But the tingling energy didn't care about logic.

Zhang Bao swung his sword, and Shen He raised his arm to block. The blade should have severed his limb. Instead, it bounced off his skin with a sound like metal striking stone.

"Impossible!" Zhang Bao stumbled backward. "Your body—it's like demon hide!"

Shen He looked at his arm. There wasn't even a mark. The energy inside him had hardened his flesh at the moment of impact.

He didn't understand what was happening. The Meridian Restoration Pill hadn't restored his meridians—it had done something else entirely. It had transformed his body into something that shouldn't exist.

*The sect master was wrong,* Shen He realized. *The pill didn't fail. It succeeded in ways they never imagined.*

"You're a monster," Zhang Bao said, backing away. "A freak."

"Maybe," Shen He said. "But I'm a freak who's going to walk out of this marsh alive."

He lunged again, and this time Zhang Bao didn't engage. The bounty hunter turned and ran, crashing through the underbrush in panicked retreat.

Shen He let him go. He didn't have the energy for a prolonged chase, and he needed to understand what was happening to his body.

He sat down in the mud, breathing heavily, and focused inward. The tingling energy was still there, but it had calmed after the fight. It seemed to respond to threats, activating only when his body was in danger.

*What am I?*

He thought back to the explosion in the sect's alchemy hall. The incomplete pill had detonated with enough force to destroy a building. And yet Shen He, standing directly over the cauldron, had survived with only minor injuries.

He'd assumed it was luck. Now he wasn't so sure.

His body had absorbed the pill's energy. Converted it into something new. And that something was still changing him, moment by moment.

Shen He pulled out the formula again, reading it by the faint light of the moon. The ancient script was dense, filled with technical terms and obscure references. But one line stood out, a warning written in smaller characters at the bottom of the page:

*"The pill that restores the broken path cannot be consumed by the worthy. Only the worthless may awaken its true power."*

Only the worthless.

Shen He laughed, the sound hollow and bitter. The sect had called him worthless for years. A cripple. A burden. Charity case.

And that very worthlessness had made him the only person in a thousand years who could survive this pill.

"What have I become?" he whispered to the empty marsh.

No answer came. Only the wind, carrying the distant howl of a demon beast.

He needed to find a safe place to rest and think. And he needed to find out more about the pill's effects before they killed him—because despite the healing and the hardening, the energy was still unstable. He could feel it building, pressing against his insides like a pressure cooker waiting to explode.

Shen He stood, brushed the mud from his clothes, and began walking.

Three days later, he reached the edge of the marsh. Before him lay a vast plain, and beyond that, the lights of a city.

The city of Clearwater. A neutral trading hub, not controlled by any sect.

A place where a cripple with a bounty on his head might find allies.

Or enemies.

As he approached the city gates, a figure stepped out of the shadows. A woman, tall and elegant, with silver hair that seemed to glow in the moonlight.

"You're the one," she said, her voice musical but cold. "The pharmacist who survived the impossible pill."

Shen He tensed, ready to fight or flee. "Who are you?"

"My name is Wei Ling. I represent an organization that has been watching you for some time." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "The Alchemy Guild wants you dead. The Azure Cloud Sect wants you forgotten. But we... we want to help you."

"Why?"

"Because you're not the first person to survive a forbidden pill." Wei Ling reached into her robes and pulled out a small jade box. "And you won't be the last."

She opened the box, revealing a single pill—black as night, pulsing with an energy that made Shen He's skin crawl.

"This pill was created three hundred years ago," Wei Ling said. "By an alchemist who called himself the Undying Lord. He claimed it could grant immortality to anyone who consumed it."

"What happened to him?"

"He succeeded." Wei Ling's smile widened. "And then he vanished. But his research survived. And we believe you're the key to understanding it."

Shen He stared at the pill. The energy inside it resonated with the energy inside him, like two halves of the same song.

"What do you want from me?"

"Come with me," Wei Ling said. "And we'll show you what you truly are."

She extended her hand.

Behind her, the city gates opened, revealing a path into darkness.

Shen He looked at her hand, then at the city, then back at the marsh he'd just escaped. He had nothing. No home. No allies. No future.

Except the one this stranger was offering.

"One condition," he said. "I want to know everything. No secrets."

"Agreed."

He took her hand.

And the moment their skin touched, the energy inside him *screamed*.

A vision flashed before his eyes—a vast laboratory, filled with bubbling cauldrons and strange machinery. A man in black robes stood at the center, his face hidden, his hands covered in blood.

*"You're too late,"* the man said, his voice echoing across time. *"The pill has already been consumed. And the pharmacist has already died."*

The vision shattered.

Shen He stumbled backward, gasping. Wei Ling's grip tightened on his hand, holding him steady.

"You saw something," she said. "What was it?"

"I saw..." He shook his head, trying to clear the lingering images. "I saw the Undying Lord. He said I was too late."

Wei Ling's expression shifted—a flicker of fear crossing her usually composed face.

"That's impossible," she whispered. "He's been dead for three centuries."

"Maybe," Shen He said, his voice trembling. "But he knew I was coming."

The city lights flickered, and somewhere in the distance, a bell tolled midnight.

Wei Ling released his hand and stepped back. "We need to move. Now."

"Why? What's happening?"

"The vision you saw—it was a warning. The Undying Lord isn't dead. He's been waiting." She looked at Shen He with something approaching pity. "And now that you

title: The Demon's Bargain
content:
Shen He had been walking for three days.

The road stretched endlessly before him, a ribbon of dust and stone winding through hills that grew increasingly barren. Each step sent jolts of pain through his body—not from physical exhaustion, but from the qi swirling in chaotic patterns through his unrefined meridians. The incomplete Meridian Restoration Pill had done something to him, something the sect master's warning hadn't covered.

It hadn't killed him. But it hadn't failed entirely either.

He could feel fragments of the pill's energy lodged in his damaged channels like shards of glass, each one pulsing with a power that wasn't quite qi and wasn't quite poison. It was something in between—a corrupted form of spiritual energy that defied everything he'd learned from his stolen scrolls.

On the third night, he found shelter in an abandoned shrine.

The building had once been dedicated to some minor deity, its walls covered in faded murals depicting scenes of harvest and prosperity. Now it stood empty, roof half-collapsed, the altar cracked and desecrated. Shen He didn't care about the shrine's history. He cared about the four walls and the fact that it wasn't raining.

He settled into a corner, pulling out his salvaged notes.

The handwriting was his own, scrawled in desperate haste during his months of secret study. Most of it was gibberish—half-formed theories and crossed-out formulas. But one passage caught his eye, written in the margins of a page about pill refinement:

*"Why does qi need to enter through the breath? The body has other pathways. The stomach digests. The blood carries. If pills can transmit qi directly to the organs, why not to the meridians themselves?"*

He'd been right about that much. The pill had delivered energy directly to his channels. But the energy was wrong—corrupted by an impurity he couldn't identify.

"You're thinking about this all wrong."

Shen He's head snapped up. The voice came from the shadows near the collapsed altar, smooth and amused, like silk sliding over a blade.

"Who's there?"

A figure stepped into the moonlight. She was beautiful in the way that a venomous flower was beautiful—pale skin, dark eyes that seemed to drink the light, lips curved in a smile that held no warmth. Her robes were black, embroidered with silver patterns that writhed and shifted as if alive.

"I'm someone who's been watching you for quite some time," she said. "The cripple who refuses to stay crippled. The thief who steals forbidden knowledge. The fool who swallows incomplete pills and survives."

Shen He's hand moved instinctively to the pouch at his belt, where he kept his remaining spirit stones and the last fragments of his pill materials.

"Don't bother," she said. "If I wanted to kill you, you'd be dead already. I'm here to offer you a deal."

"I'm not interested."

"You should be." She walked closer, her footsteps making no sound on the stone floor. "You've hit a wall, Shen He. Your body can absorb qi through pills, but your meridians are too damaged to channel it effectively. Every pill you take from now on will be like drinking seawater—it'll quench your thirst momentarily, but it will slowly kill you."

Shen He's jaw tightened. She was right. He'd felt it with the Meridian Restoration Pill—the energy had entered his body, but it couldn't flow properly. It was pooling in his core, building pressure with nowhere to go.

"What do you want?"

"Your cooperation." She stopped a few feet away, studying him with those dark, ancient eyes. "I represent an organization that has... unconventional methods of cultivation. We don't believe that the heavens dictate who can and cannot cultivate. We believe that power belongs to those who take it."

"I've heard of your kind." Shen He's voice was cold. "Demon cultivators. Those who use blood sacrifices and forbidden arts to accelerate their growth."

"Demon cultivators." She laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "Such a crude term. We prefer 'Shadow Path practitioners.' And yes, we use methods that the righteous sects consider taboo. But tell me, Shen He—what has the righteous path ever given you?"

He had no answer for that.

The Azure Cloud Sect had taken him in as an orphan, true. But they'd also marked him as worthless, assigned him menial labor, and thrown him out the moment he tried to better himself. They hadn't cared about his potential. They'd cared only that he didn't fit their mold.

"I can give you what they couldn't," the woman said. "Complete manuals for Shadow Path cultivation. Techniques that don't rely on functioning meridians. And the resources to create pills that will strengthen your body rather than destroy it."

"And what would you want in return?"

"Nothing much. Just your services as an alchemist. We have need of someone with your... unique talents."

Shen He's mind raced. This was a trap. It had to be. Demon cultivators were universally reviled, hunted by every righteous sect in the region. Allying with them would make him a permanent outlaw, a target for every cultivator who wanted to prove their virtue by killing a heretic.

But what choice did he have?

He could continue as he was—wandering the mortal world, slowly dying as the corrupted qi built up in his core. He could try to find another sect willing to take him in, but word of his exile would spread quickly. No sect would accept a known thief and forbidden pill refiner.

Or he could take the woman's offer.

"What's your name?" he asked.

"Call me Wei Yan." She extended her hand, palm open. In the center rested a black pill, its surface gleaming like obsidian. "A gift. To show my good faith."

Shen He stared at the pill. Every instinct screamed at him to refuse, to run, to find some other path forward. But he was tired of running. Tired of being weak. Tired of letting the world dictate what he could and couldn't do.

He took the pill.

It was cold against his skin, denser than any pill he'd ever held. The energy within it was dark, hungry, waiting to be unleashed.

"Swallow it," Wei Yan said. "And take your first step onto the Shadow Path."

Shen He raised the pill to his lips—

And stopped.

Something was wrong. The energy in the pill wasn't just dark—it was familiar. The same corrupted signature he'd felt in the Meridian Restoration Pill. The same impurity that had nearly destroyed his meridians.

"You," he said, his voice barely a whisper. "The explosion in the sect. The incomplete formula. You set that up."

Wei Yan's smile widened, and for the first time, Shen He saw something genuinely predatory in her eyes. "Very good. I was wondering when you'd figure it out."

"The scroll was planted. You wanted me to find it, to refine the pill, to get myself thrown out of the sect."

"Correct. The Azure Cloud Sect's security is too tight for me to approach openly. But a desperate, exiled disciple with nothing to lose? Much easier to recruit."

Shen He's hand closed into a fist, crushing the pill. Black dust trickled between his fingers. "You manipulated me from the beginning."

"I did." Wei Yan's smile didn't waver. "And you'll forgive me for it, because you need me. That corrupted qi in your core? It's not going away. Without my help, you have three months. Maybe four. Then your body will shut down, and you'll die in agony."

"You're lying."

"I'm not. And you know I'm not." She stepped closer, her voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper. "But here's the interesting part, Shen He. The pill I just gave you—the one you crushed—it wasn't a trap. It was a cure. The only cure that exists for your condition."

She reached into her robe and pulled out another pill, identical to the first. "This is the last one I have. Take it, and you live. Refuse, and you die. Simple as that."

Shen He's hand trembled. Three months. He could feel the truth of her words in the pressure building in his core, in the way his qi stuttered and spasmed whenever he tried to circulate it.

"Take it," Wei Yan said softly. "And join us. All your potential, finally realized. All your dreams, finally within reach. Or die as a cripple, alone in an abandoned shrine, forgotten by everyone who ever knew you."

Shen He reached out.

His fingers closed around the pill.

And somewhere in the darkness outside the shrine, a sword sang as it was drawn from its sheath.

title: The Vermillion Lord's Offer
content:
The journey from the Shattered Peaks took three days. Three days of walking through blighted lands where the very air tasted of copper and ash. Three days of watching Shen He's back for pursuit that never came.

They'd found shelter in an abandoned hunter's cabin on the edge of a dying forest. The wood was rotted, the roof leaked, and something had died in the corner recently enough that the smell still lingered. But it had walls. It had a door that closed. And most importantly, it had a fireplace where Shen He could test the limits of his new cultivation.

He sat cross-legged on the dirt floor, his eyes closed, his breath steady. The qi within him moved like a river now—powerful, deep, and utterly foreign. Every time he touched it, he felt like he was reaching into someone else's body.

"You're brooding again," Lian Hua said from where she sat by the window, her eyes scanning the treeline.

"I'm meditating."

"You're brooding while pretending to meditate. I can tell the difference."

Shen He opened his eyes. "How?"

"Because your eyebrows do this thing when you're upset." She scrunched her face in an exaggerated imitation. "They cave in like two caterpillars having an argument."

Despite everything, Shen He felt his lips twitch. "My eyebrows do not look like caterpillars."

"They absolutely do. I've been meaning to tell you for weeks, but you seemed fragile."

"I am not fragile."

"You cried when you stepped on that snail last month."

"It was a *baby* snail, Lian Hua. It had a tiny shell and everything."

She laughed—a real laugh, not the sharp, bitter one she'd been using since the Vermillion Lord's attack. It transformed her face, making her look younger, softer. For a moment, she was just a girl sharing a miserable cabin with a boy, not a fugitive running from a sect that wanted her dead.

Then the moment passed, and her eyes went hard again.

"We can't stay here," she said. "The Vermillion Lord's territory extends for a hundred li in every direction. We're still inside it."

"I know."

"He'll find us eventually. His scouts are everywhere. His spies—"

"I *know*, Lian Hua."

She fell silent, her jaw tight. Shen He watched her for a moment, then looked down at his hands. They were still callused from years of grinding herbs and stoking furnaces. Still stained with the residue of a thousand failed pills.

He thought about the Vermillion Lord's words. The offer he'd made. The way his eyes had gleamed when he'd looked at Shen He's pill furnace.

"What if we didn't run?" Shen He said quietly.

Lian Hua's head snapped toward him. "What?"

"What if we went to him instead?"

"Have you lost your mind? He tried to *kill* us. He sent his entire army after us. He—"

"He made me an offer," Shen He interrupted. "Before the fight. He said he wanted my alchemy. That he would give me resources, protection, anything I needed."

"And you believed him?"

"No." Shen He met her eyes. "But I think he meant it. At least partially. He's a cultivator at the peak of the Nascent Soul realm. He doesn't need anything from a crippled alchemist with a single pill furnace. But he *wanted* something. And whatever it is, it's valuable enough that he'd rather recruit me than kill me."

Lian Hua was quiet for a long moment. Then she said, "You're thinking about using him."

"I'm thinking about surviving."

"Those are the same thing, Shen He."

He didn't deny it.

The next morning, they found a note pinned to the cabin door.

It was written on blood-red paper, the characters flowing like liquid fire. The handwriting was elegant, almost artistic—the work of someone who had spent centuries perfecting every stroke.

"Dear Pill Master Shen,

I apologize for the abruptness of our last meeting. My subordinates can be... overzealous. I trust you and your companion are unharmed.

I would like to extend a formal invitation to my manor in the Vermillion Mountains. I have matters of great importance to discuss with you—matters that could benefit us both immensely.

Bring your furnace. I assure you, you will find my facilities... inspiring.

Come alone, or bring your friend. The choice is yours.

Your humble servant,
The Vermillion Lord"

Below the signature was a small seal—a crimson flame consuming a cauldron.

Lian Hua read the note over Shen He's shoulder, her breath warm against his neck. "It's a trap."

"Obviously."

"Then why are you smiling?"

Shen He folded the note carefully and tucked it into his robe. "Because he doesn't know what I've become."

---

The Vermillion Mountains rose like a wall of fire against the horizon. The trees here weren't green—they were red, their leaves burning with an inner light that never extinguished. The air shimmered with heat, and the ground beneath Shen He's feet was warm to the touch.

They'd left the cabin at dawn, following a path that seemed to appear before them as they walked. The note had been a map in itself—every time Shen He unfolded it, new characters appeared, guiding them deeper into the Vermillion Lord's territory.

"This place is wrong," Lian Hua muttered, her hand resting on her sword. "The qi here is... corrupted."

"It's not corrupted," Shen He said. "It's just concentrated. The Vermillion Lord must have established his sect over a natural qi vein."

"That's impossible. No sect would give up territory this rich."

"He's not a sect. He's a lord. He doesn't share."

They walked for another hour before the manor came into view.

It wasn't a building. It was a *city*—a sprawling complex of red stone and black iron, its towers piercing the sky like the fingers of a buried giant. Walls surrounded it, thick enough to withstand a siege, and on those walls, cultivators patrolled in synchronized formations.

At the gate, a servant was waiting for them. He was a young man in red robes, his face blank, his movements mechanical. When he spoke, his voice was hollow.

"The Lord awaits you in the Grand Hall. Please follow me."

They were led through corridors of polished obsidian, past gardens where flowers bloomed with flames instead of petals, past training grounds where cultivators moved through forms that left trails of fire in the air. Everywhere Shen He looked, he saw wealth. Power. Resources that the Azure Cloud Sect could only dream of.

And at the center of it all sat the Vermillion Lord.

He was seated on a throne carved from a single piece of blood jade, his posture relaxed, his eyes sharp. He was dressed in robes of crimson silk, and on his finger, a ring pulsed with light that matched his qi signature.

"Pill Master Shen," he said, his voice warm, welcoming. "I'm glad you accepted my invitation. I was worried I'd been too forward."

"You tried to kill me," Shen He said flatly.

The Vermillion Lord waved a hand. "A misunderstanding. My men were told to retrieve you, not harm you. They... exceeded their authority."

"They killed dozens of people."

"They were bandits. Criminals. Scum of the earth." The Vermillion Lord leaned forward, his eyes glinting. "I did you a favor, Pill Master Shen. I cleared the path for you. Now you stand at the gates of true power, and all you have to do is walk through."

Shen He felt Lian Hua's hand tighten on his arm. He ignored her.

"What do you want from me?"

"Straight to business. I appreciate that." The Vermillion Lord rose from his throne and descended the steps, his movements fluid, predatory. "I want your pills, Pill Master Shen. Specifically, I want the one you were working on when we met. The one that almost killed you."

"The Meridian Restoration Pill."

"Yes." The Vermillion Lord's eyes burned. "I've spent three hundred years searching for a way to fix what was broken in me. I've consulted the greatest alchemists in the world. I've raided ancient tombs and plundered forbidden libraries. And every time, I've found nothing but failure."

He stopped in front of Shen He, close enough that Shen He could smell the fire on his breath.

"Until I saw your work. The explosion. The qi signature. The way the energy *sang* as it collapsed." The Vermillion Lord smiled, and it was the most honest expression Shen He had seen on his face. "You're close, Pill Master Shen. Closer than anyone has ever been. And I want to help you finish it."

"Why?"

"Because if you succeed, you'll have a pill that can restore any meridian. Any damaged dantian. Any broken cultivation." The Vermillion Lord's voice dropped to a whisper. "You'll have a pill that can make the crippled whole again."

Shen He's heart stopped.

He thought about the sect master's words. The ancient alchemists who had tried and failed. The warning that the pill destroyed instead of restored.

But he also thought about the explosion. The way his qi had *changed* afterward. The power that now flowed through him like a river of fire.

"What if I refuse?" he asked.

The Vermillion Lord's smile didn't waver. "Then you'll leave, and I'll find another alchemist. But I don't think you'll refuse, Pill Master Shen." He reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll, its edges charred and crumbling. "Because I have something you want."

He tossed the scroll to Shen He, who caught it instinctively.

It was old. Ancient. The paper was brittle, the characters faded. But as Shen He unrolled it, his breath caught in his throat.

It was a formula. A complete formula. Every ingredient, every step, every technique required to refine the Meridian Restoration Pill.

Including the one ingredient that had been missing from every other copy.

"The pill requires a catalyst," the Vermillion Lord said. "Something to stabilize the qi reaction and prevent the meridian collapse. The ancient alchemists knew what it was, but they kept it secret. A failsafe, in case their work fell into the wrong hands."

He leaned in close, his voice a whisper of flame.

"The catalyst is the blood of a living Phoenix."

Shen He stared at the scroll, his mind racing. A Phoenix. Legendary beasts that had been extinct for ten thousand years. Creatures of pure fire and rebirth, whose blood could burn through anything.

And then he looked up at the Vermillion Lord, and he understood.

"You have Phoenix blood."

"I have the *last* Phoenix blood," the Vermillion Lord corrected. "A single vial, preserved for three thousand years. It's enough for one pill. One chance."

He extended his hand.

"Help me refine the pill, Shen He. Help me become whole again. And in return, I'll give you everything you've ever wanted. Power. Respect. A place in this world that no one can take from you."

Shen He looked at the hand. Then at Lian Hua, whose face was pale, terrified.

Then at his own hands—stained, callused, broken.

He thought about the Azure Cloud Sect. About Lin Xue's cold eyes. About Elder Wu's sneer. About the sect master's dismissal.

He thought about the boy who had been told he was worthless. The cripple who had been cast out. The alchemist who had almost died for a dream.

And he took the Vermillion Lord's hand.

"I'll do it," he said.

Lian Hua screamed his name.

And somewhere

title: The Serpent's True Face
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

He ran for three days without stopping, without sleeping, without eating. His body screamed for rest, but the cold fire in his veins kept him moving. The Meridian Restoration Pill—the one the sect master claimed was a death sentence—had done something to him. Something impossible.

His meridians, which had been nothing but withered husks his entire life, now pulsed with a faint, golden light. He could feel it. A trickle of qi, foreign and fragile, moving through channels that shouldn't exist.

But the pill hadn't killed him. It had *awakened* something.

On the fourth day, he collapsed beside a stream, his legs finally giving out. He lay on his back, staring up at the canopy of ancient trees, watching specks of light dance through the leaves. His chest heaved. His throat was sandpaper.

*I'm alive.*

The thought should have brought relief. Instead, it brought terror.

If the pill worked—if it *really* worked—then the sect master had lied. The Azure Cloud Sect had locked away a formula that could cure the broken, the crippled, the discarded. They'd called it poison. They'd called it death.

But Shen He had swallowed it, and he was still breathing.

He sat up slowly, pulling the crumpled page of notes from his bag. The paper was singed at the edges from the explosion, the ink smeared in places. But the core formula was still legible: *Nine Heavenly Herbs. Three Essence Drains. One Soul Core.*

The ingredients were impossible. The process was insane. And yet, somehow, he'd succeeded.

No. That wasn't right.

He'd failed. The pill had exploded. He'd barely survived.

But the qi in his meridians told a different story. A story the sect master didn't want anyone to hear.

Shen He clenched his fist, feeling the faint warmth of qi gather in his palm. It was barely a spark—less than what a first-stage Qi Condensation disciple could produce. But for a cripple who'd never felt qi in his life, it was a miracle.

"Why would they hide this?" he whispered to the empty forest. "Why would they call it forbidden?"

The wind carried no answers.

---

Three days later, Shen He reached the outskirts of Greencloud City.

It was a sprawling trade hub, nestled between the Azure Cloud Sect's mountain range and the Blackfang Wastes. Merchants hawked their wares from wooden stalls. Cultivators in expensive robes strutted through the streets, their auras pressing down on mortals like invisible leashes. Shen He pulled his hood lower and kept his head down.

He needed supplies. Spirit herbs. A cauldron. A quiet place to experiment.

But more than that, he needed answers.

The Alchemy Guild had a branch in Greencloud City. They were the sect's rivals in all things pill-related—a massive organization that spanned the continent, hoarding knowledge that the sects couldn't touch. If anyone knew the truth about the Meridian Restoration Pill, it would be them.

The problem was that Shen He was a wanted man.

He found a rundown inn on the city's outer ring, paid for a week's stay with his last spirit stones, and locked himself in his room. The walls were thin. Mould crept up the corners. But it was private.

He spread his notes across the rickety table and stared at them until his eyes blurred.

The formula was incomplete. The page he'd salvaged was only a fragment—the middle section of a much larger scroll. He had the ingredients. He had the refinement process. But he was missing the *why*.

Why did the pill work on him when it had killed everyone else?

Why had the sect locked it away?

And why did his meridians feel like they were *growing*?

He closed his eyes and focused on the golden light inside him. It pulsed in rhythm with his heartbeat, steady and warm. When he pushed his consciousness toward it, the light flared, and a wave of heat rushed through his body.

*Qi circulation. First stage. Qi Condensation.*

He opened his eyes, his hands trembling.

He was a cultivator now. A real one.

It was impossible. It was insane. It was *real*.

---

The knock came at midnight.

Shen He's eyes snapped open. He'd been meditating—trying to stabilize the qi in his meridians, to keep it from burning out like a dying ember. The knock was soft, deliberate. Three taps. A pause. Two more.

A code.

He rose silently, pressing his back against the wall beside the door. "Who is it?"

"A friend," a woman's voice answered. Low. Smoky. "Open the door, Shen He. I know you're in there."

His blood ran cold.

No one knew he was here. He'd covered his tracks. He'd used fake names. He'd paid in cash.

"I think you have the wrong room," he said carefully.

"I don't." A pause. "You're the cripple who stole the Meridian Restoration Pill. You're the one who survived it. And you're the one the entire Azure Cloud Sect is hunting."

Shen He's hand drifted to the dagger hidden in his sleeve. "If you're here to collect the bounty, you'll find I'm not an easy target."

A soft laugh came through the door. "I'm not here to collect anything. I'm here to offer you a job."

Against every survival instinct screaming at him to run, Shen He unlocked the door.

The woman who stepped inside was nothing like he expected. She was tall, with sharp features and silver-streaked hair pulled into a warrior's knot. Her robes were practical—leather and dark silk—and at her hip hung a curved blade that hummed with spiritual energy. Her cultivation base was invisible, but Shen He could *feel* the weight of her presence pressing against his newly awakened senses.

She was strong. Far stronger than anyone in the Azure Cloud Sect.

"You're either very brave or very stupid," she said, circling him like a predator sizing up prey. "I was betting on stupid, but now I'm not so sure."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Zhao Mei. I'm an alchemist. A real one, not a sect lackey playing with cauldrons." She stopped in front of him, her eyes boring into his. "I've been watching you since you left the Azure Cloud Sect. I saw what happened in the hall. I saw the sect master's face when he lied to you."

Shen He's heart hammered. "You were there?"

"I have my ways." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "The Meridian Restoration Pill is a legend among alchemists. A myth. Everyone who's tried to recreate it has died—exploded, actually. Their bodies couldn't handle the transformation."

"And yet I'm standing here."

"Yes." Her smile widened. "That's what makes you so valuable."

"I don't understand."

Zhao Mei reached into her robe and pulled out a scroll—ancient, yellowed, sealed with black wax. She held it out to him.

"The complete formula," she said. "All three sections. The ingredients. The refinement. The *purpose*."

Shen He stared at the scroll, his hands itching to grab it. "Why would you give me this?"

"Because I want to see what happens next." Her eyes glittered with something dangerous. "You've taken the first pill and survived. That means your body is compatible with the formula. Do you know how rare that is? In the last thousand years, only three people have survived the first dose. And all of them went on to become legends."

"Legends?"

"They became something more than human, Shen He. They became gods."

The word hung in the air, heavy with implication.

Shen He reached for the scroll—

And the door exploded inward.

Wood splintered. Dust billowed. A figure stepped through the wreckage, robes billowing, sword drawn.

Lin Xue.

Her eyes found Shen He instantly, and something cold and triumphant flickered in their depths. "I told you I'd find you, cripple."

Zhao Mei moved faster than Shen He could follow. Her blade was out, intercepting Lin Xue's strike before it could reach his throat. Steel clashed. Sparks flew. The two women locked blades, their qi flaring like twin suns.

"Run," Zhao Mei said, her voice tight with effort. "Take the scroll. Find the Black Sun Pavilion in the Wastes. I'll find you there."

"But—"

"*Run*, boy!"

Shen He ran.

He burst through the inn's back window, shattering glass, landing hard on the cobblestones below. His legs pumped. His lungs burned. Behind him, the sounds of battle echoed through the night—steel on steel, qi explosions, Lin Xue's furious screams.

He didn't look back.

He ran until the city walls vanished behind him, until the forest swallowed him whole, until his legs finally gave out and he collapsed in a heap of gasping, shaking limbs.

The scroll was still clutched in his hand.

He unrolled it with trembling fingers, reading by the light of the moon.

The first section listed ingredients he'd never heard of. The second section described a refinement process so complex it would take years to master. But the third section...

The third section made his blood freeze.

*PURPOSE: The Meridian Restoration Pill is not a healing pill. It is a key. Each dose unlocks one of the nine divine seals placed upon the human body by the Heavenly Emperor himself. Those who consume all nine pills will transcend mortality and ascend to godhood.*

*WARNING: The seals were placed for a reason. Breaking them unleashes what was locked away.*

*The first seal holds the soul.*
*The second holds the truth.*
*The third holds the monster.*

Shen He's hands dropped to his sides.

He'd taken the first pill.

He'd broken the first seal.

And now, somewhere deep inside him, something ancient and terrible was beginning to stir.

The golden light in his meridians flickered—and for just a moment, he could have sworn he heard a voice whispering in the back of his mind.

*Free me.*

The scroll fell from his hands. He stared at the moon, at the stars, at the vast, uncaring sky.

And for the first time in his life, Shen He was truly afraid.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Shadow in the Flame

The carriage rattled through the night, its wheels churning mud and gravel along the mountain road.
