Inside, Shen He sat with his back pressed against the wooden wall, the Grand Evaluation pill case cold against his chest. Wei Lin had been unconscious for three hours now, her breath shallow, her skin pale beneath the flickering lantern light.

"We should reach the Dawnbreak relay station by dawn," the driver called from his perch. "Assuming the road doesn't wash out."

Shen He didn't answer. He was too focused on the subtle patterns of qi flowing through Wei Lin's body. The poison had stabilized—barely. The Golden Lotus Pill was doing its work, but it was a temporary fix, like plugging a dam with twigs. She needed a proper healer, preferably one who specialized in spiritual venom.

*But we don't have time for proper healers,* he thought. *The Alchemy Guild is hunting us. The Seven Evils want me dead. And somewhere out there, someone is trying to resurrect the God-Devourer.*

He pressed his palm against the pill case. The artifact throbbed with warmth, as if responding to his touch. He'd barely had time to examine it—just a quick glance before Wei Lin collapsed. The case was made of some material he couldn't identify, neither metal nor jade, but something in between. It hummed with an energy that made his teeth ache.

"What are you?"

The case didn't answer. Of course it didn't. It was a box.

But Shen He couldn't shake the feeling that he was being watched. Not by any person, but by the artifact itself. As if it had a will of its own, waiting for the right moment to reveal its true purpose.

He shook off the thought. He was exhausted, running on adrenaline and desperation. Paranoia was inevitable.

The carriage hit a pothole, and Wei Lin groaned. Her eyes fluttered open, unfocused and glassy.

"Where...?"

"Safe," Shen He said, moving to her side. "Well, safer. We're heading to Dawnbreak."

"Dawnbreak?" She tried to sit up, but her arms gave out. "That's... that's near the Blacksteel Wastes."

"I know."

"The Wastes are cursed. Nothing grows there. The qi is corrupted."

"I know."

"Then why—"

"Because the Wastes are also the last place anyone would look for us." Shen He poured her a cup of water from the flask at his belt. "The Alchemy Guild controls every major city within a thousand li. The Seven Evils have informants in every tavern and trading post. But the Wastes? No one goes there. No one *wants* to go there."

Wei Lin drank slowly, her hands trembling. "You're planning to hide in a wasteland."

"I'm planning to survive." Shen He leaned back. "We need time. Time to figure out what the Grand Evaluation is. Time to find out who's really behind the God-Devourer plot. And time to make you well."

"And if I don't get well?"

"Then I'll carry you on my back if I have to."

Wei Lin stared at him for a long moment. Then, unexpectedly, she laughed. It was a weak, hollow sound, but genuine.

"You're insane," she said.

"People keep telling me that."

"They're right."

"Probably." Shen He smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. "But insane people don't give up. And I'm not giving up. Not until I find out the truth."

The carriage fell silent. Outside, the wind howled, carrying with it the distant cry of some nocturnal beast. The lantern flickered, casting dancing shadows across the walls.

Shen He was about to close his eyes when he felt it.

A shift in the air. A change in the pressure, like the moment before a storm.

"Stop the carriage," he said.

"What?" The driver's voice came back, muffled by the wind.

"STOP THE CARRIAGE!"

The horses screamed. The carriage lurched as the driver yanked on the reins. Shen He grabbed Wei Lin, shielding her with his body as the vehicle skidded to a halt.

For a moment, there was only silence.

Then the world exploded.

The front of the carriage erupted inward, wood splintering like matchsticks. Something massive and dark crashed through the wreckage, tossing the driver's body aside like a rag doll. Shen He rolled, pulling Wei Lin with him, as the creature's claws tore through the space where his head had been.

He came up in a crouch, a pill already between his fingers.

The creature was humanoid, but wrong. Its skin was cracked, like dried mud, with veins of molten light pulsing beneath the surface. Its eyes were pits of absolute blackness, and its mouth—if it could be called a mouth—was a jagged slit that stretched from ear to ear.

"What the hell are you?" Shen He demanded.

The creature opened its mouth. When it spoke, the voice was not its own. It was a chorus of voices, layered on top of each other, some pleading, some screaming, some laughing.

"You have something that belongs to us."

"The Grand Evaluation," Shen He said. "Of course. You're one of the Seven Evils."

"Clever little pharmacist." The creature tilted its head, its neck cracking at an unnatural angle. "But cleverness won't save you. The case carries a seal. A seal that must be broken before the ritual can begin. And you, dear boy, are going to break it for us."

"Why would I do that?"

"Because if you don't, she dies."

The creature's hand shot out, faster than Shen He could react. It grabbed Wei Lin by the throat, lifting her off the ground as if she weighed nothing. She gasped, clawing at its fingers, but her strength was gone.

"No!" Shen He lunged forward, but the creature backhanded him across the clearing. He crashed into a tree, the impact cracking his ribs.

"Tick tock, pharmacist." The creature tightened its grip. Wei Lin's face was turning purple. "Open the seal, or I crush her throat."

Shen He's mind raced. He didn't know how to open the case. He didn't even know what the seal looked like. But he could feel the artifact pulsing against his chest, humming with that same watching awareness.

*It wants me to open it,* he realized. *It's been waiting for this.*

"Fine," he gasped. "Put her down. I'll do it."

The creature smiled. Its teeth were needles.

"Good boy."

It dropped Wei Lin. She hit the ground with a thud, gasping for air. Shen He crawled to his knees, pulling the case from his robes. It glowed faintly in the darkness, its surface covered in intricate patterns that seemed to shift and writhe.

"How do I open it?"

"You already know." The creature's voice dripped with mockery. "You've always known. The seal responds to intent. To desire. You must want it open with every fiber of your being."

Shen He looked at the case. Looked at Wei Lin, struggling to breathe. Looked at the creature, waiting with patient malice.

And he realized the truth.

He *did* want to open it. Not because the creature demanded it. Not because Wei Lin's life was at stake. But because he *needed* to know.

The secrets of the Grand Evaluation. The truth about the God-Devourer. The mystery of his own body, and why the pill case had chosen him.

He wanted it all.

The case pulsed once, twice, three times.

And then it opened.

Light exploded outward, blinding him. The patterns on the case's surface unraveled, revealing a single scroll, yellow with age, bound with a crimson ribbon. Shen He's hands moved on their own, untying the ribbon, unrolling the scroll.

The text was written in a script he'd never seen before. But he could read it. Every word, every character, burned itself into his mind.

*"The Grand Evaluation is not a test. It is a key. And the key opens the door to the First Pill."*

"The First Pill?" Shen He muttered.

The creature laughed. It was a horrible sound, full of centuries of cruelty.

"The First Pill," it repeated. "The pill that created the God-Devourer. The pill that the ancient alchemists used to try and seize the power of heaven itself. They failed, of course. Their ambition outstripped their skill. But their work was not entirely lost."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean that the God-Devourer is not dead." The creature stepped closer, its shadow falling over Shen He. "It was merely sleeping. And with the Grand Evaluation, we can wake it. We can *complete* what the ancients started."

"You're insane," Shen He said. "The God-Devourer nearly destroyed the world."

"Exactly." The creature's grin widened. "And we're going to finish the job."

It reached for the scroll.

Shen He moved first.

He didn't think. He just acted. His hand shot out, grabbing the creature's wrist, and he channeled every drop of qi he had into his palm. The energy exploded outward, not as an attack, but as something else entirely.

*Absorption.*

The creature screamed. Its form began to dissolve, the veins of molten light pulling free from its body, streaming into Shen He's hand. The qi was corrupt, tainted with centuries of malice, but Shen He didn't care. He pulled it in, letting it fill his damaged meridians, letting it burn through him like acid.

"Stop!" the creature shrieked. "You don't know what you're doing!"

"I know exactly what I'm doing," Shen He said, his voice barely a whisper. "I'm surviving."

The creature dissolved into nothing. The qi settled in Shen He's body, hot and wrong, but there. Alive. *His.*

He collapsed to his knees, the scroll falling from his hands. Wei Lin crawled over to him, her face pale.

"What did you do?"

"I don't know." Shen He looked at his hands. They were glowing faintly, the same molten light as the creature. "But I think... I think I just became something else."

Wei Lin's eyes widened.

"Shen He. Look at the scroll."

He looked.

The text was changing. The ancient script was rewriting itself, forming new words, new characters. And at the bottom, in blood-red ink, a single sentence:

*"The First Pill is within you. You carry the seed of the God-Devourer. And it is growing."*

Shen He's blood ran cold.

*Growing.*

He pressed a hand to his chest, feeling his own heartbeat. And beneath it, faint but unmistakable, a second pulse.

Something was alive inside him.

And it was hungry.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Ghost of Cloudfall Valley

The man in grey didn't move. He simply stood there, rain streaming down his face, watching Shen He with an intensity that made the air itself feel thick.

"You have something that belongs to me," he said.

Shen He's mind raced. The pill formula. The notes. The fragments of the Ancient Emperor's secret. How could this man know? He'd only just begun piecing it together himself.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

The man smiled. It wasn't a pleasant expression. "Liar."

He moved.

One moment he was twenty feet away. The next, his hand was wrapped around Shen He's throat, lifting him off the ground with terrifying ease. Shen He grabbed at the man's wrist, struggling, but it was like trying to bend steel.

"I've been watching you, little pharmacist," the man said, his voice soft, almost gentle. "Ever since the Azure Cloud Sect threw you out. I saw you flee into the night. I saw you drag yourself through the forest. I even saw you swallow that half-formed pill you'd hidden in your sleeve—the one that nearly killed you."

Shen He's eyes widened. The pill he'd taken that night. The desperate gamble that had somehow worked, restoring his meridians just enough to let him cultivate. This man had witnessed everything.

"You're stronger now than you were before," the man continued, tightening his grip. "But still weak. Pathetically weak. Do you know how long I've waited for someone like you?"

He threw Shen He to the ground. The impact drove the air from his lungs. Before he could move, the man was standing over him, a foot pressing down on his chest.

"The formula you found in the restricted section," the man said. "It was incomplete. Deliberately so. The Azure Cloud Sect's founders knew that anyone who tried to refine the Meridian Restoration Pill without the missing components would die. They wanted it that way."

Shen He coughed, tasting blood. "You... you know about the pill?"

"I know everything about it." The man crouched down, his face inches from Shen He's. "I'm the one who wrote it."

The words hit Shen He like a physical blow. "Impossible. That scroll was ancient. Thousands of years old."

"I am thousands of years old."

The man's eyes shifted, and for just a moment, Shen He saw something impossible—a depth of age that shouldn't exist in any living being. The eyes of someone who had watched civilizations rise and fall, who had seen the world change a hundred times over.

"Who... who are you?"

"My name was once spoken with reverence," the man said. "Now it's been forgotten. Erased from history. But you may call me Master Jiang."

Shen He's blood ran cold. Master Jiang. The name wasn't in any of the sect's records, but he'd read about him in the forbidden texts—a rogue alchemist who'd been executed three thousand years ago for attempting to create a pill that would grant immortality.

"You're dead," Shen He whispered. "You were executed."

"Executed?" Master Jiang laughed. It was a hollow, bitter sound. "They tried. They failed. I escaped into the wilderness, into places where even the heavens couldn't find me. And I've been waiting ever since."

He released Shen He, stepping back. Shen He scrambled to his feet, his mind churning. This was impossible. A dead man, alive after three thousand years. An ancient alchemist who knew the secrets of the pill that had nearly killed him.

"What do you want from me?"

"Your body."

Shen He froze. "What?"

"Your body is unique," Master Jiang said, pacing slowly. "A meridian-crippled cultivator who can absorb qi through pills. A freak of nature that the Azure Cloud Sect tried to hide and suppress. But I know what you really are, Shen He. You're a vessel."

"I don't understand."

"Your meridians weren't destroyed by some random defect. They were sealed." Master Jiang's eyes gleamed. "Sealed by a technique so ancient that even the sect masters don't recognize it. Someone, somewhere, created you to be a container. A perfect vessel for storing and refining medicinal essence."

Shen He's mind reeled. All his life, he'd believed he was broken. Cursed. A failure of heaven's design. But now this man—this ghost from the past—was telling him it was all intentional.

"Who sealed me?"

"I don't know." Master Jiang shrugged. "And frankly, I don't care. What matters is that you exist. And I can use you."

"Use me how?"

"The pill formula I created was never meant to restore meridians," Master Jiang said. "That was a cover story, a lie to hide its true purpose. The Meridian Restoration Pill is actually a soul-transference pill. It destroys the user's body and transfers their consciousness into a prepared vessel."

The world tilted around Shen He. "You want to steal my body."

"I want to continue my work," Master Jiang corrected. "I've been trapped in this decaying shell for three millennia, unable to cultivate, unable to refine, barely able to maintain my existence. But you—your body is perfect. A blank slate, ready to be molded. With your vessel, I can complete the Ultimate Pill."

Shen He's hand moved to his storage ring, where he kept his remaining materials. "You're insane."

"Perhaps." Master Jiang smiled again. "But I'm also patient. And I've already planted the seeds for your cooperation."

"What are you talking about?"

"The pill you took that night—the one that saved your life—it wasn't complete. The Meridian Restoration Pill requires seven ingredients. You only had four. The fifth ingredient was a binding agent that would have stabilized the pill's effects. Without it, the pill is slowly breaking down inside your dantian."

Shen He's blood turned to ice. He could feel it now—a faint trembling in his core, a weakness that he'd attributed to his injuries.

"In one month," Master Jiang continued, "the pill will dissolve completely. When that happens, your newly restored meridians will collapse. Permanently. You'll be worse than crippled—you'll be dead."

"You're lying."

"I never lie." Master Jiang extended his hand. "But I can offer you a solution. Work with me. Help me complete the Ultimate Pill. And when I take your body, I'll preserve your consciousness. You'll get to watch as I achieve what no one in history has ever accomplished."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you die in thirty days, alone and forgotten." Master Jiang's smile vanished. "Choose wisely, little pharmacist. Your time is running out."

Shen He's mind raced. He had no way to verify Master Jiang's claims. But he could feel the truth in his bones—the instability in his dantian, the slow decay of the pill's essence. This man, this ancient ghost, had been playing a game that spanned millennia.

And Shen He was just a piece on the board.

"One month," Shen He said slowly. "If I help you, I want assurance that my consciousness survives."

"You have my word."

"The word of a dead man who's been lying for three thousand years."

Master Jiang laughed. "Sharp. Good. You'll need that. Fine. I'll give you proof." He reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade slip. "This contains the complete formula for the Meridian Restoration Pill—including the missing ingredients. Study it. Verify it. And when you're ready, come find me at Cloudfall Valley."

He tossed the slip at Shen He's feet.

"The Azure Cloud Sect will send hunters after you," Master Jiang said. "They've already discovered you're alive. Lin Xue is leading the pursuit personally. She'll be here within three days."

Shen He's heart clenched at the name. Lin Xue. The prodigy who'd betrayed him. The woman he'd once admired.

"Survive her," Master Jiang said, turning away. "Prove that you're worthy of my attention. And then we'll talk again."

He walked into the rain, and within three steps, he vanished completely. No trace. No ripple. Just... gone.

Shen He stood alone in the clearing, rain soaking through his clothes, the jade slip heavy in his hand. He looked down at it, then up at the darkening sky.

Lin Xue was coming.

Master Jiang wanted his body.

And he had thirty days to live.

He crushed the jade slip in his fist and started walking. Not away from the mountain. Toward it. Toward Azure Cloud Sect territory.

If Lin Xue wanted a hunt, she'd get one.

And if he was going to die anyway, he'd make sure he wasn't the only one who suffered.

Above him, hidden in the clouds, a pair of grey eyes watched. Master Jiang smiled.

"Perfect," he whispered. "The pieces are finally in motion."

Far below, in the sect's main hall, Lin Xue knelt before the sect master.

"He's alive," she said. "I tracked his aura to the eastern forest. He's heading toward the border."

"Then bring him back," the sect master said. "Alive or dead, I don't care. But recover whatever he stole from the restricted section."

Lin Xue bowed. "It will be done."

She rose and strode from the hall, her hand resting on her sword. Her face was calm, but inside, something burned.

Shen He, you fool. Why couldn't you just disappear?

She stepped into the rain and vanished into the night, following the trail of a ghost toward an ending neither of them could predict.

title: The Weight of Ancient Blood
content:
The obsidian needle hovered an inch above Shen He's heart, its tip gleaming with a venomous green light. He could feel the poison singing in his veins—not the slow death Meng Hao had promised, but something far worse. His right hand had already turned black up to the elbow, the skin cracking like old leather.

"Three choices," Meng Hao said, his voice carrying the weight of absolute certainty. "Die now by the Thousand Venom Needle. Die in three days when the Meridian Eclipse Pill finishes its work. Or accept my offer."

Shen He's left hand twitched toward his belt pouch, where six pills rattled like dying whispers. With his right arm useless and his qi reserves shattered, he had exactly one move left. One pill. One chance.

"The pill," he said, his voice hoarse, "doesn't exist."

Meng Hao's smile didn't waver. "Of course it does. You've already refined it once. The explosion at the Azure Cloud Sect wasn't a failure—it was a *test*. You destroyed the pill intentionally to hide what you'd created."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "You were watching."

"Someone had to." Meng Hao withdrew the needle, and the green light faded from Shen He's vision. "Do you think the Sword Sect simply *coincidentally* sent their inner disciples to investigate a failed alchemy experiment? Do you think Elder Wu's hatred for you is natural?"

The words hit harder than any physical blow. Shen He had spent three months on the run, three months believing he'd escaped because of luck and preparation. But hunters didn't let prey escape. They *herded* them.

"You wanted me to find the Reclusive Alchemist's legacy."

"Finally." Meng Hao clapped his hands together, and the shadows in the alley seemed to retreat. "The boy who can't cultivate but can refine forbidden pills. The prodigy who doesn't breathe qi but absorbs it through alchemy. Do you know how rare you are, Shen He? Do you have any idea what you represent?"

"An anomaly," Shen He said, echoing the sect master's words from three months ago.

"No." Meng Hao's voice dropped to a whisper. "A *solution*. The ancient texts speak of the Heaven-Defying Body—a constitution that can consume pills as if they were breath itself. The last one recorded was the Celestial Alchemist of the Third Era, who refined the Elixir of Eternity and ascended directly to godhood. You are his reincarnation."

Shen He wanted to laugh. He wanted to spit in Meng Hao's face and call him a fool. But the words wouldn't come, because deep in his chest, in that cold place where his anger lived, something stirred. Recognition.

The pills had always worked better for him than they should have. The recipes had always come easier. The Meridian Restoration Pill—the one that was supposed to kill anyone who tried to refine it—had almost worked. He'd been *hours* away from success when the explosion happened.

"What do you want?" Shen He asked.

"Completion." Meng Hao reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age, its edges crumbling. "The Meridian Restoration Pill was never meant to restore *normal* meridians. It was designed to awaken the Heaven-Defying Body fully. The ancient alchemists didn't fail—they were *stopped*. The gods of this era fear what you could become."

He tossed the scroll at Shen He's feet. It landed with a soft thump, unrolling slightly to reveal diagrams so complex they made his eyes hurt.

"Finish your work," Meng Hao said. "Complete the pill. And when you do, I will be there to witness your ascension."

"And if I refuse?"

The Thousand Venom Needle appeared in Meng Hao's hand again, its tip now dripping black. "Then you die. The Heaven-Defying Body dies with you. And I find another candidate in another century."

Shen He stared at the scroll. At the poison creeping up his arm. At the pills in his pouch that couldn't save him. He thought of Lin Xue's cold eyes, Elder Wu's sneer, the sect master's dismissal. He thought of the Reclusive Alchemist's journal, filled with warnings about the price of perfection.

"Three days," he said finally. "That's what you gave me."

"Three days until the Meridian Eclipse Pill reaches its terminal stage." Meng Hao's smile had returned, wider than before. "But you're an alchemist, Shen He. You have three days to find a cure. Or you have three days to finish what you started."

He turned and walked away, his robes dissolving into the shadows until he was nothing but a memory and a threat.

Shen He stood alone in the alley, the scroll at his feet, the poison in his blood. He could feel it spreading now—not just through his arm but through his chest, his organs, his very soul. The Meridian Eclipse Pill was working its dark magic, and somewhere in his body, his non-existent meridians were *dying*.

But something else was happening too.

The poison in his right arm—the Thousand Venom—was slowing. No, that wasn't right. It wasn't slowing. It was being *absorbed*.

Shen He looked down at his blackened hand and watched in horror as the darkness receded, sinking into his skin like water into sand. His fingers twitched. The cracks healed. And beneath it all, he felt a hunger—a terrible, insatiable hunger—that had nothing to do with food.

He fell to his knees, gasping. The scroll lay open before him, and he read the first line:

*"When the body rejects heaven's laws, it must consume heaven's treasures. The Heaven-Defying Body does not cultivate. It devours."*

Shen He's vision blurred. The hunger grew. He could smell the spirit stones in his pouch, taste the qi in the air, feel the life force of the mortals sleeping in the buildings around him. Everything was food. Everything was fuel.

He grabbed the scroll and shoved it into his bag. He stumbled to his feet, his legs shaking. The poison was gone now, consumed by whatever monster lived inside him. But the Meridian Eclipse Pill was still ticking, still working, still three days from killing him.

*Three days to find a cure. Three days to finish what I started.*

Shen He laughed, and it came out broken, desperate. He had spent his whole life being told he was worthless, a cripple, an anomaly. Now he knew the truth. He wasn't broken. He was *wrong*—wrong in a way that the gods themselves feared.

He started walking, aimlessly, through the darkened streets of Blackwood Town. The hunger gnawed at him, constant and demanding. He needed pills. He needed ingredients. He needed—

A scream tore through the night.

Shen He's head snapped up. The sound came from the eastern district, where the town's wealthier merchants lived. He should have run the other way. He was a wanted man, a fugitive, a cripple with a death sentence.

But the hunger pushed him forward.

He reached the source of the scream to find a house in ruins. The front wall had been torn open as if by a giant hand, and inside, a family cowered before a figure wreathed in black smoke. The smoke had eyes—dozens of them, all glowing red, all fixed on the family's youngest child.

"A demon," someone whispered. "A demon has entered the town!"

Shen He should have run. He should have hidden. He was no warrior, no hero.

But the hunger recognized the smoke. It wasn't a demon. It was *alchemy*—alchemy so twisted, so corrupted, that it had taken on a life of its own.

And Shen He wanted it. He *needed* it.

His hand moved to his pouch, finding a pill he'd never intended to use. The Rage of the Fallen Phoenix—a pill that would burn through his remaining qi in moments, leaving him helpless afterward.

He popped it into his mouth.

Fire exploded through his veins. His vision turned red. And for the first time in his life, Shen He felt what it was like to have *power*.

He charged.

The smoke creature turned, its eyes focusing on him with predatory interest. It spoke in a voice that crackled like burning wood:

*"The Heaven-Defying Body. Finally."*

Shen He's blood turned to ice.

The creature knew. And if it knew, others would too.

The hunt was no longer just about escaping the Sword Sect.

It was about survival.

title: The Shadow in the Ruins
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

Three days had passed since his exile, and the mortal world had proven exactly as unforgiving as he'd expected. Bandits prowled the roads. Wild beasts hunted the shadows. And every village he passed looked at him with a mixture of suspicion and pity—a boy in tattered sect robes, walking with the hollow gait of the defeated.

But Shen He didn't care about any of that.

He cared about the pill.

The Meridian Restoration Pill was supposed to be impossible. The sect master had said so himself. But Shen He had held the incomplete version in his hands. He'd felt its power thrum against his fingertips. It wasn't impossible. It was unfinished.

And he would finish it.

He found shelter in an abandoned temple on the outskirts of a town called Thorn's End. The roof had caved in on one side, and moss grew thick over the cracked stone floor, but it kept the rain off and the beasts out. More importantly, it had an old stone furnace in the back—an alchemist's kiln, left to rust and decay.

Shen He spent his first night cleaning it. His second night testing its seals. And on the third morning, he laid out his single crumpled page of notes and began to work.

The pill required seven ingredients. He had three: Crimson Lotus Petal, Star Moss, and the powdered bone of a Silverback Demon Ape. The fourth—Ghost Essence Dew—was something he'd need to harvest himself. It only formed on graves where a cultivator had died in agony, their resentment seeping into the earth.

There was a graveyard half a day's walk from Thorn's End. The locals called it the Wailing Fields.

Shen He arrived as the sun began to set.

The graveyard was a nightmare given form. Thousands of graves stretched across the rolling hills, their headstones cracked and weathered. The air hung heavy with the smell of wet earth and something else—something sharp and metallic that reminded him of blood.

He'd brought a jade vial and a bronze knife. The Ghost Essence Dew formed on the surface of grave markers, glistening like morning frost. He just had to scrape it off and collect it before it evaporated.

Easy.

The first three graves yielded nothing. The fourth had a thin layer, barely enough to wet the blade of his knife. Shen He scraped it into the vial, his hands steady despite the growing darkness.

That's when he heard the footsteps.

They came from behind him, light and deliberate. Not the shuffling of a curious villager. Not the heavy tread of a beast. These were the footsteps of someone who knew exactly where they were going.

Shen He turned slowly.

A woman stood at the edge of the graveyard, her robes the color of dried blood. She wore a veil that obscured her face, but he could see her eyes—pale gold, like coins. They gleamed in the dying light.

"You're the alchemist," she said. It wasn't a question.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"The boy who nearly blew up the Azure Cloud Sect's outer compound. The one who stole the forbidden formula." She stepped closer, and Shen He's hand instinctively went to the knife at his belt. "Word travels fast among those who listen."

"Who are you?"

"My name is not important. What's important is what you're doing here, in a graveyard, collecting the last echoes of the dead." She stopped a few feet away, her head tilting as if studying him. "You're trying to rebuild the Meridian Restoration Pill."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "How do you know about that?"

"I know many things." She reached into her sleeve and withdrew a scroll, bound with black silk. "I also know that you're missing ingredients four through seven. And that even if you managed to complete the pill, you'd likely die the moment you consumed it."

"That's what the sect master said."

"The sect master is a fool who repeats what he's been told." She tossed the scroll at Shen He's feet. "That contains the complete formula. Verified. Tested. Perfected."

Shen He stared at the scroll like it was a venomous snake. "Why would you give me this?"

"Because I want to see what happens."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only one you're going to get." She turned and began walking away, her robes trailing through the wet grass. "The pill works, Shen He. But it has a cost. By the time you discover what that cost is, it will be too late to turn back."

"Wait!"

She paused.

"If it works, why hasn't anyone used it? Why isn't every crippled cultivator in the world lining up to take it?"

The woman laughed—a sound like breaking glass. "Because cultivators are cowards. They fear the unknown more than they fear their own mediocrity. You, on the other hand..." She looked over her shoulder, and even through the veil, he could see the sharp curve of her smile. "You're desperate enough to try anything."

Then she was gone.

The scroll lay at Shen He's feet, black silk glistening in the moonlight. He picked it up with trembling hands and unrolled it.

The formula was there. Every ingredient. Every step. Every caution. It was written in elegant script, annotated with diagrams that would have taken him years to figure out on his own.

And at the bottom, in red ink:

*The user's soul must be strong enough to withstand the merger. If not, the body will survive, but the spirit will shatter. There is no known method to repair a shattered spirit.*

Shen He read the words three times.

Then he rolled up the scroll and walked back to Thorn's End.

---

Two weeks later, Shen He had gathered all seven ingredients.

The Ghost Essence Dew had been the hardest—he'd spent four nights in the Wailing Fields, scraping grave markers and fighting off the vengeful spirits that rose with the moon. But he'd done it. The other ingredients had been simpler: purchased from disreputable dealers, traded with desperate cultivators, or in one case, stolen from a merchant who'd tried to cheat him.

Now the pill sat in the crucible of the old furnace, its surface shimmering with a light that was almost alive.

Shen He watched it for an hour, waiting for it to fully coalesce. The furnace's heat had turned the mixture into a perfect sphere, smooth and black as obsidian. When he picked it up, it was warm to the touch, and he could feel something pulsing inside it.

A heartbeat.

"The pill is alive," he whispered.

He didn't know if that was a good thing.

But he was out of options. His body was failing. The strain of his failed cultivation attempts over the years had left his meridians in ruins, and without the pill, he'd be dead within a year. The Meridian Restoration Pill was his only chance.

He lifted it to his lips.

"Are you sure about this?"

Shen He nearly dropped the pill. He spun around, heart hammering, and found himself staring at a young woman sitting cross-legged on the temple's broken altar. She wore simple traveler's clothes, and her hair was tied back in a practical knot. Her eyes were sharp, assessing.

"Who the hell are you?" Shen He demanded. "And how long have you been sitting there?"

"Long enough to watch you create a forbidden pill in a ruined temple." She hopped off the altar and walked toward him, her footsteps silent. "My name is Wei Yan. I'm a bounty hunter."

"I didn't put a bounty on myself."

"No, but the Azure Cloud Sect did. Five hundred spirit stones for your capture. A thousand for your head." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I've been tracking you since the Wailing Fields. You're not very good at covering your tracks."

Shen He's grip tightened on the pill. "So you're here to collect the bounty."

"I was." Wei Yan stopped a few feet away. "But then I saw you make the pill. And I got curious."

"About what?"

"About why a crippled alchemist would risk his life for a formula that's killed everyone who's tried it." She crossed her arms. "Either you're an idiot, or you know something the rest of the world doesn't."

Shen He's jaw tightened. "Maybe I'm just desperate."

"Desperate enough to trust a stranger's scroll?"

His blood ran cold. "What?"

"The woman in the graveyard. The one who gave you the formula." Wei Yan's eyes narrowed. "I saw her too. I followed her after she left you. She disappeared into thin air about a mile outside the fields. No tracks. No qi fluctuations. Nothing."

"She was a cultivator."

"She was something." Wei Yan's voice dropped. "And whatever she was, she's been watching you for longer than you think. I checked with the locals. She arrived in Thorn's End three days before you did. She was waiting for you."

Shen He's mind raced. The woman had known about the pill. She'd known about his exile. She'd given him the exact formula he needed, at the exact moment he needed it.

It was too convenient.

"Who is she?" he asked, his voice hoarse.

"I don't know. But I know someone who might." Wei Yan reached into her pocket and pulled out a worn piece of parchment. "There's a man in the Black Lotus City. He's an information broker. If anyone knows about that woman, it's him."

"Why are you helping me?"

"Because five hundred spirit stones is a lot of money, but a living alchemist who can create forbidden pills?" She smiled again, and this time there was a glint of something dangerous in her eyes. "That's worth more than any bounty."

Shen He looked at the pill in his hand. Then at the parchment. Then at the woman who'd appeared out of nowhere with an offer he couldn't refuse.

"Fine," he said. "Take me to Black Lotus City."

Wei Yan nodded, then gestured at the pill. "Before we go, are you going to eat that or what?"

Shen He looked at the pill one last time. The heartbeat pulsed against his palm, steady and insistent.

He thought about the woman in the graveyard. The scroll. The warning at the bottom about the shattered spirit.

Then he thought about the sect master's cold eyes. Lin Xue's betrayal. The years of being treated like garbage because his body refused to cooperate.

He popped the pill into his mouth and swallowed.

The effect was immediate.

Pain exploded through his chest, white-hot and blinding. His meridians—the ruined, dead channels that had never once carried qi—erupted with fire. He screamed, his back arching, his vision going white.

And then, in the space between one heartbeat and the next, he felt something he'd never felt before.

Qi.

It flowed through him like a river breaking through a dam, wild and untamed and impossibly powerful. His body hummed with energy. His skin crackled with light. He could feel the world around him in ways he'd never imagined—the grass growing, the wind moving, the distant heartbeat of Wei Yan as she watched him with wide eyes.

The pill had worked.

But as the pain faded and the power settled, Shen He realized something terrible.

The woman had been right.

There was a cost.

And he was about to pay it.

title: The Scale of a Thousand Lives
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

He ran for three days without stopping, without sleeping, without allowing himself to think about what he'd left behind. The Azure Cloud Sect was a distant memory now—a scar on his soul that he couldn't quite ignore, but one he'd learned to carry.

His body had changed.

The Meridian Restoration Pill—the one that should have killed him—had done something else entirely. Instead of destroying his meridians, it had... awakened them. He could feel the qi flowing through his channels now, a river where there had once only been dry earth. But it wasn't normal qi. It was denser, darker, pulsing with an energy that made the hair on his arms stand on end.

*The pill didn't fail,* he thought, ducking under a low-hanging branch. *It just didn't work the way the ancient alchemists intended.*

He stopped at a clearing, his breath coming in ragged gasps. The forest around him was ancient, untouched by cultivation sects or mortal kingdoms. Massive trees stretched toward the heavens, their roots thick as serpents, their leaves blocking out the sun. Strange beasts howled in the distance, their cries carrying through the mist like warnings.

Shen He collapsed against a tree trunk, his legs finally giving out.

"How much longer can I run?" he whispered to no one.

The answer came not in words, but in sensation. Something was wrong. The qi in his body was building, compressing, reaching a critical point that he didn't understand. His skin tingled. His bones ached. And deep in his dantian, a pressure was growing—a pressure that felt like it would tear him apart from the inside.

*I need to refine another pill.*

He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page of notes—the only salvage from the explosion that had destroyed his lab. His handwriting was barely legible, scrawled in the desperate moments before Elder Wu's disciples had broken down his door.

*The Meridian Restoration Pill opens channels, but it doesn't stabilize them. The body must be fortified. The soul must be anchored. Without a Harmonizing Pill, the user will experience qi burnout within seven days.*

Seven days.

He'd been running for three.

*Four days left. Maybe less.*

Shen He forced himself to his feet. He needed ingredients. Alchemical ingredients. And in a forest this old, there were bound to be spirit herbs growing wild. If he could find them, refine them, create the Harmonizing Pill before his body gave out...

He didn't finish the thought. He didn't need to.

The alternative was death.

---

The forest grew darker as he pressed deeper into its heart. The mist thickened until he could barely see three feet ahead. The sounds of beasts grew closer, more hungry. But Shen He didn't slow down. He couldn't.

His meridians were screaming now.

Every step sent jolts of agonizing energy through his body. His vision blurred at the edges. His hands trembled. But he kept moving, following the faint traces of spirit energy that only he could sense.

*There.*

A patch of silver-green leaves glowed faintly at the base of an ancient oak. Moonpetal Grass—rare, valuable, and essential for the Harmonizing Pill. Shen He dropped to his knees, his fingers closing around the stems with desperate care.

"Got you."

He was reaching for more when a shadow fell over him.

"Move away from the herbs, boy."

The voice was old, cracked, and utterly without warmth. Shen He looked up to find an old woman standing over him—a crone with skin like bark and eyes like chips of flint. She wore robes of faded black, and in her hand, she held a staff carved from bone.

"I found them first," Shen He said, his voice hoarse.

"You found nothing. These herbs belong to the Gray Lotus Coven." The crone's lips curled into a sneer. "And you are trespassing on our territory."

Shen He's mind raced. The Gray Lotus Coven. He'd heard of them—a rogue alchemical sect that operated outside the bounds of civilized cultivation. They were known for their ruthless experimentation, their disregard for human life, and their absolute control over the Shadowmire Forest.

"I didn't know," he said, slowly rising to his feet. "I'm just passing through. I need these herbs to survive."

"Survival is a luxury you cannot afford." The crone raised her staff, and the air around her shimmered with dark energy. "You will surrender the herbs and your cultivation artifacts. Then you will leave this place and never return."

Shen He's hand drifted to his bag. Inside were the last of his spirit stones, his pill furnace, and the notes that held the key to his survival. He couldn't give them up. He wouldn't.

"I'm afraid I can't do that."

The crone's eyes narrowed. "Foolish boy."

She struck.

The staff came down with the force of a collapsing mountain. Shen He barely dodged, rolling to the side as the ground where he'd been standing exploded into a crater. Dirt and broken roots flew through the air. The impact sent shockwaves through his already damaged meridians.

He gasped, clutching his chest.

"You're already injured," the crone said, her voice dripping with mockery. "Your qi is unstable. Your channels are burning. You're a dead man walking, and you don't even know it."

"I know exactly what I am," Shen He said, forcing himself upright. "And I know what I need to do to survive."

He reached into his bag and pulled out his pill furnace—a small, bronze cauldron that had been his only companion for the past year. It was cracked from the explosion, barely functional, but it would have to do.

"What are you doing?" the crone asked, her brow furrowing.

"Making a point."

Shen He slammed the furnace onto the ground. He didn't have time for proper refinement. He didn't have the ingredients for a perfect pill. But he had something better.

He had desperation.

He threw the Moonpetal Grass into the furnace, followed by a handful of crushed spirit stones, a vial of blood from a beast he'd killed two days ago, and—as a final, reckless touch—a drop of his own corrupted qi.

The furnace began to glow.

"What madness is this?" the crone hissed. "You cannot refine without proper technique! You'll destroy yourself!"

"Probably," Shen He agreed.

He channeled his qi into the furnace, ignoring the pain that lanced through his body. The flames inside the cauldron roared to life, consuming the ingredients with a hunger that surprised even him. The mixture bubbled and hissed, releasing clouds of colored smoke that stung his eyes and burned his throat.

But he didn't stop.

The pill was forming. He could feel it—a dense, pulsating sphere of energy that was equal parts alchemy and insanity. It wasn't a Harmonizing Pill. It was something new. Something that had never been made before.

Something that might kill him.

"Stop!" the crone shouted, raising her staff for another strike.

Shen He didn't stop.

He reached into the furnace with his bare hand.

The heat was unimaginable. His skin blistered and peeled, but he didn't feel the pain. All he felt was the pill—its shape, its weight, its potential. He closed his fingers around it and pulled it free from the flames.

The world went silent.

In his palm rested a pill the size of his thumb, black as midnight, pulsing with veins of silver light. It was beautiful. It was terrifying. And it was alive.

"What have you done?" the crone whispered, her staff lowering.

Shen He didn't answer.

He opened his mouth and swallowed the pill whole.

The effect was immediate and catastrophic. His body convulsed. His vision went white. His meridians screamed as the pill's energy tore through them, reshaping, rebuilding, reforging everything that he was into something new.

He heard the crone scream something, but her words were drowned out by the roar of his own blood.

When the world came back into focus, Shen He was lying on his back, staring up at the canopy of ancient trees. His body felt different. Lighter. Stronger. The pain was gone, replaced by a hum of power that thrummed through his bones.

He sat up.

The crone was gone.

In her place stood a young woman—perhaps sixteen or seventeen years old—with porcelain skin and eyes that shifted between gold and silver. She wore robes of white silk, embroidered with patterns of clouds and stars. And she was looking at Shen He with an expression that he couldn't quite read.

"You survived," she said. Her voice was melodic, almost musical. "I didn't think you would."

"Who are you?" Shen He asked, his voice hoarse.

"My name is Lin Qing." She smiled, and it was a dangerous smile. "And I've been looking for you, Shen He. The Undying Pharmacist."

His blood ran cold.

"How do you know that name?"

"I know many things." She stepped closer, her robes brushing against the forest floor. "I know you were expelled from the Azure Cloud Sect. I know you created a pill that should have killed you but didn't. And I know that you are the only person in the world who can help me."

"Help you with what?"

Lin Qing's smile faded, replaced by something darker. Something desperate.

"There's a war coming," she said. "A war between the cultivation sects and the Alchemy Guild. The entire continent will be consumed by it. And I need your help to stop it before it begins."

Shen He stared at her, his mind reeling.

"Why me?"

"Because you're the only one who can create the pill that will end the war before it starts." She reached into her robes and pulled out a scroll, ancient and yellowed with age. "And because if you don't, everyone you've ever known will die."

She unrolled the scroll, revealing a diagram of a pill so complex, so intricate, that Shen He's breath caught in his throat.

It was the Ultimate Pill.

And someone else had already designed it.

"Where did you get this?" he whispered.

"From the ruins of the Gray Lotus Coven," Lin Qing said. "The same coven that tried to kill you moments ago. They've been working on this pill for decades. And they're getting close."

Shen He looked from the scroll to the young woman's face.

"What makes you think I can help?"

"Because you've already done the impossible." She gestured to his body, to the power that now hummed through his veins. "You survived the Meridian Restoration Pill. You created a pill that shouldn't exist. If anyone can finish the Ultimate Pill, it's you."

She held out her hand.

"So what do you say, Shen He? Will you help me save the world?"

Shen He looked at her hand, then at the scroll, then back at the forest that had almost become his grave.

He thought about the Azure Cloud Sect. About Lin Xue's cold eyes. About Elder Wu's sneer. About the sect master's final words.

And he thought about the power that now thrummed through his body—power that he didn't fully understand, but power that he could feel growing with every passing moment.

He took her hand.

"I'll help you," he said. "But not to save the world."

"Then why?"

Shen He's smile was cold.

"Because I want to show them all what a cripple can do."

Lin Qing's eyes widened, and then she laughed—a bright, clear sound that echoed through the ancient forest.

"Perfect," she said. "Then let's get to work."

She turned and began walking deeper into

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Demon of Autumn Leaves

The path down the mountain stretched before Shen He like a scar across the earth. Behind him, the Azure Cloud Sect's gates groaned shut, sealing his fate with a sound like thunder. He didn't look back. He couldn't. If he saw those walls one more time, he might break.

The mortal world. He'd heard stories about it—tales of bandits and famine, of cities where cultivators ruled like kings and mortals scraped by like ants. But stories were just stories. They couldn't prepare him for the smell.

The air changed as he descended. The crisp, qi-rich atmosphere of the sect gave way to something thicker, heavier. Smoke from cooking fires. The rot of fallen fruit. The sweat of men and beasts alike. It hit him like a wall, and he had to stop and breathe through his mouth to keep from gagging.

By the time he reached the base of the mountain, the sun hung low on the horizon, painting the sky in shades of blood and gold. A dirt road wound through sparse forests and empty fields, leading toward a cluster of lights in the distance. A town. Maybe. He couldn't tell from here.

He walked.

Hours passed. The moon rose, fat and pale, casting silver shadows across the road. His feet blistered. His stomach gnawed at his spine. He'd eaten nothing since yesterday's breakfast, and his body—still recovering from the explosion—screamed for fuel.

*I need food. I need shelter. I need—“*

A sound cut through his thoughts. Footsteps. Fast and light, coming from behind.

Shen He spun, his heart hammering. Three figures emerged from the darkness, their robes dark and nondescript. Bandits. They carried rusted blades and wore grins like hungry wolves.

"Well, well," the lead one said, his voice rough as gravel. "What do we have here? A little cultivator all alone in the dark?"

"I'm not a cultivator," Shen He said. His voice cracked. He hated how weak it sounded.

"Don't matter what you are," the bandit said. "You got spirit stones? We saw you come down from the mountain. Sect disciples always got spirit stones."

"I have three. You can have them."

The bandits laughed. "Three? That's insulting. We'll take your bag. Strip you down for good measure. Maybe sell your clothes."

Shen He's fingers twitched toward his pocket. His notes. The only thing he had left. "No."

"No?" The bandit's grin widened. "You gonna stop us, cripple?"

They moved.

What happened next felt like instinct. Shen He's hand dipped into his robe and pulled out a small pouch—his emergency stash. Three pills. One white, one red, one black. The white one was a Qi Condensation Pill, meant for cultivators with functioning meridians. Worthless to him. But the red one...

He popped it into his mouth.

The effect was immediate. Fire flooded his veins. His muscles screamed, his bones ached, and his vision went red at the edges. The Blood Rage Pill. A temporary enhancement that burned through his body like wildfire, giving him strength he didn't possess.

The first bandit swung his blade. Shen He caught his wrist.

The bandit's eyes went wide. "What the—"

Shen He twisted. Something cracked. The bandit screamed.

The other two rushed him. He ducked under a wild swing, drove his fist into the second bandit's stomach. It was like punching stone. The bandit doubled over, air exploding from his lungs. The third one hesitated. His eyes darted between Shen He's red-rimmed gaze and his fallen comrades.

"Demon," he whispered. "You're a demon!"

He ran.

Shen He stood there, breathing hard. The red pill's effects were already fading, leaving behind a bone-deep exhaustion. His hand throbbed. He looked down. His knuckles were bloody. The bandit's wrist had definitely broken.

*I did that.*

The thought should have horrified him. Instead, he felt... satisfied.

He took the bandits' coin pouches. A handful of copper and silver. Not much, but enough for a meal. He left them groaning in the dirt and continued down the road.

The town, when he reached it, was called Fallen Leaf. A fitting name. It was small, maybe a few hundred souls, with wooden buildings that leaned against each other for support. The inn was the only structure with two floors and a lantern burning in the window.

Shen He pushed through the door. The common room fell silent.

A dozen faces turned to look at him. Farmers. Laborers. A scarred man in the corner nursing a drink. They saw his robes—sect robes, still bearing the Azure Cloud insignia—and their eyes narrowed with a mixture of fear and resentment.

"I need a room," Shen He said.

The innkeeper, a heavyset woman with gray-streaked hair, wiped her hands on her apron. "Sect disciples aren't welcome here."

"I'm not a disciple anymore. I was exiled."

That got their attention. Whispers rippled through the room. "Exiled?" "What'd he do?" "Probably some crime against heaven."

The innkeeper's expression softened, but only slightly. "Exiled or not, you still wear their colors. I can't afford trouble."

Shen He reached into his coin pouch and placed three silver pieces on the counter. "I can afford to pay."

The innkeeper stared at the silver. It was more than enough for a week's stay. She sighed and swept the coins off the counter. "One night. You leave at dawn. And you burn those robes before you go. I don't want anyone thinking I'm harboring sect business."

"Agreed."

The room was small, cramped, and smelled of mildew. But it had a bed. Shen He collapsed onto it, his body screaming for rest. He held the crumpled page of notes up to the lantern light. The characters were smudged, half-destroyed by the explosion. But one passage remained legible:

*"The Meridian Restoration Pill requires a catalyst of pure yang energy, harvested from a source that has never known cultivation. The heartblood of a mortal who has touched the heavens."*

Shen He frowned. *"A mortal who has touched the heavens."* What did that mean? A mortal who'd ascended? That was impossible. Mortals couldn't ascend. Unless...

A knock at the door jolted him upright.

"Who is it?"

No answer. Just another knock.

Shen He's heart rate spiked. He slid off the bed, his hand reaching for another pill pouch. He'd refilled his stock before leaving the sect, but the Blood Rage Pill had weakened him. If it was the bandits, or worse, sect enforcers...

He opened the door.

A woman stood in the hallway. She was young, maybe a few years older than him, with sharp features and eyes that gleamed like polished obsidian. She wore traveler's clothes, practical and worn. And she was smiling.

"Shen He," she said. "I've been looking for you."

"How do you know my name?"

"I know many things." She stepped past him into the room without waiting for an invitation. "Close the door. We have much to discuss."

Shen He didn't move. "Who are you?"

"My name is Mei Ling. I'm an alchemist. Like you."

"I'm not an alchemist."

"Don't lie to me." Her smile didn't waver, but her voice hardened. "I've been tracking you since you left the Azure Cloud Sect. I saw what you did to those bandits. A cripple using pills to fight? That's impressive. Also incredibly stupid. Blood Rage Pills will destroy your organs if you take too many."

"How do you know about the bandits?"

"Because I was watching." She sat on the edge of his bed, making herself at home. "I've been watching you for weeks, Shen He. Ever since you copied the Meridian Restoration formula."

His blood ran cold. "You were in the sect? How?"

"I have my ways." She pulled a scroll from her sleeve and unrolled it. His notes. The complete formula for the Meridian Restoration Pill. "You're not the only one who's been searching for this."

"Give that back."

"In a moment." She held it up, her eyes scanning the characters. "You know why this pill was forbidden, don't you? It's not because it fails. It's because it works. Too well."

"What are you talking about?"

Mei Ling looked at him, her smile fading into something serious. "The Meridian Restoration Pill doesn't just restore broken meridians. It creates new ones. Better ones. It can turn a mortal into a cultivator overnight. Don't you understand what that means?"

Shen He's mind raced. "It would change everything."

"Exactly. The cultivation world is built on hierarchy. Those born with talent rule. Those without, serve. A pill that can bypass that system? It's not just forbidden. It's treason against heaven itself." She rolled the scroll back up. "The sects have been suppressing this knowledge for centuries. They killed the alchemists who developed it. They burned the records. But a few fragments survived."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I need your help." She stood and walked toward him, stopping inches away. "I have the formula. You have the ability. I've seen what you can do, Shen He. You refined a Rank 3 pill with scraps and stolen equipment. You survived an explosion that should have killed you. You're not just a pharmacist. You're a prodigy."

"I'm a cripple."

"You're the most dangerous kind of alchemist." Her hand shot out and grabbed his wrist. Her grip was iron. "The kind who has nothing left to lose."

Shen He tried to pull away, but she held firm. "Let go of me."

"In a moment. First, I want you to understand something." Her eyes bored into his. "The sects know you have the formula. The Azure Cloud Sect didn't exile you for stealing a pill. They exiled you because they're afraid of what you might create. Envoys have already been sent to every major sect. By sunrise, there will be a bounty on your head. Every cultivator from here to the capital will be hunting you."

His stomach dropped. "That's impossible. I'm nobody."

"You were nobody. Now you're a threat." She released his wrist. "But I can help you. I know places where the sects don't reach. I have resources. Connections. And I know where to find what we need to complete the pill."

"What do you mean, 'we'?"

Mei Ling smiled. "I want to help you create the Meridian Restoration Pill, Shen He. Not for you. For me."

"Why?"

"Because I have a daughter." Her voice cracked, just slightly. "She was born with no meridians at all. The doctors said she'd never cultivate. She'd never even feel qi. She's twelve years old, and she's dying. A wasting disease that only spiritual energy can cure. Without cultivation, she has six months left."

Shen He felt the weight of her words settle on his shoulders. "You want to save your daughter."

"I want to tear down the system that condemned her to death." Mei Ling's eyes burned with a fierceness that rivaled the explosion in the alchemy hall. "Help me, Shen He. And I'll help you. Together, we can finish what the ancient alchemists started."

He looked at the scroll in her hand. The crumpled page in his pocket. The world outside the window,

title: The Poison in the Pill
content:
Shen He pressed his back against the cold stone wall of the alchemy chamber, his breath coming in short, controlled bursts. Three days had passed since he'd fled the Azure Cloud Sect, and two since he'd stumbled into the border town of Blackwood—a place where cultivators went to disappear.

The chamber belonged to a man named Old Zheng, a retired alchemist who'd taken one look at Shen He's tattered robes and offered him a deal: work for free, sleep on the floor, and keep his head down.

It should have been safe.

But safety, Shen He had learned, was a luxury he could no longer afford.

"You're certain this is him?" a voice hissed from outside the chamber door.

"Positive. Matches the description from the sect bulletin. Boy, seventeen, crippled meridians, last seen heading toward the eastern border."

Shen He's hand drifted to his bag. Inside, nestled between the crumpled notes and the few remaining spirit stones, sat three pills he'd refined in secret last night. They weren't finished. They weren't stable. But they were all he had.

The door creaked open.

A man stepped through—tall, lean, with the sharp eyes of a hunter and the steady hands of a killer. He wore no sect colors, but the way he moved told Shen He everything he needed to know. This was no bounty hunter. This was an assassin.

"You're Shen He?" the man asked, his voice flat.

"Who's asking?"

The assassin smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "The Sword Sect sends their regards."

He moved faster than Shen He could track. A blade appeared in his hand—short, curved, coated in something that gleamed sickly green in the dim torchlight. Poison.

Shen He's body reacted before his mind caught up. He dove sideways, the blade whistling past his ear and embedding itself in the stone wall where his head had been.

"You're fast for a cripple," the assassin noted.

"I've had practice."

Shen He scrambled to his feet, throwing a handful of powder from his pocket into the air. The assassin's eyes widened as the powder ignited, filling the chamber with blinding white smoke.

"Tricks," the assassin coughed. "A child's tricks."

Through the smoke, Shen He saw the blade swing again. He ducked, felt it graze his shoulder, felt the burn of poison seeping into the wound.

*Think, Shen He. Think.*

His meridians were useless. His cultivation was non-existent. But his body—his strange, pill-absorbing body—had grown stronger over the past few days. Stronger than anyone knew.

He grabbed the assassin's wrist and squeezed.

The man's eyes went wide. "What—"

Shen He twisted. The assassin's arm broke with a sickening crack. The blade clattered to the floor.

"You're supposed to be crippled," the assassin gasped, clutching his arm.

"I am," Shen He said. "But cripples can still break bones."

He kicked the blade away and pressed his hand against the assassin's throat. "Who sent you? The sect? The alchemy guild?"

The assassin laughed—a wet, broken sound. "You think this is about the sect? You're a footnote, boy. A curiosity. The people coming for you? They want what you know."

"What I know?"

"The pill formula. The one you copied. The one you thought was destroyed in the explosion."

Shen He's blood went cold. "The Meridian Restoration Pill? It's a failure. It kills the user."

"Does it?" The assassin's smile widened. "Or does it do something else entirely?"

A tremor ran through Shen He's hands. "What are you talking about?"

But the assassin didn't answer. His eyes rolled back, and his body went limp in Shen He's grip. Dead. Poison from his own blade, Shen He realized. A failsafe.

He let the body fall and stumbled back, his mind racing. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula he'd risked everything to copy. The one that had supposedly failed a thousand times over.

*What if it didn't?*

He pulled the crumpled page from his bag, staring at the ancient script. The alchemists who'd created it were fools, the sect master had said. But the sect master was a liar. They all were.

And now assassins were hunting him for a formula that supposedly didn't work.

The door burst open again.

Shen He spun, ready to fight, but it was Old Zheng, his face pale as death. "Boy, you need to leave. *Now.*"

"What happened?"

"The town's surrounded. A dozen cultivators, at least. They're asking about you. Descriptions, names, everything."

Shen He's heart hammered. "The assassin said someone wanted the pill formula."

Old Zheng's eyes flickered to the body on the floor, then back to Shen He. "There's more to this than you know, boy. That pill—it's not what the sects say it is."

"Then what is it?"

Old Zheng hesitated. For a long moment, the only sound was the distant shouting of cultivators searching the streets. Then the old alchemist reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade slip.

"I was an elder of the Alchemy Guild," he said, his voice barely a whisper. "Before I retired. Before they erased my name from the records."

Shen He stared at him. "You knew who I was from the start?"

"I knew what you were carrying." Old Zheng pressed the jade slip into Shen He's hand. "The Meridian Restoration Pill isn't a pill. It's a *key.*"

"A key to what?"

"The truth about cultivation itself." Old Zheng's eyes burned with something between fear and reverence. "The ancient alchemists didn't fail. They were *silenced.* By the very sects who now claim the formula is forbidden."

The shouting grew closer. Boots pounded on the street outside.

"Take the slip," Old Zheng said. "Read it. Understand it. And for the gods' sake, *survive.*"

Shen He shoved the slip into his bag, slinging it over his shoulder. "What about you?"

"I'm an old man with nothing left to lose. I'll buy you time."

"You'll die."

"We all die, boy. The question is whether we die for something that matters." Old Zheng pushed him toward the back wall. "There's a tunnel behind the shelf. Leads to the eastern forest. Run. Don't stop running until you're sure you're not being followed."

Shen He wanted to argue, but the door splintered open, and cultivators poured into the chamber.

"GO!" Old Zheng roared.

Shen He ran.

He crashed through the shelf, found the tunnel entrance hidden behind it, and plunged into darkness. Behind him, he heard the clash of steel, the roar of qi techniques, and then—silence.

He ran until his legs burned and his lungs screamed. He ran until the tunnel opened into a moonlit forest, the stars cold and indifferent above him.

Only then did he stop, collapsing against a tree, gasping for breath.

The jade slip burned against his chest.

He pulled it out, turning it over in his trembling hands. A key. The truth about cultivation itself.

What could possibly be so dangerous that the sects would kill to keep it hidden?

He pressed the slip to his forehead, letting the knowledge flow into his mind.

And froze.

The information wasn't about the pill at all.

It was about *him.*

*Subject: Shen He. Anomaly detected at birth. Meridians not crippled—but restructured. Adapted to absorb qi through consumption rather than respiration. First recorded case in three thousand years.*

*Classification: Pillborn.*

*Status: Termination advised.*

The world tilted around him.

The sect had known. They had *always* known. His entire life—the pity, the charity, the whispered "cripple" behind his back—it was all a lie.

They hadn't kept him out of mercy.

They'd kept him to study him.

To watch him.

And when they realized he was getting too strong, too close to the truth, they'd thrown him out.

Shen He's hands shook as he read further.

*The Pillborn are the only beings capable of refining and consuming the Meridian Restoration Pill without dying. The pill does not restore meridians. It unlocks them. It transforms the body into a vessel capable of absorbing qi from all sources—pills, techniques, even the environment itself.*

*A Pillborn who consumes the pill becomes a Devourer.*

*A living weapon.*

*The sects have been hunting Pillborn for centuries. Exterminating them. Erasing their existence from history.*

*Because a Devourer cannot be controlled.*

Shen He let the jade slip fall from his hands, his mind reeling.

He wasn't crippled.

He was a *threat.*

And now a dozen cultivators were hunting him, a dead assassin lay in an alchemy chamber, and an old man had given his life to deliver a truth that shattered everything Shen He thought he knew.

He looked up at the stars, the cold fire of anger burning in his chest.

"Fine," he whispered. "You want a Devourer? You'll get one."

He picked up the jade slip, pocketed it, and began walking deeper into the forest.

Behind him, in the distance, he heard the howl of hunting dogs.

They were coming.

And this time, Shen He was ready to meet them.

---

Far away, in a tower of black jade that pierced the clouds, a woman watched a shimmering pool of liquid silver. Images rippled across its surface—Shen He fleeing through the forest, the assassin's body, the jade slip glowing in the moonlight.

"Interesting," she murmured.

A figure knelt behind her, head bowed. "The boy has the truth, Lady Feng. Should we retrieve him?"

"No." Lady Feng's lips curved into a smile that held no warmth. "Let him run. Let him grow. A Devourer must be forged in fire, not coddled."

"And if he refuses to cooperate?"

Lady Feng reached into the pool, her fingers disturbing the image of Shen He's face. "He won't. Once he learns what the sects have taken from him, he'll come to us willingly."

"And if he dies before then?"

"Then he wasn't worthy of the title."

The kneeling figure hesitated. "And the old alchemist? The one who gave him the slip?"

Lady Feng's smile widened. "He served his purpose. See that his bloodline is... *erased.*"

"Yes, Lady Feng."

The figure vanished.

Lady Feng turned back to the pool, her eyes fixed on Shen He's fleeing form.

"Run, little Pillborn," she whispered. "Run straight into my arms."

The silver pool rippled, and the image dissolved.

Somewhere in the darkness, Shen He felt a chill run down his spine—a warning he couldn't name, from a threat he couldn't see.

He kept running.

title: The Demon's Price
content:
The door opened before Shen He could knock.

Yue Ling stood in the doorway, her silver hair loose around her shoulders, her eyes sharp and calculating. She wore simple robes, nothing like the elaborate garments of the Alchemy Guild elders, but the air around her hummed with barely contained power.

"You're early," she said. "And you brought guests."

Shen He turned. Lin Xue and Chen Wei had followed him through the city, lingering at the edges of his perception like shadows. He'd hoped they would stay hidden, but of course they hadn't. Nothing escaped Lin Xue's sight.

"Show yourselves," he called out.

Lin Xue stepped from behind a merchant's stall, her sword already drawn. Chen Wei emerged from the opposite direction, his hands glowing with ready qi.

"Shen He," Lin Xue said, her voice flat, "step away from that woman. She's dangerous."

Yue Ling laughed—a sound like breaking glass. "Dangerous? I'm flattered. But tell me, little sword saint, do you even know who I am?"

"You're Yue Ling," Lin Xue said. "The Alchemy Guild's most wanted fugitive. Wanted for the murder of three elders, the theft of seventeen forbidden scrolls, and the destruction of the Eastern Repository." She paused. "They say you made a deal with a demon."

"And they say you've never lost a duel," Yue Ling replied. "But rumors are just stories, aren't they? The truth is always more... complicated."

Chen Wei stepped forward. "Shen He, whatever she's promised you, it's a lie. The Guild has been hunting her for years. Anyone who works with her becomes a target."

"Is that so?" Shen He asked. "Then tell me, Chen Wei—why did the Guild ban the Meridian Restoration Pill? Why did they lock it away and call it forbidden?"

Chen Wei's face went pale. "That's classified information. How do you—"

"Because I tried to make one," Shen He said. "And I was exiled for it. So don't stand there and pretend the Guild has clean hands. They hide knowledge. They punish curiosity. They're not the guardians of alchemy—they're its jailers."

Lin Xue's sword didn't waver. "That doesn't make her any less dangerous."

"No," Shen He agreed. "But dangerous is exactly what I need right now."

He turned to Yue Ling. "I'm listening."

She smiled, a thin, predatory expression. "Good. Come inside. Your friends can wait—or they can leave. I don't care either way."

She disappeared into the darkness of the building. Shen He followed without hesitation.

The interior was a wreck—books piled everywhere, half-finished experiments bubbling in corroded cauldrons, walls stained with something that might have been blood. A single lantern hung from the ceiling, casting long shadows that danced like living things.

Yue Ling sat cross-legged on the floor, gesturing for Shen He to do the same. He sat, keeping his distance, his hand resting on the pouch that held his remaining pills.

"Your reputation precedes you," she said. "The cripple who crushed the Grand Alchemy Assembly. The exile who survived the Sword Sect's inner compound. The Pharmacist who refuses to stay dead."

"I don't know about all that," Shen He said. "I'm just trying to survive."

"Aren't we all?" She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a scroll, old and yellowed, bound with red silk. "This is what you came for. The true Meridian Restoration Pill formula—not the corrupted version the Guild keeps locked away."

Shen He's heart raced. "How do I know it's real?"

"You don't. But I can prove it." She unrolled the scroll, revealing dense characters written in a script so ancient Shen He could barely read it. "This formula was created by the last true alchemist before the Guild purged our order. He knew the secret that the Guild has spent centuries trying to bury."

"Which is?"

Yue Ling's eyes gleamed. "That the Meridian Restoration Pill doesn't fix broken meridians. It replaces them."

Shen He stared at her. "Replaces them with what?"

"With demonic energy."

The words hung in the air like a curse. Shen He felt the temperature drop, felt the shadows press closer.

"Demonic energy," he repeated. "You're telling me the pill I nearly killed myself trying to make would have turned me into... what? A demon?"

"Not turned," Yue Ling said. "Awakened. The ancient alchemists believed that humanity's potential was limited by our physical forms. We're born with meridians, yes—but they're weak, flawed things, easily broken. They believed true power came from transcending those limits, from embracing the energy that exists beyond the mortal realm."

"And that energy is demonic?"

"That energy is *divine*," she corrected. "The Guild calls it demonic because they fear it. They fear anything that threatens their control. The first alchemist—the one who wrote this formula—he wasn't trying to destroy cultivation. He was trying to perfect it."

Shen He's mind raced. Everything he'd been taught, everything he believed about cultivation and alchemy, was being called into question. If what she said was true—

"Show me," he said. "Show me how it works."

Yue Ling smiled. "I was hoping you'd say that."

She reached into her robes again, this time producing a small vial filled with black liquid that seemed to absorb light. "This is a sample. A single drop, diluted a thousand times. It won't transform you, but it will show you what the energy feels like."

Shen He took the vial. The liquid swirled inside, alive with potential.

"Drink it," Yue Ling said. "Or don't. The choice is yours."

He thought of the Sword Sect, of their looks of pity and contempt. He thought of Lin Xue, who had once been his friend, now his enemy. He thought of the Alchemy Guild and their secrets, their lies, their fear.

He uncorked the vial and drank.

The liquid hit his tongue like lightning. For a moment, nothing happened. Then—

Pain. Pure, absolute pain, as if every nerve in his body was being set on fire. He doubled over, gasping, his vision going white. He felt his meridians—what was left of them—twist and writhe like living things.

But beneath the pain, there was something else.

Power.

Raw, untamed power, surging through him like a flood. He felt it in his bones, in his blood, in the very core of his being. For the first time in his life, he felt *complete*.

And then, just as quickly, it faded.

He collapsed, gasping, his body trembling. Yue Ling watched him with clinical detachment.

"Your meridians are absorbing it," she said. "Slowly, inefficiently, but they're adapting. With the full formula, you could skip decades of cultivation. You could reach heights the Guild has only dreamed of."

Shen He forced himself upright. His body ached, but his mind was clearer than ever. "What's the catch?"

"There's always a catch," she admitted. "The energy is addictive. The more you use it, the more you'll crave it. Without proper control, it will consume you. You'll become a demon in truth—mindless, hungry, unstoppable."

"And with proper control?"

"Then you become something else entirely. Something the world has not seen in a thousand years."

Shen He looked at the scroll in her hands. The formula was right there, waiting for him. Everything he'd ever wanted, everything he'd ever dreamed of, condensed into a single piece of parchment.

But at what cost?

"What do you want in return?" he asked.

Yue Ling's smile widened. "I want you to complete the formula. I've spent years trying to perfect it, but I lack something—a piece I cannot create on my own."

"What piece?"

"A pill. A specific pill, refined with a specific technique." She reached into her sleeve and produced a second scroll, smaller than the first. "This is the key. A supplementary pill that must be taken alongside the Meridian Restoration Pill. Without it, the transformation will fail."

Shen He took the second scroll, unrolling it. His eyes scanned the characters, his mind already working through the refinement process. It was complex—more complex than anything he'd ever attempted—but not impossible.

"Why me?" he asked. "Why not refine it yourself?"

"Because I've been banned from every major city in the Eastern Continent," she said, her voice bitter. "The Guild has eyes everywhere. I can't buy ingredients, I can't access refinement chambers, I can't do anything without drawing their attention. But you—you're still free. You can move where I cannot."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you walk away. Take the formula, or leave it. I won't stop you." She leaned forward, her eyes boring into his. "But ask yourself this, Shen He: how long can you survive without power? How long before the Sword Sect comes for you again? How long before the Guild decides you're too dangerous to leave alive?"

She was right. He knew she was right. The Sword Sect had exiled him, but they wouldn't forget him. The Guild had marked him. And somewhere out there, beyond the city walls, the world was waiting to crush him like an insect.

He needed power. Whatever the cost.

"I'll do it," he said.

Yue Ling's smile was the last thing he saw before the shadows swallowed the room.

---

Outside, Lin Xue felt Shen He's presence vanish, replaced by something cold and wrong.

"He's gone," she whispered.

Chen Wei grabbed her arm. "What do you mean, gone?"

"I mean his qi signature just... changed. It's not human anymore."

She turned to face the building, her sword glowing with ready energy. Whatever was inside, whatever deal Shen He had just made, she knew one thing with absolute certainty:

She was going to have to kill him to save him.

And she wasn't sure she was strong enough.

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Scavenger's Deal

Shen He took his first step beyond the Azure Cloud Sect's gates, and the weight of nine years crashed down upon him.

Nine years of sweeping floors. Nine years of cleaning latrines. Nine years of watching true disciples soar through the clouds on flying swords while he trudged through mud with a bucket and mop.

Nine years of pretending.
