The path wound downward through ancient pines, their branches creaking in the wind like the complaints of old men. Shen He's boots—patched in three places, soles worn thin—crunched on fallen needles. Behind him, the sect's protective formation hummed, a barrier that would now kill him if he tried to cross it.

He kept walking.

The anger that had sustained him in the judgment hall cooled as the sect disappeared behind the treeline, replaced by something uglier. Fear. Not of death—he'd made peace with death the first time he'd attempted a forbidden refinement. No, this fear was rawer, more primal.

What now?

The mortal world stretched before him, vast and indifferent. He had no family. No connections. No cultivation base worth mentioning. A few spirit stones, some salvaged notes, and a body that couldn't absorb qi through normal means.

He stopped in the middle of the path and laughed.

It wasn't a happy laugh. It was the laugh of a man who'd just realized he'd been playing chess while everyone else played checkers—and still lost.

"Get it together," he muttered, slapping his own cheeks. "You've survived worse."

Had he? The explosion in his furnace had nearly killed him. The judgment hall had killed something else entirely. His reputation. His hope. His—

No. Not his hope.

He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page. The ink had smeared in places, but the core formula remained legible. Meridian Restoration Pill. The forbidden pill that couldn't work.

But it had.

When he'd refined it, something had happened. His crippled meridians had *moved*. They'd responded to the pill's energy in ways the ancient texts said was impossible. The sect master claimed the pill destroyed meridians. But Shen He's meridians had been destroyed since birth. What more could they take?

The question that burned in his chest wasn't whether the pill worked. It was *why*.

He was still staring at the page when a twig snapped behind him.

Shen He spun, shoving the paper into his robes. A figure emerged from the shadows between two pines—a woman in her forties, dressed in traveler's clothes that had seen better decades. Her face was weathered, her eyes sharp, and she carried a sword at her hip that looked well-used but poorly maintained.

"You're the one they kicked out," she said. Not a question.

Shen He's hand inched toward the dagger hidden in his sleeve. "Who's asking?"

"Name's Mu Qing. I run a caravan down in Greenstone Town." She jerked her thumb over her shoulder. "Saw the whole thing from the observation pavilion. Quite a show."

"You were at the judgment?"

"Visiting. Had business with the sect master's steward." She grinned, revealing a missing tooth. "Best entertainment I've had in months. Kid tries to blow himself up making a forbidden pill, gets exiled, walks out with his head held high. That takes guts."

"Or stupidity."

"Usually the same thing." Mu Qing stepped closer, and Shen He tensed. She held up her hands. "Relax, boy. I'm not here to rob you. If you had anything worth stealing, you wouldn't be walking."

"Comforting."

"I'm offering you a job." She pointed down the mountain. "My caravan's short a hand. The last one got his arm torn off by a corrupted beast on the Eastern Road. I need someone who can read, write, and count without using their fingers."

"I'm an alchemist."

"You're a cripple who reads alchemy books." She said it without malice, just fact. "And right now, that makes you more useful than most. The job pays three spirit stones a week plus meals. It's honest work, and it gets you out of the sect's territory before they decide to send someone to make sure you never come back."

Shen He considered. Three spirit stones was nothing—he'd burned through fifty in his last failed refinement alone. But she was right about one thing. He needed to move.

"What's the catch?"

"Caravan's heading to the Blackmire Wastes."

"The Wastes?" Shen He's blood ran cold. "That's death country. Even Nascent Soul cultivators avoid it."

"Which is exactly why my cargo is worth ten times what it would be on the safe routes." Mu Qing's grin widened. "We're delivering purification arrays to a mining outpost on the Wastes' edge. The sect won't touch that territory—too far from their precious mountain. And you? You're already dead to them."

"What makes you think I won't die in the Wastes?"

"Everyone dies in the Wastes." She shrugged. "But you'll die slower with a job and a full belly."

Shen He looked at the path ahead, then back at the mountain he'd called home for nine years. The sect's towers pierced the clouds, pristine and untouchable. Somewhere up there, Lin Xue was probably already forgetting his name. Elder Wu was celebrating. The sect master had moved on to more important matters.

They'd thrown him away like a failed pill.

"Fine," he said. "I'll take the job."

---

The caravan was smaller than he'd expected—three wagons, six horses, and a handful of hired guards who looked at Shen He with naked suspicion. Mu Qing introduced him as "the bookkeeper" and told everyone to keep their hands off.

Their first night on the road, Shen He learned two things.

First, Mu Qing was not the caravan's owner. She was the scout. The real leader was a man named Iron Hoof Zhao, a hulking brute with cultivation at the fifth level of Qi Condensation and a personality to match. He took one look at Shen He and laughed.

"A cripple? We're bringing a cripple to the Wastes?" He spat on the ground. "Might as well bring a chamber pot."

"Zhao." Mu Qing's voice carried an edge that made even the big man pause. "He's my hire. You don't like it, take it up with me."

Zhao's eyes flickered between them. Whatever calculation he made ended with him grunting and walking away.

The second thing Shen He learned was that the caravan carried more than purification arrays.

He found out by accident. The second wagon's tarp had come loose during the day's travel, and when Shen He moved to secure it, he caught a glimpse of what lay beneath. Crates marked with the Azure Cloud Sect's seal. Not purification arrays—those would be marked with the Alchemy Guild's insignia. These had the sect's personal crest, the one reserved for restricted materials.

He didn't ask questions. He wasn't stupid.

But he filed the information away.

---

Three days into the journey, Shen He began to understand just how far he'd fallen.

The mortal towns they passed through were nothing like the sect. People here worked from dawn to dusk just to survive. They died of diseases that a single healing pill could cure. They worshipped cultivators like gods, leaving offerings at roadside shrines in hopes of attracting a wandering master's attention.

And they looked at Shen He with the same pity they'd show a crippled beggar.

He'd never felt more powerful, or more powerless.

At night, while the others slept, Shen He studied his notes by firelight. The Meridian Restoration Pill's formula was incomplete—he'd only managed to replicate one part of it. But that one part had worked. His meridians had responded. He could feel them now, twisted and damaged as they were, pulsing with a faint warmth that hadn't been there before.

If he could complete the formula...

But that required ingredients. Rare ingredients. And ingredients cost spirit stones.

The caravan's pay would take him years to save enough. Years he didn't have, not if the sect decided to send assassins. Not if he wanted to stay ahead of Elder Wu's inevitable political maneuvering.

He needed a shortcut.

---

On the fifth night, he found one.

They'd stopped at a waystation near the edge of the Blackmire Wastes. The air had changed—thicker, heavier, carrying a faint sulfurous smell that made Shen He's nose burn. The guards were on edge, hands never far from their weapons.

Shen He was checking the wagons when he heard voices from Mu Qing's tent.

"—can't keep it secret forever." That was Zhao, his voice low and angry. "The sect's going to find out we're carrying more than arrays."

"They won't." Mu Qing sounded calm, but there was tension underneath. "The manifest is clean. By the time anyone thinks to check, the cargo will be delivered and we'll be long gone."

"And if the buyer backs out?"

"Then we sell it elsewhere. There are always buyers for sect secrets."

Sect secrets. Shen He's heart hammered. He pressed closer to the tent's fabric, straining to hear.

"A hundred thousand spirit stones," Zhao said. "That's what this shipment is worth. You think the cripple can be trusted?"

"I think the cripple is useful, and useful people don't talk. Not if they want to stay useful."

"Fine. But if he causes problems—"

"He won't."

Shen He backed away from the tent, his mind racing. A hundred thousand spirit stones. Enough to buy ingredients for a dozen Meridian Restoration Pills. Enough to fund years of research. Enough to make him powerful enough that even the Azure Cloud Sect would think twice before crossing him.

But he couldn't steal the cargo. He was one man, a cripple with no cultivation to speak of, against a caravan of trained guards and a scout who'd clearly survived worse.

He needed another angle.

---

The answer came the next morning, when they crossed into the Blackmire Wastes.

The landscape changed instantly. The forests gave way to twisted black trees and bubbling pools of toxic sludge. The sky turned gray, as if the sun itself refused to shine on this cursed land. Creatures moved in the shadows—things with too many legs and eyes that glowed like embers.

And Shen He felt something he'd never felt before.

Qi. Not the refined, controlled qi of the sect's cultivation chambers. Raw, untamed qi, surging through the air like a storm. It pressed against his skin, seeped into his pores, tried to force its way into his dead meridians.

And failed.

But something else happened. A pill he'd swallowed three days ago—a minor energy condensation pill, meant to sustain him through the journey—suddenly activated in his stomach. The qi in the air rushed toward it, drawn like moths to flame.

Shen He gasped as energy flooded his system. His body convulsed. The guards shouted. Mu Qing ran toward him.

And then everything went black.

---

He woke to Mu Qing's face hovering above him, her expression unreadable.

"You're awake," she said. "Good. You've been out for six hours."

"What happened?"

"I was hoping you'd tell me." She sat back. "One minute you were walking. The next, you collapsed and started glowing."

"Glowing?"

"Like a firefly on fire." She studied him with new eyes. "I've seen a lot of things in the Wastes, boy. But I've never seen a cripple absorb ambient qi."

Shen He's blood ran cold. She'd seen. She'd seen everything.

"I don't know what you're talking

title: The Beast's Awakening
content:
The mountain path blurred before Shen He's eyes. Each step sent jolts of pain through his battered body, but he kept moving, kept walking, kept putting distance between himself and the only home he'd ever known.

The sun hung low in the sky, painting the clouds in shades of amber and crimson. In another life, he might have stopped to appreciate the view. Now, it only reminded him of all he'd lost.

*Three years.* Three years of scrubbing floors, organizing scrolls, and enduring the contempt of every disciple who crossed his path. Three years of sneaking into the restricted section, reading by moonlight, memorizing formulas that could reshape his destiny. Three years of hoping that if he just worked hard enough, learned enough, sacrificed enough—someone would see his worth.

Instead, they'd seen a thief.

Shen He's hand drifted to his pocket, where the crumpled page of notes rested against his thigh. The Midnight Lotus and Phoenix Tail Grass. The ratios were burned into his memory now, etched there by the explosion that had nearly killed him. But the third ingredient—the one he'd been searching for when Lin Xue caught him—remained a mystery.

A stone turned beneath his foot, and he stumbled, catching himself against a gnarled tree trunk. The bark scraped his palm, drawing blood.

"Watch where you're going, cripple."

The voice came from above. Shen He looked up to find three figures blocking the path ahead. Outer disciples. Young, arrogant, their cultivation bases barely above the first stage of Qi Condensation. But to Shen He, they might as well have been immortals.

"Step aside," he said, his voice flat.

"Or what?" The leader, a stocky boy with a scar above his left eyebrow, stepped forward. "You'll throw another exploding pill at us? I heard about what happened. Nearly took out half the compound. The elders said you were dangerous."

"They said I was exiled."

"Same thing." The boy's companions laughed. "A cripple who can't cultivate, playing with fire. It's almost sad, really. Did you really think you could use alchemy to fix your broken meridians? That's like trying to fill a sieve with water."

Shen He's hands curled into fists. "I said step aside."

"Make us."

They spread out, forming a loose semicircle around him. The leader cracked his knuckles, a lazy smile spreading across his face. "You know, I've always wondered what it would feel like to hit someone who can't hit back. The elders always said we shouldn't bully the weak, but you're not a disciple anymore, are you? You're just a vagrant."

Shen He's mind raced. He had no pills left, no weapons, no cultivation base to defend himself. The three disciples were nothing special, but they were still miles beyond him in raw power.

*Use what you have,* a voice whispered in his mind. *You're not helpless. You're just underestimated.*

He reached into his pocket and pulled out his notes, holding them up like a shield. "You want to know why I was really exiled?"

The leader paused, curiosity flickering in his eyes. "What are you talking about?"

"This." Shen He waved the paper. "The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula the sect has hidden for centuries. I found it. I decoded it. And I figured out something they didn't want anyone to know."

"You're lying."

"Am I?" Shen He's voice dropped, taking on a conspiratorial tone. "Why do you think Elder Wu was so eager to have me thrown out? Why do you think Lin Xue herself came to watch my trial? They're scared. Scared that someone like me—a cripple, a nobody—might have unlocked a secret they've been guarding for generations."

The disciples exchanged uncertain glances. The leader's bravado wavered.

"Give me the paper," he said, holding out his hand.

Shen He smiled. It was not a friendly smile. "Come and take it."

The leader lunged.

Shen He didn't try to dodge. Instead, he dropped to his knees, letting the boy's momentum carry him forward. As the disciple stumbled past, Shen He grabbed a handful of dust and threw it into his eyes.

"AHH! MY EYES!"

While the leader screamed and clawed at his face, Shen He scrambled to his feet and ran. He didn't look back, didn't slow down, didn't stop until his lungs burned and his legs threatened to give out.

The path ended at a crossroads. To the left, the road wound toward a small village that clung to the mountain's base. To the right, it led deeper into the forest, toward the untamed wilds where demon beasts roamed and cultivators feared to tread.

Shen He chose the forest.

The trees closed around him like a cage, their branches weaving together to block out the fading sunlight. Strange sounds echoed through the underbrush—the rustle of leaves, the snap of twigs, the distant roar of something large and hungry.

But none of that mattered. What mattered was the paper in his hand, and the knowledge burning in his chest.

The sect master had said the Meridian Restoration Pill didn't work. That it killed everyone who tried to use it.

But Shen He had read the original scrolls. He'd seen the diagrams, the calculations, the meticulous notes of the ancient alchemists who'd created it. And he'd noticed something the sect master had conveniently omitted.

The pill hadn't failed because the formula was flawed. It had failed because the alchemists had been missing one crucial ingredient.

The third ingredient.

And Shen He knew what it was.

He stopped walking, his heart pounding against his ribs. The realization had hit him during the trial, when Lin Xue had looked at him with such cold, unwavering certainty. She knew he'd stolen the formula. She knew he'd attempted the refinement. But she didn't know what he'd discovered.

The third ingredient wasn't a plant. It wasn't a mineral. It wasn't anything that could be harvested or bought or stolen.

It was blood. The blood of a Meridian Beast.

The ancient texts had mentioned them only in passing—creatures born with twisted, corrupted qi channels that allowed them to absorb and manipulate energy in ways that defied natural law. Their blood, if properly refined, could stabilize the volatile energies of the Meridian Restoration Pill, turning it from a death sentence into a rebirth.

But Meridian Beasts had been hunted to extinction a thousand years ago. Or so the records claimed.

Shen He sank to his knees, the paper crumpling in his grip. Even if he knew what he needed, how could he possibly obtain it? He was a cripple with no cultivation, no resources, no allies. The closest Meridian Beast was likely a corpse buried in some ancient battlefield, its blood long since dried and worthless.

"Damn it," he whispered, his voice cracking. "Damn it all."

He sat there for a long time, the forest growing darker around him. A part of him wanted to give up, to lie down and let the beasts have him. What was the point of fighting? He'd been born broken, had spent his entire life being told he was worthless, and now he'd been cast out with nothing but a useless formula and a dying dream.

But then he thought of Elder Wu's smug face. Of Lin Xue's cold eyes. Of the sect master's dismissive tone when he'd said the pill didn't work.

They'd called him a cripple. A failure. A nobody who would never amount to anything.

*I'll show them,* he thought, the anger rising like a tide. *I'll show them all.*

A twig snapped behind him.

Shen He whirled around, his heart leaping into his throat. A figure stood at the edge of the clearing, silhouetted against the last rays of sunlight. Tall. Lean. Wearing robes of deep crimson that seemed to drink the light.

"Who's there?" Shen He demanded, his voice steadier than he felt.

The figure stepped forward, and the light fell across a face that was both beautiful and terrifying. A woman, perhaps thirty years old, with sharp features and eyes that glowed faintly red in the twilight. Her hair was the color of dried blood, pulled back in a severe ponytail that fell to her waist.

"You're the one," she said, her voice like velvet wrapped around steel. "The alchemist who tried to recreate the Meridian Restoration Pill."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "How do you know about that?"

The woman smiled, and it was not a kind smile. "I've been watching you, Shen He. For weeks, ever since you first accessed the restricted section. I was curious to see if you would succeed where so many others had failed."

"You're from the sect?"

"Hardly." She laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "I'm from somewhere far more interesting. A place where cripples like you are given the tools to become something more."

Shen He's mind raced. This woman knew too much. She'd been watching him, which meant she had access to the sect's grounds without being detected. That alone made her dangerous.

"What do you want?"

"Your formula," she said simply. "And your services. The Alchemy Guild has need of someone with your... unique talents."

"The Alchemy Guild?" Shen He had heard of them, of course. Everyone had. They were the shadowy organization that controlled the trade of pills and elixirs across the continent. They were also rumored to be involved in far darker pursuits—experiments that blurred the line between alchemy and forbidden sorcery.

"Don't look so surprised," the woman said. "You think the Azure Cloud Sect is the only power that takes an interest in promising alchemists? Your talent is wasted on them. They see you as a cripple. We see you as an opportunity."

"And if I refuse?"

The woman's smile widened, and her eyes flared brighter. "Then you'll die here, alone and forgotten, just as everyone always expected. But that's not what you want, is it? You want to prove them wrong. You want to show them what you're capable of."

She extended her hand. In her palm rested a single pill, black as obsidian and pulsing with a faint, malevolent light.

"This is a gift," she said. "A taste of what the Guild can offer. Swallow it, and your crippled meridians will open. You'll be able to cultivate. You'll be able to fight. You'll be able to take your revenge on everyone who ever wronged you."

Shen He stared at the pill. It was wrong. Everything about it screamed danger, from its color to the way it seemed to writhe in the woman's palm. But the promise it held was irresistible.

"You'll be mine, of course," the woman continued. "Bound to the Guild through a contract that cannot be broken. But isn't that a small price to pay for power?"

Shen He's hand trembled as he reached out. His fingers brushed against the pill's surface, and a jolt of energy shot through his arm, making his teeth ache.

*This is insane,* he thought. *I don't even know who she is. I don't know what the Guild wants from me. This could be a trap.*

But the alternative was death. A slow, cold death in the forest, alone and forgotten.

He closed his eyes.

And swallowed the pill.

The effect was immediate. Fire erupted in his chest, spreading through his veins like molten metal. His meridians—those twisted, broken channels that had never carried qi—suddenly blazed with light. He screamed, his body convulsing as the pill rewrote the very fabric of his existence.

The woman watched, her red eyes

title: The Poison That Saves
content:
Shen He walked.

That was all he could do. Walk forward, one foot after another, down the mountain path that twisted through ancient pines and moss-covered stones. The morning fog clung to his skin like a shroud, and somewhere behind him, the Azure Cloud Sect's bells chimed the hour of the snake.

He'd been walking for four hours.

His legs screamed. His lungs burned. But he didn't stop. Stopping meant thinking, and thinking meant remembering Lin Xue's cold eyes, Elder Wu's sneer, the sect master's final judgment.

*Exile.*

The word echoed in his skull like a gong.

He reached into his pocket and pulled out the crumpled page of notes—the only thing he'd salvaged from his workshop after the explosion. The ink had smeared in places, but the core formula was still legible:

*Meridian Restoration Pill - Stage 3 refinement requires:*
*- Seven-leaf Soul Grass (fresh)*
*- Blood Essence of a hundred-year demon beast*
*- Three drops of Phoenix Tear*
*- One meridian of a living cultivator*

He'd ignored the last ingredient. Deliberately. Because the idea of harvesting someone's meridian while they still breathed...

Shen He crumpled the page and shoved it back into his pocket.

"Focus," he muttered. "Find shelter. Find food. Survive."

The path leveled out as he descended below the cloud line. The air grew warmer, thicker, carrying the smell of earth and wildflowers. Mortal territory. He'd only been here once before, during a supply run with the sect's后勤 team. The memory felt like a lifetime ago.

An hour later, he reached the first village.

It was a small settlement—maybe thirty houses clustered around a central well, with fields of rice paddies stretching into the distance. Farmers looked up from their work as he approached, their eyes narrowing with suspicion. Outsiders weren't welcome here. Especially not outsiders wearing the tattered remains of a sect uniform.

Shen He ignored them. He found the village's only inn—a two-story building with a faded sign reading *The Sleeping Crane*—and pushed through the door.

The innkeeper, a heavyset woman with calloused hands and a scar across her jaw, looked him up and down. "We don't serve sect trash."

"I'm not sect anymore." Shen He placed a spirit stone on the counter. "I need a room. One night."

The woman's eyes widened. A single spirit stone was worth more than this village saw in a year. She snatched it up, her expression shifting from hostility to greed. "Second floor, last door. Don't cause trouble."

"Wouldn't dream of it."

The room was small, barely large enough for a cot and a rickety table. Shen He locked the door, collapsed onto the bed, and stared at the ceiling.

*What now?*

He had no sect, no master, no future. The alchemy knowledge in his head was worthless without ingredients. The pills he'd managed to salvage in his bag were basic healing formulas—useful, but not enough to buy his way into another sect.

He closed his eyes.

*Think, Shen He. You've survived worse.*

Except he hadn't. He'd never been alone before. Even as a cripple, the sect had provided food, shelter, purpose. Now he had nothing.

Nothing except the strange, persistent hunger in his qi that pills could satisfy.

He sat up, grabbed his bag, and pulled out the three pills he'd managed to save: two Minor Recovery Pills and one Qi Gathering Pill. The latter was meant for cultivators in the Qi Condensation realm—far beyond his supposed capabilities.

But when he swallowed it, the energy flooded through him like a river breaking through a dam.

Unlike normal cultivation, where qi was absorbed through the breath and circulated through the meridians, Shen He's body absorbed the pill's energy directly. It seeped into his muscles, his bones, his organs—bypassing his shattered meridians entirely and settling into his dantian like water filling an empty well.

He felt stronger. Clearer. The aches in his legs faded.

But the hunger grew.

It was always there now, a gnawing emptiness that demanded more pills, more energy, more *power*. It scared him. Because he knew—he *knew*—that if he kept feeding this hunger, he would eventually cross a line he couldn't uncross.

A knock on the door shattered his thoughts.

"Boy." The innkeeper's voice, rough and impatient. "Someone's here to see you."

Shen He tensed. "Who?"

"Didn't ask. Downstairs. Now."

He considered ignoring her, pretending he wasn't here. But the innkeeper had his only spirit stone, and if he wanted to eat tomorrow, he couldn't afford to make enemies.

He opened the door.

A man stood at the bottom of the stairs. Tall, thin, with graying hair tied back in a scholar's knot and robes of deep purple silk. His eyes were sharp, intelligent, and they locked onto Shen He the moment he appeared.

"Shen He of the Azure Cloud Sect," the man said. "Or should I say, former disciple of the Azure Cloud Sect?"

"Who's asking?"

The man smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "My name is Wei Chen. I represent certain... interested parties who have been watching your progress for some time."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't you?" Wei Chen reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small jade bottle. He tossed it to Shen He, who caught it reflexively. "Open it."

Shen He hesitated. Then he uncorked the bottle and sniffed.

His breath caught.

It was a pill. But not just any pill. The fragrance alone told him this was a third-grade refinement—something that even the Azure Cloud Sect's chief alchemist couldn't produce consistently. The qi radiating from it was so dense it made his fingers tingle.

"What is this?"

"A gift." Wei Chen's smile widened. "A demonstration of what my employers can provide. You see, Shen He, we've been very interested in your... unique abilities. A cripple who can absorb pills directly. A boy who attempted the Meridian Restoration Formula without a single master's guidance." He stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "Do you have any idea how valuable you are?"

"I'm not valuable. I'm broken."

"Broken tools can be reforged." Wei Chen gestured to the jade bottle. "That pill is a Meridian Awakening Pill. Not a restoration—that's impossible, as your sect master told you. But an awakening. It won't fix your shattered meridians. It will create *new* ones."

Shen He's heart stopped. "That's impossible."

"Is it?" Wei Chen's eyes gleamed. "My employers have spent centuries perfecting forbidden arts. Arts that the orthodox sects would burn entire libraries to destroy. Arts that could make you something more than a cripple." He paused. "Arts that could make you a god."

"And what do your employers want in return?"

"Your loyalty. Your talents. And your silence." Wei Chen extended his hand. "Join us, Shen He. Leave the mortal world behind. We can give you what the Azure Cloud Sect never could: a path forward."

Shen He stared at the pill in his hand.

*New meridians.*

The words sang in his blood like a promise. For fifteen years, he'd been told he was worthless, broken, a burden. For fifteen years, he'd dreamed of what it would feel like to cultivate like a normal person. To breathe qi into his lungs and feel it flow through his body like a river of stars.

This pill could give him that.

But at what cost?

"What's the catch?" he asked.

Wei Chen's smile didn't waver. "The catch is that the process is... irreversible. Once you take the pill, your body will be bound to our methods. You won't be able to cultivate using orthodox techniques ever again. And there are side effects."

"Side effects?"

"The new meridians are not natural. They're forged from demonic essence, mixed with human blood and refined through methods that the righteous sects would call heretical. You will be stronger than any cultivator of your age. But you will also be... changed."

"Changed how?"

"Your qi will be tainted. Your appearance may shift. And some of our members have reported... cravings." Wei Chen's eyes flickered to Shen He's bag of pills. "Desires for things that normal cultivators would find repulsive."

Shen He's hand tightened around the jade bottle.

*Cravings.*

He thought of his own hunger. The way his body demanded more pills, more energy, more everything. If this pill amplified that hunger...

"I need time to think," he said.

"Of course." Wei Chen bowed slightly. "I'll be staying at this inn tonight. If you decide to accept our offer, meet me in the dining hall at sunrise. If you don't..." He shrugged. "Well, I hope you enjoy farming. You'll have plenty of time for it."

He turned and walked away, his purple robes swishing against the floor.

Shen He retreated to his room, locked the door, and sat on the cot, staring at the jade bottle in his hands.

*New meridians.*

He wanted it. He wanted it so badly it hurt. But the price...

A scream erupted from downstairs.

Shen He was on his feet before he knew what he was doing, racing down the stairs and into the dining hall. What he saw stopped him cold.

Wei Chen stood in the center of the room, his hand wrapped around the innkeeper's throat. The woman's face was purple, her feet dangling inches above the floor. Her scarred jaw was twisted in terror.

"Let her go!" Shen He shouted.

Wei Chen turned, his smile gone, replaced by cold, calculating fury. "She tried to steal from me. Thought she could take my bag while I was distracted." He tightened his grip. The innkeeper's eyes rolled back in her head. "I cannot tolerate thieves."

"Let. Her. Go."

"And if I don't?"

Shen He's hand dipped into his pocket, fingers closing around the only weapon he had left: the Meridian Awakening Pill. If he took it now, without preparation, without understanding the consequences...

But the innkeeper was dying.

And Shen He had spent fifteen years watching people die because he was too weak to stop it.

He crushed the pill in his palm.

The energy hit him like a thunderbolt.

It wasn't warm like normal qi. It was *cold*, so cold it burned, spreading through his body like liquid ice. He felt something crack inside him—his shattered meridians, maybe, or something deeper, something more fundamental.

Then the pain began.

It started in his chest, spreading outward like fire racing through dry grass. His veins bulged, turning black beneath his skin. His vision blurred, then sharpened into impossible clarity. He could see the dust motes in the air, the individual threads in Wei Chen's robes, the terror in the innkeeper's eyes.

And he could feel *power*.

Raw, untamed, demonic power.

The world seemed to slow as he moved, crossing the distance between them in a single step. His hand shot out, grabbing Wei Chen's wrist.

"Let her go," he said again.

Wei Chen's eyes widened. "You took it. You actually took it."

"Let her go, or I'll break your arm."

"Boy, you have no idea what you've-" 

Shen He twisted.

Wei Chen screamed, releasing the innkeeper. She crumpled to the floor, gasping for air. Shen He kept his grip on Wei

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Debt Collector's Smile

The mountain path twisted downward through layers of morning mist, each step carrying Shen He further from everything he'd known. He'd walked for three hours, his legs burning, his lungs aching, but the physical pain was nothing compared to the hollow emptiness in his chest.

*Exiled. For a pill that doesn't even work.*

He stopped at a rocky outcropping overlooking the valley below. The Azure Cloud Sect's pagodas rose behind him, their golden spires catching the morning light like spears piercing heaven. He'd spent ten years scrubbing their floors, cleaning their libraries, enduring their contempt. And now he had nothing to show for it except a crumpled page of notes and the lingering taste of failure.

Shen He pulled the salvaged page from his bag. The ink was smudged, the characters barely legible, but he could still make out the core formula: *Seven-leaf Spirit Grass, ground to powder. Phoenix Tail Root, aged twelve years. Meridian Awakening Dew, extracted under a blood moon.*

The last ingredient caught his eye. He'd never heard of Meridian Awakening Dew. The scroll he'd copied had been fragmented, parts of it lost to time and decay. Maybe that was why the pill killed people—they were missing a crucial component.

*Or maybe I'm just fooling myself again.*

He crumpled the page and shoved it back into his bag. It didn't matter now. He had no workshop, no ingredients, no future. He was just a cripple with delusions of grandeur, walking toward a world that had no place for him.

The path leveled out as he reached the base of the mountain, and the forest gave way to a small trading post. A dozen wooden buildings clustered around a dirt road, their roofs sagging under the weight of years. Merchants hawked their wares from carts, their voices carrying across the morning air.

Shen He's stomach growled. He hadn't eaten since yesterday, and his last meal had been a bowl of thin gruel that tasted like regret. He had a few spirit stones—meager currency in the cultivation world, but enough to buy a meal in a mortal village.

He approached a food stall run by an old woman with kind eyes and calloused hands. "Two meat buns, please."

The woman looked him up and down, taking in his tattered robes and hollow cheeks. "You a cultivator, boy?"

"Was," Shen He said. "Not anymore."

She nodded as if she understood something he hadn't said. "Two buns, two copper coins."

He fished out a spirit stone—a small, cloudy crystal that pulsed with faint qi. "I don't have copper. Will this do?"

The woman's eyes widened. "Spirit stone? Boy, that's worth more than this entire stall. I can't—"

"Then consider it payment for the buns and some information." He placed the stone on her counter. "I'm looking for work. Alchemy work. Is there anyone in this area who deals with pills and elixirs?"

The woman pocketed the stone quickly, as if afraid he'd change his mind. "There's a man called Old Chen. Runs a medicine shop three streets east. But he's... particular about who he works with."

"I'm particular about who I work for," Shen He said. "Thank you."

He ate the buns as he walked, savoring their warmth. They were simple fare—pork and vegetables wrapped in dough—but to his starving body, they tasted like a feast. The qi from the spirit stone had already begun to seep into his damaged meridians, healing the small tears and fractures that had accumulated over years of failed cultivation attempts.

*At least my body can still absorb pill qi,* he thought. *Even if I can't cultivate normally.*

The medicine shop sat at the end of a narrow alley, its wooden sign so weathered that the characters were barely legible: *Chen's Curatives and Cures.* The windows were grimy, the door hung crooked on its hinges, and the smell that wafted out was a strange mixture of herbs, rot, and something that might have been burning hair.

Shen He pushed open the door.

The interior was chaos. Jars and bottles lined every surface, their contents ranging from dried herbs to preserved organs to things Shen He couldn't identify and didn't want to. A man sat behind a cluttered desk, his face half-hidden by a wild beard and his eyes sharp as a hawk's.

"You're not a customer," the man said. It wasn't a question.

"I'm looking for work," Shen He said.

Old Chen laughed—a hacking, phlegmy sound that turned into a cough. "You? Boy, you look like you couldn't crush a pill, let alone refine one. What makes you think you can work for me?"

Shen He reached into his bag and pulled out the crumpled page. "I understand alchemy. Advanced alchemy. I've studied the fundamentals, the intermediate techniques, and I've attempted forbidden formulas." He paused. "I nearly blew up a quarter of my sect trying to refine a Meridian Restoration Pill."

Old Chen's laughter stopped. His eyes narrowed. "Meridian Restoration? That's a death sentence, boy. Everyone who's tried it has ended up as fertilizer."

"I know. But I also know that the formula is incomplete. The scroll I copied was missing a key ingredient—Meridian Awakening Dew. Do you know what that is?"

The silence stretched. Old Chen's fingers drummed on the desk, a steady rhythm that matched Shen He's heartbeat. Finally, the old man spoke.

"I know what it is. And I know where to find it." He leaned forward, his eyes gleaming. "But it's not something you can buy. It's something you have to earn. And earning it requires a journey that most people don't survive."

Shen He's heart raced. "Tell me where."

"The Crimson Bog," Old Chen said. "Three days east of here. Deep in the marsh, there's a shrine dedicated to a forgotten god. The dew forms on the shrine's altar once a month, under the light of the blood moon. But the bog is filled with venomous beasts, poisonous plants, and worse—cultivators who've gone mad from the swamp's miasma."

"And you expect me to go there?"

Old Chen smiled—a predator's smile. "I expect you to die. But if you somehow survive and bring me the dew, I'll give you a job. More than that—I'll give you access to my laboratory, my ingredients, and my library. Everything you need to perfect your little death pill."

Shen He stared at the old man, searching for the lie. He found none.

"Deal," he said.

---

Three days later, Shen He wished he'd never made that deal.

The Crimson Bog was worse than Old Chen had described. The air was thick with humidity and the stench of decay. Mosquitoes the size of his thumb buzzed around his face, their bites leaving welts that oozed a foul-smelling liquid. The ground squelched beneath his feet, threatening to swallow him with every step.

He'd prepared as best he could. Old Chen had given him a map, a pouch of repellent herbs, and a small knife for self-defense. But the old man's sneer as Shen He left the shop said everything: *You're going to die out there.*

*Maybe,* Shen He thought, *but not today.*

He'd been walking for hours, following the map's faded lines through a landscape that seemed designed to kill him. Strange creatures slithered through the murky water—serpents with multiple heads, frogs that glowed with toxic light, insects that swarmed in clouds so thick they blocked out the sun.

The shrine appeared suddenly, rising from the mist like a ghost. It was small and crumbling, its stone walls covered in moss and strange symbols that pulsed with faint light. The altar at its center was a slab of black stone, smooth as glass, and on its surface—

Dew. Glowing, silvery dew that seemed to hum with power.

Shen He's breath caught. *Meridian Awakening Dew.*

He approached cautiously, his senses on high alert. The symbols on the walls were old—older than any language he'd studied. They seemed to writhe in the corner of his vision, whispering secrets he couldn't quite hear.

He reached the altar and pulled a small glass vial from his pouch. The dew was thick, almost viscous, and it shimmered with an inner light that made his fingers tingle. He carefully scraped it into the vial, filling it to the brim.

That's when the ground shook.

Shen He spun around, the vial clutched to his chest. The mist had parted, revealing a figure standing at the edge of the clearing. It was a woman—tall, beautiful, and radiating a pressure that made his bones ache.

"Well, well," she said, her voice like honey laced with poison. "A little rat, come to steal from the shrine."

Shen He's mind raced. He'd heard stories of cultivators who guarded ancient sites, bound by oath or madness to protect them from intruders. He'd hoped the shrine would be unguarded.

He'd hoped wrong.

"I'm not stealing," he said, his voice steady despite his fear. "I'm taking what I need to save my life."

"Save your life?" The woman laughed, the sound echoing through the swamp. "You're a cripple. A waste of qi. You couldn't save your life if I handed you the elixir of immortality."

She took a step forward, and the ground trembled. "But I'm feeling merciful. Give me the dew, and I'll let you leave with your limbs intact."

Shen He's hand tightened around the vial. This was his only chance. Without the dew, he'd never perfect the Meridian Restoration Pill. He'd never prove the sect wrong. He'd never—

*I'd rather die trying than live as a failure.*

"No," he said.

The woman's smile vanished. "Then you'll die here, forgotten, your bones feeding the swamp."

She raised her hand, and the mist around her coalesced into blades of shimmering qi. They shot toward Shen He with the speed of falling stars.

He dove to the side, rolling through the mud as the blades tore through the space where he'd been standing. The altar cracked, the ancient stone shattering under the impact.

*Think, Shen He. Think.*

He had no cultivation. No combat skills. No weapons except a small knife and a pouch of repellent herbs.

*Wait. Repellent herbs.*

He ripped open the pouch and threw its contents into the air. The herbs exploded into a cloud of bitter-smelling dust, and the woman recoiled, her qi blades faltering.

"What is this?" she snarled.

"Something that keeps bugs away," Shen He said. "And apparently, it works on snakes too."

He ran.

The woman's screams followed him through the swamp, growing fainter as he plunged deeper into the mist. He didn't stop. He didn't look back. He just ran, the vial clutched to his chest, his lungs burning, his legs screaming.

When he finally collapsed, hours later, he was at the edge of the bog. The sun was setting, painting the sky in shades of blood and gold. Shen He lay on his back, gasping for breath, the vial still pressed against his heart.

He'd done it. He'd survived.

But as he looked up at the darkening sky, a shadow fell over him. A man's voice, cold and familiar, cut through the air.

"Shen He. Did you really think you could escape the sect's judgment so easily?"

He turned his head.

Lin Xue stood over him, her sword drawn, her eyes cold as

title: The Corpse That Spoke
content:
The mountain path twisted downward through layers of mist, each step carrying Shen He further from the only home he'd ever known. His boots crunched against loose gravel, and somewhere in the distance, a crow cawed three times before falling silent.

*Bad omen*, he thought. *But then again, what isn't these days?*

He'd walked for two hours when the forest began to change. The bamboo gave way to gnarled oaks with twisted roots that seemed to reach for his ankles. The air grew thick, heavy with the smell of damp earth and something else—something metallic.

Blood.

Shen He stopped, his hand instinctively going to his waist. But he had no weapon. He'd never needed one. Pills weren't much use in a fight.

The road ahead curved around a massive boulder, and from the other side came a sound that froze the blood in his veins.

A wet, gurgling cough.

Then, a voice. Weak. Desperate.

"Please... help..."

Shen He's mind screamed at him to run. He was banished, powerless, a cripple alone in the wilderness. Whatever waited around that corner could kill him without effort.

But the voice sounded human. And it was dying.

He moved forward, keeping close to the trees, his heart pounding so hard he could feel it in his throat. When he rounded the boulder, what he saw made him stop dead.

A man lay sprawled across the path, his robes torn and soaked through with blood. The fabric was rich—deep purple silk embroidered with silver threads that formed the character for "Medicine" across his chest. An alchemist. A high-ranking one, by the look of his robes.

But it was his face that drew Shen He's attention. Young, maybe twenty-five, with sharp features and skin so pale it was almost blue. His eyes were open, staring at the sky with a glassy emptiness that spoke of approaching death.

"Help me," the man whispered, his lips barely moving. "They... they took it..."

Shen He dropped to his knees beside him, his hands moving automatically to assess the wounds. Three deep gashes across the man's chest, each one precise, surgical. A fourth wound in his side, where something sharp had been driven in and twisted.

"This is bad," Shen He muttered. "I need—"

"Pills," the man gasped. "Belt pouch... left side..."

Shen He found the pouch and opened it. Inside were five pills, each one glowing with a soft golden light. He recognized them instantly. Grade-3 Life Preservation Pills. Each one worth more spirit stones than he'd seen in his entire life.

"Take one," the man said. "Crush it... mix with water... apply to wounds..."

"You're losing too much blood. You need to ingest it."

"No!" The man's hand shot out and grabbed Shen He's wrist with surprising strength. "Topical only. The pill... it's too strong for me. My meridians... they're shattered. If I absorb the qi directly, my heart will stop."

Shen He froze. That shouldn't be possible. Life Preservation Pills were designed to be gentle, to flood the body with healing energy without overwhelming the recipient. Unless—

*Unless the attacker already shattered his meridians completely.*

Which meant the attacker knew exactly what they were doing. This wasn't a robbery gone wrong. This was an assassination.

"Who did this to you?" Shen He asked as he crushed the pill between two stones and mixed the golden powder with water from his canteen.

"Doesn't matter," the man said, his voice growing weaker. "They got what they wanted. The formula... it's gone."

"What formula?"

The man's eyes focused on Shen He for the first time, and something flickered in them. Recognition. Or perhaps desperation.

"You're from the Azure Cloud Sect," he said. "I can tell by your robes. Outer disciple."

"Former outer disciple. I was exiled an hour ago."

A wet laugh escaped the man's lips. "Exiled. Good. That's... that's good. Means you have nothing to lose."

He coughed, and blood sprayed from his lips. Shen He pressed the mixture into the wounds, and the man hissed in pain but didn't pull away.

"Listen to me," the man said, his voice urgent now. "I don't have much time. My name is Wei Chen. I'm a Grade-5 Alchemist from the Celestial Pill Pavilion."

Shen He's hands stopped moving. The Celestial Pill Pavilion was one of the three great alchemy organizations in the empire. They produced pills for kings and sect masters. Their alchemists were treated like royalty.

"Someone from your own sect did this?"

"No. Someone from *inside* your sect. The Azure Cloud Sect."

"That's impossible. We don't have alchemists skilled enough to—"

"They didn't hire an alchemist. They hired me. Three months ago, a man approached me with a proposition. He offered me ten thousand spirit stones to create a single pill. A pill that could restore shattered meridians."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "The Meridian Restoration Pill."

Wei Chen's eyes widened. "You know about it?"

"I tried to make one. It... didn't go well."

"Then you know why it's forbidden. The formula was sealed away because it's incomplete. The ancient alchemists who designed it never finished the final step. Everyone who's tried to complete it has failed. The pill kills the user."

"That's what the sect master told me."

"The sect master is a liar."

Shen He's hands stopped moving. "What?"

"The pill doesn't kill the user. It *saves* them." Wei Chen grabbed Shen He's arm, his grip weak but desperate. "I completed the formula. I found the missing ingredient. The pill works. It *works*."

"Then why—"

"Because the sect master doesn't want it to work. He wants the formula destroyed. He's been hunting down everyone who's ever tried to replicate it. I'm the seventh alchemist he's had killed in the past year."

Shen He's mind reeled. The sect master. The man who had raised him, who had given him a place when no one else would. The man who had just exiled him with cold indifference.

Was it possible?

"Why?" Shen He asked. "Why would he want to suppress a pill that could heal crippled cultivators?"

"Because crippled cultivators are useful," Wei Chen said, his voice dropping to a whisper. "They're easy to control. They can be used as pawns, as test subjects, as sacrifices. A cripple who can suddenly cultivate becomes a threat. And the sect master doesn't like threats."

Wei Chen's hand went to his chest, fumbling beneath his robes. He pulled out a small jade slip, no bigger than his thumb. It was cracked, one corner chipped off, but it still glowed with a faint inner light.

"The formula," he said, pressing it into Shen He's hand. "The complete formula. Take it. Finish what I started."

"I can't," Shen He said. "I'm a cripple. I have no cultivation. I can't even refine a proper pill."

"You already tried. You got close. The explosion in the outer compound—that proves you have potential." Wei Chen's eyes were growing dim, his voice fading. "The missing ingredient... it's not a herb or a mineral. It's a technique. A way to weave the qi through the pill in a specific pattern. The ancient alchemists couldn't figure it out because they were too focused on ingredients. But it's not about ingredients. It's about control."

"Control of what?"

"Life force. Your own life force. The pill requires... a sacrifice. A piece of the alchemist's own vitality to complete the transformation." Wei Chen's grip tightened. "I tried it. On myself. That's why my meridians are shattered. It worked, but I didn't have enough strength to complete the process. I need... I need someone else to try."

"You want me to test a pill that shattered your meridians?"

"I want you to *complete* it. The theory is sound. The execution was flawed. You're different. You have no cultivation, which means your life force is pure. Uncorrupted by external qi. That's what the pill needs."

Shen He looked at the jade slip in his hand. It felt warm, almost alive. Inside it was the answer to everything he'd ever wanted. The ability to cultivate. The ability to be more than a cripple.

"I don't know if I can trust you," Shen He said.

"Good," Wei Chen whispered. "Trust no one. Not the sect master. Not the elders. Not even yourself." His eyes closed. "But trust the formula. It's the only thing that won't betray you."

His hand went limp. His chest stopped moving.

Wei Chen was dead.

Shen He sat there for a long moment, holding the jade slip, feeling the weight of it in his palm. Then he heard something that made every hair on his body stand on end.

Footsteps. Multiple sets. Coming down the mountain path.

He looked up just in time to see figures emerging from the mist. Six of them. All wearing the robes of Azure Cloud Sect inner disciples. And at their head, walking with the casual grace of a predator who had already cornered its prey—

Lin Xue.

Her eyes found him immediately, and her lips curled into a smile that didn't reach her eyes.

"Shen He," she said, her voice carrying through the mist like a bell. "The sect master sends his regards."

In his hand, the jade slip pulsed once, warm and urgent.

*Run*, it seemed to say. *Before it's too late.*

Shen He ran.

title: The Serpent's Invitation
content:
Three weeks.

Three weeks since Shen He had fled the Azure Cloud Sect, cast out like garbage, branded a thief and a failure. Three weeks since he'd discovered that being a mortal in a cultivator's world meant sleeping in ditches, eating scraps, and constantly watching over his shoulder.

The cold truth of it was worse than he'd imagined.

Mortals in the lands controlled by sects were little more than cattle. They farmed, they built, they served—and they died, usually young, usually forgotten. The only ones who lived past forty were those lucky enough to have a cultivator's patronage, or those desperate enough to sell their children to the sects in exchange for protection.

Shen He had neither.

He sat in the corner of a muddy tavern called The Drowned Rat, nursing a cup of water he couldn't afford to pay for. The innkeeper had given him until sundown to produce coin. After that, he'd be thrown out into the streets of Red Stone Town, where the bandits who roamed at night were known to collect "volunteers" for the mines.

"I need a plan," he muttered, staring at the single crumpled page in his hands.

The Meridian Restoration Pill was a dead end. The sect master had been right—the formula was flawed, fundamentally broken. The original alchemists had tried to force the body to regenerate meridians that didn't exist, which only resulted in the existing pathways collapsing. Every attempt in history had ended in death.

But Shen He had noticed something. Something small, something that had been bothering him for weeks.

When he'd taken the Meridian Awakening Pill, his meridians hadn't just appeared. They'd *reacted*. It was like watching a dead seed suddenly sprout roots that withered before they could grow. The pill had tried to create pathways where there were none, but his body had rejected them.

*What if the problem isn't my lack of meridians? What if the problem is that my body doesn't recognize them?*

It was a radical thought. Revolutionary. And probably suicidal.

"The Ultimate Pill," he whispered.

The concept had been forming in his mind since his exile. What if, instead of trying to force his body to accept cultivation, he could create a pill that *became* his cultivation? A pill that, once consumed, would transform his very physiology into something that could process qi directly—not through meridians, but through the blood itself?

It was insane. It was brilliant. And he had no ingredients, no equipment, and no idea where to start.

The tavern door slammed open.

A figure stood silhouetted against the dying sun, tall and lean, wearing robes of deep crimson. The moment they stepped inside, every cultivator in the room stiffened. Shen He felt it too—a pressure, like having a boulder placed on his chest.

This was a Core Formation cultivator. Maybe higher.

The figure scanned the room, their eyes lingering on each face. When they reached Shen He, they stopped.

"Out," the figure said. "All of you. Now."

The tavern emptied in seconds. Drinkers spilled their cups, chairs scraped across the floor, and within a minute, only Shen He remained, frozen in his corner.

The figure walked toward him, their footsteps silent on the wooden floor. As they got closer, Shen He saw that it was a woman, perhaps in her early thirties, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to glow faintly red in the dim light.

"Shen He," she said. It wasn't a question.

"Do I know you?"

"No. But I know you." She pulled out a chair and sat across from him, crossing her legs with an elegance that spoke of noble birth. "I've been tracking you for two weeks. You're harder to find than I expected for a cripple."

Shen He's hand moved instinctively toward the knife hidden in his sleeve. "What do you want?"

"To offer you a job." She reached into her robe and produced a small jade token, placing it on the table between them. The token was carved with a serpent coiled around a mortar and pestle—the symbol of the Alchemy Guild.

Shen He's blood ran cold.

"I'm not interested."

"You haven't heard the offer yet."

"I know what the Guild does. You hunt down rogue alchemists, steal their formulas, and—"

"And we pay extremely well," she interrupted. "Better than sleeping in ditches and dodging bandits." She leaned forward, and Shen He caught a whiff of something strange—ozone, like the air before a lightning strike. "My name is Yun Mei. I'm a senior evaluator for the Guild's Eastern Branch. And I've been watching your work since before the Azure Cloud Sect kicked you out."

Shen He's heart hammered. "What work?"

"The forbidden library was cleaned out three months ago. The sect blamed a rogue disciple who'd been expelled, but I checked the records. Nothing was missing except one scroll—the Meridian Restoration Pill formula." She smiled, and it didn't reach her eyes. "That scroll was copied, not stolen. And the copies were found in a certain outer disciple's quarters after an explosion."

"You can't prove anything."

"I don't need to. I'm not here to arrest you." She tapped the jade token. "I'm here to recruit you."

"Recruit me for what?"

"The Guild has a problem. Our best alchemists can create pills that heal wounds, boost cultivation, even extend life. But they can't create anything truly *new*. They've been refining the same formulas for a thousand years, and the results are getting worse, not better." Her eyes locked onto his. "You, on the other hand, tried to recreate a forbidden formula that nobody has succeeded with in five hundred years. You nearly blew up a building doing it, but you *tried*. That kind of ambition is rare."

Shen He's mind raced. This was a trap. It had to be. The Alchemy Guild didn't recruit failures—they either bought them out or buried them.

"What's the catch?"

"The catch is that you'll be working with dangerous materials in a Guild facility. The catch is that if you fail, you'll owe the Guild a debt that can only be paid with your life. The catch is that if you succeed, the Guild owns the formula." She shrugged. "But you'll have equipment, ingredients, and protection from every sect that wants you dead. I'd say that's a fair trade."

"And if I refuse?"

Yun Mei's smile vanished. "Then I walk out that door, and within a week, every major sect in the region will know that the boy who stole the Meridian Restoration formula is hiding in Red Stone Town. The Azure Cloud Sect has already put a bounty on your head—did you know that? Fifty spirit stones for your capture. A hundred for your corpse."

Shen He's stomach dropped.

He'd known the sect would be angry, but a bounty? That meant they wanted him dead enough to pay for it.

"Why do they want me dead so badly?"

"That's the interesting part." Yun Mei's voice dropped to a whisper. "They're afraid of what you might discover. The Meridian Restoration Pill isn't just a failure—it's a *dead end*. Everyone who's tried it has died. But you... you managed to survive the attempt. That makes you either incredibly lucky or incredibly special. The sects don't like special people they can't control."

Shen He stared at the jade token. It glowed faintly, pulsing with a rhythm that matched his heartbeat.

"What would I be working on?"

"Whatever you want. Within reason." Yun Mei leaned back. "The Guild has a saying: 'A caged alchemist is a dead alchemist.' We don't cage our talents. We point them in profitable directions and let them fly."

It was a lie. Shen He could feel it in his bones. But what choice did he have?

The Drowned Rat's innkeeper was going to throw him out at sundown. The sect had a bounty on his head. The only thing he had to his name was a crumpled page of notes and a half-baked theory about a pill that didn't exist.

"I have conditions," he said.

Yun Mei raised an eyebrow. "Name them."

"First, I work alone. No assistants, no overseers watching my every move."

"Acceptable."

"Second, I get access to the Guild's library. All of it. Including the restricted sections."

Yun Mei's eye twitched. "That's... unusual for a probationary member."

"Those are my terms."

She was silent for a long moment. Then she nodded slowly. "Fine. But if I catch you copying forbidden formulas without authorization, I'll personally remove your hands."

"And third."

"I think two conditions is more than generous for a crippled ex-disciple wanted for theft."

"Third," Shen He repeated, his voice hard, "if I succeed—if I create something truly new—I own fifty percent of the rights. Not the Guild. Me."

Yun Mei laughed. It was a cold, sharp sound, like breaking glass. "You have ambition. I'll give you that." She stood up and extended her hand. "Deal. But understand this, Shen He—the Guild doesn't reward failure. If you waste our resources and produce nothing, you'll wish the sect had killed you."

Shen He took her hand. Her grip was iron, her skin burning hot like she'd been holding a flame.

"When do we leave?"

"Now." Yun Mei turned and walked toward the door. "I have a carriage waiting. We'll reach the Guild's Eastern Branch in three days. Try not to die on the way."

Shen He grabbed his bag and followed her, his mind already racing with possibilities.

The Alchemy Guild was dangerous. Everyone knew that. They were the ones who controlled the flow of pills and potions across the continent, who decided which formulas were "safe" and which were "forbidden." They had more power than most sects, and they answered to no one.

But they also had resources. Libraries full of ancient texts. Ingredients from every corner of the world. Equipment that could measure qi down to the smallest particle.

If Shen He was going to create the Ultimate Pill, he needed those resources.

And if the Guild tried to betray him?

He touched the knife hidden in his sleeve.

He'd find a way out.

As he stepped through the tavern door, the sun finally dipped below the horizon, plunging the street into twilight. Yun Mei's carriage was waiting—a sleek black vehicle pulled by two spirit beasts that glowed with inner light.

"Get in," she said. "We have a long journey ahead."

Shen He climbed into the carriage, and the door closed behind him with a soft click.

Inside, the seats were lined with velvet, and a small lantern hung from the ceiling, casting warm light across the compartment. Yun Mei sat across from him, her eyes closed, her breathing steady.

Shen He pulled out his crumpled page of notes and stared at it.

*Ultimate Pill. Blood-based qi absorption. No meridians required.*

It was still crazy. Still probably impossible.

But as the carriage lurched forward and the wheels began to turn, Shen He felt something he hadn't felt in weeks.

Hope.

The journey to the Eastern Branch took four days, not three. A storm had forced them to take a longer route through the mountains, and Shen He had spent most of the trip staring out the window, memorizing the terrain.

When they finally arrived, he understood why the Guild was so powerful.

The Eastern Branch wasn't a building. It was a city.

Sprawling towers of white stone rose from the valley floor, connected by bridges that glowed with formation arrays. The streets were packed with cultivators in every color of robe

title: The Poison's True Master
content:
The night air tasted of rot and regret as Shen He stumbled through the outskirts of Greenport Town. Three days since his exile. Three days of sleeping in ditches and eating wild herbs that weren't quite poisonous enough to kill him but weren't quite nutritious enough to keep his strength up.

His stomach growled. Loudly.

"Shut up," he muttered to himself, pressing a hand to his abdomen. "You're not helping."

The town's outer wall loomed ahead, patched with wooden planks where the original stone had crumbled. Even the mortal world couldn't escape decay. Shen He had hoped to find work here—a healer's apprentice, a herb gatherer, anything that would keep him fed while he pieced together what remained of his research.

But the guards at the gate had taken one look at his sect robes—torn, singed, and barely recognizable—and chased him off with spears. "No vagrants," they'd said. "No cultivators down on their luck."

So here he was. Skirting the edge of civilization like a stray dog.

He found shelter in a collapsed barn half a li from the main road. The roof sagged dangerously, but enough of it remained to keep off the morning dew. Shen He settled into a corner, pulling out his crumpled page of notes.

The Meridian Restoration Pill.

The sect master had called it a failure. A death sentence in pill form. But Shen He had read the ancient text differently. The original formula wasn't flawed—it was *incomplete*. The alchemists who created it had lacked a crucial ingredient, something that would stabilize the volatile qi reaction and prevent the meridians from tearing apart.

He'd been so close to discovering it before the explosion.

A sound interrupted his thoughts. Footsteps. Light, deliberate, approaching the barn from the east.

Shen He tensed, his hand moving to the dagger at his belt. A pathetic weapon against a cultivator, but it might buy him a second to run.

"Come out," a voice said. Female. Young. Familiar.

Lin Xue stepped through the broken doorway, her white robes immaculate even in the dim light. She carried no weapon, but she didn't need one. Her cultivation base alone could crush Shen He into paste.

"What do you want?" he asked, rising slowly. "Come to finish the job? Make sure I don't embarrass the sect further?"

Lin Xue's expression was unreadable. "You look terrible."

"Three days of eating weeds will do that to a person."

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a cloth bundle. Tossed it to him. Shen He caught it instinctively, unwrapping it to find bread, dried meat, and a small flask of water.

"I don't understand," he said, suspicion warring with hunger.

"It's not poisoned. If I wanted you dead, I wouldn't need poison."

He believed her. Lin Xue was the Azure Cloud Sect's prized disciple—a genius who had reached the Qi Condensation realm at sixteen. She could kill him with a thought.

So why was she feeding him?

"Eat," she said. "Then we talk."

Shen He ate. Pride was a luxury he couldn't afford.

When he finished, Lin Xue sat down across from him, her back straight, her hands resting on her knees. "I lied to you," she said.

"You lied about a lot of things."

"About the Meridian Restoration Pill."

Shen He froze. "What?"

"The sect master told you it was a failure. That it destroys meridians instead of healing them." Lin Xue's eyes met his. "He was wrong. Or rather, he was half-right. The pill does destroy meridians—*if* it's made correctly. But the version you found in the restricted section? That was a fake. A deliberate misdirection placed there centuries ago to protect the real formula."

"The real formula?"

"The one that *works*."

Shen He's mind raced. If what she said was true, then everything he'd been through—the explosion, the trial, the exile—had been based on a lie. The sect hadn't punished him for attempting a forbidden refinement. They'd punished him for stumbling too close to their most closely guarded secret.

"Why are you telling me this?"

Lin Xue was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, her voice was barely a whisper. "Because I need your help."

"You? Need *me*?" Shen He laughed, the sound hollow and bitter. "You're the sect's prodigy. I'm a cripple who can't cultivate."

"You're a cripple who can refine pills that shouldn't exist."

She pulled a scroll from her sleeve, unrolling it to reveal a complex array of characters and diagrams. Even in the dim light, Shen He recognized the structure—it was an alchemical formula, but one far more advanced than anything he'd ever seen.

"This is the true Meridian Restoration Pill," Lin Xue said. "I stole it from the sect master's private vault three years ago."

"Three years—" Shen He's eyes widened. "You've had this the entire time?"

"I've had it, but I haven't been able to use it. The formula requires a type of qi control that I don't possess. It's not about cultivation level—it's about a specific affinity for pill refinement. A talent that the sect hasn't produced in generations."

Shen He looked at the scroll. At the intricate sequence of ingredients and techniques. At the final line, written in a script so ancient it predated the current cultivation era.

*The one who refines this pill must possess the body of a mortal and the soul of an alchemist.*

He looked up at Lin Xue. "You think I'm the one."

"I know you are. The explosion in the library wasn't a failure, Shen He. It was proof. You stabilized a reaction that should have been impossible. You channeled qi through a body that has no meridians." She leaned forward, intensity burning in her eyes. "You didn't fail that night. You succeeded beyond what anyone thought possible. You just didn't realize it."

Shen He's hands trembled. He looked down at the scroll, then at his own palms. The same hands that had been called useless. The same body that had been declared worthless.

"What do you want in return?" he asked.

"I want the pill."

"For yourself?"

"For my mother."

Lin Xue's composure cracked, just slightly. For the first time, Shen He saw something other than cold perfection in her eyes. He saw desperation.

"She was a cultivator. A powerful one. But she was poisoned by a demonic cultivator during a raid twenty years ago. The poison has been eating away at her meridians ever since. The sect's healers said there's no cure." Lin Xue's voice hardened. "They lied. There is a cure. The Meridian Restoration Pill is the only thing that can save her. But I can't refine it alone."

"And the sect master? Elder Wu?"

"They would rather see her die than admit that an ancient technique they deemed 'impossible' is actually real. It would make them look foolish. Weak."

Shen He stared at the scroll. At the promise it held. A pill that could fix his crippled meridians. A pill that could make him a real cultivator. A pill that could save Lin Xue's mother.

It was too good to be true.

"What's the catch?"

Lin Xue's expression darkened. "The ingredients. Three of them are incredibly rare. One is nearly extinct. And the last..."

"The last what?"

"The last requires a sacrifice. The alchemist who refines the pill must channel their own life force into the final stage of refinement. If they survive, they lose a decade of their lifespan. If they don't..."

"They die."

"Yes."

Shen He sat back, the weight of her words pressing down on him. A decade of his life. Or death. For a pill that might not even work.

But if it did...

If it fixed his meridians...

"Let's say I agree," he said slowly. "Where do we find these ingredients?"

Lin Xue smiled—a genuine smile that transformed her face from cold beauty to something almost warm. "I know where the first one is. But getting it won't be easy."

"Nothing ever is."

"The first ingredient is the Blood Lotus. It grows in the heart of the Black Marsh, a swamp overrun by venomous beasts and miasma. It's guarded by a demonic python that's been alive for three hundred years."

Shen He swallowed. "And the other two?"

"The second is the Starfall Crystal. Only found in the ruins of a fallen celestial palace, hidden somewhere in the Storm Ridge Mountains. The storms there can tear a cultivator's soul from their body."

"Lovely."

"The third... the third is the most dangerous. It's the core of a living demon. Not a beast, not an elemental—a true demon that has attained human form."

Shen He was silent for a long moment. Then he laughed—a genuine laugh, full of irony and dark humor.

"You're asking a cripple to hunt down a demon's core."

"I'm asking an alchemist to save my mother."

The words hit harder than he expected. Save someone. Not for power, not for status, but because it was the right thing to do.

Shen He thought of his own mother. Dead for ten years now. He'd never been able to save her.

"I'll do it," he said.

Lin Xue's eyes widened. "You will?"

"On one condition."

"Name it."

"When this is over, you help me find a way to refine the pill without sacrificing my lifespan. I'm not dying for anyone. Not yet."

Lin Xue nodded slowly. "Agreed."

They shook hands, and Shen He felt a spark of something he hadn't felt in days.

Hope.

"One more thing," Lin Xue said, rising to her feet. "The sect master knows I've been investigating the restricted section. He suspects I have the scroll. Which means..."

"He's watching you."

"Yes. Which is why I can't be seen helping you openly." She pressed a jade token into his hand. "This will lead you to a contact in the next town. She'll provide supplies and information. But trust no one else."

"What about you?"

"I'll meet you at the Black Marsh in three weeks. If I'm not there by then..."

"You'll be dead."

Lin Xue's silence was answer enough.

She turned and disappeared into the night, leaving Shen He alone with a stolen scroll, a jade token, and a promise that might cost him everything.

He looked up at the stars, scattered across the sky like scattered spirit stones.

"Alright," he said to himself. "Let's go hunt a demon."

He packed his meager belongings and stepped out of the barn, heading east toward the Black Marsh.

Behind him, a shadow detached itself from a nearby tree. It watched him go, then melted back into the darkness.

Far away, in the Azure Cloud Sect's inner sanctum, Elder Wu smiled.

"Perfect," he whispered. "He's taking the bait."

title: The Last Ingredient
content:
The mortal world smelled of smoke and desperation.

Shen He hadn't expected a welcome party at the base of Azure Cloud Mountain, but the five men waiting for him with drawn swords came close enough. Their robes were too fine for bandits, their stances too practiced for common thugs.

"Shen He," the leader said, his voice carrying the unmistakable arrogance of someone who'd been paid well. "The Sword Sect sends its regards."

Shen He's hand moved to his pouch. Three pills remained. Two healing, one explosive. Not enough. Never enough.

"I'm flattered," he said, forcing a calm he didn't feel. "But I'm afraid I don't have time for formalities. I have a schedule to keep."

The leader laughed. "You think you're walking away from this? The bounty on your head is enough to retire ten times over. We've been watching the sect gates for three days."

Three days. They'd known he was coming. Someone had tipped them off.

"Who sent you?" Shen He asked, buying time as his fingers found the explosive pill.

"The better question," a new voice cut in, "is why they're still alive."

A figure dropped from the trees above, landing between Shen He and the bounty hunters. She wore black—form-fitting silk that moved like water—and her face was hidden behind a plain white mask. But Shen He recognized the way she moved. The precise economy of motion. The absolute stillness before violence.

He'd seen it once before, in the sect's training grounds, when Lin Xue had demonstrated a sword form for the outer disciples.

"Get back," the masked woman said, her voice distorted by the mask. "This will be messy."

The bounty hunters didn't wait for introductions. They charged, weapons drawn, and the woman met them halfway.

Shen He had seen cultivators fight before. He'd watched Lin Xue cut through training dummies like paper, had witnessed Elder Wu shatter boulders with a single palm strike. But this was different. This was butchery elevated to art.

The woman moved through the bounty hunters like wind through wheat. No wasted motion. No flourish. Just efficient, brutal violence that ended with five bodies on the ground and the leader pinned beneath her boot, gasping for air.

"Who sent you?" she asked, her voice flat.

"Jade Bamboo Trading Company," the man wheezed. "They posted the bounty three days ago. We were just hired muscle—please—"

She pressed down, and the man went still.

"Jade Bamboo," she repeated, turning to face Shen He. "Does that name mean anything to you?"

Shen He's mind raced. The trading company was one of the largest in the region, with connections stretching across three provinces. But he'd never dealt with them. Never even spoken to their representatives.

"No," he said. "But someone wants me dead badly enough to pay for it."

The woman pulled off her mask, and Shen He's breath caught in his throat.

She was young. Younger than him, maybe. Her face was sharp, angular, with eyes that held a strange golden tint in the dying light. But it was the scar that drew his attention—a thin line running from her temple to her jaw, puckered and white against her dark skin.

"You're the alchemist," she said. Not a question.

"I'm a pharmacist," Shen He corrected automatically. "There's a difference."

"Not to the people who want you dead." She sheathed her sword—a blade so dark it seemed to drink the light around it. "My name is Mu Qing. I'm an information broker. And I think we can help each other."

"You saved my life," Shen He said carefully. "I'm grateful. But I've learned not to trust strangers who appear at convenient moments."

"Smart." Mu Qing smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "But I'm not here out of kindness. I'm here because I have information you need, and you have something I want."

"What could I possibly have that someone like you would want?"

"The Meridian Restoration Pill." She said it like she was commenting on the weather. "You attempted to refine it. You failed. But you know the formula."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "How do you know about that?"

"I know everything that happens in the Azure Cloud Sect. I know about the explosion. I know about your exile. I know Lin Xue testified against you, and I know Elder Wu pushed for the harshest punishment." She tilted her head. "I also know you've been copying restricted texts for the past six months, and that you've successfully refined seventeen different pills without any cultivation base to support you."

"That's not possible," Shen He said. "The sect's archives are warded. No one can—"

"I'm very good at my job." She pulled a scroll from her sleeve and tossed it to him. "Read it. Then decide if you want to hear what I have to say."

Shen He caught the scroll and unrolled it. His heart stopped.

It was a list. A detailed, impossibly accurate list of every pill he'd ever created, every experiment he'd attempted, every failure and success. The dates were right. The ingredients were right. Even the quantities he'd produced were documented with precision that bordered on obsessive.

"This is impossible," he whispered.

"And yet here it is." Mu Qing crossed her arms. "I've been watching you for two years, Shen He. Ever since you sneaked into the restricted section for the first time. I was curious—a cripple with no cultivation potential who somehow managed to bypass wards that would stop a Nascent Soul cultivator. That's not normal."

"I got lucky," Shen He said weakly.
