"You got lucky seventeen times? You got lucky enough to refine a pill that requires precise qi control when you have no qi at all?" She laughed, and it was genuine this time. "No. There's something special about you. And I want to know what it is."

"What do you want?"

"Your help." She stepped closer, and Shen He had to resist the urge to step back. "There's a pill that no one can refine. A legendary formula that's been lost for centuries. The Resurrection Pill. It can bring back the dead, heal any wound, cure any disease. It's the holy grail of alchemy."

"I've heard of it," Shen He said. "It's a myth. No one in recorded history has ever refined it."

"Wrong." Mu Qing's golden eyes gleamed. "Someone did. A thousand years ago. And the proof is locked in the Black Lotus Vault—a treasury hidden beneath the ruins of the Ancient Alchemy Sect. I have the location. I have the key. But I don't have an alchemist capable of navigating the vault's defenses."

"And you think I am?"

"I think you're the only person who could be." She held out her hand. "Help me get into the vault. I'll give you the Resurrection Pill formula. With your ability, you might actually be able to refine it."

Shen He stared at her hand. A part of him screamed danger—this woman knew too much, had arranged too much. But another part, the part that had spent years being dismissed and belittled, whispered a different truth.

This was his chance. A real chance to prove himself. To become something more than a crippled pharmacist.

"What about the ones who put the bounty on me?" he asked.

"I'll handle them." Mu Qing's smile turned sharp. "I have connections. People owe me favors. By the time we reach the vault, the Jade Bamboo Trading Company will have bigger problems than a single alchemist."

"And if I refuse?"

"You won't." She said it with absolute certainty. "Because you know as well as I do that your exile was just the beginning. The Sword Sect wants you dead. The Alchemy Guild wants your secrets. And sooner or later, someone will figure out exactly what makes you special." She leaned in, her voice dropping to a whisper. "When they do, they won't want to study you. They'll want to dissect you."

Shen He's hand trembled. She was right. He'd known it since the moment he'd been dragged before the sect master. His ability wasn't a gift—it was a death sentence waiting to be carried out.

"Fine," he said, taking her hand. "I'll help you. But if you try to betray me—"

"I won't." She released his hand and turned toward the forest. "We need to move. The bounty hunters won't be the last ones sent, and I'd rather not be here when the professionals arrive."

Shen He followed, his bag bouncing against his back. Three pills. The last of his resources.

"One question," he said as they entered the tree line. "The Resurrection Pill. Why do you want it so badly?"

Mu Qing was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, her voice was barely audible.

"Because I'm dying."

Shen He stopped walking. "What?"

She turned to face him, and in the moonlight, he saw the truth written in the lines of her face. The scars. The golden tint in her eyes. The tremor in her hands that she'd hidden so well until now.

"Meridian decay," she said. "I have six months left. Maybe less. The Resurrection Pill is the only thing that can save me."

She held his gaze, and for the first time, Shen He saw something other than confidence in her eyes.

He saw desperation.

"Six months," he repeated.

"Six months to find the vault, navigate its defenses, and refine a pill that no one has made in a thousand years." She smiled, and it was almost sad. "So you see, Shen He. We're both running out of time."

She turned and continued into the darkness.

Shen He followed.

But as they walked, his mind raced with a new thought, a terrible possibility that had been lurking at the edges of his consciousness since the moment he'd read the Meridian Restoration Pill formula.

What if the legends were wrong? What if the pill didn't kill the user?

What if it worked perfectly—but only for people with no meridians at all?

The thought burned in his chest like a flame he couldn't extinguish. Because if it was true, then the Meridian Restoration Pill wasn't a death sentence.

It was the key to everything.

And the Azure Cloud Sect had just thrown him out with the formula still burning in his memory.

He quickened his pace, catching up to Mu Qing as the forest swallowed them whole.

Behind them, on the road leading up the mountain, a figure stepped out of the shadows and watched them disappear into the trees. He was tall, lean, with silver hair that seemed to glow in the moonlight.

In his hand, he held a small jade token carved with the symbol of the Alchemy Guild.

"So," he murmured, his voice like honey laced with poison. "The cripple has found a friend."

He crushed the token in his fist, and the pieces fell to the ground like tears.

"Inform the Grandmaster," he said to no one visible. "The Pill Seeker has left the nest."

From the darkness, a shadow detached itself and vanished into the night.

The silver-haired man smiled.

"Let the hunt begin."

title: The Alchemist's Bargain
content:
The mountain path wound downward through layers of mist, each step taking Shen He further from everything he'd known. The weight of exile pressed against his shoulders, but lighter than the realization that had been forming in his mind since the trial.

*They were right about one thing.*

The Meridian Restoration Pill was dangerous. But not because it failed.

Because it worked.

He'd felt it in that fraction of a second before the cauldron exploded—the pill had *responded* to him. Not as a formula, but as a living thing. It had recognized something in his qi-depleted body, something that shouldn't exist in a meridian-crippled cultivator.

Shen He stopped walking, his hand drifting to the crumpled page in his bag. The notes were incomplete, charred at the edges, but the core formula was there. He'd memorized the rest.

"The ancient alchemists didn't fail," he whispered to the empty path. "They succeeded too well."

A twig snapped behind him.

Shen He didn't turn. He'd been expecting this.

"Come out, Elder Wu. Your footsteps are heavier than a wounded boar's."

Silence. Then a dry chuckle.

"Perceptive. I always wondered if you were truly as useless as you appeared."

Elder Wu emerged from the treeline, his gray robes immaculate despite the forest's damp. Behind him, three outer disciples fanned out in a semicircle, blocking the path back to the sect. Each held a formation talisman, their edges glowing with activation energy.

"I thought exile meant I was supposed to leave," Shen He said, keeping his voice flat.

"Exile means you're no longer under the sect's protection," Elder Wu replied, smiling. It was the smile of a man who'd waited years for this moment. "And a cripple wandering alone through demon-beast territory? Such a tragedy. The sect will mourn your passing for at least a week."

"You want the formula."

"I want *you* dead. The formula is merely a bonus." Elder Wu drew a sword from his sleeve—not his cultivation weapon, but a simple iron blade, untraceable. "You see, Shen He, I've spent twenty years as the Outer Sect's alchemy instructor. Twenty years watching talentless fools waste ingredients that could have made me wealthy. And then you come along—a cripple—and nearly succeed where I've failed a hundred times."

"Jealousy doesn't suit you, Elder."

"Jealousy?" Elder Wu laughed, the sound hollow. "No, boy. Pragmatism. If a cripple can create a Meridian Restoration Pill, what does that say about every properly trained alchemist in the sect? It says we're *inferior*. And I refuse to be inferior to a dead man walking."

He raised his hand, and the three disciples' talismans ignited.

Shen He moved.

Not fast—he couldn't move fast, not without qi. But he moved *smart*, ducking behind a massive oak as the first wave of formation energy screamed past him. The tree exploded, showering him with splinters, but he was already rolling, already calculating.

Three disciples. All at the second level of Qi Condensation. Elder Wu was at Foundation Establishment—at least the third level. He couldn't win a direct fight.

But he didn't need to win.

He needed to survive.

"Clever," Elder Wu called out. "But cleverness doesn't matter when your body can't keep up."

Another talisman blast. Shen He threw himself sideways, feeling the heat singe his back. His robe caught fire, and he smothered it with his bare hands, hissing at the pain.

*Think. What do you have?*

His bag. A handful of spirit stones. One crumpled formula.

And his body.

Shen He's eyes widened. Of course. His *body*.

He reached into his bag and pulled out one of the spirit stones—low quality, barely worth a single breath of qi. A normal cultivator couldn't absorb spirit stones directly without refining them first. But Shen He wasn't normal. His body had never needed to *breathe* qi.

He crushed the stone in his palm.

The raw energy flooded into his skin like liquid fire, burning through his blood, his bones, his empty meridians. It was agony—the same agony he'd felt during every failed attempt to cultivate. But this time, he didn't fight it.

He *directed* it.

The qi surged through his body, not settling in his dantian (which was useless anyway) but spreading through his flesh, his muscles, his nerves. His body was a cauldron, and for one brief, brilliant moment, it *contained* the energy.

Shen He rose to his feet, his eyes blazing with stolen power.

"What—" one of the disciples started.

Shen He punched him in the chest.

The disciple flew backward, crashing through three trees before slamming into a boulder. He didn't get up.

The remaining two disciples hesitated, their talismans wavering. They'd never seen a cripple fight before. They'd never seen anyone *absorb* a spirit stone directly.

"Don't be intimidated!" Elder Wu shouted, but his voice had lost its smugness. "He's burning through his life force! He'll collapse in moments!"

He was right. Shen He could feel it—the qi was already fading, his body rejecting the foreign energy. He had maybe thirty seconds before he collapsed.

Thirty seconds to escape.

He turned and ran.

Not down the mountain—they'd expect that. Instead, he veered into the densest part of the forest, where the ancient trees grew so thick that even moonlight couldn't penetrate. He crashed through underbrush, ignoring the branches that tore at his skin, the roots that tried to trip him.

Behind him, he heard Elder Wu's enraged roar.

"AFTER HIM! DON'T LET HIM ESCAPE!"

But Shen He was already gone, swallowed by the darkness.

---

He woke to the sound of dripping water.

His body felt like it had been beaten with hammers, then set on fire, then beaten again. Every muscle screamed in protest as he tried to move. He was lying in a shallow cave, barely deep enough to shelter him from the rain that was falling outside.

*How long was I out?*

He had no way to know. His bag was still with him—miraculously—and the spirit stones were still inside. But there were only four left, and each use would bring him closer to death.

*I need a better solution.*

Shen He forced himself to sit up, gritting his teeth against the pain. His body was a wreck. He'd pushed it too far, too fast. If he tried to absorb another spirit stone, he'd probably die.

But if he didn't, Elder Wu would find him. And death at the hands of a jealous instructor was far less dignified.

He pulled out the crumpled formula, smoothing it against his knee. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The forbidden formula that could either save him or destroy him.

*The sect master said it kills the user.*

But the sect master had also said it didn't work. And Shen He had felt it working. For that single, glorious moment before the explosion, his empty meridians had *twitched*.

*What if they were wrong? What if the pill works, but only for certain people?*

He thought about his body—the way it absorbed qi from pills instead of breath. The way it stored energy in his flesh instead of his dantian. The way it had *responded* to the Meridian Restoration formula.

*What if I'm not meridian-crippled at all?*

*What if I'm something else?*

The thought was terrifying. It was also exhilarating.

Shen He looked at the formula again, his eyes tracing the complex character arrays, the precise measurements, the forbidden ingredients. Most of them were impossible to find outside a sect's vault. But one ingredient—the core ingredient—was surprisingly common.

Phoenix Tears Grass. A herb that grew in any place where death and life touched. Battlefields. Graveyards. Execution grounds.

Shen He knew exactly where to find it.

He struggled to his feet, leaning against the cave wall for support. Outside, the rain was letting up, revealing a sky that was beginning to lighten. He'd been unconscious for an entire night.

*Elder Wu will have organized a search party by now. He'll have trackers, formation experts, maybe even hunting beasts.*

*I need to move fast.*

He stepped out of the cave, squinting at the pale dawn light. The forest stretched before him, vast and indifferent. Somewhere beyond it lay the mortal world. Somewhere beyond it lay the ingredients he needed.

And somewhere, in a hidden valley that no map showed, there was a place where Phoenix Tears Grass grew in abundance.

The Valley of Echoing Sorrows.

A forbidden zone. A place where countless cultivators had died, their regrets seeping into the soil, feeding the herbs that grew from their bones.

Shen He had read about it in the restricted section, in a book that was supposed to have been burned centuries ago. The book had described the valley as a place of death, but also a place of *transformation*. Those who survived its trials emerged changed—sometimes for the better, sometimes unrecognizable.

He had no choice. He had no allies, no resources, no cultivation base. But he had knowledge. And he had a body that refused to die.

Shen He began walking.

He walked for three days, surviving on wild berries and rainwater. He avoided the main roads, sticking to game trails and dry riverbeds. Twice he heard the sound of pursuit—Elder Wu's men, searching for him—and twice he hid, pressing himself into hollow logs and muddy ditches, holding his breath until they passed.

On the fourth day, he found the entrance to the valley.

It was marked by a stone archway, ancient and crumbling, covered in warning inscriptions that had long since faded. The air on the other side felt *wrong*—heavy with death qi, thick with the scent of blood and rot.

Shen He hesitated at the threshold.

*This is insane. I should turn back. I should find a mortal village, disappear into obscurity, live out my pathetic life as a cripple.*

But he couldn't. Not after what he'd discovered. Not after feeling that spark of possibility.

He stepped through the archway.

The world changed.

The sky turned gray, the light dimming as if the sun itself had abandoned this place. The trees were black and twisted, their branches reaching out like grasping hands. And the ground—the ground was *soft*, spongy with centuries of decay.

Shen He took a step forward, and his foot sank into something that squelched.

He looked down.

A bone, bleached white and ancient, protruded from the soil. And beyond it, a hundred more, scattered across the valley floor like forgotten memories.

*This is where they died. All of them.*

He swallowed his fear and kept walking.

The Phoenix Tears Grass grew near the valley's center, according to the book. It would be guarded, protected by something that had killed everyone who'd come before. But Shen He didn't care. He had nothing left to lose.

He found the grass three hours later, growing in a circle around a small, clear pond. The water was perfectly still, reflecting nothing—as if it absorbed light instead of bouncing it back.

And standing beside the pond, her robes pristine, her expression unreadable, was Lin Xue.

"You're alive," she said. "Good. I was worried I'd have to dig up your corpse."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "Did Elder Wu send you to finish the job?"

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Poison That Heals

The Black Lotus Pavilion stood at the edge of the Crimson Serpent Bazaar, its walls draped in silks that shifted between colors like oil on water. Shen He had passed it a dozen times in the past three days, always finding an excuse to walk the other way.

Today, he had run out of excuses.

His fingers brushed the jade token in his pocket—the one the stranger had pressed into his palm before vanishing into the crowd. *"When you're ready to learn what the sects don't teach, come find me."* The words had haunted him ever since.

The pavilion's entrance was a gaping maw of darkness, unlit by any lamp or candle. Shen He took a breath and stepped inside.

The darkness lasted exactly three steps. Then the world exploded into light.

He found himself in a circular chamber lined with shelves that stretched up into shadow. On each shelf sat bottles of every shape and size—crystal vials, clay jars, iron flasks, bamboo tubes, and things that looked like they'd been carved from bone. Some glowed with internal light. Others seemed to drink the light around them.

"Three days." A voice came from everywhere and nowhere. "I expected you sooner."

Shen He turned, searching for the speaker. "I had things to prepare."

"Liar." A woman stepped out from behind a curtain of hanging beads. She was older than he'd expected—perhaps forty, with streaks of grey in her black hair and lines around her eyes that spoke of decades of hard living. But her movements were fluid, graceful, the movements of a cultivator who had long since mastered her body. "You were afraid. Good. Fear keeps fools alive."

"I'm not a fool."

"No?" She circled him, and Shen He felt like a specimen under examination. "You're an expelled disciple with no cultivation base, no backing, and a bounty on your head for stealing forbidden techniques. You walked into a Black Lotus pavilion—the most notorious information den in the eastern provinces—without so much as a defensive talisman. If that's not foolish, I don't know what is."

Shen He's hand went to his pocket. "I have the token—"

"I know what you have." She stopped in front of him, close enough that he could smell the herbs on her breath. "I gave it to you. The question is whether you know what you're getting into."

"You said you could teach me things the sects don't teach."

"I said I could teach you what the sects don't teach." She smiled, and there was nothing friendly about it. "There's a difference. The sects don't teach you because they don't know. I don't teach you because the knowledge will kill you if you're not ready."

Shen He felt the anger rise, hot and familiar. "I've been told that my whole life. That I'm not ready. That I'm too weak. That I should know my place." He met her eyes. "I'm still alive."

"Barely." She turned and walked toward a desk cluttered with papers and half-empty tea cups. "But alive is enough. Sit."

He sat.

The woman—she still hadn't given him her name—poured two cups of tea. The liquid was black as ink, and it steamed despite the heat of the room. "Drink."

Shen He looked at the cup. The tea smelled wrong. Not spoiled, but... different. Like medicine and rot and something sweet underneath.

"It won't kill you," she said. "Probably."

He drank.

The tea hit his stomach like a punch. For a moment, everything went white. Then the pain faded, leaving behind a warmth that spread through his limbs, his chest, his head. He felt his meridians—broken as they were—pulse with something that wasn't quite qi.

"What was that?"

"A test." She sipped her own tea, unbothered. "You passed."

"What kind of test?"

"The kind that tells me your body can handle what I'm about to teach you." She set down her cup. "You're interesting, Shen He. Do you know why?"

He shook his head.

"Because you're a contradiction. You have no cultivation base, yet you absorbed a fifth-grade Meridian Restoration Pill—poorly made as it was—without dying. You have no spiritual roots, yet you can sense the properties of herbs with an accuracy that would make master alchemists jealous. And you have no teacher, yet you managed to reconstruct a forbidden formula from a single fragmentary scroll."

She leaned forward. "That's not talent. That's something else entirely."

"What is it?"

"I don't know yet." She smiled again, and this time there was something almost genuine in it. "But I intend to find out. And in the process, I might teach you a few things."

"You haven't told me your name."

"I haven't decided if you deserve it yet." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. "But I'll give you this. A gift. Consider it an investment."

Shen He unrolled the scroll. His eyes went wide.

"This is... this is a complete refinement method for the Meridian Restoration Pill."

"Obviously. The version you found was a fragment. This one has all six stages, including the purification technique that prevents the pill from turning into a poison."

Shen He's hands trembled. This was it. The key to everything. With this, he could—

"The pill won't fix you."

He looked up. "What?"

"The Meridian Restoration Pill restores damaged meridians. Yours aren't damaged, Shen He. They never developed in the first place. It's a congenital condition, not an injury." She reached for a book on her shelf and flipped it open to a page marked with a red ribbon. "See here? 'Meridian genesis occurs during the first breath of life. Those born without complete meridians cannot cultivate, regardless of external intervention.'"

Shen He felt the hope drain out of him. "Then why did you give me this?"

"Because you need to understand what you're working with." She closed the book. "The Meridian Restoration Pill won't fix you. But it might break you open enough for something else to grow."

"What something else?"

"That's what we're going to find out." She stood, and Shen He realized the interview was over. "Study the scroll. Memorize it. Then burn it. Come back in three days, and we'll begin your real education."

She turned away, dismissing him.

Shen He stood, the scroll clutched to his chest. "One more question."

"Ask."

"Why help me? You don't know me. I have nothing to offer."

She was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, her voice was different. Softer. Almost sad.

"Because I knew someone like you once. Someone who was told they couldn't. Someone who refused to believe it." She looked over her shoulder, and her eyes held a depth of pain that made Shen He's breath catch. "She proved them wrong. But it cost her everything."

"Did she survive?"

"No." The word hung in the air like a curse. "But she changed the world before she died. That's more than most cultivators can say."

Shen He wanted to ask more, but the woman raised her hand. "Enough. Go. Three days."

He left the pavilion with the scroll hidden beneath his robes, his mind racing with possibilities and fears.

Three days.

Three days until he started learning what the sects didn't teach.

Three days until he found out if he could become something other than a cripple.

He was halfway back to his rented room when he felt it—a shift in the air, a presence that hadn't been there before. He stopped, his hand going to the knife at his belt.

"Who's there?"

A figure stepped out of an alley. Tall. Dressed in grey. His face was hidden behind a cloth mask, but his eyes—cold, calculating, familiar.

"You have something that doesn't belong to you, cripple."

Shen He recognized the voice. He'd heard it three years ago, on the worst night of his life.

The night his sister disappeared.

"Where is she?" The words tore out of him before he could stop them.

The figure tilted his head. "The girl? She's alive. For now." He took a step forward. "But she won't stay that way if you don't hand over what you stole."

"I didn't steal anything."

"You stole from the Azure Cloud Sect. You stole forbidden knowledge. And now you've made contact with the Black Lotus." The figure's hand moved, and a blade appeared, gleaming with a faint green light. Poisoned. "Give me the scroll, and I'll let you live."

Shen He's mind raced. This man knew about his sister. Knew about the scroll. Knew about the Black Lotus.

"How do you know all this?"

"I know everything that happens in this city." The figure took another step. "Last chance, cripple. The scroll. Or your sister dies."

Shen He's hand tightened on the scroll beneath his robes.

He could give it up. Save his sister. Find another way.

But if he gave it up, he'd never know what he could become. He'd never be strong enough to save anyone.

"I don't have it," he said.

The figure laughed. "Liar."

"Search me if you don't believe me."

The figure's eyes narrowed. He took a step closer, close enough that Shen He could smell the rot on his breath. "You're bluffing."

Shen He met his gaze. "Try me."

For a long moment, they stood frozen. Then the figure smiled behind his mask.

"Interesting. You've grown a spine since the last time we met." He stepped back, sheathing his blade. "Very well. Keep the scroll. But know this—I'll be watching. And when you least expect it, I'll come for what's mine."

He vanished into the shadows, leaving Shen He alone in the empty street.

Shen He stood there, his heart pounding, his hands shaking.

His sister was alive.

And someone was watching.

He ran back to his room, locked the door, and unrolled the scroll with trembling fingers. The characters swam before his eyes, but he forced himself to focus.

He had three days to learn everything he could.

Three days before his real education began.

And somewhere out there, his sister was waiting for him.

He didn't sleep that night. He read by candlelight until his eyes burned, memorizing every character, every diagram, every footnote. By dawn, he had the entire refinement method committed to memory.

Then he burned the scroll, just as the woman had instructed.

The flames consumed the paper, and Shen He watched his only chance at normal cultivation turn to ash.

But something else had taken root in his chest. A new understanding. A new possibility.

The scroll had said the Meridian Restoration Pill couldn't fix congenital conditions.

But it had also mentioned something else—a footnote, barely legible, that the woman's version of the scroll had preserved.

*A refinement of the technique, using the Pill as a base rather than a solution, may open paths previously thought impossible.*

It was a single sentence. A hint. A possibility.

Shen He smiled.

Three days.

Then he would find out if impossible was just another word for *not yet*.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Debt Collector's Return

Three weeks had passed since Shen He found refuge in the Hollow Flame Sect. Three weeks of hiding, of healing, of slowly rebuilding his shattered cultivation base. Three weeks of waiting for the other shoe to drop.

It dropped on a rain-soaked evening.

Shen He sat cross-legged in his assigned chambers, a modest stone room carved into the cliffside. Before him floated three pills—his latest attempt at the Meridian Restoration formula, modified with the Hollow Flame Sect's unique ingredients. The pills rotated slowly in a sphere of controlled qi, their surfaces rippling with unstable energy.

*Closer,* he thought. *But not close enough.*

The explosion three weeks ago had cost him everything. His home. His identity. Nearly his life. But it had also taught him something invaluable: the original formula was fundamentally flawed. The ancient alchemists had tried to force meridians to regenerate through pure spiritual pressure. That approach didn't work because it treated the symptom, not the cause.

Meridians weren't just energy channels. They were living tissue. They needed to be *fed*, not forced.

Shen He had spent the last twenty-one days testing this theory. He'd consumed over forty experimental pills, each one slightly different in composition. The results were promising. His damaged meridians had begun to show faint signs of regeneration—tiny sparks of green where before there had been only dead gray.

But the process was slow. Agonizingly slow. At this rate, it would take years to fully restore his cultivation.

*I don't have years.*

A knock at his door shattered his concentration. The pills wobbled, nearly falling from their qi sphere before he caught them and set them aside.

"Enter."

The door slid open to reveal a young woman in Hollow Flame robes. Her face was familiar—one of the outer disciples assigned to "monitor" him. Her expression today was different, though. Tense. Worried.

"Senior Shen," she said, bowing. "You have a visitor."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "A visitor? Who knows I'm here?"

"The sect master has been... discreet about your presence. But word travels." She hesitated. "It's a woman. She says she's from the Azure Cloud Sect. She demands to see you."

Lin Xue.

Shen He's mind raced. How had she found him? The Hollow Flame Sect was three provinces away from Azure Cloud. He'd taken every precaution to cover his tracks. Unless—

"Did she come alone?"

"No, Senior. She brought two elders. They're waiting in the main hall."

*Two elders.* That meant this wasn't a social call. This was an extraction.

"Tell them I'm not here," Shen He said. "Tell them I left yesterday. Tell them anything."

The disciple shook her head. "The sect master already confirmed your presence. He... he didn't have a choice. The Azure Cloud Sect holds significant political power. Refusing would cause an incident."

Of course. Politics always won over principle.

Shen He stood, his body protesting after hours of meditation. "Then I suppose I have no choice. Lead the way."

---

The main hall of the Hollow Flame Sect was a grand chamber carved into the mountain's heart, its walls lined with ever-burning torches that cast dancing shadows across ancient murals. At the center of the room stood three figures, their Azure Cloud robes a stark blue against the orange firelight.

Lin Xue looked exactly as Shen He remembered—flawless, cold, untouchable. Her cultivation had grown in the weeks since his exile. He could feel it now, the way her qi pressed against his senses like a blade to the throat. She was stronger. Much stronger.

Beside her stood Elder Wu, his thin face twisted in familiar contempt. The third figure was unfamiliar—a middle-aged man with graying temples and eyes that missed nothing. An elder Shen He had never seen before.

"Shen He," Lin Xue said, her voice carrying no warmth. "You've been difficult to find."

"I wasn't aware I was being sought." He kept his voice level, betraying nothing. "I was under the impression that your sect wanted nothing more to do with me."

"Circumstances change."

The unfamiliar elder stepped forward. His presence was immense—not aggressive, but absolute. He studied Shen He with the careful attention of a scholar examining a rare specimen.

"Young man," he said, "my name is Elder Feng. I am the Azure Cloud Sect's chief archivist. I've come to discuss the events surrounding your... departure."

"Discuss?" Shen He almost laughed. "You exiled me. What's left to discuss?"

"That was a decision made in haste," Elder Feng said. "Based on incomplete information."

Elder Wu's face reddened. "Incomplete? The boy stole a forbidden formula. He nearly destroyed—"

"I am aware of what happened, Wu." Elder Feng's voice carried a sharp edge. "I am also aware that the restricted section was left unguarded that night. That the seals on the formula's container were weaker than they should have been. That someone *wanted* that scroll to be taken."

The words hung in the air like a suspended blade.

Shen He's mind snapped into focus. He'd always wondered why the restricted section had been so easy to access that night. Why the seals had crumbled at his touch. He'd assumed it was luck. But maybe—

"Someone set me up," he said slowly.

"Possibly," Elder Feng said. "Or someone wanted to test whether you could succeed where others had failed."

"And you're only investigating this *now*?" Anger crept into Shen He's voice. "After I was publicly humiliated. After I was exiled. After I nearly died from the backlash of a flawed formula."

"We had to be sure." Elder Feng's eyes never left Shen He's. "The Azure Cloud Sect has enemies, young man. Enemies who would love to see our greatest secrets fall into the wrong hands. We needed to know whether you were a thief or a pawn."

"Which am I?"

"That depends on what you've discovered in your time here."

Shen He felt the trap closing around him. They didn't want him back. They wanted his research. The Hollow Flame Sect had given him sanctuary, resources, ingredients. But he'd been careful not to share his findings—not fully. They thought he was just recovering, not *experimenting*.

Now the Azure Cloud Sect wanted to collect.

"The formula is poison," Shen He said flatly. "You already know that. The sect master told me himself."

"It doesn't work," Elder Feng agreed. "But you've been here for three weeks. And you're still alive. That's more than anyone else who's attempted the formula can claim."

"Maybe I just didn't take it yet."

"Don't lie to me, boy." Elder Feng's voice dropped, and suddenly the temperature in the room plummeted. "I can see the traces of pill refinement on your qi. You've consumed dozens of pills since arriving here. And your meridians—" He paused, his eyes narrowing. "Your meridians are *regenerating*."

The hall fell silent.

Lin Xue's composure cracked for just a moment. She stared at Shen He with something that might have been shock. Or fear.

"That's impossible," Elder Wu sputtered. "He's meridian-crippled. He was born that way. You can't regenerate what was never there."

"And yet." Elder Feng took a step closer to Shen He. "Show me. Show me what you've discovered."

Shen He's hands clenched at his sides. Everything inside him screamed to refuse. This knowledge was his—his alone. He'd paid for it in blood and pain and exile. They had no right to demand it.

But Elder Feng's cultivation was immense. And Lin Xue was watching him with those cold, calculating eyes. And somewhere in the shadows of this hall, he knew the Hollow Flame Sect's master was watching too, waiting to see where his loyalties lay.

He was trapped.

"Fine," Shen He said through gritted teeth. "I'll show you."

He reached into his robe and pulled out a single pill—one of his experimental formulas, still imperfect but functional. It glowed with a faint green light, pulsing like a living heart.

"The original formula tried to force meridian regeneration through spiritual pressure," he said. "That approach fails because it damages the meridian walls faster than they can heal. The correct approach is to provide the body with the raw materials it needs to heal itself, while using the pill's qi to guide the regeneration process. It's slower. But it works."

He tossed the pill to Elder Feng, who caught it with practiced ease.

"This one's incomplete," Shen He continued. "It can regenerate minor damage, but not full restoration. I need at least six more months of refinement before I can create the final version."

"Six months." Elder Feng examined the pill, his eyes gleaming with scholarly interest. "And if we provided you with resources? Access to the Azure Cloud Sect's full library?"

"Then I'd need three months."

"Then it's settled." Elder Feng pocketed the pill. "You'll return with us to the sect. Under my personal supervision. Your exile will be rescinded."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you'll be considered an enemy of the sect." Lin Xue's voice cut through the air like a blade. "And treated accordingly."

Shen He looked at her—really looked. There was something in her eyes that hadn't been there before. Not just coldness. Something else. Something that looked almost like *desperation*.

"Why do you care so much?" he asked. "You were the one who demanded my exile. You were the one who called me a thief. Why the sudden change of heart?"

Lin Xue's jaw tightened. "That's none of your concern."

"Everything about this is my concern." He turned to Elder Feng. "I'll return to the sect. But not as a prisoner. I want full access to the restricted section. I want my own workshop. I want—"

"You're in no position to demand anything," Elder Wu snarled.

"Then I'll stay here." Shen He crossed his arms. "The Hollow Flame Sect has been generous. I'm sure they'd love to have exclusive access to my research."

"You wouldn't."

"Try me."

Elder Feng laughed—a dry, rasping sound that echoed through the hall. "You have your father's stubbornness, boy. Very well. You'll have your workshop and your access. But the sect master will want to meet with you personally. And Lin Xue will be your... handler."

"My what?"

"Your guardian," Lin Xue said, her voice flat. "I'm to ensure you don't run. And that you don't share your research with anyone outside the sect."

"Wonderful." Shen He's voice dripped with sarcasm. "Just like old times."

He turned to leave, but Lin Xue's voice stopped him.

"One more thing, Shen He."

He looked back.

"Your mother is alive."

The words hit him like a physical blow. His mother had died when he was seven. He'd watched her body burn on the funeral pyre. He'd scattered her ashes in the river.

"That's not funny," he whispered.

"I'm not joking." Lin Xue's face was expressionless. "She was taken from the sect the night of her 'death.' Replaced with a corpse that looked enough like her to fool everyone. We only discovered the truth three days ago."

"She's alive? Where is she?"

"That's what we need you to find out."

Shen He's world tilted. His mother. The only person who had ever believed in him. The only person who had told him his crippled meridians didn't define him.

She was alive.

And someone had taken her.

He looked at Lin Xue, at

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Emperor's Alchemist

The road to the capital stretched before Shen He like a serpent of packed earth and scattered stones. Six days had passed since he'd left the Azure Cloud Sect, and each day had taught him something new about the world beyond the mountain's shadow.

First lesson: the mortal realm was loud.

Merchants shouted over each other at crossroads markets. Children screamed and laughed in equal measure. Carts creaked under mountains of goods, their wheels groaning like wounded beasts. The constant noise pressed against his skull, a far cry from the disciplined silence of the sect.

Second lesson: spirit stones bought everything, but attracted the wrong kind of attention.

He'd learned that one the hard way when three men had tried to rob him on the second night. They'd learned that a "cripple" could still break bones with surprising efficiency. Shen He had left them groaning in a ditch, their stolen spirit stones scattered across the road.

Third lesson: the Alchemy Guild's reach was longer than he'd imagined.

Every city he passed had a guild hall. Every guild hall had a notice board. And every notice board had his face sketched in charcoal, accompanied by the words: "WANTED: Shen He, former disciple of Azure Cloud Sect. Suspected of stealing forbidden alchemical formulas. Reward: 500 spirit stones."

Five hundred. For a cripple. He almost felt flattered.

The capital's walls appeared on the horizon as the sun began to set, painting the stone in shades of amber and blood. Shen He stopped at the crest of a hill, taking in the sight. The Imperial City of Vermilion Dawn was everything the sect wasn't—grand, bustling, and utterly indifferent to the plight of a single cultivator.

Massive walls rose fifty feet high, their surfaces etched with defensive formations that pulsed with faint blue light. Guards in crimson armor stood at attention on the battlements, their eyes scanning the approaching roads. Beyond the walls, towers and pagodas stretched toward the sky, their roofs curved like the wings of resting birds.

"The alchemist registry," Shen He muttered, pulling out the crumpled page from his bag. The salvage notes described a technique called "Meridian Weaving"—a theoretical method for repairing damaged cultivation channels without the forbidden pill. But to attempt it, he'd need ingredients. Expensive ingredients. The kind that required guild membership to purchase.

The kind that required a license.

He tucked the notes away and started down the hill. The guards at the main gate stopped him, their spears crossing to block his path.

"State your business," one said, his voice flat and professional.

"I'm an alchemist," Shen He said. "Here to register with the guild."

The guard's eyes narrowed. "Alchemists carry identification tokens. Show yours."

"I don't have one yet. That's why I'm registering."

"No token, no entry. Those are the rules."

Shen He reached into his bag and pulled out a small vial—a healing pill he'd crafted from salvaged ingredients during his journey. "I'm not lying. I can prove my skill."

The guard took the vial, uncorking it and sniffing carefully. His eyebrows rose. "This is... high quality. Grade two at least."

"Grade three, actually."

The guard exchanged a look with his companion. "Wait here."

Twenty minutes later, Shen He found himself standing in a waiting room made of white jade and polished wood. The Alchemy Guild's capital branch was a palace in its own right, with ceilings that soared thirty feet high and walls covered in murals depicting legendary alchemists of ages past.

A door opened, and a woman walked in.

She was tall, with silver-streaked hair pulled back in a severe bun and eyes that seemed to weigh everything they saw. Her robes were embroidered with golden flames—the mark of a Grandmaster Alchemist. Shen He's throat went dry.

"I am Grandmaster Wei," she said, settling into a chair across from him. "I understand you wish to register."

"Yes, Grandmaster."

"And you claim to have refined a grade three healing pill. Without formal training."

"I never said without training. Just without a guild affiliation."

Grandmaster Wei's lips twitched. "A fine distinction. Very well. I'll test you myself. If you pass, you'll receive a provisional license. If you fail, you'll be barred from registering for one year. Do you accept?"

Shen He nodded. "I do."

"Good." She produced a scroll and unrolled it, revealing a list of ingredients. "Refine this pill. You have three hours. Use the station in the adjacent room."

Shen He looked at the list and felt his confidence waver. The pill was a Meridian Stabilizer—a complex formula that required precise timing and control. It was also one he'd studied extensively during his time in the sect.

He took the scroll and walked into the adjoining chamber.

The alchemy station was beautiful—a bronze cauldron set into a stone pedestal, surrounded by shelves of ingredients in crystal jars. Shen He lit the burner, adjusted the flame, and began.

His movements were practiced, fluid. He crushed the Starfall Petals with exact pressure, ground the Moonlight Powder to the perfect consistency, added the Silver Meridian Grass at precisely the right moment. The cauldron hummed as the ingredients merged, releasing a scent like morning dew and lightning.

One hour and forty-seven minutes later, Shen He stepped back, sweating but satisfied. Inside the cauldron sat a single, perfect pill—pale blue, swirling with silver threads, radiating gentle warmth.

Grandmaster Wei examined it in silence. She held it up to the light. She sniffed it. She even touched her tongue to its surface, closing her eyes in concentration.

Finally, she set it down.

"Grade three, upper tier," she said. "Near flawless. The Meridian Stabilizer is notoriously difficult to balance. Where did you learn to refine this?"

"Sect library," Shen He said. "I read a lot."

"Clearly." She studied him with renewed interest. "You also matched the description of a certain wanted criminal, but I suspect that's a coincidence."

Shen He's heart stopped. "Grandmaster—"

"Relax." She waved a hand. "The guild doesn't care about sect politics. We care about skill. And you have it." She produced a bronze token, engraved with the symbol of a mortar and pestle. "This is a provisional license. It grants you access to guild facilities and ingredient markets. Use it wisely."

Shen He took the token, his hands barely trembling. "Thank you, Grandmaster."

"Don't thank me yet. There's a reason the guild is interested in talent like yours." She leaned forward, her voice dropping. "The Imperial Alchemist is dying. He's been the emperor's personal pill refiner for forty years, but his qi is failing. The court is desperate for a replacement."

"I'm not—"

"I know. You're a cripple. But you can refine pills. And the emperor doesn't care about cultivation—he cares about results." She paused. "There's a competition in three weeks. The winner becomes the new Imperial Alchemist. The prize includes access to the Imperial Vault—the largest collection of forbidden formulas in the kingdom."

Shen He's breath caught. "Forbidden formulas?"

"Everything the sect locked away, the empire kept. Including the original Meridian Restoration Pill formula." She smiled, and it was not a kind smile. "I saw the way you looked at that stabilizer formula. You're chasing something. Something dangerous."

"I don't know what you mean."

"Of course not." She stood, smoothing her robes. "But if you want to find it, win the competition. Or die trying. Either way, it'll be entertaining."

She left without another word, her footsteps echoing down the marble hallway.

Shen He stood alone in the alchemy chamber, the bronze token cold in his palm. The Imperial Vault. The original formula. Everything he needed to fix his broken meridians, to finally become a true cultivator.

Three weeks. He had three weeks to become the best alchemist in the capital.

He tucked the token into his robe and walked out into the evening air. The streets of Vermilion Dawn were alive with lantern light and laughter, but Shen He barely noticed. His mind was already racing through formulas, ingredients, techniques he'd need to master.

He was so lost in thought that he almost missed the figure standing in the shadows of a nearby alley.

"Hello, Shen He."

The voice was familiar. Too familiar. He turned slowly, his blood running cold.

Lin Xue stepped out of the darkness, her sword at her side, her expression unreadable. She wore civilian clothes instead of sect robes, but she was unmistakable. The Azure Cloud Sect's prodigy. The one who'd helped sentence him to exile.

"What are you doing here?" Shen He asked, his voice hard.

"Following you." She said it like it was obvious. "The sect sent me to retrieve what you stole."

"I didn't steal anything. The notes were destroyed in the explosion."

"Liar." She took a step closer. "I know you copied them. I saw you in the restricted section, remember?"

Shen He's hand drifted toward his bag, where the salvaged notes lay hidden. "They're mine. I earned them."

"The sect disagrees." Lin Xue's hand rested on her sword hilt. "Give me the formula, and I'll let you live."

"And if I refuse?"

She drew her sword. The blade caught the lantern light, gleaming like a sliver of moonlight. "Then I'll take it from your corpse."

Shen He's mind raced. He couldn't fight her. Even with his pill-enhanced body, she was a true cultivator, years ahead of him in skill and power. But he couldn't run. Not anymore. Not when the Imperial Vault was so close.

He reached into his bag and pulled out a handful of pills—stamina enhancers, pain nullifiers, combat boosters. He'd been saving them for emergencies.

This counted.

"Last chance," Lin Xue said.

Shen He popped three pills into his mouth, feeling their effects crash through his system like wildfire. His muscles tensed. His senses sharpened. The world seemed to slow down around him.

"I'm not giving up," he said. "Not now. Not ever."

Lin Xue's eyes narrowed. "Then you've chosen death."

She lunged.

title: The Poison Garden's Secret
content:
The forest swallowed Shen He whole.

Three days had passed since his exile, and the mortal world had proven exactly as he remembered it—brutal, indifferent, and filled with people who would kill you for a single spirit stone. He'd avoided the main roads, traveling instead through the untamed wilderness that bordered the Azure Cloud Sect's territory. The beasts here were weak, barely first-rank, but they were persistent. He'd killed six spirit rats in the past night alone.

His stomach growled.

Shen He sat against a gnarled oak, the crumpled page of notes spread across his knees. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula that had gotten him exiled. The formula that the sect master claimed was impossible.

But something didn't add up.

He'd studied the scroll for weeks before attempting the refinement. The alchemical principles were sound. The ingredient combinations made sense. If the pill truly didn't work, why had the ancient alchemists gone to such lengths to document it? Why had they locked it away instead of destroying it?

"Because they wanted someone to find it," he muttered, tracing the faded characters with his finger. "They knew it would take someone desperate enough to try."

Someone like him.

He was about to fold the page when he noticed something he'd missed before—a small symbol in the corner, nearly invisible against the yellowed paper. It was a lotus flower, its petals arranged in a spiral pattern that seemed to pull his eyes inward.

The Alchemy Guild's mark.

Shen He's blood ran cold. The Meridian Restoration Pill wasn't a forbidden relic of the Azure Cloud Sect. It was a formula stolen from the Alchemy Guild. And if the guild knew he had it—

A branch snapped behind him.

Shen He moved on instinct, rolling to the side as a blade whistled through the space where his head had been. He came up in a crouch, a handful of powder already pinched between his fingers.

Three figures emerged from the shadows. They wore black robes, their faces hidden behind porcelain masks painted with laughing demons. Assassins. Professional ones.

"Hand over the formula," the lead assassin said, his voice distorted by the mask. "And we'll let you live."

"You're with the guild," Shen He said, his mind racing. "They sent you."

"The guild doesn't send us. We're independent contractors. But the guild pays well for information about stolen property." The assassin tilted his head. "You're Shen He. Exiled disciple of the Azure Cloud Sect. Thief of forbidden alchemy. The guild has placed a bounty on your head—five thousand spirit stones for your capture, ten thousand for the formula."

Ten thousand spirit stones. That was enough to buy a small city.

"I don't have the formula," Shen He lied, tucking the page into his sleeve. "I memorized it and destroyed the original. Kill me, and it dies with me."

The assassins exchanged glances. "Then we'll just have to take you alive. Brothers, if you would."

The two flanking assassins lunged.

Shen He threw the powder in his hand, a mixture of crushed sleeping herbs and concentrated qi disruptor. The first assassin inhaled it, his eyes going wide before he collapsed mid-step. The second dodged, but the movement put him off balance.

Shen He didn't have a weapon. He didn't need one.

He closed the distance in two steps, driving his palm into the assassin's throat. The man gurgled, clutching his neck as he fell. Shen He grabbed his falling blade and spun to face the leader.

The lead assassin hadn't moved.

"Impressive," the man said, clapping slowly. "For a cripple, you fight well. But you're out of tricks, boy. I've been watching you for three days. I know you only had one dose of that powder. I know you're exhausted, hungry, and running on fumes."

He drew his own blade—a curved saber that hummed with qi. "I, on the other hand, am a fourth-rank cultivator. I can cut through your pathetic defenses like paper. So I'll ask one more time: give me the formula, or die."

Shen He's grip tightened on the stolen sword. His body screamed for rest. His qi reserves were nearly empty. He had nothing left.

But he'd learned something in the sect's library that the assassins didn't know.

"I'll make you a counter-offer," Shen He said, forcing calm into his voice. "You let me walk away, and I won't kill you."

The assassin laughed. "You? Kill me? Boy, you can barely stand."

"Then why are you still talking?"

The laughter stopped.

Shen He reached into his bag and pulled out a small jade vial. The liquid inside glowed faintly green—his last creation, brewed the night before his exile. He'd meant it as a backup, a final desperate measure if things went wrong.

Things had gone very wrong.

"Do you know what this is?" he asked, holding up the vial.

The assassin's eyes narrowed. "Poison?"

"Not just poison. This is a Soul-Binding Toxin. One drop on your skin, and your qi will start to decay. Your cultivation will erode, layer by layer, until you're nothing but a mortal. And then, your organs will follow."

"You're bluffing."

"Am I?" Shen He uncorked the vial, and a sickly sweet smell filled the air. The assassin took an involuntary step back. "I spent six months developing this formula. It's never been tested on a human before. Would you like to be the first?"

The assassin's hand trembled on his saber. "That's impossible. Only a fourth-rank alchemist can create a Soul-Binding Toxin. You're a cripple."

"I'm a pharmacist," Shen He said, his voice cold. "And I'm very, very good at my job."

For a long moment, they stood frozen, two figures in the dark forest, each waiting for the other to blink. The assassin's qi flared, testing him, probing for weakness. Shen He held his ground, the vial steady in his hand.

Finally, the assassin lowered his saber.

"You're insane," he said. "You know that?"

"I've been told."

"The guild will send others. Better ones. They won't give you a chance to talk."

"Then I'll have to make sure they don't find me."

The assassin sheathed his blade. "This isn't over, boy. The formula belongs to the guild. They'll hunt you to the ends of the earth if they have to."

"Let them."

Shen He backed away slowly, never taking his eyes off the assassin. When he was twenty paces clear, he turned and ran, crashing through the underbrush, his heart pounding in his chest.

He didn't stop running until dawn.

---

The cave was small, barely large enough for him to sit upright, but it was hidden behind a waterfall and would have to do. Shen He collapsed against the damp stone wall, his body shaking with exhaustion and adrenaline.

He'd survived.

Barely.

He looked at the jade vial in his hand. The green liquid was still glowing, still ready to deliver a death that didn't exist. The Soul-Binding Toxin was a bluff—it was nothing more than fermented spirit berries and crushed fireweed, a concoction that would cause mild nausea at best.

But the assassin hadn't known that.

Shen He laughed, the sound hollow and bitter. He was a fraud. A cripple with a head full of alchemical formulas and nothing to show for it. He couldn't cultivate. He couldn't fight. All he could do was pretend.

He was about to close his eyes when he noticed something strange.

The lotus symbol on his formula page.

It was glowing.

Shen He scrambled to unfold the paper, his heart racing. The symbol pulsed with a soft, golden light, and as he watched, it began to shift, the petals rearranging themselves into new characters.

*Follow the path of seven stones. The garden awaits.*

Shen He stared at the message, his mind reeling. The Alchemy Guild's mark was more than just a stamp of ownership—it was a key. A guide. A map to something hidden.

But what?

He didn't have time to ponder. Outside the cave, he heard voices—more assassins, tracking him, closing in. He had minutes, maybe seconds.

He looked at the message again.

*The garden awaits.*

There was only one garden he knew of that the Alchemy Guild would care about. The legendary Poison Garden of the Forgotten Sage, a mythical location said to contain herbs that had long since gone extinct. Herbs that could cure any disease. Herbs that could kill immortals.

Herbs that could make a cripple cultivate.

The goal had been right in front of him all along. The Meridian Restoration Pill wasn't the answer. It was only the beginning.

Shen He folded the paper and tucked it into his robes. The assassins were getting closer, their footsteps echoing through the forest.

He smiled.

"Seven stones," he whispered. "Let's see where this path leads."

He slipped out of the cave's hidden entrance, moving deeper into the wilderness as the assassins' voices faded behind him. The hunt was far from over.

But for the first time in his life, Shen He wasn't running away from something.

He was running toward it.

---

*Deep within the Alchemy Guild's headquarters, a woman with silver hair and piercing golden eyes watched a glowing map on her desk. A single point of light moved across the wilderness, heading east.*

*"He found the message," she murmured. "Faster than I expected."*

*Beside her, an old man with alchemical burns scarred across his face chuckled. "The boy has potential. Shame the sect threw him away."*

*"The sect's loss is our gain." The woman traced the light with her finger. "Prepare a welcoming party. If he reaches the Poison Garden, I want to be there to greet him."*

*"And if he doesn't?"*

*She smiled, and the temperature in the room dropped. "Then he wasn't worth our time."*

---

**END OF CHAPTER 16**

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The City of a Thousand Poisons

The cart jolted over another rut in the road, sending a spike of pain through Shen He's ribs. Three days of travel had done nothing to heal the damage from Elder Wu's attack, but at least he was still alive. That counted for something.

He pressed his hand against his chest, feeling the faint warmth of the jade token beneath his robes. The Alchemy Guild's invitation. His only lead.

"You sure about this place, boy?" The cart driver, a grizzled old man named Huo, spat over the side. "Thousand Poison City ain't exactly welcoming to strangers."

"I don't have much choice."

"Hmph. Your funeral." Huo cracked his whip, and the donkey picked up its pace. "Just remember—don't drink the water. Don't eat the food. And for heaven's sake, don't make eye contact with anyone wearing purple robes."

"Purple robes?"

"Death Lotus Sect." Huo's voice dropped. "They run the city. Every alchemist there answers to them, one way or another. You want to join the Guild? You'll have to kiss their rings first."

Shen He's hand drifted to the pill pouch at his belt. He'd spent the journey refining what little he had left—three Meridian Cleansing Pills, two Qi Recovery Pills, and one experimental concoction he'd named "Last Resort." The name felt fitting.

The city appeared on the horizon like a wound in the earth. Black walls rose from the gray landscape, topped with spires that seemed to drink the light. Plumes of colored smoke rose from countless chimneys—green, purple, red—each one marking an alchemist's workshop.

"The City of a Thousand Poisons," Huo said, pulling the cart to a stop. "This is as far as I go. The gates are a mile ahead. Good luck, boy. You'll need it."

Shen He climbed down, his legs shaky from days of sitting. He pressed a spirit stone into Huo's hand. "Thank you."

"Don't thank me yet." Huo turned the cart around and left without another word.

The walk to the gates was silent. The road was empty, the fields barren. Nothing grew within sight of the city walls. Shen He could taste the air—bitter, metallic, laced with a hundred different toxins. His body hummed in response, his meridian-crippled system recognizing the poisons as fuel.

*Interesting.*

The gates were open, but not unguarded. Two figures stood in the shadows—tall, gaunt, their faces hidden behind bone-white masks. Purple robes hung from their thin frames like funeral shrouds.

"State your business," one said, his voice hollow.

"I'm here to join the Alchemy Guild."

The guards exchanged glances. "You have an invitation?"

Shen He produced the jade token. The guard's eyes widened—or at least, his mask seemed to shift.

"Where did you get this?"

"Someone gave it to me. Said I'd find answers here."

The guard took the token, turning it over in his hands. "This is a Master's token. Only seven exist. The last one was given to an alchemist who died fifty years ago."

"I'm not dead."

"No. You're not." The guard returned the token. "You'll need to see the City Lord. He'll decide if you're worthy of the Guild."

"Where do I find him?"

"The Obsidian Spire. In the center of the city. Don't wander. Don't touch anything. And if you hear screaming, ignore it."

Shen He nodded and stepped through the gates.

The city was a nightmare dressed in color.

Every building was stained with years of chemical residue—greens, blues, purples, reds, all bleeding into each other like a painter's palette left in the rain. The streets were narrow, winding, filled with people who moved like shadows. Some wore masks. Some wore veils. Some wore nothing at all, their skin covered in tattoos that glowed with trapped qi.

Alchemists. All of them.

Shen He kept his head down and walked. He passed shops selling bottled qi, stalls offering pills of every color, and alleyways where cultivators traded body parts for medicines. The smell was overwhelming—sweet and rotten and sharp all at once.

A hand grabbed his arm.

He spun, a pill already between his fingers, ready to throw.

"Easy, easy." The man who'd grabbed him was short, round, with a face that looked too cheerful for this place. He wore a stained apron and carried a basket of mushrooms. "You're new. I can tell. No one walks like that unless they're fresh off the cart."

"What do you want?"

"To help." The man smiled, revealing teeth stained green. "Name's Bao. I'm a guide. For a small fee, I can show you around, keep you from making fatal mistakes."

"I don't have much to pay."

"You have that token." Bao's eyes flickered to Shen He's belt. "That's worth more than anything in this city. But I won't take it. Just... a favor. Someday."

Shen He hesitated. But the city was labyrinthine, and he had no time to get lost.

"Fine. Show me to the Obsidian Spire."

Bao's smile widened. "Follow me. And try not to breathe too deeply."

The journey through Thousand Poison City was a lesson in survival.

Bao pointed out landmarks—the Poison Lotus Temple, where the Death Lotus Sect held their rituals; the Alchemy Bazaar, where deals were made in blood and spirit stones; the Execution Square, where failed alchemists were displayed as warnings.

"Don't fail your Guild trial," Bao said, nodding toward the square. "The last failure is still hanging there. Has been for three years."

Shen He looked. A skeleton in tattered robes dangled from a post, its bones stained black from accumulated toxins.

"I'll keep that in mind."

They reached the Obsidian Spire as the sun began to set. The tower was a monolith of black stone, windowless, doorless, surrounded by a moat of bubbling green liquid.

"How do I get in?"

"You don't." Bao pointed to a bridge that was lowering from the tower's upper levels. "The City Lord comes to you. Or he doesn't. It's his choice."

The bridge touched down, and a figure emerged.

She was tall, elegant, her face hidden behind a veil of purple silk. Her robes were embroidered with golden lotuses, and her hands were covered in rings—each one a different poison, each one potent enough to kill a cultivator in seconds.

"Shen He," she said, her voice musical and deadly. "I've been expecting you."

"You know my name?"

"I know everything that happens in my city." She descended the bridge, her steps silent. "I am Lady Yue, City Lord of Thousand Poison City and Head of the Death Lotus Sect. You carry a Master's token. That makes you my guest."

"Guest? Or prisoner?"

Her lips curved beneath the veil. "That depends on what you can do. Come. We have much to discuss."

She turned and walked back up the bridge. Shen He followed, his heart racing.

The inside of the Obsidian Spire was nothing like the city outside. It was clean, white, filled with light that seemed to come from nowhere. The walls were lined with shelves of scrolls, each one glowing with preserved knowledge.

Lady Yue led him to a chamber at the tower's heart. A circular room, empty except for a single table and two chairs. She sat, gesturing for him to do the same.

"You want to join the Alchemy Guild," she said. "Why?"

"Because I need answers."

"What answers?"

Shen He met her eyes. "Why my meridians are crippled. Why I can absorb qi through pills. And why someone gave me a token that belonged to a dead man."

Lady Yue was silent for a long moment. Then she laughed.

"Oh, this is rich." She leaned forward. "You don't know, do you? You have no idea what you are."

"What am I?"

She reached into her sleeve and produced a scroll, unrolling it on the table. The characters were ancient, written in a language Shen He didn't recognize. But the diagram was clear—a human body, its meridians glowing, with a single, massive pill at its center.

"Your condition isn't a defect," Lady Yue said. "It's a feature. You're a Pill Body. A human born to be consumed."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "What?"

"The ancient alchemists created the first Pill Bodies a thousand years ago. They were meant to be living cauldrons—humans who could refine pills from within their own bodies. But the process was flawed. The Pill Bodies were unstable. They were hunted, killed, and their pills harvested."

She smiled, and there was nothing kind about it.

"You, Shen He, are the last known Pill Body in existence. And there are people in this city who would kill you, grind your bones, and sell your organs for a single drop of your blood."

Shen He's hand moved to his pill pouch.

"Don't," Lady Yue said. "I'm not your enemy. I'm your only friend. Because I can teach you how to use what you are. I can make you strong enough to survive."

"And what do you want in return?"

"Simple." She leaned back. "When the time comes, I want you to refine a pill for me. The Ultimate Pill. The one that can make a mortal a god."

Shen He stared at her, the words echoing in his mind.

*The Ultimate Pill.*

The same pill he'd been trying to create since the beginning.

"How do you know about that?"

"Because I've been watching you, Shen He. Since the day you were born." She reached into her robes and pulled out a familiar object—a jade token, identical to the one he carried. "Your mother gave you that token. Did you know that?"

His world tilted. "My mother?"

"She was a Pill Body too. The last one before you. She fled the sect that created her, hid in the mortal world, and gave birth to you before they found her." Lady Yue's voice softened. "She died to protect you, Shen He. Don't let her sacrifice be in vain."

Shen He's hands were shaking. "Where is she? Where is she buried?"

"She's not buried." Lady Yue stood, walking to the room's single window. "They took her body. Dissected it. Studied it. And when they were done, they burned what was left."

The words hit him like a physical blow.

"Who?"

"The Azure Cloud Sect." Lady Yue turned to face him. "The same sect that exiled you. They've been hunting Pill Bodies for centuries. Your mother was their greatest prize. And now that they know you exist, they'll come for you too."

Shen He's mind raced. The sect master's words echoed in his ears—*the ancient alchemists who created it were fools*—but Lady Yue's words painted a different picture.

"They lied," he said, his voice hollow. "About everything."

"Of course they did." Lady Yue smiled. "But now you know the truth. And with truth comes choice. You can run. Hide. Try to survive on your own. Or..." She extended her hand. "You can stay. Learn. Grow strong enough to make them pay."

Shen He looked at her hand. At the rings on her fingers, each one a weapon.

He thought of Lin Xue's cold eyes. Elder Wu's sneer. The sect master's lies.

He thought of his mother. Burned. Diss

title: The Poison in the Pill
content:
The gates of the Azure Cloud Sect groaned shut behind Shen He, the sound reverberating through his bones like the closing of a tomb. He stood at the edge of the mountain path, the wind tugging at his robes, carrying the scent of pine and distant rain. Below him, the mortal world sprawled like an unkempt garden—chaotic, unpredictable, and utterly indifferent to the fall of a crippled alchemist.

He took a step forward. Then another.

The path wound down through layers of mist, each step carrying him further from the only home he'd ever known. Three years. Three years of scrubbing floors, organizing scrolls, and enduring the sneers of disciples who could cultivate qi with a single breath. And now this.

His hand drifted to his pocket, where the crumpled page of notes rested like a dying ember. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula that was supposed to fix him. The formula that the sect master claimed would kill him.

*But I'm still alive,* Shen He thought. *The explosion didn't kill me. The backlash didn't kill me. So what did I miss?*

He stopped mid-path, pulling out the page. The ink was smeared in places, the characters half-destroyed by the blast, but enough remained to read. His eyes traced the ingredients, the heating times, the condensation cycles. Everything seemed correct. Everything except...

A shadow fell over the page.

Shen He looked up to find a young woman standing ten feet away, her robes pristine white, her hair bound in a silver clasp. She held no weapon, but her cultivation aura pressed against him like a wall of water.

"You're Shen He," she said. No question. Just a statement of fact.

"Who wants to know?"

"My name is Yun Fei. Outer disciple of the Crimson Lotus Sect." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I've been watching you for some time now."

Shen He's muscles tensed. "I don't recall ever meeting you."

"You wouldn't. I was never close enough for you to notice." She stepped closer, and he caught the faint scent of burning herbs—something sharp and medicinal. "But I noticed you. The crippled boy who somehow survived a failed pill refinement that should have incinerated him. The crippled boy who walked into the sect master's hall and walked out alive. That's not nothing."

"What do you want?"

"Information." She tilted her head, studying him like a curious cat. "The Azure Cloud Sect threw you out for attempting a forbidden pill. But they didn't kill you. Do you know why?"

"Because the sect master has a soft spot for charity cases."

Yun Fei laughed—a short, sharp sound. "No. They didn't kill you because they couldn't figure out how you survived. The Meridian Restoration Pill has a hundred-year history of killing everyone who attempts it. The formula itself is cursed. But you? You walked away with nothing more than a few bruises and a destroyed alchemy furnace."

Shen He's blood ran cold. "How do you know all this?"

"Because the Crimson Lotus Sect has been studying that formula for thirty years." Her smile widened. "And we've made some... modifications."

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a scroll, unrolling it to reveal a series of diagrams and characters that made Shen He's breath catch in his throat. It was the Meridian Restoration Pill formula. But different. The ingredients were rearranged, the heating times altered, and at the bottom, a single note written in crimson ink:

*"Insert True Phoenix Blood at the final condensation stage. Failure to do so results in meridian destruction within three hours of consumption."*

Shen He's hands trembled. "True Phoenix Blood? That's a myth. Phoenixes don't exist."

"They don't anymore," Yun Fei agreed. "But their blood does. The Crimson Lotus Sect has three vials, passed down through generations. And we're willing to give you one."

"Why?"

"Because we want what you have." She rolled up the scroll and tucked it away. "You're a natural alchemist, Shen He. You might not have cultivation potential, but your body processes pill energy in ways that defy explanation. The sect that controls you controls the future of alchemy."

"I'm not interested in being controlled."

"Then don't be controlled. Take the blood. Refine the pill. Become something more than a cripple." She stepped back, her aura receding. "The Crimson Lotus Sect doesn't want a slave. We want an ally. Someone who can help us unlock the secrets of the ancient formulas."

Shen He stared at her, his mind racing. This was a trap. It had to be a trap. The Azure Cloud Sect had exiled him, and now another sect was offering him exactly what he needed? The timing was too perfect.

But the alternative was returning to the mortal world as a cripple with nothing but a pocket full of failed dreams.

"What's the catch?" he asked.

"No catch. Just a request." Yun Fei's eyes glittered. "When you succeed—and I believe you will—you'll owe the Crimson Lotus Sect a favor. One favor. Nothing more."

"One favor that could get me killed."

"Or make you a god." She shrugged. "Take the risk, or walk away. The choice is yours."

Shen He looked down at the crumpled page in his hand. The Meridian Restoration Pill. The formula that had ruined his life. The formula that could save it.

He thought of Lin Xue's cold eyes. Elder Wu's sneer. The sect master's final words.

*I'll go.*

But where? Back to nothing? Or forward into the unknown?

"I'll take the deal," he said.

Yun Fei's smile widened into something genuine. "Excellent. Follow me."

She turned and walked down the path, her steps light and confident. Shen He hesitated for only a moment before following.

---

Three hours later, they arrived at a small clearing hidden in the mountains, far from any sect territory. A single hut stood in the center, its roof patched with dried leaves, its walls covered in climbing vines. It looked abandoned.

Yun Fei pushed open the door and gestured for Shen He to enter.

Inside, the hut was surprisingly clean. A single table dominated the center, covered in alchemy tools—mortars, pestles, measuring cups, and a small furnace that glowed with residual heat. Scrolls lined the walls, hundreds of them, each labeled with names Shen He recognized: Foundation Establishment Pill, Qi Gathering Elixir, Meridian Expansion Decoction.

"This is our hidden laboratory," Yun Fei said. "We've been operating here for five years, studying the forbidden formulas that the major sects have locked away."

"Why?"

"Because the major sects are cowards." She picked up a mortar and began grinding something—a dark root that smelled of earth and copper. "They claim the forbidden formulas are too dangerous, but the truth is simpler: they're afraid of what they don't understand. The ancient alchemists knew things that modern cultivators have forgotten. The Meridian Restoration Pill is just one example."

Shen He approached the table, his eyes scanning the tools. "You said the formula needs True Phoenix Blood at the final condensation stage. Why?"

"Because the pill creates new meridians. But without a stabilizing agent, those meridians collapse under their own weight. True Phoenix Blood is the only substance resilient enough to hold them open." Yun Fei set down the mortar and turned to face him. "But there's something else you should know."

"What?"

"The Azure Cloud Sect's master lied to you."

Shen He's heart stopped. "What?"

"He said the pill kills the user. That's true—if you refine it without the blood. But he also said it's impossible to refine it correctly. That's a lie." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "The Azure Cloud Sect has a vial of True Phoenix Blood hidden in their treasury. They've had it for three centuries. They could have fixed the formula at any time."

"Then why didn't they?"

"Because the sect master's father was an alchemist who died attempting the pill. The sect master blames the formula for his father's death. He's been suppressing it ever since, destroying any research that might lead to its completion." Yun Fei's eyes burned with anger. "Your exile wasn't justice. It was revenge."

Shen He staggered back, the words hitting him like physical blows. The sect master had known. All this time, he'd known the pill could be perfected, and he'd chosen to let it rot. He'd chosen to let Shen He believe he was broken.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I want you to understand what you're fighting against." Yun Fei reached into her robes and pulled out a small vial—crimson liquid that seemed to glow from within. "This is the True Phoenix Blood. Our last vial. If you fail, we won't have another chance."

Shen He stared at the vial. The key to everything he'd ever wanted was sitting in front of him, inches from his grasp.

"One question," he said. "Why does the Crimson Lotus Sect care so much about a crippled alchemist?"

Yun Fei smiled, but this time there was something sad in her eyes. "Because we're all cripples here, Shen He. Every alchemist in our sect was born without cultivation potential. We were discarded by our families, rejected by the major sects, left to die in the gutters. The only thing we have is our knowledge."

She held out the vial.

"Help us prove that we're more than our limitations."

Shen He took the vial. The glass was warm against his palm, pulsing with a faint energy that seemed to resonate with his own pulse.

"When do we start?"

"Now." Yun Fei gestured to the furnace. "I've prepared the base ingredients. All that's left is the final refinement."

Shen He approached the furnace, the vial clutched in his hand. The heat washed over him, familiar and comforting. He'd spent countless hours in alchemy chambers, dreaming of this moment. And now it was here.

He began.

The base ingredients were already mixed, a dark liquid that steamed in the heat. Shen He added the secondary components one by one, measuring each with surgical precision. The liquid changed color, shifting from black to deep purple to a shimmering gold.

His hands moved faster, muscle memory taking over. He'd refined this pill a hundred times in his mind, tracing each step, each temperature change, each condensation cycle. He knew the formula better than he knew his own name.

The final condensation stage approached.

Shen He uncorked the vial of True Phoenix Blood. The liquid inside seemed to writhe, alive with ancient power.

"Remember," Yun Fei said. "Add it slowly. Drop by drop. The blood needs time to integrate."

Shen He nodded, his focus absolute. He tilted the vial, letting a single drop fall into the golden liquid.

The furnace exploded.

Shen He was thrown backward, slamming into the wall. The world spun, his ears ringing. Smoke filled the hut, thick and acrid.

"What happened?" he coughed. "I did everything right!"

Yun Fei's voice came from somewhere in the smoke, cold and distant. "You did. That's the problem."

The smoke cleared, and Shen He saw Yun Fei standing by the furnace—completely unharmed. In her hand, she held the vial of True Phoenix Blood. Still full.

"You never added it," Shen He realized.

"No." She smiled, and this time there was nothing but cruelty in it. "I didn't."

"Why?"

"Because we don't want you to succeed, Shen He. We want to study you." She stepped

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Ghost in the Porcelain Mask
